title: Braised Lemon Pepper Chicken Legs Recipe
author: Sohla El-Waylly

comments.commentBody: The flavor is wonderful. The recipe is needlessly brief in places where a little more info would help. The oven time is way too short; mine needed another 20 minutes at 350. (A half hour might do it at 375.) I served the finished legs over rice with lots of the sauce poured over. Delicious.
Do I even need the oven?  I could just cover the skillet and simmer on the stovetop.
@AJPeabody I made it on the stove and just let it simmer covered for thirty ish minutes and it turned out great!
@AJPeabody you’ll likely get a similar result. But I always like to put things in the oven so I don’t have to watch the heat and the liquid level as you do on the stove top.

Yes, I would cover the skillet and simmer on the stovetop for about 30 minutes this time of year when I am still using the air conditioning in the late afternoon and evening so as not to further heat the house.

Thank you to TonyNC! The Only commenter -  out of 10 - who seems to have actually tried the recipe!..  
I mean, maybe I’m missing something…. 
Now I’m beginning to wonder from other 9 if I could add salted caramels with chicken to possibly thicken the sauce and then put it all in a Vita-mix  blender if I ever get one.

A chicken leg is a pretty puny portion for a grown adult. I'd be inclined to use the whole leg/thigh portion instead of just the drumsticks. This recipe is right up my alley; cheap, simple, and lemony. Maybe even throw in a few wings for a little something extra. Perhaps wilt some sorrel leaves to serve alongside. This is the ideal tariff-free dinner for summer. Grow your own sorrel (this is super-easy with a large container and a bag or two of good soil), and you're really getting off cheap.
A chicken leg consists of a thigh and a drumstick and that's what the picture shows.

@Linda Kaufmann most grocery stores do not sell the leg and thigh portion still in tact. In Decatur, Georgia we have Kroger, Publix and Walmart. You would be hard pressed to find the whole leg/thigh portion at any of these. I shop at Dekalb International Farmers Market, where you can find anything!  You can also purchase the exact amount you need. Say enough for two people and not a pre-packaged portion of meat, like from a butcher. The chicken sold here is Bell and Evans. The spice wall is unbelievable, from all over the world, fresh and inexpensive compared to other purveyors. We are quite fortunate to have such a wonderful place  so near by. If ever in Atlanta, drop by, you will be overjoyed.

I substituted chicken broth for water at each step, then simmered covered on the stove top until the thigh reached 180° rather than in the oven .  Then removed the chicken to a plate and boiled the liquid to a sauce consistency. Served over rice with the sauce.

My sauce tasted like raw onion mixed with bitter lemon peel (and it was greenish brown??) Maybe too big an onion and then not enough water? And perhaps the lemon had too thick a peel? Would be much preferred if onion was by weight.

This was lovely as is, though I did need to cook the marylands a bit longer and higher to achieve crispy skin. I added some halved chat potatoes to cook with the chicken and steamed broccoli to serve. Next time I might try sautéing the onion and garlic before blending for a bit more flavour.

I was looking forward to making this recipe, especially the sauce, but it was not what I hoped.  The chicken was tender and juicy, but I thought the lemon and garlic were a little too much for me.  Maybe I didn't salt and pepper it enough.

I intend to try this recipe soon. However, one comment on reading thru the recipe - If you intend to follow the cooking instructions, makes sure to begin the stove-top preparation in an oven -proof frying pan! Not all frying pans have oven proof handles or lids!
Wonder if an immersion blender will work for this (that’s all I have at the moment). It doesn’t seem likely with raw onion unless I add too much liquid. Hmmm.
Yes. In step 6 add the water as called for so it is half way up the pieces. Now remove the chicken and you’ll have all the liquid needed for an immersion blender.
This recipe was great! 10/10. I used bone-in, skin-on chicken thighs instead of legs but everything else I followed to a T. I also paired it with the recommended green salad- that was great too!

I found this recipe a little light on flavor. It had a lemony taste but lacked a contrasting flavor like the brine and umami of olives. I know that's not Bangladesh. I can't quite place what's missing.

I made this with 8 drumsticks and doubled the recipe. It had more of an onion taste than I preferred and this did not 'carmalize' in my cast iron pan. I would definitely add more turmeric than called for.  I made toasted brown rice and added currants, turmeric. I also added cut asparagus to the chicken when it came out of the oven and it cooked it perfectly (al dente for us). I salted liberally like the recipe called for.
Did I miss something?  The writer lists “heat oven to 350 F” but what goes in the oven?  The chicken is still sitting in the plate & still raw inside.
see step 6
i love this food
Has anyone adapted this for bone-in chicken breasts?

@Karen I bet it would work but you would definitely want to cook it for less time - breasts aren’t really fatty enough to braise so it might over cook. Just take it out of the oven when the temp is 145-150 I would say (I know they are supposed to be cooked to 165 per the FDA but that’s actually overkill - as long as they get to 155 for a few minutes they are fine). Let us know how it goes!
I just noticed the recipe is written by Sohla! Definitely trying it this weekend now!

Yummy, and easier than anticipated.
Substitutions: bone broth for water, shallot for onion, and just five chicken drumsticks in a 10” cast iron skillet. LG induction cooktop set at level 4 of 10 was plenty hot; an Inkbird probe thermometer, inserted before moving to the oven, began at 147° and reached 165° maybe 20 to 25 minutes later (convection, actual temp 340°).
Spooned the gravy over boiled Yukon gold potatoes. 
This will be back soon on our table!

title: Mustardy Sheet-Pan Salmon With Greens  Recipe
author: Sohla El-Waylly

comments.commentBody: Good honey mustard sauce. Cooked the salmon to 120 F.  Just right.  I don’t think a sheet pan is the way to cook chard/spinach, it dries it out.  Just sauté it on the stove while the salmon cooks.  Don’t take this sheet pan thing too far.

@RPR
Maybe sheet pan too big?  There’s a lot of liquid and only 15 minutes
Yes, remove the salmon skin after cooking. Then put it back on the now-empty sheet pan and back in the oven for another 5 minutes. Crumble it over everything else. It's my favorite part.
This was just great, easy and tasty. I doubled the sauce and might even triple it next time, and sliced the garlic which was fine. Used Swiss chard which we prefer to spinach.
This is a great recipe!  Comes together easily and clean up is a breeze - weeknight keeper. I made it a little more brothy which we liked with rice.

SO GOOD!!! I used Swiss Chard, as my garden is lush with it currently, but this would be amazing with mustard greens, kale, you name it!!! Pick your favorite!!! I added coarsely-chopped yellow onion to the greens and didn't have an issue with anything getting super dried out. I like when my greens have a touch of char on them (onions, too!). And I used honey, as I don't care for maple syrup. Took the hint of other posters and doubled the sauce; VERY glad I did! Amazing!!!
But removing the skin removes a lot of taste, since that is where the fat is.
@Paladin and healthiest part
Even if i omitted the spinach or chard i wouldn't make this again

Excuse me. The crisp salmon skin is the best part. Don’t waste it. 
Also, I cooked greens and salmon separately, then plated. Don’t see the point of cooking together.
Delicious! And very easy. I had a lot of spinach so I divided everything between two sheet pans. Next time I will make extra sauce. Thank you for the recipe!

I only had broccoli on hand, so I roasted that separately and didn't make it in the same pan as the salmon. Followed the rest of the recipe exactly, halved for two people and baked for 15 minutes at 400 in an 8x8 ceramic dish. Served with mashed potatoes and the roasted broccoli. Absolutely delicious flavors. My husband, who is not a salmon fan, even liked it enough to finish it which is about as high praise as one could hope for. Another winner from NYT Cooking!
Cooking the spinach with the salmon is a terrible idea. It absolutely scorched the spinach.
@Not My Fav really?
@Not My Fav did you use baby spinach by chance? I can see it happening with that. The recipe calls for mature spinach or chard and I think there’s a reason for that

@Not My Fav
I’m wondering how accurate your oven is if your greens were scorched. Periodically I use an oven thermometer to make sure the oven temp is accurate.
Has anyone tried this on a grill with aluminum foil?

Do you think this would work with cod?  I just bought some at the market and saw this recipe when I got home--I'd like to use up some greens I have in the fridge and was thinking about putting them to use, but swapping the fish.
Probably too late for you now, Lisa, but yes, I'm sure it would work well with cod. You'd possibly just need to adjust the cooking time? I don't know ... just a thought!

I wish I had seen this recipe before I went to the grocery store, where I happened to pick up salmon and baby spinach  (as opposed to adult spinach -- and no dill). So I used tarragon (different flavor profile, I realize). And I added the baby spinach to the sheet pan in just the last three minutes. Served over rice. Bottle line: a quick and tasty dinner!
Has anyone made this with mustard greens? I'm wondering if they would be good here.

@Mendy. I do it all the time, but without using any foil. Just dump it directly on grill skin side down. When you are done cooking, the salmon meat will pull away from skin when you pick it up. The skin will be slightly stuck to the grill (but usually just barely).  You can then spick that off and eat right on the spot. Crunchy and delicious.
We loved it. Kept the salmon rare by cooking at 375° for 14 minutes.
Absolutely love this, sued chard for it and will definitely make this recipe again.
Great recipe, the maple syrup in the dill sauce is a brainwave!

I followed the recipe exactly, and it came out perfect. I used Swiss chard, and it was so good with a little taste of vermouth, and some of the edges were a little crispy, which I liked. I only used 2 salmon fillets, but made all of the mustard sauce. It worked out just right. Cooked at 400 for 15 minutes and both the salmon and Swiss chard were cooked just right.

Great recipe! Salmon came out great at the described time and temperature, and the mustard sauce is just aggressive enough. We cooked ours in an 8x8 metal pan with rainbow chard, and used half sake and vegetable broth since we had sake to use up. The chard closer to the surface became like kale chips when cooked, while the chard on the bottom got more of the juices.

This is excellent! I left the skin on and used Swiss chard. I may apply the technique of putting the fish on sliced lemons and wine to other recipes because it tastes great and the fish can't stick to the pan. I agree with the suggestion of adding rice - it would really complement this.
Another Sohla banger. We absolutely loved this - the white wine is a great add. Used baby spinach and was diligent about shifting it around every 3 mins or so, so it didn’t burn. Highly recommend!

Believe it or not, the grocery was out of lemons! I used a navel orange. Was delicious. I cooked the (pre-washed baby) spinach separately on the stovetop as recommended by others. Served with baked sweet potato (microwaved) and crusty bread. Had this meal on the table in 30 minutes.

Made this with collards, otherwise as written. The salmon and marinade were excellent, but the collards turned out not to be a great choice; they were a bit too tough. Will make this again, using spinach.
This was delicious! I used skin on salmon filets from Costco and then peeled them off (and gave them to the dog) after cooking. I used a dry white Bordeaux. Adding this to the weekly rotation.
Wonderful meal

This is a fantastic recipe to play with. I used honey and lime in place of maple syrup and lemon and doubled the garlic. Next time I’ll double the marinade and drizzle it on the accompanying carb when I serve it. Thank you for sharing this

While I wouldn’t describe this as a pretty dish (apologies to the author) it more than made up for it with flavor. I only baked two portions so had extra sauce leftover, which I think would be good on just about anything. I would like to try it on pork chops. At the temp and time the greens do wilt significantly. I only used 1 medium bunch of spinach for the two but will definitely use extra spinach next time. Very good! I used vegetable broth instead of wine. It mostly evaporates.

I used salmon pieces with skin that I marinated in the sauce for a few hours (in a plastic bag that I have cleaned and reused a number of times, gentle NYT readers).

I grilled the salmon at a low temperature to avoid having the sugars burn and I wiped the marinade off the skin before putting it on the grill. I could see doing the same marinade with chicken. 

Meanwhile, I sautéed spinach on the stove and boiled some pasta. I drizzled the pasta with some of the sauce that I had kept separately and added a little olive oil, but I don’t know if I’d do that again with the pasta. I didn’t bother with the lemon anywhere.

It’s definitely dill forward, so if that is not your herb, I recommend switching it out for parsley, or just use an entirely different recipe.

I thought the salmon was great!

@cocomero

Cocomero….i too clean reuse plastic bags.   Thanks for posting that…..it reminds me not alls lost.
The sauce was a little sweet for my taste, but my family thought it was well balanced. If I were making it to my taste again I would omit the maple syrup.
I don’t use maple syrup because I don’t think salmon needs sweet and kept the skin. I eat it for the minerals. I also used swiss chard. Love this recipe for its ease and quickness!
When I cooked this dish last night, the liquid from the spinach watered down the sauce. You might try cooking the spinach separately.

Loved the sauce, but doubled it as many suggested, which I think was a mistake as I could taste the sauce more than I could the pricey wild-caught Alaskan Coho salmon I'd purchased.  Seems like the balance was off and I should have stuck with the recipe exactly.  Usually, doubling the sauce in most NYT recipes works great - this time, no.   Chard from our garden cooked fine in about 13 minutes.

@dee

Yeah, I’d only use this sauce for less fancy salmon, other fish, or chicken. I wondered about that when I was buying the salmon.

This was really tasty. I was very glad @Phoebe suggested doubling the sauce, as that's what I did and it was *barely* enough for me, we would have been sad had we had only half as much. The flavor punch is great. I also was glad I used 1 1/2 lbs of greens as once they cooked down it felt like the right balance with the fish. Followed all of the other instructions (except I always keep the skin!) and it came out perfectly.
"too mustardy" and "too lemony" said the family.

Extra garlic, use something chunky like Swiss chard, foil on the sheet pan means that skin-on salmon delivers some flavour but it effortlessly sticks to the foil when you plate up. Served with jasmine rice. Delish! Will make again!

We liked this.  I cut recipe in half for two. Not a fan of maple syrup, next time I’ll try hickory bark syrup, honey or maybe just skip it. I had one large, thick piece of salmon so took longer to cook. Took spinach out at 15 minutes, then put fish back in 425 oven for 5-10 minutes. Easy and very good!
Used kale and it worked great. A good, adaptable recipe. Also only had whole grain Dijon and it was marvelous, added a nice texture.
This was less than the sum of its parts. Will not repeat.
Sam, what was it that didn't seem to work?  Was it the ingredients, or the method of cooking? I'm thinking of making this, so I'd like to hear why you didn't like it. Thanks!
I wish the photo showed the sheetpan with the greens “tucked” so I could see what that means (underneath? on the side? where do they go?)

Sohla’s recipes have a really solid track record and this just reaffirms it!

Easy, quick, stupidly delicious and versatile as it can go with many carby sides. 

Had some mix of brown rice and quinoa that I paired with this dish. Also, used agave in place of maple syrup as I was unexpectedly out of it.
Spinach got dry and more was more like dried seaweed and looked like dried tobacco.
The salmon was fine, but the spinach was pretty sad. I would make another recipe next time!
I liked it OK. Husband didn’t. Won’t make again. I think the texture of the lemon zest was probably the issue, maybe also the sweet with the dill. Not for everyone.

I would rather cook the Swiss chard separately so I can use the stems which are delicious (just sauté them first and then add the leaves). This is so wasteful, throwing away the best part of the chard.

If you can only find skin-on salmon fillets, roast the fillets with the skin side down and remove after cooking.
 double the sauce or  even triple it next time, and sliced the garlic which was fine.
sauté the spinach the stove while the salmon cooks, otherwise it dries out
can skip maple syrup--just use some liquid to loosen the sauce
Kept the salmon rare by cooking at 375° for 14 minutes.  can use lime
Much, much too sweet with 1T maple syrup.

Made this tonight with fresh caught Nehalem Bay salmon. Tossed the Swiss Chard with the olive oil and salt, rather than drizzling. it cooked up beautifully. Lovely combination of flavors. My husband rated it a ten. The NYT recipes never disappoint.
Great recipe. Next time I’ll double the marinade and baste it again half way through.
Made with Trader Joe's chopped kale. Delicious and very popular.

This was excellent.  It could use a video though to show what "tucking" looks like in this context.  I put the chard pretty much (75%?) under the salmon and it turned out perfectly with a few kale chip-like edges.  Was also skeptical of what a 1/2 c of wine would do flavor-wise across the mostly empty expanse of a sheet pan, so I drizzled it directly over the salmon and protruding chard.

Delicious, inventive combo of Swiss chard and salmon. Low on dill, I supplemented with tarragon, a perfect pairing with Dijon mustard and vermouth. Served with plain, multicolored quinoa. Keep the starch bland (e.g., white rice cooked in water), so as not to compete with the great flavors already on this pretty plate.

Cooking time for my oven was 30 minutes 
My fish was raw at 15 minutes
Also prep time was longer
So For me this is 1 hour prep plus cook time

This was a terrible mess. Yes, I used mature spinach, but even still it was swimming in wine and completely inedible. The salmon was only OK. A tsp of pepper in the sauce was way too much, and I wish I had trusted my instinct on that. Honestly this was a waste of good ingredients that would have been better more simply and separately prepared. I’m done with sheet pan dinners!

Loved this recipe!  I used spinach but agree that sheet pan cooking of spinach not ideal but still tasted good.  I used more than recommended sauce, like double recommended.  Baked for 25 minutes (I had more salmon) and it was delicious and perfectly done.  I used sake in sauce and had nice flavor.
I bake salmon for 20 minutes at 350.  Always perfect. When I do it skin side up, it goes under the broiler for 2 minutes: this blisters the skin so it comes right off.
My husband and I loved this. Followed the recipe exactly (cut in 1/2 for two of us), and it was perfect.
I omitted greens and had my salmon with a green salad. it turned out very tasty and tender!
Tried this tonight for the first time -- very tasty. Used the Swiss chard (which I'll cook separately in the future).  But ten minutes prep time?!? Impossible.
We had quinoa as a side dish. And it was very good—a quick and healthy meal.
Made per recipe.  Doubled sauce.  Delicious!

I just made this and thought the flavors wonderful. Nothing new and crazy but aniother good salmon recipe to add to the mix. I cooke the spinach separately. Maybe the perfect pan would have convinced me to add it to the salmon, but I just used my standard oven roasting pan and cooked it to 120 degrees as suggested in the comme
nts
I made using rainbow Swiss chard and dry vermouth. It was absolutely delicious.
Excellent!  Spinach did not dry up. Followed recipe for the sauce. Had some extra red pepper and broccoli so I threw that in with the spinach. Everything came out beautifully.

I felt compelled to leave a comment for the first time despite NYT being my go-to most nights of the week for several years now. This was FANTASTIC. Made it as written using Rainbow Swiss Chard and Sake. Highest accolades came from our 14 year old. My kids love salmon, but anytime I make it, they always ask for my Cedar Plank Salmon recipe which is absolutely delicious. He said, “I think I might like this better than Cedar Plank.” Hubby and I loved it, too!

Very delicious and easy. Reading comments, I did some of the greens on the sheet pan and took the rest and cooked them in a pot for 5 mins. The result was the greens in the pot were much more vibrant and probably maintained more of their nutritional value. I realise this deviates from the ease of the "one sheet pan" concept, but it all goes in the dishwasher anyway. Will make again. Thanks

This should've been broiled. The sauce is alright but otherwise a big old bag of meh. 

And I am begging everyone to come up with some other way of cooking greens that doesn't involve stewing them into mush.

this is in regular rotation and a recipe that i have widely shared. I found if I toss the greens in the olive oil and stock/wine and then place around the salmon, it doesn't dry out and takes on some of the mustardy-dill flavor from the sauce. Yum!

This dish was not good. The Swiss chard was crispy on top and mushy on the bottom. However, more egregiously, the sauce was absolutely inedible. It was far too lemony, far too mustardy, and ruined a perfectly good piece of salmon.

Do better, Sohla and NYT Cooking.
These are pretty standard ratios for a basic maple and dijon mustard sauce. One can always tweak the elements to better suit their own tastebuds. And sometimes, not all recipes are for you.

I’m not going to cook that chard in the oven. I don't trust it. But I love the strong flavors so it's not that they need to do better, it's that you know to adjust it next time you see something with lemon and mustard, and use less.

Delicious, SO much unique flavor, and very quick. A client made it for a dinner at her house and I demanded the recipe straight away. I'll always prefer whole grain mustard to smooth Dijon, but I did try it. I'll switch that for next time ... Also whole grain and maple syrup are so divine together ... And I like my salmon medium so I go about 18-20 minutes. Aside from that, a total keeper. Lots of dill and lemon, and a good opportunity to get some Swiss chard in! Thank you, Sohla ... Miss you and respect you always! NYT, she deserved better and you know it.

This recipe one me over; I''m now a fan of salmon! The maple sweet with the tangy flavors- so good. I only had baby spinach which didn't work out too well; next time will use chard which should be heartier. Thank you.
This is so good.  Used asparagus and it was perfectly done.  I made the full amount of sauce and half as much salmon.  More sauce would have been great.
Please recommend an alternative to vermouth.
@Peggy the ingredient list provides alternatives including nonalcoholic
What would happen if you don’t use maple syrup? I just hate maple syrup.

@Brooke

You could skip it, or try using honey or agave instead. It depends if you are looking for the sweet flavor profile or not. You might need to add a little water to loosen the mustard to a spreadable consistently if you don’t add a sweetener.
@Brooke I used agave as I was out of maple. Honey would work too as it is a classic combo, but the whine gave it a lil something extra which was delicious!
not a winner for me and my gf :/ didn’t like the different textures of over/undercooked chard. the sauce tasted good uncooked on its own but not my favorite after cooking with the salmon.

Delicious! I used only 3 fillets but kept the sauce amount. Used soju for liquid as that’s what I had. Lemony and pungent in the best way. I did chard and some became crisp like chips and some still soft and wet from moisture, and it gave different flavors and textures, which I liked. Served w steamed rice. Liked by whole family!

I made this as is and it was delicious- everyone in my family loved it! The only change that I would recommend making is to cook the spinach separately. Overall, this recipe is great and I will definitely be making it again

I made this exactly by the recipe, except I did not have a lemon or greens on hand, and it still turned out amazing. I used BBQ cut salmon and served with rice. It was easy and delicious. Will definitely be on our weeknight list going forward.
I've made this twice, now, and it is a real winner. I've used wine each time but we both honestly felt that it doesn't add much and will use veg stock next time.
So so. Followed recipe exactly; salmon was cooked well. Didn't include greens. Sauce didn't have much flavor. There are better ways to cook salmon.
yummy, made just as is, except did not have dill, no problem, will definitely make again
I have kale to use with this recipe. Any thoughts on how it might work?
Kale is a type of green, so why not?

Very lemony.  Made a full recipe of sauce to about 60% of the salmon (for 2), didn’t use all of it. Cooked in an open foil packet on the grill during kitchen remodeling - worked pretty well, could have used more convection/broil.
This time I did what others had suggested and cooked the fish and the veg separately. I think it was more successful.
We really did like this although I think next time I would use honey instead of maple syrup.

Skipped the dill because my husband doesn’t like it.  Used chard on the sheet pan as directed and minced the garlic because I’m too lazy to grate it.  Served with a baked potato because I over bought.  Simply delicious- thank you!!  Will make this again very soon.

Cooked per instructions. Baked salmon & chard for about 13 minutes. Salmon came out great. Chard felt a little wine-y/salty, but maybe I’m just not a big fan of chard. Also thought I probably needed more chard in ratio to the salmon.
I stirred the chard halfway through- to keep it from burning. Worked well. I also cut it rather large. Prob wouldn’t use spinach- as it would get more mushy.
I doubled the sauce, used finely minced garlic and spinach. As someone else suggested I may triple the sauce for my family.
delicious

I do this but swap out the maple syrup for a 1/2 teaspoon of brown sugar and sprinkle granulated garlic, sea salt, pepper and sesame seeds on top of the dijon mix. I also saute the spinach (I prefer mustard greens) on the stovetop with a little olive oil, salt and pepper. It's really a great dish. The dijon really elevates everything.
I would omit the greens and cook the salmon in the recipe.
Could this be marinated overnight? I am very pro-mustard and it never seems to permeate the fish.
Would mustard greens work as a substitute for the spinach? I have a bag I need to use up soon…..

Question for those of you who eat salmon skin-on:  Does your salmon come scaled?  I can't find salmon without scales anywhere, and on the rare occasion there's actually someone available at the fish counter, you'd think I asked for the moon if I request they scale a piece of salmon for me.
I did it exactly as described here. Disappointed!

Google says when you cook something like a leafy green on a sheetpan, tucking refers to tucking the greens under the main component  which would be salmon in this case.
I hope that helps.
I’m supposed to avoid spinach due to its high oxalate content (kidney stones!). I checked the Ingredient Substitution link but didn't see any suggestions for spinach. Does anyone have any ideas? TIA.

I think I’ll try it with half the maple syrup next time- the sauce is delicious but it’s a little sweet on salmon for my personal taste.  This sauce will become a household favorite for chicken, though!
Made with Swiss chard (but but without dill, which I lacked--used a bit of Everything But the Bagel, realizing that that made no sense)--perfectly delicious!  Thanks for a great recipe!
Served it over Israeli couscous- great!

Much of the spinach gets soggy (of course…because it is in wine in the sheet pan). Recommend cook spinach for one minute in a separate skillet and add more wine (or water) to sheet pan to replace the spinach water content that helps cook the salmon so beautifully. 

If using fillets, may need more topping.
It was great! Adults and kids alike enjoyed. Put the sauce on a whole large salmon, skipped the last 2 ingredients (wine and greens). Delicious the second day too!
Fast, tasty, and healthy. I cooked the Swiss chard with the fish. I got a few crispy bits, but not burned. I would consider doubling the sauce to have on the side.
we did not enjoy the taste of the glaze so this recipe is not a keeper.
Not my fav.  Sauce too strong and chard both wet and dry.

Not for me.   Love sheet pan dinners and salmon and roasted greens, but the mustard mixture did not appeal.  The sauce was too sweet and the flavors didn't come together, they just clanged together on my tongue.  I did depart from the recipe by using kale, which I recommend as it did hold up to this cooking method.  I also tossed some asparagus that needed used up onto the pan, which worked well.  At the end, I broiled the salmon for 3 min after removing the veg but it didn't help the sauce.
@~Chrissie O. I suspected as well that this dish would work with other sturdy greens as well and look forward to trying it with them: thanks for the suggestion.
Made using Rainbow Chard.
Perfect. Except I did listen to comments re: cooking the spinach separately and that was perfect.
Cooking the greens on the sheet pan absolutely does not work. They were inedible.
One of the best recipes I’ve made in a long time. Use salmon and tempeh (vegetarian).  Only suggestion would be to use a little more wine.

This was really mediocre. First off it all looked an unappetizing color akin to canned spinach. And the sheet pan is not a good way to fix chard. It got very overcooked and burnt in spots. And it was so tangy from the wine that my husband gagged when he took a bite of it. I won’t make it again.

This dish was so delicious, I will make it again and again. I used North Atlantic Sea Trout from our local fish monger, and baked the dish for 20 minutes; it retained its moisture and melted in your mouth, enhanced with all the flavors of the slathered sauce. I did not skip anything!
made it with side of chayote squash and carrots, it doesnt matter what vegetable you choose to use, use whatever you have fresh at hand. just followed recipe for salmon and it was delicious and easy.

This is going to be a stand by. So good. Doubled the sauce and used the extra in wheat berries served along with the salmon. Make sure to put lots of sauce on the greens or it gets crispy. Cooked in oven for 12 minutes. Used Swiss chard.
I’m so sorry but this recipe was vile. The greens are literally inedible due to the raw wine in the sheet pan……
Surprised that the dill was not more pronounced. Overall, not that exciting.

I made the salmon as directed, but I didn't have any dill, so I just left it out. Also, I didn't make the greens. I served the salmon, which was juicy and delicious, with a side of roasted sweet potatoes and a salad. Good, quick dinner.
I only had baby spinach, so made it with roasted sweet potato and delicate squash seasoned with thyme, garlic, cinnamon, salt, and ancho chili instead. Very tasty.
super quick and easy. didn't add any liquor or broth... I use stoneware and it was very moist.  AND the skin stays on! :) served with roasted rutabaga and peas.
Cooked exactly as per the recipe and it was delicious. The  liquid from the veg broth and lemon slices kept the chard from drying out. Will def make again.
Worked well sautéing spinach on stove instead of on sheet pan. You do not need wine on sheet pan if you do this.

I make this probably 2 or 3 times a month, it is an easy and healthy weeknight favorite when all I have is salmon in the freezer, a bunch of greens and a lemon,  I always use a sturdier green and double the sauce!! I have made it in a 12 inch oven proof skillet with smaller fillets and it works out great, and easy clean up.
Served this over Orzo.  It was delicious!!!

I made some thinly sliced Yukon golds (seasoned with garlic powder and paprika) to go alongside this and thought it was such a wonderful meal. Filling and flavorful without being too rich. I'll definitely make more sauce next time!

Fantastic!  I followed the recipe pretty closely and the results were excellent.  I found this method of cooking spinach to be very easy and satisfying.  I stirred the spinach once mid-cooking to make sure all the spinach could soak up the lemon/wine goodness.  Easy and very flavorful!  I served with mashed Yukon Gold potatoes - they were the perfect foil.

This was so easy and tasty. The lemon added a lot of flavor. Double the sauce. Remove the salmon skin after cooking. Then put it back on the now-empty sheet pan and back in the oven for another 5 minutes. Crumble it over everything else. It's my favorite part. Good w Swiss chard.
THIS should not be a five-star recipe- 3 at best. Won’t make again.
Can I use frozen spinach? Frozen chopped spinach?
We loved this recipe- I did double the sauce based on the comments.  The only other change was mincing instead of grating the garlic.

The salmon is delightful, but the complex interplay of the wine acidity and the bitterness of the greens is what instantly put this on rotation in our house. We usually prep four filets, set two aside for the following night and reserve half the greens. Works beautifully both nights in <20 minutes with minimal active time and chopping. The Dijon mix could definitely be made ahead to make it even faster. A perfect meal for parents of littles who just want to eat really delicious food.
Unfortunately I wasn’t a fan - we found the sauce way too strong with mustard flavor. The sauce overpowered the salmon.
Made this dish as written, using Ora King Salmon and red Swiss Chard. It was sublime! And yes, the sauce was great.

Really liked this. Exceedingly easy, really tasty and light. I cooked the greens (collards... thats what was available and didn't look bad) separately. Otherwise kept everything as written. Definitely making again.

I made this last night but am avoiding sugar, so subbed in about 8 drops of monk fruit (the healthiest of the non-sugar sweeteners) and 2 tablespoons of miso paste. I added a bit of water so it was the right texture to spread on fish. It was superb. I subbed in dandelion greens for the spinach but that was a mistake. They were too chewy to even eat (they were not supposed to be drenched in the wine but just sit on top of it. My bad.) have never made a salmon in the oven that comes out so moist.
Very very good!!!!

Truly awful.  I felt I should apologize to the salmon who gave his life, only to be covered with an overpowering, gloppy sauce (way, way too much mustard which overwhelmed the fish).  And the chard was tasteless due to being crisp.  Usually, I am happy with NYT recipes but this one was wrong on all levels.
Really delish! I had fresh broccoli and sugar snaps on hand. Next time I make, I’ll add them later in the rice cook. 20 minutes made the veg a little overcooked. Definitely season salty as directed.

Flavor was good but took twice as long to cook the rice as the recipe said. Maybe it was the frozen veggies? I used riced cauliflower and halloumi because I couldn’t find frozen cauliflower florets or paneer at my grocery.

Make sure paneer is firm and well drained or it can stick and crumble. If you can mince finely, you don't need to grate ginger and garlic. I usually do the ginger, garlic, onion first which helps cook down and release their flavour. Then I go in with powdered spices and tomato to prevent the spices from burning and to reduce the chance of sticking. And don't be too stingy with oil, it is your friend.

If you have them and like them, I suggest bell peppers a worthy addition.
Can one substitute firm tofu for paneer? Paneer is utterly unavailable in our isolated mountain town.
@Lynn I nearly always do this and it works great. I like to cut it in cubes and bake it before adding to the dish. Seems to stay together better
I think fried firm tofu would work fine, but with a bit different flavor.  However, you probably can't find fired tofu in your town either (I get mine at an H Mart).

@Lynn Tofu would absolutely work, but paneer is ridiculously easy to make, with commonly available ingredients, and tastier than store bought. If you have an afternoon, look up any recipe online and give it a go!
Delish. Subbed tofu - firm - for paneer and it worked, as it does for all recipes requiring squares of paneer.

Under no circumstances should you brown paneer in super hot Dutch oven with only ghee. It will stick and it will destroy your paneer. The note about how to prevent sticking is nonsense. Use a nonstick pan. Period.Paneer is difficult to buy unless you have an Indian grocer nearby, so I made my own. Now I have crumbles of nothing.
@TKG I bought paneer at Costco.
I have learned from culinary mishaps that if you're making your own paneer, it really, really needs to be firm and well drained.
@TKG worked fine for me. I used a rubber spatula to flip it and it worked fine. As the recipe said, I started with a hot pan

I made this as directed (mostly). Added cubed tofu because I only had about 1/2 the paneer I needed. With that addition it took about 1 hr to get to step 6. I found the outcome bland. Could have been my store bought paneer, or my inexpert blend of garam masala. Suggestions: use vegetable broth in place of water. Add a sliced jalapeño or similar. Serve with lemon and other condiments.
@Cervantes I often sub halloumi in for paneer as I find paneer often doesn’t have a lot of flavour. Works nicely.

Made this for the first time and doubled it to freeze some for later, and am glad it turned out well! Seasoned heavily and used about a tablespoon of better than bouillon in the broth. Was short on garam masala, maybe only had half the amount, so added in a tablespoon of curry powder as well. Very yummy!
@Rebecca did it freeze well? This was so tasty I was hoping to freeze some for later.

I found this to be a very poor recipe. Rice was nowhere near cooked after 20 minutes and required more water and much more time. Even still there were hard grains in places.  Overall lacking in flavour despite all the calls for copious amounts of salt.

I really like this recipe but it took way longer than 20 minutes to cook the rice both times I’ve made it — even with the soaking. Added a lot of extra Indian spices as garam masala only felt a bit flat. Love the ease of one pot.

This was delicious, but spicier than I expected. I guess that’s the variation in brands of garam masala … so in the end it was a no-go for the 6 year olds at my house. More for the grownups to enjoy as leftovers :) I also was less than aggressive with my salt, and wound up adding more at the table, so be brave.

At the point where you salt the broth, I was having trouble getting it to be truly flavorful, so I added a tablespoon of garlic-flavored better than bouillon. We also found the rice a little crunchy and will probably add a little more water next time. Otherwise it was excellent and flavorful, a good one-dish dinner that we will make again.
This is a delicious recipe!
Could I cook the veggies separately and combine with the rice at the end?

I honestly think you can but the whole joy of a pilaf or a pulao as we call it in India, is the merging of flavours; the veggies lending their sweetness and slight grassy taste to the starchy fragrant rice, and the spices infusing everything with a little heat and a lot of flavour. 
My one tweak to the recipe: gently stir fry the veggies in that gorgeously messy sticky tomato paste before adding water to make the broth. This way the veggies get a bit of char & don't fully disintegrate later.
Any ideas for a replacement for the peas?  I'm just not a fan of peas in dishes.
Frozen or fresh sweet corn would complement the other ingredients quite nicely
@Dorothy I used frozen edamame because I also don’t love peas, and it worked great

Lynn, when I was in grad school I wanted to make an Indian curry with paneer and could not find it.  An Indian woman friend suggested taking ricotta cheese, spread it about 1 inch thick in a baking dish and bake at 350F for about an hour.  Then cut into cubes.

I cannot get the paneer not to stick, which ruins the dish. I’ve tried this twice now, with the second time making sure the Dutch oven was thoroughly hot. Maybe it’s too hot? Used plenty of ghee. And using homemade paneer if that matters.

Looking for tips as others don’t seem to have this issue.
@John I browned the cubes of paneer in a nonstick skillet. It was an extra dish but I had no issues with sticking

This was really good. I did not have a bell pepper so I substituted jarred salsa for the tomatoes and diced pepper.  I l also had about a 1/4 cup of canned corn that I added. This recipe minimizes meat and provides approximately 17 grams of protein and, as I made it, 7 grams of fiber.
Typically adore Sohla’s recipes, but this one didn’t land for us. Despite using veggie broth instead of water, the dish lacked flavor and felt a bit one note.
Eh - I tried to halve the recipe and it just didn't work. It was bland and disappointing. Adding a tablespoon of spicy chili garlic paste saved it as my dinner
Could I cook the veggies separately?
I will definitely make this again! I used  frozen edamame because I didn’t have any peas, and fresh cauliflower. So, so good!

Used air-fried tofu.
Made rice separately with vegetable broth, then mixed it all together and let it sit a while and rewarmed .
Added mushrooms with the onions.
Made this recipe exactly as is, and oh my god. It was delicious, fairly simple yet complex. I’m happily adding this to my regular dinner rotation.
Followed the recipe as directed, but found the outcome bland like some other commenters.  I also found the flavor to be very garam masala heavy, to the detriment of the other ingredients.

As mentioned by others, the rice did not come out well. Some of it was underdone but some of it was mushy. The flavour was also quite flat although the yogurt and lemon added at the end did help a bit. Will try again with a mix of spices (not just garam masala) and with fresh instead of frozen cauliflower, which I hope will help the rice cook more evenly in the 20 min timeframe. Might also cook the rice in the oil with the seasonings briefly before adding the liquids.
This just did not work. Rice on top raw, rice on bottom mushy. Yuck.
Can I use spinach instead of peas?

title: Broiler-Popped Oysters With Tomato Butter Recipe
author: Sohla El-Waylly

comments.commentBody: As a timid shucker, I like the method of broiling the oysters to get them to open. I think I will skip the tomato treatment, since I love oysters in their briny simplicity!

This was good! I had been wanting to try this, finally got 6 fresh oysters and made it for myself. 51, never ate oysters before and preferred cooked over raw. 1 out of 6 stayed closed so I chucked it. 5 delicious oysters- tomato butter was yummy. Will make again someday. No shucking is awesome.

Absolutely delicious but depending on the size and source of the oysters, don't expect them to open themselves. I waited 5 minutes at high and still had to shuck them (all be it more easily) to add the tomato butter and rebroil. Mine were big Texas oysters.
Sounds delicious and easy, as long as the sauce doesn't overwhelm the oyster. Wull try this when we gather oysters in September.

Oyster shucking with training wheels on?  Anyone who has ridden a bike will probably not want to throw a pair of training wheels back on the bike and the same is true for anyone who has shucked an oyster.  It does not make it easier to cook oysters twice in the broiler.
@Joe you speak for yourself, this works for me
The oysters opened up while they were cooling. Excellent recipe.
Great recipe for the compound butter. I ended up just shucking the oysters normally rather than precooking them.

The warming spice blend in this is an absolute winner. A few things I’d do differently next time:
- I didn’t have turbinado sugar and the apples alone weren’t enough sweetener. Next time I’m going to add some maple syrup into the mix before baking
- I also think the mixture could use a bit more salt to keep the oats from tasting bland and really enhance the spice flavors
- I’ll probably add a chopped nut for texture variation!
Halve the tumeric and consider cardamom.
@Mike B Thanks for this rec!
@Mike B agreed! Could back off a bit on the other spices too.

This was pretty good and a nice variation on baked oatmeal.  I only had 2 apples so I substituted slices from 2 bananas instead which really added some much needed texture and variation in flavour. I also used soy milk instead of coconut milk and I think any kind of milk would work here. Next time I will reduce the turmeric and completely eliminate the black pepper.
Fabulous- I added some shredded coconut as well. Really good with Greek yogurt. Shall be making this again!
I made this today and it is wonderful!  It is great as is, but you could also add golden raisins or dried apricots!

Nice variation from our regular breakfast food rotation. Made double batch in 9x13 pan using brown sugar and date molasses on top instead of turbinado. I'll definitely add nuts or coconut next time because it lacked texture - and probably slip in some chia seeds and hemp seeds for nutrition boost. Once fully cooled, this can be cut into squares and tucked into lunch boxes.

As for salt, I am a retired Family Physician, and remember hearing a speech from an expert on hypertension who argued that only people with salt-sensitive hypertension need to limit their salt intake. My blood pressure is usually on the low normal side, so I use salt whenever needed to improve the flavor. But for those who have hypertension, reasonably limiting salt is of course, sensible.
I made this exactly as instructed. Delicious and easy - plus it looks good and makes the house smell great while cooking. A winner!
Excellent as is.
Delish and baby   preschooler approved. Served with yogurt. Used leftovers in smoothies.
What a hit! I only had coconut cream and that was great. I added 1/4 teaspoon of cardamom, which complemented the other spices. My 15 y/o boy went back for fourths!!!

I have made a lot of baked oatmeal, but this is a new favorite. I was actually surprised with the pleasant level of sweetness using just the listed amount of sugar (thank you apples!). I did add some pumpkin seeds and a tablespoon for flax meal to up the fiber and protein even more. Look forward to making again
Do you think I could substitute applesauce for the eggs in this recipe to accommodate allergies?
Since it already has apple in it, maybe use flax eggs instead.

@Hope-an even easier hack is to use 1 Tbsp aquafava (the liquid from canned or homemade chickpeas) for each egg needed. When opening a can, I’ll freeze the aquafava in tablespoon amounts so they’re easy to use when I need them.

The first helping I had I topped with Greek yogurt. It was kind of meh. This morning I topped with warm milk as suggested in the recipe and oh my god that’s the ticket. Way better, as the milk softens up the bake into almost a bread pudding like texture.

Just made this and am thoroughly enjoying a bowl as I type. I accidentally added an additional 1/2 t. turmeric which did no harm, but will try adding some cardamom next time. I don't have demara or turbines sugar on hand but do have coconut and dark brown, so I made a 50-50 mixture and browned it up. Topped it with a few raisjns and walnuts. Delicious!
Could this be baked in a muffin tin to make individual portions?

Made this exactly as written, and it was very good. I love the texture—it's exactly how I like oatmeal. I will definitely make this again, but next time I think I will dial back the cinnamon just a little. It overpowered the rest of the spices. Still, this recipe is a keeper. Looking forward to eating the leftovers for breakfast this week.

I halved the turmeric and it was still a bit much for me personally! also added cardomom, that was lovely. I didn't have turbinado sugar, and forgot to add honey but I've drizzled some on top post cooking which is nice. otherwise the apples aren't sweet enough on their own for my taste.. maybe pink lady apples next time.

This was so good!  Almost made as written, I added the toasted oats and spices to the apple mixture and combined in that bowl.  This allowed me to spritz the cast iron pan I was using with a light coat of oil.  I also added about 1.5 Tablespoons of sour cherry jam to the apple mixture.  I feel the sugar topping is optional (at least with the added jam in the mix), and agree with some of the posters that some nuts on top would be awesome.  Don't be afraid of the pepper!

I made this exactly as written. I used two tablespoons of sugar for the topping, and those crunchy sweet bits are the best bites. This is better without pouring milk over it. Overall, I found this bland. My immediate thought was, this could use raisins and/or nuts. Not my favorite baked oatmeal, so I’ll try other recipes.
Can this be put together one day and baked the next?
Did you try it? I'd also like to know.

Amazing recipe! I had some unsweetened applesauce I needed to use up, so I used two grated Fuji apples and one applesauce cup. The Demerara/turbinado sugar carmelized on top is non-negotiable: it adds the right amount of sweetness and the crunch is perfect! I will probably add a bit more salt next time. 

Topped with a big dollop of Greek yogurt for extra protein and it’s fantastic.

I did not like the turmeric in this, and I think that is what made it inedible to my children (I already left out the pepper). I do like turmeric in other things, but I would leave it out if I made it again.

Very nice! I used a mix of old fashioned oats and quick cooking steel cut oats.  Used coconut cream instead of coconut milk because that’s what I had on hand. Upped the cinnamon and ginger and slightly reduced the tumeric, which worked for me - still a bit of a kick. Added some nuts and raisins for texture. Had no demara or turbinado sugar, so broiled with brown sugar and date syrup on top.  Took awhile to assemble and bake, but a nice weekend breakfast.

This is SO GOOD, it’ll be on my weekly rotation from now one. I usually like to do a recipe as is before changing, but I had to do some slight alterations to this to accommodate the ingredients I already had, and it still turned out so good:
- I only had two apples, so I replaced one apple for a banana. I haven’t tried with all apples but the banana added some incredible natural sweetness. 
- I had just run out of turmeric so the teaspoon of turmeric was instead a teaspoon of turmeric+ginger, but it was still plenty of turmeric. 
- Instead of cooking the oats first, I used quick cooking oats and put it straight into the oven with the mixture, no extra liquid. I honestly think that’d work for regular oats too…
Instead of the sugar, I mixed in two teaspoons of maple syrup, and on the top I broiled a thick layer of pecans instead of sugar (I only added the pea and to the broil part). It turned out amazing, and remained crispy through the week. 
- I tried adding a splash of milk as suggested, and also tried with some yogurt. It’s always good but honestly my favorite way to eat this is on its own, after a 45 sec microwave warmup.

Tried to half sugar so did 1 T sugar in the raw and 1 T and used sweetened coconut with sugar. This recipe did not taste sweet to me but adding slivered almonds and raisins to dry pudding seemed great
Made this exactly as written. No substitutions or modifications. Absolutely delicious. I would eat this for dessert!

This has a delicious flavor! Especially with the broiled sugar on top. Here are a few changes I made that elevated it further: 
-when toasting oats, add about 1/2 cup pecans and toast them too
-add raisins
-serve with a dollop of Greek yogurt on the side
-3 minutes was too long under the broiler; do 2 minutes and turn around after 1 minute
Made as is and found it to be very bland. My husband literally eats everything and was not interested in seconds.
How would this recipe be adjusted for steel-cut oats?

A good starting point, but was surprisingly bland (old spices?). I halved the turmeric and added cardamon, which was good, but the cinnamon and ginger were very muted. Will make again but may add coconut flake or nuts for flavor and texture. Might also add some cherry preserves.

title: Japanese Curry With Chicken Meatballs  Recipe
author: Sohla El-Waylly

comments.commentBody:
I love Japanese curry and have been waiting for a new way to use the curry box sitting in my pantry!

This recipe is super delicious but here are my thoughts for future triers:
- Up the breadcrumbs to 3/4 cup and let the meatballs sit for like 15-20 minutes to let the moisture absorb (mine were pretty wet as directed)
- If you, like me, have a 7.8oz package of curry cubes (Golden in my case) just use half the pack
- Use a large dutch oven if making the full recipe. Those meatballs need space.

Mine turned out more like a japanese curry, chicken, and greens ragu this time but honestly I’m not complaining
@Mark P added 3/4 panko per your suggestion and they were perfect!
@Mark P, wrong solution. Double the recipe and use all the curry roux!
@Mark P mine too…mushy and the meatballs weren’t fully cooked at 3 min/side. A very tasty mush but next time I’d rather bake the meatballs first so they’re cooked through!
The curry roux incorporates much smoother if you chop it and mix it with the simmering broth in a small bowl, then add to the pot, instead of adding the pieces directly to the pot.

Delicious.  Like the other comments, I needed to double the panko to keep the boulettes in tact.  I also had to add a lot of water to the curry in order to keep it at the right consistency.  Still, very good.

One bite and my 4 year old said, "You need to make this again!" I thought it was wicked good, my wife thought it could have used more curry in the curry to other ingredient ratio. It took me an hour. I will be making this again. Very satisfied after that meal.
Delicious. I made as instructed and found the meatballs a bit hard to shape but they turned out moist and tasty. Highly recommended!
I made as directed and it was delicious. I would add more time to the prep…prob 15 min.

This was SO good. I upped the breadcrumbs to 3/4 cup as another commenter suggested since the mixture was so wet that I was afraid the meatballs would just come apart. This is going into the rotation.
Golden brand Japanese curry…800 mg of sodium and a warning that consuming the product can expose you to lead?!

I really liked this. So simple but works. And a one pot meal! I had a few subs. I used ground turkey and it worked fine. Maybe not as flavorful a meat but was still tasty. I also used madras curry powder. I used about 3 tbsn in a roux with about 4-5 tbsn butter and about a quarter cup of flower. It needed salt too to bring out the best flavor. I also used only chard. I could not fold in so much.  I only got in about 12 oz. Overall, it was good dish and easy to make. Well worth it.

Stop. Read no further. Just go buy the ingredients…and triple them. You will want to have a week of leftovers. This was by received by such raves from my family that it will be made again very soon. I was worried I would not find that Japanese curry mix but I did easily at my large supermarket. Truly outstanding and so healthy!
I added a bit too much soy sauce, so I mixed in some extra panko and a few pinches of flour to get the meatballs to bind better. We used Golden Curry brand curry mix, and it came out great.

Made this tonight (exactly as written FYI). The meatballs  weren’t so much balls as patties because the chicken mixture was so wet. I’d double the panko next time. Really nice flavor and texture however. The Japanese curry sauce is best consumed as a glaze rather than a stew. That is, I would serve this next time on a bed of rice and just let the sauce cling to the meat and veg, rather than swim in it. Hope that helps!

I made this almost exactly as written and it was delicious. I forgot to add sugar to the meatballs and no one thought it needed it so I’ll probably omit that in the future as well. I used the amount of bread crumbs shown in the recipe and found the meatballs easy to shape. The kneading and letting them sit instructions are pretty vital as well as having hands wet with cold water. This was quick and easy with inexpensive and easily found ingredients. I love Sohla’s recipes!

I've never commented on NYT cooking before, but this recipe has left me no choice it's that good. I cook with Japanese curry roux blocks often. The chicken meatballs take it to the next level. The ginger and garlic bring a freshness that balances the richness of the roux perfectly. Plus they reheat so much better in the microwave than, say, cubed chicken thighs. It's a great meal prep option.

This is truly a 5/5 star recipe.  I only made one modification suggested by others - using 3/4 cup of panko bread crumbs.  The ground chicken is usually very moist and this was the right amount of panko to permit the mixture to make meatballs that hold together.  The meatballs had great flavor and the medium-spicy Golden Curry roux I used had great flavor as well.  I used Japanese sweet potato which seems perfect for this recipe as opposed to the typical sweet potato varities.
@Matt I also used the Japanese sweet potatoes and they’re perfect. All if you have a Trader Joe’s they usually carry them.

Made this for my boomer parents who loved it! Used pork instead of chicken and bok choi instead of spinach as I had both of those and didn’t want to go to the shops. 

1/2 cup panko crumbs was plenty for the ground pork.
Sohla is the GOAT (curry)
I added 1/3 of a grated apple and some chopped carrots while I let the potato’s simmer and it turned out great!

Upped the panko as many others did and baked the meatballs for about 20 min at 425 flipping once so they got some browning on both sides. Otherwise cooked as directed and it was absolutely delicious! Will be making again.

This was pretty good. Texturally, it was all too soft without variation. We agreed with another poster that the sugar either needed to be reduced or eliminated. Too sweet. Also, needed acid. We increased the panko to about 3/4 C and probably could have gone to 1 C, and used chicken stock, not water. Next time, maybe add pickled ginger to the meatballs and pickled ginger juice to the stock. The sweet potatoes were pretty, but added to the overall softness. I like the idea of carrots instead. We topped it with sweet potato chips, which was a good idea.

my husband and teenage sons don't like sweet potatoes. so I used a small honeynut squash (mostly b/c I like squash and sweet potatoes) and added cut-up fingerling potatoes and chunks of peeled apples (potatoes and apples typically go in Japanese curries). for greens, threw in bok choy and some leftover broccoli rabe. YUM!

the meatballs were the star - followed the tips to increase panko to 3/4 cup and let mixture rest for 10-15 mjns before forming the balls. I also chilled the meatballs on a sheet pan covered in parchment for about an hour. to keep them intact as much as possible when cooking them, use plenty of oil, give them enough room, so fry in two batches, don't turn until they release on their own (2 mins for the first "side"), then also brown a second side and even one or two more surfaces, so they are kind of "sealed." they'll finish cooking in the curry. 😋😋😋

I haven’t been this happy with a recipe in a long time. The meatballs were soft and flavorful, the sauce was luscious. I took Mark P’s advice and did 3/4 cups of panko - I also stuck the balls in the fridge to chill while I prepped the veg for the curry. 

Instead of the sweet potato, I did a mix celery root, carrot, and turnip. The swiss chard at the end was chef’s kiss. Thank you for this wonderful recipe!

Yum!! So good. I only had one bunch chard so I added a chopped-up cabbage (added at same time as the potatoes), which I actually liked more than the chard. I also used 3/4 C panko in meatballs and broth instead of water, as commenters suggested. Next time (def will make again), I think I’ll try just cabbage and maybe peppers — no need for the sweetness of the potatoes, though I love sweet potatoes.

I made my own roux :
	•	2 Tbsp butter or oil
	•	2 Tbsp flour
	•	1 Tbsp curry powder 
	•	1 tsp garam masala
	•	1 tsp soy sauce
	•	1 tsp honey
	•	Optional: ½ tsp grated ginger or garlic
I followed the rest of the recipe as is and my meatballs were intact and perfect. I used a lot of avocado oil and got it very warm before putting the meatballs  a dutch oven. Delicious! 

How:
Make a roux with olive oil + flour, then stir in spices, soy sauce, and sweetener. Add directly to your curry and simmer until thick.

Good, but probably won't make again. It's Japanese curry, so the real difference is in swapping out the meat and veggies.  As to those changes:

-Meatballs were good, but not really worth the added effort over chicken/beef for me personally.
-Sweet potatoes are great in this!
-Spinach is really good the first day, less so after.  Will probably sub in a heartier green.

Solid variation on a standard Japanese curry, and I'll def. integrate some of what I liked here into my standard version.

Delicious and simple dish. My first time using Japanese curry roux cubes (Golden Curry medium hot). I added the entire 3.5pz box, and the flavor is strong but good. It also allows for adding water, which I did as the greens made it all too thick. One caveat: the chicken meatballs are definitely wet and struggled to hold round. It was tempting to add more panko.

The curry roux blocks had little actual curry spices, so I used S&B curry powder along with a Japanese togarashi hot chili paste added when sautéing the onions and garlic. Opened the fridge and threw in broccolini, chard with some of the chicken left over from last night’s dinner. Chicken broth was used to make the sauce. Really lovely meal over rice and bound to be cooked again. Thanks for the inspiration!
I’m new to curry flavored dishes. I would like to make this dish but don’t like anything too hot/spicy. Should I stay away from this recipe? TX
@Janet Finkel most Japanese curry bricks come in several levels of spice- the Golden brand, which is available at my supermarket, has a mild option that has no heat at all. I say try it!

Japanese style curry is usually not very spicy. It's usually more sweet and mild. If you search  the internet for japanese curry roux or mix you should  find some reviews that compare different brands.

If you substitute regular curry for Japanese curry like several commenters here have done then it will be spicier.

@Katie
I prefer the mild, which has great flavor as followed. We had Gochugaru Korean chile flakes at the table.  Choose your own adventure!

This was fine. Using homemade curry roux would be much better. Check out Just One Coolbook’s recipe. But the store bought stuff is much faster. 

Also the meatballs were ok. They held their shape easily, but weren’t special in any way.

This was very good! I increased the bread crumbs to 3/4 cup as others suggested. The meatballs still seemed too wet to stay together when frying so I instead baked them in the oven at 350 F for 20 minutes. This worked well for me and was an easier prep.
Prepared as written. Delicious. Just the right amount of kick from the roux. Definitely will make again

We thought this was quite delicious, but alas it won’t be a reapeat.  After dinner I happened to glance at the ingredients in Golden Curry.  Palm oil, hydrogenated rapeseed oil, MSG, disodium this and disodium that…I’m also not sure if the chicken meatballs were cooked all the way through.  Hope we emerge unscathed.
@Laurel W. There are products that don’t contain those ingredients, in case you’re interested in trying the recipe again.

@andi
What are the other curry products that don’t have MSG?
@andi Would love to know the healthier options because I did like the recipe a lot.  Though maybe it was the taste of all those forbidden ingredients I enjoyed!

Make the meatballs first and precook on a sheet pan do say 12 min at 400-425. Then toss into the curry once the sweet potatoes are cooked and simmer for a few. Toss in spinach at the end to wilt last minute.
Upped the breadcrumbs to 1c and added 2 more cups of water after the roux. So good!! Definitely making again.

As others have said, you probably don't have to double the bread crumbs, just let the mixture sit for 15 minutes or so. I also think mixing up the curry brick in broth ahead of time is a very good idea. Made it super easy to incorporate.

Was surprised by how not overly salty this was. Will make again probably, but it didn't knock my socks off.

I put the meatballs in the freezer for a bit, which helped, along with additional panko. I also added some carrots, as well as a bit more water. This recipe takes more time than listed - anything with meatballs does, normally - but it was good and I would make it again.

This might be my favorite recipe from NYT. I don’t have the biggest Dutch oven so my meatballs were a little bigger, which required an additional 5 minutes of cooking at the end to make sure they were cooked through. One of the few recipes where I didn’t have to prep everything at the beginning: you can cut the onion and sweet potato while the meatballs are cooking, and clean and trim the spinach/chard while everything is simmering.
Absolutely make this. I used carrots instead of sweet potatoes and upped the bread crumbs. Totally delicious.

Made this tonight and it was delicious. Followed other reader’s suggestions and upped the panko to 3/4 for every pound of ground chicken. Also ground chicken thighs myself, highly recommend. I tripled the meatballs, as I had a family pack of Vermont Curry to use up. Added 3 tablespoons of hoisin to the meatballs, and baked for 16 mins at 400. Used mushrooms and chard, out of sweet potatoes. Also added ginger and garlic to the sautéed onions, and put in another 2 tablespoons of hoisin sauce to the curry. Served over fresh udon noodles. So going on the rotation.

I agree with everyone else about the Panko not being enough.  But I also added tofu to the mix, as well as chicken consume.  That added some good nutritional content, without sacrificing the flavor.  I also cooked the meatballs in my air fryer, and increased the amount of liquid in the curry.

I mostly made this according to the directions, though I was out of panko (no idea how that happened) so I used crushed saltines instead and added in bok choy in place of spinach and upped the vegetable matter with the addition of carrots and green beans. I also used a small disher to make the meatballs because they were a bit wet and it worked like a charm. I also put the meatballs in the oven after I browned them as recommended in another post. It was delicious and the meatballs were perfect.

Excellent. The meatballs come out tender and delicately flavorful (go for white pepper if you have it). The fond from browning them first really deepens the flavor of the onions and sauce. And this recipe really lends itself to frozen spinach — I dumped in a 12-ounce bag of leaf spinach. I had no trouble keeping the meatballs intact with 1/2 cup of breadcrumbs, but you do need to resist the urge to flip them too soon. It also helps to use tongs (carefully) to position and turn them.

Amazing! A few adjustments
- increased breadcrumbs to nearly 1 cup per earlier suggestions
- let the meatballs sit for 15 minutes 
- increased water added to 5.5 cups and also used 7/8 of a golden curry package (used the medium spice)
- used Tuscan kale instead of spinach since that’s what I had in the fridge and used two sweet potatoes

Used this recipe to make curry roux. It uses a can of "Oriental Spice Mix" that has no salt and no MSG. I bought it on Amazon. I cut down the cayenne, but I don't like it very spicy: https://www.justonecookbook.com/simple-chicken-curry/
I couldn’t find the roux cubes so I followed the link in the recipe and made my own.  They were very easy to make and they came out really well.

Used 3/4c panko and let the mixture sit for a bit really helps make the meatballs easier to make. I needed an extra cup of water otherwise I found the curry too thick and salty. Tried it with and without the sugar in the meatball mixture and while you can’t taste it I think it adds some balance. This recipe readily accepts extra Swiss chard and sweet potato!

Part Indian curry, part stew, 100% delicious. Cooking the meatballs on a sheet pan in a hot oven solves the problem of disintegrating meatballs—just set them in the sauce at the very end, or simply spoon the curry over the meatballs and rice as you serve. Double the recipe if you have any hopes for leftovers.

I've made this several times now, and it's a favorite! I leave out the sugar because it makes the meatballs weirdly sweet, and with the sweet potato it's too much. By all means, up the panko to 3/4 c and they do hold together but they're delicate. When little bits break off and carmelize it's delicious. Chop the curry bar finely before adding to the pot and it melts beautifully, and I always need the extra half cup of water (probably because my veg measurements are always inflated).
I’m finding it difficult to buy a no msg curry.  Msg leaves me a terrible headache the next morning.
I used a delicata for my winter squash of choice. Lovely flavor and texture!

My family loves it.  I'm not sure how difficult it is to find the Japanese curry paste where you all are, but I've found it at the super Fry's in Phoenix and at the Mekong Supermarket in Mesa. White guy tip at Mekong, it isn't in the aisle that says curry. It's 2 aisles over to the west and half an aisle to the north on the right.  There are several heat options in 7 oz packages that are split in to two 3.5oz packs.

I didn't have Japanese roux blocks but did have regular curry powder (not a turmeric heavy one). here's how i Japan-i-fied it. for 3oz final paste, melt 3tbsp butter, simmer with 3tbsp flour until nutty brown. Add 3tbsp curry powder and mix into paste. add 4tbsp soy sauce, 3tbsp apple sauce or grated apples, and a few dashes of cinnamon and nutmeg. Voila! sweet cozy curry paste :)

I used the SB brand roux, 2 blocks (out of 5 for the package) about 36 grams or 1.3 oz was enough.  Using a pair of scissors to cut the roux over the pot worked well.  This was delicious, but it did take me a lot longer than 15 minutes of prep time.  I guess I am a slow chopper, have two kittens hovering over me didn't help.
I baked the meatballs at 350F for 45 mins. I added a tbsp of olive oil to the turkey mince to keep it moist.  It was delicious.

I read other comments before cooking and increased panko bread crumbs to 3/4 of a cup. I increased ginger and garlic as well. The texture was great. I left the mix stand for about 10 minutes before shaping into meatballs and cooking. They were perfect. I wasn’t able to find Japanese curry roux so I substituted green curry paste. I used coconut milk instead of water. So quite different from the recipe! It was delicious! I loved the meatballs and it worked with the curry. I’d make it again this same way until I am able to find Japan curry roux.
Double the bread crumbs. Mix curry cubes first with a little hot water to crush and mix
It was delicious and I will certainly make it again. No problems with making the meatballs or that it  was to moist. Perfection in the Dutch oven

Upped the panko to 3/4 cups per many comments and it worked out well. I had carrots lying around so used them instead of sweet potatoes - I think they take longer to cook even though I tried cutting them not too big. Used lacinato kale for greens. I was surprised by how much the curry block thickens the dish! Used mild curry for my toddler but I sprinkled some cayenne on my plate and it worked well. Next time I’d use less sugar in the meatballs as the whole dish was pretty sweet - I know it’s part of Japanese flavor profiles but we don’t have sweet teeth. Great recipe!

Really amazing meal.   I added some sleeks since I had  them.   Added a little miso for extra flavor boost.  For meatballs, I followed advice here with just under a cup of panko, but also added a touch of heavy cream for fat, and put them in fridge for ten minutes to solidify a bit.    All in all, a really good recipe and tasty dinner.
Doubled the recipe & used my mixer stand and a small ice cream scoop to form the meatballs. Much easier. Served for 8 & had yummy leftovers. Will add more veg next time cuz that’s how I roll.

flavorful but time consuming to make as others have mentioned. the tips to increase bread crumbs and let the meatball mixture rest for 15-20 mins was helpful. overall I think this recipe was worth trying but for a quicker, simpler meal, I might prefer it with tofu or chicken instead of the meatballs. the garlic and ginger are a nice touch though! leftovers reheat well.
Delicious, filling, and green! Served with a healthy amount of rice, it gave us 5 servings (and we are hungry people).

I made this in my work, which worked really well for frying the meatballs and adding the huge bunch of greens at the end. Some of my eaters are not fans of sweet potato or pumpkin so I used potatoes, carrots and daikon instead. I also subbed pork for chicken, as it's what was on hand.

This recipe was a complete winner! I did have to make some substitutions; my grocery store didn't have mature spinach or swiss chard, so I bought kale. The ground chicken available was "extra lean" and extra expensive, so I bought a higher-fat ground turkey instead. The meatballs were very wet, but I was able to cook them no problem and they came out tender and delicious.

@Denise You can make your own ground chicken. I did this for the first time for this recipe. Just pulse cubed chicken breasts a few times in a blender or food processor (I put the breast in the freezer for about 20 minutes beforehand).

@Denise You can make your own ground chicken, which I did for the first time for this recipe. Pulse cubed chicken in a blender or food processor a few times. I put the breasts in the freezer for about 20 minutes beforehand.

Wow this was a hit. I cook regularly for guests and they commented on the aroma and flavor. The meatballs were really soft and sticky to work with initially but browning was easy and they tasted amazing. I would not change a thing about this recipe.
I may have made mine a bit too spicy but delicious!

Cooked as written, but used kale as my greens and put them in with the sweet potatoes. I liked the combination of the curry sauce with the veggies. The meatballs were tender but overly salty with the curry sauce, I’ll reduce the soy sauce next time.

I loved this recipe, but I was a bit turned off by the ingredients in the Golden Curry brand.  I ended up making my own curry sauce and it turned out to be delicious and similar in texture and flavor.  To do this, I ended up adding chopped potatoes, corn starch, flour, a little bouillon paste, chicken broth, fresh and powdered ginger, chopped garlic, and garam masala.  My vegetarian daughter even made an exception to her rule of no meat.
This looks delicious, but I can’t find Japanese curry that doesn’t have MSG.  Any suggestions?
No suggestions. MSG is completely safe.
@Ella Thank you!  I’m go for it & use the pkg.  Recipe sounds too good to pass up

I agree with the person who said MSG is fine, but in the note, there is a link to a recipe for making your own curry brick. A much bigger pain than just buying the stuff, but doable if you're trying to avoid something specific.
This was an instant hit. I might reduce the size of the sweet potato if I want to fit more greens.  Used the green curry box, not so spicy.

This was delicious and a huge it. (I doubled the panko as recommended) I think in future I will fold in greens before adding the meatballs, it was difficult not to break apart the tender meatballs with all those greens. Served with Japanese pickles. So good!

This recipe was a hit in our house. Like others suggested I upped the panko to 3/4 cup. 
We have a lot of Korean markets in our area and like the Ottogi Mild curry. It comes in a bag, no need to break up any cubes. Just sprinkle it in the pot and stir gently. It mixes very easily.
Made this last night, and definitely going to add more breadcrumbs to the mix. I added around 1/3 cup more, but the meatballs were still too wet and fell apart while pan frying.
WOW this was good. I followed the tip on using 3/4 cup of breadcrumbs and recommend that others do the same.
This recipe is so good! It was quick and easy and is healthy. My husband loves Japanese curry so this is about to become a very regular staple for us!

This recipe is AMAZING! I made one change to it by using Impossible Ground Beef rather than ground chicken and it was still great. I would recommend watching the cook on the meatballs if you do that though, when I left them sit for 3 mins they were a smidge burnt. Still really good with the curry, but something to be aware of.

The curry roux came ~6.5 oz, so I doubled the recipe. It was delicious, and my husband said, " This is a keeper. " I hardly ever make the same dish twice, but I will abide by his wishes. I used the Java brand and it was very flavorful and a teensy bit spicy.

Personal mods: 

- increase panko to 3/4 cup
- add carrot chunks, shiitake mushrooms
- use veggie or chicken stock (2:1 stock to water ratio)

Followed the recommendations to double the panko.  This was delicious!  I couldn't find Japanese curry so I used Massamam curry with a bit of coconut milk.  Really great!  Even the family members who are foodies loved it.

I judge recipes based on how many spoonfuls I eat directly from the pot instead of a proper plate/bowl, and let’s just say I lost count with this one. I followed the recipe exactly except I only used half of an 11 oz container of spinach, which seemed like plenty. Next time, I’d add a serrano or jalapeño to the meatballs, as I like a little spice in a curry. I used the S&B Golden Curry brand roux. Due to the sodium content, I don’t think this will go into the regular rotation, but it will definitely be revisited at some point. Thank you Sohla!
added swiss chard. upped the bread crumbs like other commenters recommended. DELISH
