They didn't give a crunch but I still liked the textural result. Because they were more absorbant I had to make a more of the melted chocolate than the recipe normally calls for. They look festive when cut up. I brought them to a work party and received positive reviews.
I made and gifted 6 batches of this recipe, stealing a thin slice for myself from each batch. Yummy and so easy to make.

Quick and easy. I subbed craisins for the gum drops, and cut each slice into about 8 small pieces about the size of a large caramel. With the combination of textures in each piece, the result reminded me of a Chunky candy bar. My husband loved it!
This was fun, easy, and yummy! Will definitely be making again next holidays. I used half the cookie butter so the texture would firmer and more like the rocky road we had in Sydney. Thanks

Vegan option!
Did this with my grandson (4). Spectacular!
added quinoa puffs... really cute.
Forgot the marshmallows, so, now it's vegan.
(I already only buy vegan chocolate and candies; speculoos is vegan, the spread too. There are of course vegan marshmallows, so might try that next time. Though, those are usually, weaker, meltier, so not sure about that)
Posting from the Netherlands

We call this crack at my house.  The combination of chocolate, gummies (Target worms that I cut into 1/4" pieces), salty, spicy is addictive.  I have made this 4 times.  Found the speculous bitter.  The Nutella version was much better.  I liked the milk chocolate version better than semisweet though the chips don't melt well.  Ended up putting it in the food processor to get it smooth. Though I am not usually a fan of sprinkles they add a crunchy texture that really complements the candy.

Although the intro to the recipe rightly points out that you can swap in any kind of nut butter or mix-ins you want, I highly recommend giving this a try as written (I could not find Speculoos-brand cookies or cookie butter, but in North America the Biscoff brand of is almost the same thing and readily available in supermarkets). The spiced cookies and cookie spread, and the pumpkin pie spice, really add a special, moreish flavour to the whole thing that feels a bit more festive than usual.
the easiest and most crowd pleasing recipe. Take this to all of your holiday parties people will be so impressed and they look amazing! Truly idiotproof.

1. I don't have speculoos cookie butter in my local stores, we found Biscoff cookie butter and that works great. 
2. My picky kids, who refuse to eat nuts in any form, will eat any that I put in this, it's so good they don't notice. 
3. Genuinely, it has no business being this delicious. It's now a part our official Christmas cookie line up.

Made a version with Oreo’s, Nutella, and gummy worms for my 2025 Cookie Box. Stoner and kiddo favorite alike, most of 2nd most talked about this year. We do 40-50 boxes each solstice and this one helps ease the baking pains a bit.
What the heck are speculoos cookies?
Mmm,… fudgey goodness. I’m thinking of using some leftover candied fruits from fruitcake baking instead of the gumdrops, plus mini-marshmallows, almond butter, and whole roasted almonds.
I looked up cookie butter and speculoos. These are expensive ingredients so I'm hesitant to try this recipe.
Similar to peanut butter but made from speculoos cookies.  Trader Joe's has a great version.

In the U.S., we mostly know speculoos by the brand name Biscoff. Biscoff makes a jarred spread out of the cookies, with the consistency of peanut butter, known generally as cookie butter. Not sure how to make your own, though.

We've made this since I was a child (I'm 53), with butter, chocolate chips, small multicolored marshmallows, and chopped pecans. We call it "stained glass windows"; the recipe came from a "Cookies and Candies" book from the 60s or 70s. I'm excited to try this variation!

In the US you can find “Biscoff” brand speculoos-type cookies in most grocery stores. “Cookie butter” refers to Biscoff cookie butter sold in small jars similar to Nutella—it’s a creamy, Biscoff cookie flavored spread.
In the video, she uses a jar of Biscoff cookie butter—and says you can sub in peanut butter or tahini. Never seen it Biscoff in stores, but will look!
Trader Joe’s usually has cookie butter.
To those asking about cookie butter - Biscoff is the main brand!
Trader Joe carries Speculoos  (cookie butter).  It is a sweet cinnamon forward spread.

If you live in the US and have a Trader Joe's near you, they have cookie butter year round. In any store that has it, it will likely be near the peanut butter. It is a bit thicker than peanut butter and is basically made with ground up speculoos (spiced, kind of like a crunchy gingerbread) cookies and a few other ingredients. I've never made my own cookie butter but Google would probably have an answer on that one.
People are asking about the cookie butter - I presume they mean something like the biscoff cookie spread (?), found next to Nutella and nut butter in well-stocked stores.
You can buy cookie butter at Trader Joe’s.  My local grocery has it too.
Trader Joe’s makes a speculoos cookie butter for those of us lucky enough to live near one.

To our friends asking about cookie butter, Trader Joe's makes a product called Speculoos Cookie Butter Spread. I'm assuming that is the ingredient that is called for here. Could brand names be specified when specialty items are used in NYT recipes?
Yes, what is speculoos cookie butter and where in the world do you find it besides Australia.

Sohla, you are a wonderful presenter! Fantastic video demo. 

These are super cool, I definitely want to try making them (I doubt my upstate chain grocery store has the cookie butter, but Nutella and PB will be available).
Lots of folks asking where to get this. Trader Joe’s sells this, next to the peanut butter.
Can you freeze this? How long can you make it ahead and keep it in the fridge or freezer? Thanks!

Dear Sage, re: "What is cookie butter?" Cookie butter is found with the Peanut Butter in the grocery store. Brand name is "Biscoff Cookie Butter", named after their yummy Biscoff cookies. There are also store-brand versions called "Speculoos" Cookie Butter. Both brands are found at Kroger's (Fred Meyer), Safeway, and Winco. Good Luck.

For those asking about cookie butter: https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cookie_butter A lot of supermarkets have this on the shelf next to peanut butter. Trader Joe’s sells a version. I’ve also found it on line. It’s delicious…..

Trader Joe's has a Speculoos Cookie Butter, and I have seen the Lotus Biscoff Cookie Butter in large USA grocery stores in the European/imported foods section.  The Biscoff cookies that Sohla uses can be found in that section as well.  They're also available on Amazon.

My 5 year old and I watched all the new cookie week videos, and she voted for this one as the first recipe we try. We took Sohla’s fancy route, with Nutella and mixed quality dark/milk chocolate bars. Very rich and surprisingly delicious - appealing to kids and adults alike! They don’t carry speculoos cookies at our grocery store so subbed Dare spice snaps and worked in a pinch. Would highly recommend!

Next up, Lemon-Tumeric Crinkles…
I live in suburban Florida, and our local Publix stocks speculoos cookies and butter under the Biscoff brand. Trader Joe’s also stocks cookie butter.
Interesting that the recipe calls for chocolate chips. In Australia chocolate chips don't melt very well, which makes them hold shape when baked. Why not use blocks of chocolate, which melt easily?
Could you use dulce de leche instead of the biscoff?
In the US I found speculaas from Bostonspice and king Arthur baking co. Hope to make these for family Christmas party
Speculoos = Biscoff. Speculoos Cookie Butter is Biscoff Spread. Look for it next to Nutella and Peanut Butter. It’s in most larger supermarkets these days, but also at places like Target.
The classic UK version that i grew up with includes both Nutella and also some golden syrup, which would make the set-up chocolate more malleable, and just biscuits, and is sliced as a cake.

For everyone asking what cookie butter is, its a strange and wonderful American invention. If I were y'all, I'd maybe sub with Nutella, peanut butter, or some other type of sweet spread. Marmite need not apply.
Would these freeze well?
I just learned my Meijer store in a small town in Michigan has Lotus Biscoff cookies AND Biscoff cookie butter!   This recipe is totally doable!
Cookie Butter is in every Trader Joe’s and almost every other grocery store I’ve ever been in. By the peanut butter.
Question: would this melt/soften too much if left at room temperature?

Not sure where I went wrong. I followed the ratios but turned out so dense could barely cut through it.  Maybe because used Nutella?  Expected mine wouldn't be as pretty because used dried cranberries instead of gum drops but since there's no separation between elements there's none of the mosaic beauty.  That said it is really delicious with balance of salt from the pecans & tartness of cranberries.  Nice crunch from the Amaretti I used but no flavor & not a hint of the pumpkin pie spice.

I just made this with what I had on hand, trusting that the recipe is a flexible as promised. My ingredients included: almonds, dried cherries, tahini, coconut cookies, Swedish fish candies, marshmallows. I doubled the recipe, eyeballed a bunch of measurements, and topped with holiday sprinkles. It’s chilling now, and fingers crossed for success! We have a cookie swap at work today so these are headed that way.
Maria - how did the cookies go over at the swap?!

These were fantastic!
I have made them twice this week both times with sun butter instead of speculoos butter it adds so much rich fudginess. The first time I made it pretty much as written the second time I added mint extract and trador joes holiday mint stars. Wow great recipe!
This was weird and delicious and so much fun. They look beautiful, and, even though I knew the ingredients going in, I was so surprised by just how much it tasted like THE HOLIDAYS.

SO GOOD.  I omitted the pumpkin spice (I didn't have any) (yes I know I can make some, TYVM) and my mix-ins were Biscoff cookies, toasted pecans, dried cherries, and marshmallows.  A full slice was a bit too much so I've cut mine into squares, as you would for fudge.  Thanks Sohla for this recipe - I'm sure this will be a forever holiday indulgence!
We loved these! Simple and delicious.
After cutting, do you store the slices in the fridge or at room temperature?
@Meow I have had mine out at room temp for a couple of days now and there’s no melting or stickiness.

I'm in Mexico City, so it's weird that cookie butter is more widely available here than in NYC?, if not ubiquitous or well known. Mercado Libre has a Great Value version and Soriana down the street has Biscoff on the shelves.

My 6 year old and I had fun making this.  We used Christmas (peppermint) gum drops and walnuts instead of pecans. We bought the cookie butter and Speculoos cookies at TJ’s. My 6 year old claimed to like it, but I have never seen him not finish a cookie.  I thought it was just ok. I think the peppermint gum drops ruined it.  I would make it again with dried fruit and a higher quality chocolate.  I do love how easy it is to make and that it is super kid friendly.

Made this tonight and had a lot of fun!  I love cookie week!  Couldn't find the gumdrops so substituted Skittles gummies and used chili crisp cashews for the nuts.  If you are in the USA, the speculoos (biscoff) cookies and the cookie butter are sold in Target stores if you have one near you.

For those asking what is 'cookie butter', I found Biscoff Cooke Butter on the same aisle as peanut butter, almond butter, nutella, and used Biscoff cookies as the cookie crumble. I think this is fun and beautiful, and super easy to make.  Possibly up the pumpkin pie spice, maybe add candied ginger?  It was lovely and festive on the serving tray, I can also see it being very pretty in little treat bags.  Yes, to someone else's comments, this would be a great make with kids and seniors recipe.
I don't like biscoff or cookie butter. What would be a good substitution?

After our travels to the Netherlands and Belgium, we fell in love with speculuss. I find it many places, Trader Joe’s  has cookies butter , even Kroger.  You can make the spice blend yourself and have around similar to pumpkin pie spice .  The spice blend is different. Many spices including pepper.

While visiting New Zealand over Christmas last year, I was very confused as to what Rocky Road and Lollie Slices actually were (they were often seen next to each other).  When I asked about them, one was often used to describe the other, adding to my confusion.  However, after making Sohla's recipe for Rocky Road, I am a happy convert!  An easy cookie to make and share.  And now I have a use for my Bischof Cookie Butter that does not involve simply eating it by the spoonful! Thanks Sohla!

For those who love an insanely sweet treat, this recipe is just right! Made it with my kids who had loads of fun, although they now refuse to eat it as it's very sweet indeed. Would recommend omitting the speculous cream and add some dark chocolate in the mix. Used cinnamon and coffee spice in the chocolate which achieved a great result. T

Made this as described except my add-ins were marshmallows, glace cherries, Nilla wafers, and pecans. Cut into bicotti-like slices and then into thirds. VERY sweet but also addictive. One loaf pan makes more than enough—we are keeping a quarter of the batch and giving the rest away, otherwise we’d never get through it all. A fun and fast recipe, highly indulgent, looks pretty, good for potlucks or cookie exchanges.

I could not find any spice drops that weren't mint flavor (I didn't think mint would go well with all these flavors) so I used a holiday version of the candy "dots." I was still a little weary so I only used a few of them but it turned out great! I also added pretzels and it added another salty element that we liked.

Standard containers of Biscoff cookie butter, Biscoff cookies, spice drops, and marshmallows make enough for this recipe X3. So I would recommend getting enough chocolate and pecans to triple the recipe! It is so so easy and tasty. The slices of rocky road are adorable and very festive looking. It's the perfect low effort, high reward recipe for holiday cookie giving.
Omitted pecans and increased amount of cookies and marshmallows to compensate, a new holiday tradition!

I found speculoos cookie butter at Walmart (of all places). I ordered the cookies, but the order was canceled due to "location." Trader Joe's allegedly has them, so I'll look there today. Sounds like this recipe is endlessly flexible, so if I have to use gingersnaps, I will.

Bon appetit!!
Trader Joe's does carry it.  It's called Speculoos Crunchy Cookie Butter Spread.  It's what I used in this recipe and it worked well.
Made as written.  Used DOTS for the gum drops.  A big hit for something gooey and sweet at the end of dinner. I think this would be great to make with kids.
You can purchase the cookie butter at Trader Joe's.
Looks delightful! Trader Joe’s sometimes has a product called Speculoos Cookie Butter (it’s hit and miss.)
Biscoff and Trader Joes both have this kind of cookie butter. You can buy both on Amazon.
I'd like to make a gluten free version of this recipe.  It looked promising until the "speculoos" and "speculoos cookie butter".

Like numerous other people, I came here to look for info on cookie butter and substitutes. Here you go: https://www.foodnetwork.com/how-to/packages/food-network-essentials/what-is-cookie-butter I have some of the Trader Joe's one, but not enough so I'm going to use some almond butter or tahini. I like the idea of this but don't want gumdrops, so I'm just gonna break up a bunch of cookies I found on sale, marshmallows and pecans.
I was able to find both Speculoos butter and cookies on Amazon.

I made this with Golden Grahams cereal, mini marshmallows, mini-Junior Mints and some macadamia nuts left over from Thanksgiving. Great pop of peppermint from the mints. So easy to put this together with anything you might have in the pantry.
Rocky Road is also known as a candy in the U.S., not just in Australia and U.K. See's has been selling it for decades.
Trader Joe's sells speculoos Cookie Butter in my area,
Oh no, my  chocolate chips seized, with lots of things mixed in.  Even though I microwaved it all do only 10 seconds at a time.  What can I do now?
Nope. Made these as described and just NOPE. Too sweet, strange flavor combination, and a waste of perfectly good chocolate.

Oh Man! The only thing I did different on this recipe (I did watch the video) was substitute dried cherries and pistachio pieces for the red and green gum drops. And it came out like cardboard. I have a friend that is a chef who suggested that using chocolate chips was the wrong kind of chocolate to use for this recipe and that my chocolate had seized. So she suggested I melt the whole mess and add nearly scalding whipping cream to see if that would loosen it up. It did just a tiny bit.

I made this the other day, and like others had to substitute based on what I had. I used salted pistachios instead of walnuts and Welch’s fruit chews instead of gum drops - not as pretty! But the end result is pretty interesting. I also added cinnamon to round things out. Looking forward to reactions from the people I bring it to :-)

I had a hard time locating the spice drops, finally found them in our CVS store. Personally, I didn’t like the flavor they added, I did like the texture though. Maybe, next time I would use gummies or dots
@Terri I swapped gum drops for red Swedish fish and it was the perfect mild flavor! Same texture and look achieved too!
Trader Joes has speculoos with all of their other cookies and speculoos cookie butter is in their peanut butter aisle.

Made these with Tony’s Chocolonely - 1dark, 1 milk bar combined. Next time I’ll probably use a nut butter and dried fruit with pecans. I didn’t like the spice flavors, especially with the gumdrop/cookie bits. These remind me of the cathedral cookies from the 60’s.
These were fun to make! The first time with spice drops. The second batch we swapped them out for M&Ms.

This was a great recipe for kids to work on together as a kick off to winter break! A great recipe for young bakers who are learning to be independent in the kitchen. So forgiving and the best part for the kids was they could lick the bowls and spoons clean afterwards for a change.

I did biscoff cookies, sponge toffee, praline nut mix and dried tart cherries. Instead of the biscoff spread I added a tablespoon of fine espresso powder to cut some of the sweetness. Turned out great! Will make again next year.

Not all mini marshmallows are equal in weight when measured by volume, & vice versa. Strongly recommend measuring the volume for the marshmallows, and if you like that ratio, stick with it. I first weighed the marshmallows and came up with about 2/3 cup.

This was so fun to make and really delicious! The salt at the beginning really enhanced all the other fun flavors that were added after the chocolate and cookie butter was melted and incorporated. For friends asking what cookie butter is or having trouble finding it, I used Biscoff cookie butter and the Biscoff cookies. It was readily available in my Midwest grocery store.

YUM. This was my first rocky road experience and thanks to Sohla’s whimsical recipe and charming video, it was a resounding success. This was so little effort, and really good. The crunchy cookie bits, the squishy marshmallow buts, and the chewy gummy bits were texturally so fun.  Don’t try to slice it too thin, like I did, or you risk a falling apart mess.
I overdid the add-ins. Next time I may double the chocolate base but start with the additions for a single batch.  I can always add more.
Will they store or melt at room temperature?
This turned out great! It’s definitely going to become one of my go to holiday recipes. The speculoos cookies/butter and the pumpkin pie spice really punch up the flavor.
This is totally worth making. Couldn’t find spice drops, so used holiday Dots and it was good.

Made this tonight to take to a friend’s place for Christmas Eve dinner tomorrow. My one change was to use Graham Crackers instead of Speculoos, because they were out. ( the y were out of marshmallows too…was it because of this recipe?!) 
The recipe reminds me (in appearance and general concept) of a more child- friendly version of a recipe from the Green & Blacks cookbook — Konditor & Cook’s Chocolate Biscuit Cake. That one is made with 60% dark chocolate, unsalted butter, golden syrup, digestive biscuits, walnuts, sultanas, glacé cherries and an egg. 
This recipe is much simpler - easy for kids to make, and suitably whimsical.  
Can’t wait to taste it!

Wonderful to see Rocky Road in the NYT! As an Australian overseas who has been eating Rocky Road for 40  years, I’ve never had it with speculoos or cookie butter. So don’t sweat it if you can’t find specific ingredients, you can sub in any plain cookie. One of the many wonderful things about Rocky Road is that you can easily vary the ingredients to match your pantry or even food allergies - gluten free pretzels are a yummy addition!
I added dried cherries instead of gumdrops drops, and lightly salted the edge of the bars. So good!

I loved this recipe and will make it again. Great for gifting. Next time I won’t use Dots gumdrops (too chewy, too blah), but a sugared, soft gummy of some kind. Candied ginger might be the ticket. It’s probably just as well I went with the Dots because I would have eaten it all in one sitting if it were any more delicious.

I don't know where this easy candy has been all my life! I, too, know many of the people I cook for would object to spice drops. And I couldn't find any near me, so I used a mix of crystalized ginger, dried cherries, and Annie's fruit snacks.    And the second batch, I swapped the Biscoff cookies and cookie butter for Gluten Free Tates Zinger Ginger Cookies and Tahini.  So good!  And super fun to make.  A great addition to the cookie box.

My 6 year old son enjoyed making these alone! He was able to pick out different ingredients from the pantry to add. The fatter type sprinkles look much prettier then the tiny ball ones so I would go with those. We added chocolate covered pretzels and everyone love that.

These were initially unappealing with the gum drops, etc. I wrote them off as kid food. But I watched the video and took Sohla's suggestion for a variant... which was excellent!
--Used a blend of good dark and milk chocolate bars (mostly dark)
--2 big spoonfuls of peanut butter
--3/4 t cinnamon
--1/4 t kosher salt
--1 cup each: salted peanuts, marshmallows, Corn Chex, speculoos

This version of Rocky Road is so unique and was a great addition to our Christmas dessert table.  It’s very rich though, so I cut the 12 pieces into thirds for a yield of 36 pieces. In the U.S. Lotus markets speculoos as Biscoff cookies and cookie butter.

Made these with my 9 year old and the recipe was very easy and adaptable.
We used Nutella, roasted pecans, rice krispies, marshmallows and lots of sprinkles.
Our mix was way pared down from the original recipe, but it was delicious and will definitely be a part of our holiday baking rotation.

This was a grownup type of rocky road. The cookie / speculo0s butter made all the difference. A lovely presentation in the the cookie boxes I gifted this Christmas. Easy to execute. Will be making these again.

Absolutely no one in my family liked these and I ended up throwing the whole thing out. What a waste of my speculoos cookies and paste, not to mention my good chocolate! We love all the ingredients but not together, apparently. I think it was the pumpkin spice flavor that really was off-putting in these so if I did these again, I would leave that out but I honestly doubt I’ll ever do them again. We love pumpkin spice but I wish I’d omitted it this time.
@Kathryn M or getting tinfoil between your teeth! I still have some old style fillings! OUCH!!

I made these last holiday season for gifting. I got VERY creative with them. My favorite mixi-in? Pretzels! These are so easy and so much fun. I would highly recommend making these with children. Or better yet let your children make these on their own! Everyone who received them wanted the recipe.
Amazing delicious, great for those types of events when you don’t know what to bring. I put a few in clear gift bags during the holidays for presents

Made this last year and it was SO good. Definitely don’t skip the gingerbread pieces. Question: Has anyone put this in a cookie box? If so, did the flavors take over the entire box like peppermint does?

I know it’s a kid friendly recipe or whatever but it would be way better with butter or something in the chocolate to be more fudgey and less like just hard chocolate. And pretzels would also be nice in it.
For those wondrring about speculoos, it's dutch.
Nigella Lawson version is much better
The effort - low. Result - spectacular.  A real show stopper when the cookie box is open.
I used peanut butter instead of speculoos cookie butter, but did have Biscoff's for the special taste. These are delicious, easy to make, very versatile, and incredibly festive. A big hit!
Can I make this on a half sheet pan for more of a “bark” or drop it on wax paper lined cookie sheets?  First time it was hard to slice as a loaf shape.
I made these with pomegranates instead of gum drops and they were amaaaaazing

I was surprised by the comment that Rocky Road might be synonymous with ice cream in the US. I've always associated it with a soft chocolate cookie, with chunks of semi-sweet chocolate, walnuts, and marshmallow. No speculoos, pumpkin spice, or gumdrops. Archway's Rocky Roads (now, I believe, discontinued) used to be my favorite cookie.
I'd like to give this to neighbors as a holiday gift. Would you make one loaf per family? Would one loaf be a decent amount for 2 families? Thanks for an guidance.

I made this with chopped candied ginger this year instead of gumdrops. Last year I found that grocery store gumdrops were unpleasantly chewy. Anything soft and fruity would make a nice substitute taste-wise, if not for the cute color. I’ll try adding candied pineapple next. Just gotta find something with a similar texture that is colorful.

I made these as a part of a holiday cookie table I like to set up for my coworkers.  I make about 12 - 14 types of cookies to have a variety and serve with homemade hot chocolate and homemade peppermint marshmallow.  These were a hit, and they were gone quite fast.  Personally, the spiced gumdrops were not my favorite, but they look beautiful and festive when they are sliced.  I might try it with candied citrus peels sometime.  This recipe can be changed up to fit your tastes.  The marshmallows, crunchy cookies and nuts add such great texture.  Mix it up.  They’re fun and festive!
Delicious! I thought it would look pretty on my cookie plate, but I’ll make it again because it’s a delight to eat.

stats.Cook Time: 1 hour 35 minutes, plus at least 6 hours’ resting and proofing

comments.commentBody: Hol-ee Cow!!! This is the best new bread-y thing I've enjoyed this much!! From the muffin bottoms to the pull-apartness to that amazing dough, these are the greatest! And the tops are browned more, plus the flaked salt, so they taste VERY pretzelly (if the pretzel had a recessive gene for croissantism)!! Parker House WHAT?!?! Nope, these gorgeous buns are more fun to make than they are to eat, and they are sublime to eat! WOW.

@Louise Thompson. I had NO plans to makr dinner rolls yesterday, but this recipe came up and based on your candid - and inspiring - review, I made a double batch for no particular reason other than to freeze half.   I share your "Hol-ee-COW" opinion!  However for those trying to use free-standing silicone muffin cups instead of a muffin tin.....it doesn't work.   The rolls  don't brown and puff up well.  When I "properly" made another batch (because I had doubled the recipe) with a popover pan, they came out so good that nobody ate much of anything else I served lolololol.
Love it... these are my new dinner rolls. I only baked for half the time and froze them in freezer bags and I take them out and cook them for the remaining time to serve.

Funny that it refers to rolling into a square, then says 11" wide (which would be an 11 inch square). Then it says to cut lengthwise and squares don't have length. So... confusing for a second.
Roll 1/2 the dough into an 11 inch square and cut  the square into 6 even strips... Stack the strips... and cut the stack into 6 even pieces...    Repeat with other 1/2 of the dough. 
Fun and easy recipe with option to add herbs, garlic, or cinnamon sugar to tweak. Looks fancy for special dinner.
That confused me for a bit, too. :-)

Made these using the linked enriched dough recipe. They puffed up more than the picture when baking and taste great. I'd recommend putting the tin on a rimmed sheet as the butter from the rolls leaked out and dripped. Some layers fell away when I removed them from the muffin tin and, despite buttering everything, I did have some sticking to the tin. Don't skip the salt on top. It is essential. Fussy and a lot of work but I will happily make again for Thanksgiving.

Made these rolls yesterday. I have made bread with Clair Saffitz All-Purpose Enriched Bread dough recipe, and loved the bread for toast and sandwiches. However using this dough to make these rolls produced a beautiful looking roll but one that was disappointing.  The rolls stuck to the buttered pan, the vertical slices  easily separated  and even with all the butter and salt the roll was not satisfying. The next day they were even less tasteful. Make the bread, not the rolls.
The times do not add up to 7 hours 40 minutes.  Where is step 6?  How do I brush the top of the muffin tin (Step 1)?

@Ann Marie
You have to make the All Purpose Enriched Dough recipe linked in the Ingredients list. That’s where the time is. Also, there is no Step 6. Step 5 is the completion.

@Ann Marie you’re meant to brush the butter in the muffin cups and on the metal that connects all the cups. The whole pan has to be painted with butter so the rolls don’t stick when they overflow the cups.

The step 6 they are referring to is in the recipe for the All-Purpose Bread Dough.

One of the ingredients for THIS recipe is the dough that you make in the All-Purpose Bread dough recipe.  Follow that recipe to make the dough (but only go up to Step 6 in that recipe) and then come back to this recipe to finish making these rolls with that dough.

I've made these twice now and they are awesome! First time I used a metal muffin pan and the second time I used a silicone muffin pan. I had to use the full 22 min to get the right color in the metal tin. I had to go up to 27 min for color on the bottom in the silicone pan. Worth every moment either way!

My husband wished for  a soft bread for dipping in olive oil, remembering doing this at a favourite restaurant. This is the perfect bread for that. I made a whole charcuterie board. The way the bread split apart was perfect for all the condiments ready to eat with it: cheese, pate, prosciutto. I enjoyed the process of making them. I imagine substituting a whole wheat dough using the same assembling mode.

Oh my!! The pictures for this recipe got me. I first saw the recipe for Thanksgiving, but it seemed too labor intensive to tackle. So I waited until I was asked to bring rolls for Christmas.

I prepared by reading and rereading the recipe, and watching the tutorial twice, then reading all comments.

What I learned: much easier than I thought. Make the rolls on the smaller side (3 layers each) make 2 dozen instead of 12, I don’t have a rolling pin so a wine bottle worked just fine and my 11 inches was only approximate, can make a few days ahead.

Absolutely would make again (in the beginning I swore I would NEVER make them again) the finished project was fabulous!
Can you make these with vegan butter?
@dh. if you are asking me, NO.

These rolls are delicious, but absolutely massive. They melded together on top of the muffin pan and looked a little grotesque. 

Because they are much too large as an accompaniment to a meal, I would like to try again and limit the layers to three, doubling the amount made.

I made these for Thanksgiving and they were a huge hit! I made sure to butter the pan all around the edges so they wouldn’t stick, and they came out pretty well although a couple spots needed some gentle wiggling to remove them. Most of them did also fall apart when I put them in a bread bowl as others have mentioned, but my guests were happy because they could choose how much they wanted (a big roll or just a few pieces). I served them with softened salted butter for spreading and everyone raved about them. Don’t skip the flaky salt! Maldon brand worked great — I bought mine on Amazon.
Do you think I could make and refrigerate the dough two days before I cook these?

Excellent, and a huge hit with the family. I used my favorite enriched dinner roll recipe (Williams Sonoma bread cookbook, buttermilk and eggs) and divided each half into 12 rolls, not 6, because when I did 6, the rolls totally overwhelmed my muffin pan. Used lots of butter but I skipped the egg wash and sprinkled kosher salt onto the last butter application, which worked just fine.

I am obsessed!  The fan-tans were difficult for me to arrange and cut, so I changed the shape to crescents for the remaining half, and those are the ones that flew out of the bread basket.  I also thought that the portions were too big, so I'm now making 24 crescents.  I've also replaced ½ cup of the all-purpose flour with ½ cup of who!e-wheat flour.  Cool, freeze, then thaw and reheat for only 5 minutes.  My spouse is now asking if I could sprinkle cinnamon sugar on a few. Sure!

I did a trial run last week to see how these would work for Thanksgiving. First off, these things are huge! Im probably going to cut them smaller and make 24 rather than 12. They are really too big to serve with such a big meal and they looked a bit deformed in the pan because of the size. They almost reminded me of a popover. I froze them after they were baked and they reheated beautifully.  I placed them on a sheet pan covered half time with foil and then uncovered to crisp up about 15 min at 400. Over all they were really good albeit a little more work on the front end but once they are in the fridge you dont have to worry about them for 24 hours then can either bake them fresh or reheat from the freezer which is what I am going to do!
How does the dry yeast get activated? I thought you need to dissolve the yeast in water that is about 110F to activate it.

@Karen the activation that many recipes call for is meant to prove that your yeast is alive. It’s absolutely not necessary if you know a particular jar isn’t old, though the instant yeast does a little better at hydrating in a dough  without the activation period than the active dry

I made these for Thanksgiving for my white roll loving husband.  Since I was going to be in the kitchen all day it did not seem a problem to keep track of them for all of that time. I followed the directions exactly.  OMGosh!  These are amazing!  We loved them.  The recipe is now in my Thanksgiving folder and they will appear again on our table…maybe for his birthday?  We are splitting the leftover slices and toasting them gently in the toaster oven.  No butter needed (:  Thanks for this recipe!

Hi! I used Claire Saffitz's All-Purpose Enriched Bread recipe as base. I do not have a stand mixer but made the tangzhong, made the dough then left in fridge over night. Cut into strips, shaped, another rise and baked. Forgetting butter did not matter. These are delicious! Soft, salty, yellow, great! Recommend!

"Great, but what do you do with all that leftover oil?". If you used EVO, throw it away. If you used peanut oil or some other high-smoke oil IT IS STILL GOOD. Strain through a fine mesh strainer and put back on shelf. The oil should be good for at least a few fry sessions provided you do not abuse it. Label the bottle with what it was used for. My experience is that fried chicken oil can be used for french fries, etc. Oil used for fried fish should only be used for frying fish.

Never just discard oil!  It is considered a hazardous material.  Cool it, poor it back into the container and take to your local dump that takes hazardous materials.

What we do is cool it in the freezwer in a plastic container, then simply put it in the garbage. Since oil is not considered a hazardous substance by the EPA (see https://www.hazardouswasteexperts.com/is-used-oil-hazardous-waste/), and https://www.epa.gov/emergency-response/vegetable-oils-and-animal-fats, you do not need to take it to a dump that takes hazardous materials. "Hazardous materials" are very carefully defined and used cooking oil does not fall within the definition.

@Nancy if your community takes food waste, they will take the used oil.  Our community has curbside food waste and a location has large containers for food waste, that’s where I would drop off my used oil.

Lots of questions and comments about what to do with the leftover oil. You can simply strain it and use it again.  You should be able to use it four or five more times before it’s no longer viable. Made this for Thanksgiving for family of 4. The directions were very clear, especially the frying part which was new to me. Used regular all purpose flour and breading was very crispy and not heavy. The spice mix was terrific (used fresh instead of dried sage because had it on hand) and the lemon zest was surprisingly perfect and added a fresh note. An elegant tasty fried chicken recipe! Appreciated other comments about how to clean up used oil. I feel empowered now having made fried chicken! Wasn’t difficult or too messy at all.

This was my first time making homemade fried chicken and deep frying and this 100% worth it! So delicious and extra crispy! Although it tasted too salty to me so I will probably reduce the MSG and salt when I make it again. I brined bone in chicken thighs for about 36 hours.

Game changer! A lot of work but well worth it. Iso delix’d recommend an electric fryer and cook outside or in the basement as we did since it was raining. Otherwise your house will smell like fried food for days.

A game changer! So delicious. I’d recommend cooking outside in an electric fryer or in the basement as I did since it was raining. Otherwise your house will smell like fried chicken for days. Our guests were ecstatic. I didn’t tell them about the MSG, I remember that being a thing but it really enlivens the flavor. Also get some good chicken bouillon powder from a Chinese market.

This is my first Sohla recipe-  would give this 3.5 stars. It’s easy to make and flavorful, but I found the flavor profile to be more unique than your standard fried chicken. To the point where after eating 2 pieces, I’m tired of eating this flavor. It’s like if you ate a m extravagant chocolate sundae with all the fixings, you could only take a handful of bites before you call it quits

Made the whole menu - excluding only the sweet potatoes - and it was DIVINE. Garnered raves from my beloved Gen Z offspring and my equally beloved yet far more grumpy Gen X husband. Can't gush enough!!
I made this and it was delicious!  I followed every step to the letter, and also made some tenders too.  Everyone loved it.
@Nancy R same! Made it for Thanksgiving and it was perfect and delicious. Everyone raved. Her instructions and the video was invaluable. I set up a fry station just as she did and had zero trouble.

I made these without MSG (because of a dietary restriction) but followed the recipe to a T otherwise. Be aware that the cayenne pepper level may be aggressive for children or spice-averse adults, but I loved it. It was my first time frying chicken and I made these for Thanksgiving instead of a Turkey: HUGE HIT! We will be repeating this recipe for Christmas.

Made this for Thanksgiving and it was excellent. Brined for a bit more than 24 hours, and the meat was juicy and tender with a crispy, flavorful breading. We used an induction cooktop to keep the temperature consistent, which worked very well. Would make this again!
With the skin, the chicken is juicier; without the skin, it has a slightly more delicate flavor and is less fatty - I prefer the second option.

Big mistake not using baking powder to cut the flour. Makes a huge difference in the crust. I've been doing fried chicken turkey for years at Thanksgiving by combining Samin Nosrat's buttermilk turkey recipe with serious eats fried chicken recipe. Highly recommend.
Used cooking oil should be taken to your local Tox-away site.

First time I've tried frying anything. I was always scared of handling the oil but Sohla's video gave me an extra boost of courage. The temperatures and times worked out exactly as scripted. The chicken was crispy on the outside with lots of tasty craggy bits. Inside was juicy, well-seasoned, and flavourful. By far - BY FAR - the best fried chicken I've ever had. I'm a longtime Sohla fan and this recipe was truly next level - both in deliciousness, and in making the technique accessible.
Delicious. Will reduce sugar in brine next time and change up the sprinkle seasoning to fit the mood.

I typically don’t eat fried food through out the year. So, this recipe was new and exciting to make for the holidays! My husband and I typically enjoy none traditional proteins (duck, lamb, or ribs) for the holidays so fried chicken was perfect. Instead of the salt water brine, I used buttermilk and fried the chicken in sunflower oil. It was SO juicy, then skin was crispy and crunchy. Best idea ever!

I made the dish without msg and couldn’t tell I actually brined the chicken. With such a small amount of salt in the brine it did nothing for the chicken flavor. How about suggesting a different amount of salt if not using msg? (Who has msg lying around?)

Came out exactly as described in the video. Used the 00 Italian flour and was watchful to start temp at 325 and not get below 275. Cooked perfectly. Stayed crisp for hours, juicy, perfectly seasoned inside and out. It was a revelation, first homemade fried chicken that was better than what I’ve had at restaurants. The herb seasoning a big part of the revelation, can never have fried chicken without it. Would love variations on the seasoning.

Wow. Surprisingly crispy. I've made fried and oven-fried chicken many times before and usually need to do a very goopy/messy coating to get a crunchy skin. I think the 00 flour really makes a difference. Will be making this again. Brine was easy and so was the coating and the seasoning.
We made with half the batch chicken, half the batch tofu. Excellent on both accounts!

I've made this 3 times in the past year.  Twice for Thanksgiving, and once just because it's great!  Although it takes some time to marinate, the actual cooking part is very straightforward.  "Best fried chicken I've ever had" seems to be the top eval from my table guests.
I did the "00" flour this past Thanksgiving, and the chicken was even more crispy than my previous times making the dish.
I've never used MSG in this recipe yet, but I can't imagine it being better than the results I've gotten

I didn’t have legs but had a whole chicken. I cut it apart and used it instead. It was delicious. The fried sage, while delicious, was unnecessary since sage was in the herb mix already. I saved the fried sage for other recipes.

This is really delicious. I substituted maple syrup for the corn syrup and it was no problem. The cake was light and fluffy, and the topping was great, I will use it when I make pumpkin bread as well.
This might be the best cake I’ve ever made. So incredibly fluffy, and the caramel pecan topping adds the right level of sweetness.

@Allison I agree that the technique of whipping the eggs, sugars, and corn syrup yields such a fluffy and moist cake. I made the cake with 1 tablespoon of pumpkin pie spice as I like a spicy cake. It was delicious. The caramel topping came out easily and perfectly adding a unique topping to a wonderful cake! I’m making this for Thanksgiving!

This cake is delicious but should come with a warning for possible dental work. I, too, cooked the caramel mixture according to the recipe for 4-6 minutes not realizing that would result in a very hard candy coating. Luckily, I was the first to try it when serving my guests but unluckily, I lost a crown in the process! Lucky again the next day when my dentist was able to just reglue the crown- perhaps using the toffee that was left on the tooth. LOL
Can you substitute maple syrup for the corn syrup

@Linda Bell
Recipe specifically states “there is no substitute for it.” That looks like your answer.

I have been baking the pecan pie recipe in Rose Beranbaum's The Pie and Pastry Bible for years. That recipe calls for Lyles Golden Syrup https://www.lylesgoldensyrup.com/ a welcome alternative to the over the top sweetness of Corn Syrup - I bet you could replace it here as well...

@Linda Bell If you can spare a few minutes, Sohla explains why you need corn syrup for this recipe and addresses … at somewhat comic length … the health considerations in the YouTube video where she makes the whole meal. You can find it on The NY Times Cooking channel or there’s a link in the feature.

I misread the instructions in step 6. You’re supposed to bring the frosting to a vigorous boil, which will take 4-6 minutes, and then take it off the heat. I brought it to a full boil and then kept it boiling 4-6 minutes resulting in a caramel that cooled to the consistency of a Sugar Daddy.
It uses a whole can of pumpkin!  Hallelujah! I hate to measure that stuff or use only part of a can; the rest always goes bad in the 'fridge. Can't wait to make this.

In other recipes, I have successfully used both brown rice syrup and/or Lyle's Golden Syrup as 1:1 substitutions for corn syrup - specifically to those recipes where I want a caramelized or gooey result.

The cake itself is divine, but, like Merideth I had problems with the caramel.  I let it boil for about a minute then cool for several minutes until it settled.  The cake had risen to a domed shape so when I poured the caramel onto the cake, it ran off towards the edges.  I spent about 5 minutes scraping sauce back toward the center of the cake until it had cooled and thickened enough to stay in place.  The end texture of the caramel was pretty rubbery. Any suggestions on where I went wrong?

@betsy I ALWAYS use cake strips around my pans round or rectangle.  Wet them thoroughly, size appropriately with adjustable Velcro and place tightly around pan before placing in oven.  The strips are fabric and metallic on the outside  so they keep the edges of the cake cooler so the middles doesn’t rise  but stays level with the more cool edges.  Works every time and I bake perfectly level cakes even at 5000 feet elevation.
Would this work if using gluten free flour as substitute?
@Marishka I used Bobs red mill gf flour and the big difference is texture of the cake. It came out more dense than expected.

Plainsgal: I think you could use the glaze without the pecans… they are just sprinkled on top. The glaze (without the pecans) is basically a version of penuche frosting… it doesn’t include cream, but is otherwise the same, so you could make a traditional penuche which should have a very similar flavor to the recommended glaze.
Measured everything by weight and this turned out perfectly, no notes. My spouse claims to not like pumpkin but he had two slices and was talking about how I'll have to make it again.
Can I use gluten free flour?
Light or dark Karo?

Hmmm…I have a super cute Nordicware pumpkin cake pan. Thinking of chopping the pecans and putting them into the bottom of the pan before adding the batter. Then, I’d glaze the unmolded cake before serving.
Think I’ll play with this before the big day…

Very tasty cake, came together quickly. Fun to watch her video and see the process. I would definitely make again. Don’t be intimidated by this one, just pay close attention to the ingredients and instructions. We loved this for thanksgiving and leftovers!

First, I refuse to use corn syrup so I subbed pure maple syrup and it turned out perfectly!  I agree those who experience the topping hardening must have stirred it after it’s directed in recipe.  I accidentally mixed up my orange fruit/vegetable and this got sweet potato.  Every raved, ate seconds and requested some to go.  Served with fresh whipped cream.

So dang delicious and worth the effort.  I made this in my MIL's kitchen, so it was a hassle finding everything I needed. I almost gave up before I started, but I'm glad I finished it. Used a hand mixer, salted butter, dark Karo syrup, and two 8" square foil pans because that's what I had to work with, and it came out perfectly. 10/10.

I never post reviews, but this was a great cake and a big hit at work. The video was extremely helpful. It is not too sweet, and so convenient to use a whole can of pumpkin. The texture is light, fluffy, and not heavy like some pumpkin desserts can be. It was hard to judge the right amount of flaky salt because it is hard to see on the background of chopped pecans. I might put some salt in the caramel next time. A keeper!

Just finished making this for the first time for Thanksgiving.  It looks and smells amazing.  Have to wait to taste tomorrow.  The other bakers comments about the topping were very helpful!  It's important not to overcook it or it will be too gooey hard.  Total cooking time for me was about 7 minutes total.  Once I saw it expanding and foaming I removed from heat. I toasted my pecans for 8 minutes on a timer while the oven was preheating.  I made sure they were fragrant when I removed them. Thank you to all who took the time to share your experiences.  Happy baking!

Made as directed except maple syrup subbed for corn syrup and the cake and topping turned out perfect. Maybe corn syrup makes this cake last longer and nothing else will do that?  I can’t think of any other reason not to use a substitute. For my family I don’t have to concern myself with this cake lasting a few days, they loved it and it’s going fast!
Made this gluten free using King Arthur's 1:1 measure GF flour. Recipe worked beautifully. Fantastic crumb. Big hit with celiac and non-celiac guests alike.
I have a family full of nut allergies.  Any ideas for an alternative frosting?  I don't think leaving the nuts out of this would end well.

You could leave out the nuts altogether, maybe making 1 1/2 times the caramel.  Or you could try using toasted coconut shreds.  Coconut and caramel are a good combination and sound pretty tasty with pumpkin cake.

If you do make it without the pecans, please, please tell us how it went!

@TriciaPDX
I haven’t made this yet but I have made another pumpkin cake that uses pepitas sautéed in butter in the topping. It’s delicious.
I made this today, and it would be delicious even without the pecans! A cream cheese icing would be great too!
I don't have pumpkin spice, but I have allspice, cinnamon, cardamom, ginger, probably everything that pumpkin spice contains.  Anyone know proportions to simulate pumpkin spice?
@Carol for 1 TBSP of pumpkin pie spice, I use 1.5 tsp cinnamon   .5 tsp ginger   .25 tsp nutmeg   .12 tsp cloves

Recipe is easy to follow, delicious, uses a whole can of pumpkin, and can be made a day ahead. My kind of recipe.   Accidentally added the vanilla at the beginning making the topping and it turned out fine, phew. I think I should have let it cool a little longer cause it spread more to the edges. Next day, the topping slid off some of the sides but it is a forgiving cake and I was able to put it back on and cut it fine. Maybe FL humidity? Enjoyed watching Sohla cook the fried chx Thanksgiving!

I enjoyed the cake, very moist. I didn’t like the topping’s texture with the corn syrup. It felt a little rubbery to me. I suggest the salted caramel glaze from Melissa Clark’s banana snacking cake instead.
I made it with an excellent gluten-free flour blend and it was fantastic. 5 stars
@Nicole which flour did you use?
Was this ever delicious! The only exceptions I used was to bake a butternut squash for the recipe and used Lyles Golden Syrup. It was moist and came out perfectly. Thank you!!!

Made it for Thanksgiving and it is DELICIOUS! Many guests commented how good it was and asked for more. I'm making more for Christmas and will probably be making this every Thanksgiving and Christmas from now on.
Not enough pumpkin flavor if I made them again, I would double the spices. I also added chocolate chips to the topping, which helped a lot.

Tend to add more salt than called for in desserts but this was still just too sweet for most guests to have more than a small piece. I get that pies are very sweet as well but maybe the crust helps to balance more than the pumpkin cake does in this case

Made this cake for 2025 Friendsgiving. Unfortunately, I didn’t love it. I felt like the cake tasted too eggy and it just wasn’t flavorful enough. I would add more pumpkin spice if I make it again. Also, wish I had read the comments because the corn syrup topping is way too sweet and incredibly unhealthy.
Can the cake be frozen, and if so, before or after glazing?  Thanks
I don't think caramel, penuche-type frostings freeze well.
I would love to know if this would freeze well if I make it ahead.
can anyone advise whether light or dark corn syrup would be better, or is it personal preference?
In the video, Sohla uses dark corn syrup but she also says you can use any type of corn syrup you have. :)
I used light, and it came out great!
This is so good. Not too sweet, not too spiced but perfectly balanced. We all really liked it. I will definitely keep this one to repeat.
Regarding freezing, I’d consider freezing just the cake, defrosting it, and then making the topping. But it’s big — I’d have to cut the cake in half to fit in my freezer.
OK, I think the sheet pan thing has jumped to the shark. I’m off for convenience, but why not just bake it in a regular baking pan?

I would let the caramel cool for longer than the 3 minutes. You’d need to experiment, but I’ll try a 5 or 6 minute rest next time. My cake didn’t even have much of a dome but so much of it went down the sides and I had to keep pushing it back up. Applying when it’s a bit cooler would solve that.

The cake itself is divine. The topping is a bit sweet for my tastes, so I’ll probably cut back the amount used next time.

Followed this recipe almost exactly (added an extra half teaspoon of pumpkin spice in the cake) and it turned out perfectly. It took about 3 minutes for the caramel to boil and as soon as it was roiling and rising I turned off the heat and let it sit. Gave it the perfect gooey but solid texture.

Yum!!! I had no corn syrup- I substituted a combination of some pancake syrup (main ingredient is corn syrup) and sugar syrup. Followed the instruction to remove the syrup from heat immediately after the coming to a rising boil. The texture was perfect (chewy & soft).
I’d like to incorporate chocolate in some way - a drizzle on top or folded into the batter. Not sure which is the best way to go.
Can this be made dairy-free by substituting margarine for the butter?
When do you add the oil?  Recipe says pour into measuring cup.  Does not say when to mix into ingredients.  Or did I miss something?
@PK it is before you add flour/pumpkin and fold. Add oil in a thin stream while mixing.

absolutely lovely cake. subbed the corn syrup for agave and otherwise followed the recipe to a T. came out moist and delicious. will definitely make again and a very welcome substitution for traditional pumpkin pie which we all know can be boring 🧡
@pk...step 3

Phenomenal. As usual I thought I knew better than the author and I was wrong. Follow their instructions, timing and order and you will not be disappointed. I cooked the caramel too long and added the pecans to it before pouring on which was like spreading viscous praline on top. You will enjoy this!

The cake came out light and about inches high, but after adding the topping, it compressed and got soggy. The final product was a dough-y and dense cake level that barely tasted like pumpkin with gooey topping. Could this be because I made it the day before? Any other ideas? I did use Malden salt flakes and so the topping was a hit with a little ice cream.

@Aviva
I agree. I believe the sauce was too liquidy and changed the texture of the cake into a leaden mass. The sauce also made the cake too sweet.

I used the can of pumpkin and pumpkin pie spice.,I took the cake to a community Thanksgiving dinner. Someone had made two pumpkin pies with fresh sugar pumpkins and classic spices, cloves, allspice and cinnamon. We all preferred the mellow taste of those pumpkin pies and full agreement that this recipe would not be repeated.
This recipe didn’t work well for me. The cake was not only soggy but also it had an “egg-y” taste that overwhelmed the pumpkin flavor.

Made this Wednesday night for Thanksgiving. It was a perfect dessert. It is now Friday morning and I’m eating it as my coffee cake.  I’m obsessed. My sister in law asked me to make it for Christmas. I didn’t change anything. I think I was not as careful about not stirring the brown sugar mix after removing from the heat but that did not seem to matter.

Not a fan of this. The liquid topping soaked in and made the cake a leaden mass. Perhaps it’s ok if eaten immediately (still warm), but it is definitely not a good cake to make a day ahead. The Times guide has much better pumpkin recipes. I recommend the pumpkin scones.

This is a lovely cake with the perfect level of warm spices and an easy gooey pecan caramel topping. I baked a half-recipe in an 8x8 pan with great results. Just don’t stir the topping once it’s off the heat so you don’t crystallize the sugar, and for heaven’s sake, use the small amount of corn syrup. Ya want gooey? That’s the only way you’re gonna get it.
The icing- delicious but I did not really enjoy the texture of the cake and will make again without the corn syrup and see if I love it! Otherwise, pretty tasty.

Spectackular success! Everyone looked a bit skeptical when I explained it was a combination of pumpkin and pecan but then they all raved. The cake was light and fluffy but somehow it felt like pumpkin pie in the mouth. And I saw some comments about tooth breaking so I made sure the caramel topping was not too firm. Excellent dessert.
Absolutely delicious and a big win for our thanksgiving this year. My family loved this cake and appreciated not having an excess of crust on their plates from all those pies!
Absolutely wonderful!  I microwaved some that had been put in the fridge and I think l like it even better warm!

I made this for a Friendsgiving.  I substituted agave for 1/3 of the corn syrup in the batter and 3/4 of the corn syrup in the topping.  It worked beautifully.  The result was a softer topping that was not cloyingly sweet.  My guests raved about it.

I'm not sure how... but this cake only gets better with time! I made this for Thanksgiving and it was divine on the day of but then I let it set, covered loosely with plastic wrap, on the counter. The topping helped keep the cake decadently moist while also creating an even more pecan-pie like flavor. It's been six days of me sneaking little bites every time I pass it and it still tastes amazing! Definitely one I will be returning to again and again.

Everyone LOVED this cake at Thanksgiving. 
Awesome recipe.

These were a big hit!  I don't really like pumpkin pie, but they were delicious and I will make them again to take for special people.  They were a bit tricky putting them together, but well worth the trouble!
To watch the video of the cake, start at 18:00. Enjoy!
Made this for a crowd of 20 with dark corn syrup and dark brown sugar. There was a single little square left, and I got a lot of compliments.
I sent my partner to the store for corn syrup and he came back with dark corn syrup.  I imagine it would work the same way but what about the taste.  Think it would make much difference?

Delicious! I made it another time with some substitutions to make it a little healthier but still tasty… used allulose instead of the sugars, added more pumpkin, used whole wheat pastry flour, cut the oil in half but then supplemented with whole fat yogurt for moisture, and cut the pecans by 1/3rd. I left the butter and corn syrup. The topping didn’t caramelize as much (but it did have a nice caramelized flavor) so I poked small holes the cake for it to soak into the cake. It came out super moist and delicious.

Everyone loved this! Deliciously moist, good crumb (not too dense), and not overly sweet (unless you devour the edges of caramel 🙃). I used a hand mixer as I had cut the recipe in half. Beating took FOREVER or what seemed like forever, so will use a stand mixer next time.

Baked this cake for the first time for Thanksgiving.  Total silence and rapture while all ate!  This is the best cake I have ever eaten or made.  I found the flaky salt was crucial--it offset the very sweet syrup topping.  Highly, highly recommend.

This was a last-minute addition to the dessert table.  I replaced the white sugar w/ allulose/monkfruit sugar- I had run out of white sugar. Used organic corn syrup that was in the pantry for 3 years & added more spices. (No one died.) The final product was slightly underwhelming the first day, but was fantastic the following day. Not sure if my helper didn't get the caramel to the right temperature, so it should have hardened a bit to a crackle...but it seeped into the cake overnight & yum!

Got pumpkin? Do this cake. The cake is very good. The topping sets it apart. Done right - the cake becomes quite fluffy, almost custardy. Pecans sit on top with just a bit of syrup, most of which soaks into the cake. And, as said, an entire can of pumpkin. If you like pumpkin, you gotta do this cake.

title: Crushed Green Bean Salad With Cranberry Recipe
author: Sohla El-Waylly

stats.Cook Time: 20 minutes, plus at least 6 hours’ marinating

comments.commentBody: I smashed the green beans in a single layer on a cutting board to save a plastic bag; I needed to chase a few errant beans across the counter, but it otherwise worked. The beans didn't taste raw or grassy after overnight salting. This was a fresh take on green beans that I'd make again.
Sounds tasty but I’m thinking of blanching the green beans. Personally I am not eating a scoop of raw green beans!
@Leslie I have made a different recipe either smashed green beans. It was delicious. The beans softened a bit so the texture was similar to blanched green beans.
I think if you use the thinner, french green beans it'll work. I'm going to try iti!

Those concerned about the "raw" green beans should also watch Sohla's video that goes along with the menu featured in: You Know What You Need? A Fried Chicken Thanksgiving. Sohla explains about salting the green beans. Her video also shows that the green beans were not haricot verts.
The dressing looks interesting but i will probably blanche the beans and use less salt.

I’m really amazed how good this was. Green beans were crunchy, loved  the sweet and tang of the dressing. Very easy as long as you give yourself time to do it. We let the beans sit overnight. Will definitely make again.

Made this for thanksgiving along with Sohla’s other dishes- overall delicious meal with great flavors! This salad didn’t really come out though. The green beans were way too salty and still raw, I ended up eating around them. I think green beans blanched or straight out of a can would have been much better. Also I wish I had tossed the whole salad in the dressing, the stacking idea is fun but wasn’t easy to eat on a plate full of other things. The dressing was delicious though, I also added extra dried cranberries and fresh pomegranate seeds which added a fun pop. Thanks Sohla for the fun menu, we had a great time executing it!!

We were ok with taste and texture of the uncooked green beans, but did not find the flavor of the dressing appealing.  Also, the dressing pooled on the plate and its flavor was too "weak".  It really needs a higher viscosity to help it adhere to the greens.
Raw green beans?  I was taught that raw green beans can severely upset the digestive system.
Raw green beans?  I thought raw green beans cause digestive upset.  Is that a myth?
@MarianK It’s totally a myth! I eat them all the time for a snack, just like raw carrots.
@MarianK thin haricot verts are delightful raw. But even if they’re not the thinnest, salting and sitting also breaks them down somewhat like a blanching would.

It depends on the individual. It is usually not a problem, but everyone is different.  I don't tolerate raw zucchini well, green beans I am fine with.  Eat a few and try them.  The salt breaks down or alters the cellular structure so it will not taste raw.

The raw beans left a bit to be desired. I decided to use French beans and blanching would be a much better idea than pounding and leaving with salt. I skipped the layering of lettuce because no one in my family was interested in the 'salad' part. Just put a layer of lettuce on bottom for decoration.

I have to admit, I was prepared to be underwhelmed  as we subbed this for a green bean casserole. But I should have known Sohla wouldn’t disappoint. This was absolutely fantastic!!! Made it as written with canned cranberry sauce. Will be a staple in future years.

If you do not like crunchy green beans, skip. Although the salt removed the raw flavor, it did not soften the green beans. People had a hard time serving themselves with the layered salad set up. I had to cut the salad up on my plate to get bites. 
The cranberry dressing was great.
This was not a popular item at the Thanksgiving table. Most people avoided it and those who did try it told me gently, “it wasn’t their favorite.”
My family loved this recipe for thanksgiving. It was a novel cooking method. Family fun!
The cranberry sauce based dressing looks interesting but I am not sure heavily salted raw green beans is a good idea.
what kind of green beans? round? runner?
Won't the salad be too salty with all that salt? I'm making it for Thanksgiving and am worried.
This is a great recipe, will definitely make again! My 7 year old even asked me "how I made the beans so good".

10/10 brilliant recipe.
Will be using this method to prep green beans all the time now. (Mine spent less than 6 hours in the fridge and still came out great.)
All hail Sohla!!
Went over well at Thanksgiving dinner. A fresh take on green beans. My only change was to blanche the green beans instead of salting. Will be making again!
Pardon me, but would Grey Poupon arriving in my 1979 Rolls work just as well?
Any suggestions for something vegetarian to put this gravy on? Whole roasted cauliflower perhaps?

I made the recipe as instructed and unfortunately it was a disappointment. We served it with mashed potatoes, green beans, and fried chicken. It added a punch of color to the plate, so that was nice visually. Perhaps I made mistake but I’m usually good with instructions… very little flavor and as someone who likes mustard, this was flat.

I agree with others that this recipe fell flat. So, when adding the prepared mustard, I added 1 TBSP apple cider vinegar and 1 tsp sugar. Stir well and then strain. The vinegar & sugar help the other flavors pop.

I’m sad to say I was very excited for this and it was really gross. I’m an ex chef and I was thrilled with the idea of it, but it just didn’t work and curious if others had very different results because they are so few ingredients I don’t know how that could be.

Okay, this is a bit quirky and maybe not for everyone, but if you follow Sohla’s instructions closely, you get a lusciously smooth gravy … with an undeniable but not overwhelming overtone of hot mustard. And it totally works! Served with mashed potatoes and friend chicken, it’s just what that combo needs to refresh your palate.
turned out really really well. had it on a few different options

I liked this gravy. I used No Chik’n broth. Served it over mashed potatoes, greenbeans, and fried chik’n fillets. It’s interesting and pretty. I imagine I will make this one occasionally to change things up a bit.
What to add if I've only got normal American mustard?
I'd say make a different recipe. They're not remotely the same.
Did not like this at all.  Couldn't think of anything to do that would fix it.  Tossed it and made a different make ahead gravy.
This was tasty enough, but I think I was hoping it would have more of a punch, it was kind of like a typical gravy with a mustardy kick, so not bad, but probably won't make again.
I believe it's the blossom end, not the stem end, that one should cut off before pickling because it has enzymes that can soften the pickles after they are brined.
@Rob  That’s exactly what my dad did in every batch he made. And he made enough every fall to last the whole winter. Including green tomatoes and celery. Perfect.

These are amazing! So easy to make, and I love the freshness of these pickles. They are slightly sweet with amazing crunch. I used Diamond salt. If one were to use a different kosher brand, I'd recommend cutting down on the amount, or, better still, weighing the salt.
Could one leave out the sugar?  It doesn't sound like it would go with mustard seed or dill... and I HATE sweet pickles...

I’ve read that Kosher salt is best for refrigerator pickles because it has no additive to make the salt free flowing, and that additive is bad for some reason.  Also I was instructed to use filtered or distilled water, not tap water.  But I don’t boil the brine so maybe these precautions are moot in this context?

If you are making "real" pickles--the kind that depend on lactic acid-producing bacteria culled from the environment--the chlorine in tap water can kill the bacteria that are responsible for fermentation. It shouldn't matter for this recipe though. It calls for distilled vinegar, so there are no live bacteria cultures to start with. 

Personally, I refuse to buy water. I dechlorinate by letting it stand for a few days, or by bringing it to a boil for a few minutes and then letting it cool down.

Filtered water is a nice touch, but by no means necessary.  You use kosher salt rather than table salt because the iodine added to table salt can make the pickles turn blue.  So can using raw garlic - I prefer to use granulated garlic.

Rather than refrigerate them, water bath can them for 15-20 minutes.  The pickles will be shelf-stable for at least a year.  When processing, grate in some horseradish and a few small, Japonica peppers, and to ensure crispness, if you've got some, a fresh grapeleaf.
Kirbys are hard to find this time of year, but the local Kroger has 6 packs of small Persian cukes that worked very well!
These sound more like bread and butter pickles. Is the sugar necessary?
It is definitely the blossom end that gets cut. Don’t think it matters what you do to the stem end.
Love these so much. Can I reuse the brine by boiling it and pouring it over a fresh batch? They only last a week in the fridge before they are all eaten
Blsam, I have that same question. Can you reuse the brine?

I would honestly rename this recipe as “Quick Sweet Dill Pickles”. I have never made pickles, so I didn’t realize how much sugar this recipe has in it. Not what I wanted. Otherwise it was nice and easy to follow.
These have great flavor. However, a little too sweet for my liking and teeters on the edge of a bread and butter for me. I’ll likely cut the sugar to 1/8 cup next go-around.
Where can you find Kirby cukes at this time of year? I haven't seen one in weeks.
Lei-Lei Market, Tucson, AZ  $1.49/lb   Fresh dill, two bunches for $1.99
These are superb! Has anyone had success waterbathing them? I'd love it if they could be shelf stable.
Way too salty for me, I used kosher salt but would maybe use just 1 tablespoon

I followed the basic directions the first time while skipping the coriander and mustard seeds and substituting a couple small handfuls of freeze dried dill weed for the dill sprigs into the jar with the cucumbers and garlic.
These were good but more like a blend between sweet and dill pickles. I haven’t made pickles very often so I couldn’t remember if sugar was standard. I’ll use a different recipe next time.

I have to say this is a great recipe for my three kids to make! They are nine, seven and six, and they were excited to help out. They enjoyed monitoring the pickles to see when they would be ready. This easily pairs well with the smash burger, which I made a few days later for dinner. I’m not a big pickle fan but wanted to enjoy them with my kids, and I even put my pickle on my smash burger, which added great flavor to my dinner. Easy to make, easy to clean, and kid friendly- can’t beat that!

title: Sour Cream and Onion Mashed Potatoes Recipe
author: Sohla El-Waylly

comments.commentBody: Sohla, can we leave the skin on the Yukon gold potatoes also without affecting the result (other than the flecks of gold skin amidst the red)?
@A Reader  Yes! I always leave the skins on.
@A Reader I always leave skins on. The contain most of the nutrients.

Any mashed potatoes, or potato puree, van be made more interesting by adding finely chopped purple onions. Just add some at a time and tasting in between, making sure you do not make the onions overwhelming.
@GS Sounds more flavorful than the scallions. Thanks!  Do you add them raw?
@GS Yes I didn’t have scallions so used very finely diced yellow onion, O% fat greek yogurt, a bit of cream and olive oil. I’ll try red onion next time

This is similar to the way I make my mashed potatoes.  My kids would even devour any leftovers  straight from the refrigerator. However, add butter and garlic powder and omit the onion powder and salt.  Simply delicious!!

I've used Greek yogurt in mine (non-fat can be a bit of a different texture but still pleasant) instead of the sour cream, and added melted butter and gotten a great tangy taste! Love this recipe and all the possibilities.
I have alway made mashed potatoes with thick homemade Greek yogurt. I add a clove of garlic in the water when boiling potatoes. And add a tbs of butter.
No matter what recipe you make - mashed potatoes need butter & salt & this is no exception

IYKYK about Claim Jumpers’ legendary mashed potatoes, and if you don’t, making this recipe will let you in on what you missed. This is as delicious as it is easy, with the tangy creaminess of the sour cream and the pungent in all the right ways onions powder deliver something so much more interesting than regular spuds. Great at a Thanksgiving feast but so simple, it’s a great anytime recipe, too.
Steam the potatoes rather than boiling them, and do not peel the Yukons... yum!

I never peel potatoes of their nutritious skin that also adds a wonderful texture to mashed potatoes. I’ll be quite content when I finally see a recipe that does not call for removing a potatoes beneficial covering.
Rinse your raw, peeled potatoes, and then rinse your cooked potatoes. Lessens the starch.
What’s the reason for using Onion Powder vs. raw or sautéed onion?
Lots of flavor! Added about 3/4 stick of butter. Will make the next time but omit the powdered onion since the scallions seemed to take care of the job.

Wow!  Perfect as is.  And Incredibly easy.  Definitely will replace my usual mashed potatoes.
Will try the small dice of red onion next go-round.
I made these for thanksgiving and they were the most delicious mashed potatoes I’ve ever eaten or made, relatively simple and easy to find ingredients. These will be a go to for me from now on!
Yes, leave the skins on. I always do and it adds a nice element!
If serving immediately heat the milk before adding.

I LOVE homemade mashed potatoes. Can’t find a single restaurant that makes decent potatoes. I tried these for Thanksgiving. They’re very tangy from the sour cream. I also found that there were too many scallions. 

Hubby loved them but if you’re looking for traditional mashed potato flavor, this isn’t it.
Nor does it claim to be. A glance at the ingredients will confirm this.
Can these be reheated in the oven?
This recipe is flawless and easy to make. This has become my standby mashed potato recipe.

I feel like a lone voice sticking up for this recipe! This is my second comment in 3 days because I made it again and loved it just as much. I'm not a sweet potato person but this changes my mind. Sohla El-Waylly likes to reach for new uses of traditional ingredients and it brings surprises. So what if it's a béchamel sauce changed up with some cheddar?! Give it a try! It's creative and delicious and very, very interesting.
This was delicious! I made the whole recipe lol. The cheese sauce was great
Are you supposed to start the sweet potatoes in a cold oven?
Sounds like it. It's not a universal belief, but people swear by starting sweet potatoes in a cold oven. Something about caramelization.
@Mary Dempsey I see in the recipe it says to heat the oven to 425
@Mary Dempsey yes you are. Do not preheat. This is a great technique for roasting sweet potato halves even without the embellishments.

I tried this recipe with one sweet potato and scaled back the proportions of the sauce ingredients accordingly. (although I did make 1/2 of the sauce proportions b/c it was easier to manipulate. Hard for me to make a small sauce for 1 person/serving). To those who think it sounds dreadful, I encourage you to try it -- I thought it was sensational and basically can't wait to make it again. PS - my sauce was not as thick as in the picture but the flavors were absolutely fantastic together!
Smoked paprika from penzeys.com!
@Chris I used their 33rd and Gallego blend yum

Home run! A breeze to make, and so simple to eat, yet so rich and playful! Served it with the simplest of mixed lettuces with vinaigrette, and an array of hot sauces. Such a joy, for the whole family! Yum!
Bake sweet potatoes first! Just enough to be able to cut them at least. I don’t know why that isn’t a first step.
Penzeys 33rd and Gallego spice blend perfect for this ( we don’t use garlic)
this was fantastic. made exactly as the recipe reads. loved it. just an aside, there is plenty of cheese sauce for dipping while cooking. Like a fondue. Please try it. I think you will like it.
It says heat the oven, then roast. I take that to mean the oven preheats.
@freja no, it says put the dish with the potatoes in the center of the oven, then turn the oven on and roast the potatoes. Sweet potatoes cook better if started in a cold oven.
Roasted in jacket? and then sliced and spiced etc? Best flavour by far if roasted before peeling. But recipe doesn’t specify  - peeled or unpeeled?
Don't peel. There's a video on Youtube of her making these.
Put the potatoes in the oven (cold) then turn on to 425 and bake.

I made this as directed. I don't get it. The cheese sauce was very good. The potatoes were fine. They made no sense together. It was not a main (even with a salad) and would be a very heavy side. A rare miss here.
This was good but not worth all the effort. The Times recipe for black bean and cheese sweet potatoes is tastier and easier
