Licked the plate, this was soooo good. Hoping they reheat well in the air fryer.
This was incredibly flavorful. The sauce wasn’t as thick as anticipated so added some corn starch slurry and it eventually became a queso-like consistency. Make this! Thank you Sohla!
Novel concept, but I will have to change the execution. The cheese sauce was delicious. My potatoes must have not been “medium”, tho. By the time they were cooked, the butter/spice mix was burnt.

Made this for Thanksgiving yesterday and not only is it easy but everyone loved it. The roasted sweet potatoes were perfectly tender, the seasoning was great (tho I had to omit onion due to dietary issues) and the cheese sauce and hot sauce added a nice twist to the plate.  We used Franks Hot Sauce because we had it and it was just the right vinegar hit.

I made these using light olive oil rather than butter and the potatoes alone (without the sauce) were divine! I would make those again without the sauce: the spice combination is delicious. I think the sauce portion is generous, and can be halved. Will be making again!
so delicious! and easy to make while you complete other household chores.
Pretty time consuming and not really worth the effort. Kinda weird ultimately.
The recipe flavors are inspired, but no need for a fussy sauce. After baking or microwaving the potatoes with the spiced butter, just top the potatoes with slices of cheese and broil until melted.

So easy! I roasted several sweet potatoes whole earlier this week and just cut one open, poured the sauce over and added a bit of the paprika and cayan to the sauce. It was incredible. My husband and I were scraping our plates and going back to the pan to dip crackers in the bruleed sauce. I'm gluten free and substituted cornstarch for the flour and had no issues. We want to try the sauce with cauliflower next.
Easy and delicious. Can’t wait to make this again.

I plan to add a beaten egg or two to bind the mixture, then I’ll chill it in the refrigerator for at least 30 minutes before frying. Not surprised about your caveat that they’re delicate—but adding egg and chilling the mixture should make them considerably less prone to breaking.
I just used recipe but glad I was warned they were delicate.
Try refrigerating them to get them to firm up before frying.

Try this:  When making these or crab cakes, use a small size biscuit cutter and pack the mix into it to the top.  Put some panko on a plate, put the biscuit cutter on the panko, fill with fish mix, pat panko on top.  This makes a pattie that is firm and holds its shape.  They will be super crispy on the outside.

If I’m reading the NYTimes correctly, and one never knows, I understand that I’m to eat THE MEDITERRANEAN DIET!!! Which drew me to this recipe. You gotta like canned tuna and dill to like this but luckily I do. I used my hands to mash the tuna mixture. It wasn’t dry for me but I did dip the patties into a beaten egg before frying and as frying I topped with more breadcrumbs for crunch. The sauce was delicious. Plenty of salt needed all around to pop the flavors.

Made last night, stuck with the recipe apart from subbing parsley for dill and adding a few hot pepper flakes.  Used hands to bring the mix together and chilled for 3 hours. Cakes were NOT dry, did NOT fall apart so I suggest making from original before adding eggs.  Excellent pantry dinner. Served with sweet potato air fries and blistered green beans.  Dressing served  as sauce for the cakes.  Made as mini cakes will be excellent with tartar sauce as part of tapas spread.
Did you refrigerate after you made each cake or before, with the whole mixture in a bowl?
I refrigerated each cake on parchment paper as they were formed.

I made this tonight and it was very flavorful indeed. The buttermilk dressing was perfect and I look forward to using the leftovers. I used 1 egg to bind per the comments but I think next time I would use 2. I also used panko crumbs and parsley plus a little dill  as I ran out of dill from preparing the dressing first. Mashing the beans completely is also key to help bind the ingredients. I added Mama Lil’s peppers on the salad as a topping and that was a hit.

I did add the egg, plus substituted one can crab meat for one can tuna! 
Delicious!
Made spicy cocktail sauce and tartare sauce to go with it.

Delicious! Made no modifications and had no problem forming patties or keeping them intact. We did really enjoy the dressing—olives and buttermilk make for a zesty tang and as a dill lover, I was a happy consumer. Definitely going to be a repeater recipe.

Loved these. It was tricky squashing the beans but I used my hands and then a fork and got it done. I didn’t find the patties to be very delicate but I did chill them in the freezer for half an hour first and then cooked them slowly in a cast iron pan. Especially loved the dill and lemon. I could eat these every day.
We made the tuna cakes only and sauteed zucchini instead of the dressing a iceberg.  The tuna cakes will be our new go-to for tuna.
For every cup of buttermilk needed add 1 tablespoon of lemon juice or plain vinegar to a cup of milk. Let sit for 10-15 minutes.. Use in place of buttermilk

This is an update to my original note. Lower the bean-to-tuna ratio: use just half a can of beans (the patties will be lighter), omit added salt, fry them in a neutral oil rather than olive oil (higher smoke point and more delicate flavor), add a beaten jumbo egg (will bind better), add a dash of cinnamon (mitigates fishiness), and chill mixture in refrigerator at least a half hour before frying. This recipe will also work with any leftover mild white fish you happen to have.
This sounds great except for one thing: my family absolutely hates dill! Anyone out there have any substitutes? I suppose I could just leave it out?
Try some fresh chopped parsley; I always combine parsley and tuna.  Maybe a little onion powder, too.
Parsley and lemon!
I would go with tarragon, and use much less due to the licoricey flavour, which can be overpowering

Reading comments about people finding this dry and lacking in tuna taste makes me wonder if they are not reading that the recipe calls for TWO 5 oz cans of tuna. When I initially began prepping I had only 1 can in the mix and thought it dry .  A quick re-read altered me to 2 cans needed.
I added celery stalk, small white onion, egg and 1 teaspoon of the old bay seasoning, 1/4 cup of cassava flour instead of breadcrumbs. And baked in 400 for 12 minutes, delicious!
Really enjoyed this. Even though the patties are fragile, the result is super tasty. together with the lettuce and dressing, it tasted like a fancy tuna salad.
Made it to the recipe although salad I added cranberries and blue cheese. Hubby really liked it. It was easy. It was ok in my opinion but if he liked it there’s no argument from me!
Dill leaves are NOT a pantry staple and often not available in the winter.  A substitute of dried dill would be helpful.
I buy fresh dill and freeze it.

I went a little rogue but super happy with the results!  I subbed canned salmon for the tuna.  Added 2 eggs.  More breadcrumbs (Italian seasoned) than suggested.  Chilled the patties in the fridge overnight.  Highly recommend.

My modifications: my wife doesn’t like dill so I replaced it with parsley. I also thought using garlic powder as the sole seasoning didn’t add much to the flavor so I added some Old Bay. Lastly I added a tablespoon of mayonnaise, an egg, and chilled the patties for 1/2 hour. Quite good!

I followed another commenters advice and added an egg to the mix and I had no issues with the patties staying in perfect form.  (I also put them in the fridge for 30 minutes before cooking.)  I'm not a fan of dill but I remembered how much I loved an old recipe I had for white bean cakes that added curry powder so I added about a tablespoon of good curry powder to the mix.  The lemon and curry flavors really popped but next time I may sub Old Bay, because I grew up in Maryland, hon!

This recipe is ridiculously good and so easy, I’ve made them several times and it’s a great weeknight dinner. The first time I made it I used tuna canned in oil and the patties were very delicate, chilling for a couple of hours or overnight really improved this, no need for egg. Using the recommended tuna canned in water improved the structural integrity. I use whatever fresh herbs I have hand, I don’t always have dill so I often use fresh parsley and chives, and lots of green olives in the dressing and it’s delicious!
I added a little extra lemon juice and some red pepper flakes. I didn’t have fresh dill, so I subbed in some herbes de provence. Super delicious!!
I prepared this as written and we really enjoyed it! I had no trouble with the cakes falling apart…mashed the beans thoroughly and used fine breadcrumbs. I served some kimchi along with it.
For the sake of time, I used a food processor to mix everything. It was not a good idea as the result was a fine paste that turned out to be too compact and made the tuna cakes too dry.
This recipe was very good. The mashed beans keep the patties together, so did not need egg $$. Used Trade Joe canned tuna. Coated in panko and put in fridge before cooking. Sauce was very good too !

I’m not sure what everyone else is up to - but these do NOT need the addition of the egg to bind them. Followed the instructions to the letter and they were perfect! Mashing the unrinsed beans until very smooth created an amazingly flavorful binder that made for an unexpected but pleasant texture when cooked. Will gladly make these again!
Can you mix it up in a food processor instead?

Maybe I'm sensitive to the rather powerful flavor of dill, but a dense quarter cup sounds like decidedly too much of that flavor.  Personally, I'd swap fresh thyme leaves coupled with Italian parsley.  

 The tuna cake sounds terrific.  The mashed cannelini is a brilliant approach, possibly adding a chopped shallot.  Good stuff!
I'm tired of buying a quart of buttermilk for a recipe that only needs 1/2 a cup. Any substitution suggestions?
I keep a bag of powdered buttermilk on my pantry shelf: "Bob's Red Mill Sweet Cream Buttermilk Powder."  Just mix with milk.  If I have plain yoghurt, I sometimes use that.

Carol - modern store bought buttermilk is just a type of cultured milk, as is yogurt. If you have any type of yogurt on hand, you can use it. If it's greek or skyr, just water or "milk" it back. There's generally enough acid in there to still perform the same task.
I always use Greek yogurt, which we always have at home. I do have powdered buttermilk as well but it's on a high shelf.

Made this exactly as directed and it was wonderful because Sohla is a genius and her recipes are perfect. Don’t over mix and don’t over pack the patties. We did add bacon as a wedge salad reflex, but I think Sohla would approve.
any try this in an air fryer?

I used great northern beans, because that's what I had in the pantry.  The flavor was good and definitely held together, so no need for the egg as many have suggested, but the cakes were dry.  More lemon would be a good addition, but it needed some type of sauce. Curious if the difference would be so great between cannellini beans and great northern?    My husband added Tabasco for a little heat, which was also good.
I didn't make the salad or dressing, but the cakes by themselves were very bland.  We couldn't taste the tuna at all.

Cooked 1/2 recipe, with egg as binder as the only addition per notes. Loved, loved, loved the texture but spices were underwhelming. More garlic powder, pepper, parsley, dill and lemon zest next time…and will also riff Asian with garlic, ginger, scallions and cilantro and green chilies. Definitely a keeper recipe.
Agree to the seasoning suggestions ;)
Ice way to add beans into diet

We thought these were great---used panko, 3 cans tuna, and 1 cup dry white beans cooked in the Instant Pot (plus the dill, salt, and garlic powder).  I made them early in the day and put them in the fridge until about 20 minutes before cooking---they stayed together beautifully and were tasty.  We made burgers of them and skipped the dressing/salad suggestion.

I crushed a few Ritz crackers and lightly coated the tuna cakes before frying, and I had no issue with them sticking or falling apart.  I also grated a large garlic clove instead of using garlic powder. These were great.
these were okay. kind of mushy on the inside even after being pan fried for a long time. really good flavor though, especially with the dressing.
Reducing the beans by half and frying longer in neutral oil should do the trick.

My son and lovely daughter-in-law are pescatarians! I made this dish for them! To add a tangy taste, 
I added a teaspoonful of Dijon mustard to this recipe. To bind the mixture, I added one beaten egg. I rolled the tuna cakes in Panko bread crumbs that I had seasoned with chopped dill. Scrumptious AND nutritious.
Made according to recipe except broiled on low instead of pan fry. Way too dry and not flavorful enough. Too much lemon. Beans take away from tuna flavor. I’m sticking with my salmon cakes instead!

Does anyone have 3/4 cup fresh dill in their pantry? How does this recipe use "pantry staples"? I grow dill, but even in Texas I don't have any in my garden in February. NYT recipes seem to assume we all have entire shrubberies of fresh herbs 24/7/365.

Made as written and it was delicious. The cakes held together just fine. The recipe says to mash the beans until smooth, and then to mash in the rest of the ingredients “very well”, so it’s not a crab cake situation. If you follow directions, they won’t fall apart!

We loved the dressing and the iceberg wedge, too, but look forward to making the tuna cakes to go with other sides.

This was very good. Per comments below, we used two eggs in the mix; panko on a plate, top with a ring mold, top with panko. Very crispy on the outside and delicate on the inside. Nearly 1" thick made six patties. Want to try a) thinner, b) half tuna and half smoked salmon. Dressing was VERY tangy. I added thyme and some honey. Try backing off on lemon juice and adding lemon zest. Also try Meyer lemons in season. Added chopped Mama Lil's peppers to salad per suggestion below - good idea!

I followed the recipe in every respect. It was the bomb. We cleaned our plates. Yes the tuna cake mixture is tender but just flip it carefully. It will work fine. The buttermilk dill green olive dressing is fantastic and pulls it altogether. So good on the sweet juicy iceberg and so good on the dense rich tuna cake.
AM - loved the dressing made with capers instead of olives😘

These look amazing.  I will make this weekend and plan to use egg.  Do you think that after the tuna cakes are prepared, they can be frozen in waxpaper and aluminum foil?  I make lunch ahead of time for the week and prefer not to have the same thing every day.
I'm going to make them into patties and then freeze.  that way I can still crisp them up when we're ready to use them.

Loved these with two small mods: oil packed tuna and, critically, a bit more bread crumb and egg to dredge and lightly bread it before the pan fry. Tuna schnitzel! Lighter and creamier for the beans. Great wedge variation. Could sub dill pickle if olives aren’t in the house.
Subbed chickpeas for beans and yogurt for buttermilk. No need to add egg. Great pantry recipe!
Also, I think adding an agg for binder is a good idea to try.

These were tasty, my husband really liked them. 
I didn’t make the salad and instead served with roasted veggies with romesco sauce (another NYT recipe). 

I mixed the cake ingredients and found it dry and crumbly so added 1/3ish cup of mayo to hold everything together and chilled the mix for a couple days before forming and frying them. Had to be very gentle flipping with two utensils but all but 2 held together nicely. 

I also used canned salmon because that’s what we had. Very good!

Added 2 eggs to bind and increase protein, and dredged patties in additional breadcrumbs (panko   black pepper and a smidgie of cinnamon to cut fishiness) after forming into patties for extra crunch. Didn't have fresh dill, used dried, was still excellent. Ate on burger buns with lettuce and pickles, everybody hit the clean plate club on this one. Had seconds in the middle of the night, standing in a dark kitchen, cats circling at my feet like crabs beneath a whale fall.
My concoction was delicious, but it was not crispy.  I would rename it Crumbly Tuna Cakes.

Five stars for what it is: a quick meal with stuff in the cupboard that tastes really good. I like that it wasn't heavy and greasy like typical "crab cakes". I did use fresh dill (I think it works really well) but had no lemons so used balsamic vinegar instead.  They don't fall apart if you drain things well with the lids, press together tightly when forming, press bit in pan to flatten slightly, make sure there is a brown crust, and flip using two strong spatulas (not the flexible ones.

Made this last night as per the recipe. Tuna cakes were crispy and held together well, but I think the key to that is to make the beans as pasty as possible and make sure there's a bit of liquid clinging to them (it's viscous and helps the mixture adhere). The dressing was fabulous -- dill and lemon is such a good combination -- and it occurred to me that the addition of some chopped capers might go well next time.
Capers! Yes! that's what it needed!

This was slammin’!  Followed the recipe to a “t” and came out perfectly delicious.  Love the simple ingredients. And making your own ranch?  I can never go back. Next time will try turning this into sliders.
Has anyone tried using fresh tuna instead of canned?
Made as written. They were very good and there was no issue with them falling apart - they stick together well. No need to add eggs.

Since it's just me eating it, I halved the recipe with no problem.  (But I do have half a can of beans to use up now, probably in a soup.)  It was very good, and I don't usually care for the texture of "bean burgers."  Mine held together just fine, with no egg.  I pretty much followed the recipe exactly, though I did add a little Old Bay in place of the salt and pepper, and just fork mashed the beans.
Excellent! Based on other comments I used two eggs, and chilled for about 1.5 hours, then coated in panko. Make sure you drain the tuna well!!!

I liked this dish so much I give it 4.5 stars! Made just as directed, without extras, but made red butter lettuce salad instead of iceberg wedges. The consistency was perfect and the patties held together well. I tend not to add salt to tuna but with the beans, salting is necessary. Also not quite have as many green olives on hand as required for the sauce; Could add some capers next time. Both the tuna-bean mixture and the dressing benefit from sitting in the fridge for a while. Delish!
For people use to Southern Tuna cakes this has no flavor and the texture is off.  Too much bean flavor and not enough tuna flavor.

Like many people are saying they are a little delicate and hard to form as is, so you will def want to add an egg in, I did and they were PERFECT. I also used panko for the bread crumbs, and then coated in a beaten egg and more panko before frying, SO SO SO GOOD. Personally I also used plain yogurt instead of buttermilk and that was amazing as well, will be a regular weeknight dinner for me!
To quote my husband: "These taste a lot better than they look". These are very good and will definitely make them again.

I read the comments, added an egg and rolled the chilled balls on panko. They held together beautifully. Tasted great, outside was so crisp, but I found the refried beans-like texture inside the cake a little mushy. I’ll make again and either add another can of tuna or a can of crabmeat as another commenter suggested. 

Both of my neighborhood supermarkets were out of dill so I used dry - about 1.5 tblsps. This was not enough for a dill lover.

Overall - great recipe!

just made these   not fan of the iceberg lettuce but dressing was good

i used the suggestion and used a biscuit cutter to make the patties with panko bread crumbs on top and bottom.  i put them directly on the tray for my airfryer toaster oven and refrigerated for a bit.  slide the tray into toaster oven and worked like a charm.
Incredible. I added extra breadcrumbs and garlic powder and made it even better!
Anyone consider baking these…?

I substitute leftover cooked salmon for the tuna, and have found that the patties freeze well if wrapped individually. It’s become a go-to recipe—when I buy salmon I make sure I buy twice what I need for the first meal so I can make and freeze a batch of these.
This is such a great pantry recipe. I’d like to try it with canned salmon.

This recipe needs help..  because the tuna cakes have very little moisture and don’t readily stick together I first upped the flavor using chopped green onions, capers and caper juice. I added more lemon juice and finally had to add some mayonnaise to bind the cakes so that they would hold together when frying

This recipe needs help to increase flavor and moisture to bind the ingredients into cakes.  For flavor I added capers,  caper juice, green onions . Then I upped the lemon juice.and finally I had to add mayonnaise . The result was good but not special

Made these and needed to add an egg for moisture and to help these hold together. They were still really dry, so I’ve advise adding 2 eggs and maybe cutting back on the breadcrumbs. I even used tuna and oil, which somehow didn’t help with moisture. Taste was fine, not sure I’d make these again
try adding a tbls of Mayo, if it's still too dry.
Any suggestions for cooking these in the air fryer?
360-370° for 10-12 minutes, flipping halfway through, should do the trick.
I'm curious how this would change if using fresh, raw tuna instead of canned tuna. Anyone tried that out?
Can these be frozen?

Love the idea of using mashed beans in the fish cakes. 
Definitely recommend adding a beaten egg and chilling the cakes before frying. 
Could substitute Great Northern or navy beans for the cannelini, no?
And, last but certainly not least, replace the tuna with canned salmon. Salmon cakes used to be a go-to Friday supper growing up in an observant Catholic home.
No chopped onion?
Do you see chopped onion?

I, too, have powdered buttermilk, but I don’t always think it has enough oomph. I don’t want to buy a whole quart and I don’t have enough room in my freezer to keep it for later. 

Has anyone actually tried this recipe using a substitute for the buttermilk, like using yogurt or the powdered buttermilk? How did it taste?
My tuna cakes started to break in the pan during frying. I wonder if adding more breadcrumbs would have helped.

I was excited to make these, especially using the mashed cannellini beans as binding. I prepared as directed and chilled in the fridge for 30 minutes. They need a lot of lemon juice and salt for flavor. Unfortunately when I went to fry them, they fell apart. I also coated them in egg and panko to no avail. The flavors were great though next time I will add more liquid or an egg to hold the cakes together.
Has anyone actually TRIED this recipe with a buttermilk substitution? If so, what did you use, and how did it turn out?

I was house bound due to weather so I substituted 1 cup Greek yogurt in the dressing since I didn't have mayo or buttermilk and dried dill weed (used half the amount) since I didn't have fresh. I always add an egg binder for any fish cakes. This is a good recipe
Patties will hold together better if you make them ahead and let them sit in the fridge for an hour or so.

This was OK.   I probably won't make it again.   Easy recipe and the dressing was very nice on the iceberg.  The tuna cakes had a nice consistency but were a lot of work - next time, I'd opt for a nice tuna fish sandwich.
Has anyone tried this same recipe with canned salmon?

Made these this afternoon, using 1 egg strategy. The cakes were delicate but not fragile and everything was tasty. I didn’t have any buttermilk or kefir so I used lite sour cream. Since I haven’t had the other sauce I don’t know how it compares but the sour cream seemed to work well.

Adding beaten egg makes careful cooking more important. The egg should be fully cooked for bacteriological reasons. Without egg there is no issue with getting the outside crisp and inside only nice and warm. The kind of bread crumbs will make a diff. Panko is coarser than other breadcrumbs, so a half cup will have less weight and drying effect than a half cup of the Italian breadcrumbs I sometimes buy.

Loved this! Subbed a can of garbanzo beans, which is what I had on hand; added an egg to bind the patties; and used kefir instead of buttermilk, as suggested in the recipe. Delightful! Will add to our regular rotation.

This was a nice change from tuna salad and melts. Used small white canned beans mashed them well w the tuna. Went light on the dill and garlic. Added minced celery along w 3 tbsps egg white and mashed again. Made 8 x 1/2 cup servings. Mix held together well even when pressed into burger shape on the skillet. Sauce was also very good. Ranch dressing like.
Don’t know what I did wrong, but these did not hold their shape when made as directed. And they tasted like the “mystery meat” of a middle school cafeteria. The sauce was good.

Made this, unfortunately could not find dill anywhere in town, so a little bland. I did add an egg in and chill before cooking. Took leftovers to work for lunch and found the perfect dipping sauce: Chick-fil-a Sweet and Spicy Siracha. Try it please!
I love these.  I read the comments and decided to use beaten egg to hold together and loved it.
We’re trying to keep our carbs now, so I used ground pork rinds for the bread crumbs and they turned out beautifully. Otherwise, I didn’t need to change a thing.
Maybe this is a dumb question, but could I use tuna packed in olive oil for this recipe?
Bland and underwhelming.

Season to taste, as always. I added granulated onion and cinnamon and they did the trick. I didn't bother making the sauce--serve with the sauce of your choice. I recommend ketchup, ranch dressing, or tartar sauce.

No egg needed! The cakes hold together perfectly. I added a little more bread crumbs than the recipe calls for, added some mushroom umami spice blend (the trader joe's kind) for a bit of kick, and fried in a butter/ olive oil mixture on high heat. So tasty! Simplified the dressing to just mayonaise, lots of lemon juice, garlic powder, dried dill, and a little olive oil.
Can tuna packed in olive oil be substituted for the recommended tuna packed in water?
Why not, especially if you're frying in olive oil? I think it would work with any leftover mild white fish, btw.

There is something absurd about this recipe — namely that it has only four stars.  This is stupendously flavorful!

Sohla mentions that the "tuna mixture can be refrigerated up to 3 days in advance; form and fry the cakes just before serving."  I let it chill for just under two hours, and this seems to have helped in forming the cakes into defined shapes (though as she notes, they remain delicate).

The dressing and the cakes are both packed with scrumptiousness.  Make this!!
Has anyone tried this with canned salmon? Looks like it could be a nice variation on salmon cakes.
Unsalted beans and tuna will cut the sodium content drastically.
Tuna cakes fell apart completely while cooking. I followed the recipe exactly. Would not make again. The final result was an unappealing mess. The dressing was delicious, though.

I only made the tuna cakes, not the dressing and salad. They're quite tasty and indeed delicate in handling. I added juice of one lemon but otherwise stuck to the recipe. Yum! I could see adding capers?!
Would this work if you used tuna packed in olive oil?
Are the olives necessary?  I really don't like them.  Any ideas on substitutes?  I usually use capers but a cup seems like a lot.

I was suspicious mixinf tuna and whire beans but these were excellent!   My only tip to amke sure the patties hold togehter is to  sueeze them in your palm thoroughly before forming into patties.  The turned out perfectly without falling apart.  It is an old Mideatern trick for various purposes.

I made this and it was delicious and came together well. I let the cakes chill while I was making the dressing. They held together perfectly. Personally, I would use less lemon juice in the dressing. It was a bit sharp as is for me.
I subbed in a TBSP dry dill weed for fresh. I also coated the cakes in bread crumbs mixed with Everything bagel seasoning. Used store bought dressing.

Made these cakes as written and they were great. A couple of them lost a few edge pieces in the flipping process, but nothing drastic. I also made 6 larger cakes (there are 3 of us, so 2 each), and cooked them all at once, so I probably could have been more careful with more room in the pan. Crispy on the outside!

Did not make the salad in the recipe, but served with a simple green salad and they were great. Going into the rotation for any easy, pantry-staple dinner.
These need a lot more salt and lemon juice. I also added red pepper flakes otherwise they are a bit bland.

In the spirit of pantry cooking, here's a list of substitutes I made to this versatile recipe: 
-Ran out of dill so I replaced a half cup with fresh thyme and a little dried dill
-Replaced buttermilk with 2% Greek yogurt + 3 dashes of vinegar-based hot sauce
-Replaced olives with 3/4 cup capers and 1/4 cup muffaletta mix
-Replaced iceberg with mixed greens
The recipe made too much dressing (even when using it s a dip for the cakes). The cakes were prone to breaking apart, but not impossible.
What’s the logic behind not adding an egg to this?

No need to add egg. The mashed beans serve as the binder and mine held together quite well with that and crushed saltines. It does want a good amount of salt and pepper to be well seasoned. You can definitely play around with the seasoning. I went for finally, chopped onion and parsley with the lemon zest and lemon juice.  Simple week night or weekend meal, can serve with the sauce of your choosing if you don't want to go to the buttermilk route.
Fabulous! Added egg and a bit of mayo per suggestions. Definitely a keeper! Will try with crab next time!

This was a great meal. Will definitely make again. I loved the sharp and tangy notes in the dressing from the kefir, lemon and olives; the dressing complimented the cakes well. I did not have any issues with the cakes falling apart, but they were delicate. I made the cakes and let them sit for about 30 minutes before cooking, perhaps that helped.  Used panko crumbs, which worked well. Adding egg could help, but don’t think necessary.
Wonder how they would hold up in an air fryer?

These were an immediate hit at our house. I made them according to the recipe, and the consistency was great. I don't put egg in crab cakes either. But I do like to pan fry in 50/50 peanut and vegetable oil since Olive oil is heavy and tuna is already oily. The peanut oil does have its own flavor but it is light and works with this.

As suggested by others, we put one egg in the mixture and then proceeded with everything else unaltered.  These tuna cakes are delicious!  We did not make the salad, and served the tuna cakes with homemade cocktail sauce, instead.

Excellent. Never thought of making tuna patties prior. I used two 7-oz cans, squished out the water which resulted in 10.12 oz of tuna. Chilled the mix overnight, making no adjustments to the contents except to dip the formed 1/3 cup patty into an egg and then crumbs. Additional S&P while cooking. Made 8 patties, including salad/dressing - so leftovers tonight!

This recipe is as close to perfection as it gets. The combination of flavors in the tuna cakes results in a perfectly balanced bite - nothing dominates the flavor profile. Make the recipe as it’s written or make some of the additions recommended by others - this is a bulletin-proof recipe. I would recommend making the cakes ahead of time time and chilling in the refrigerator for at least 30 minutes before cooking.
This was excellent using left-over salmon. I recommend, like others, chilling the mixture for 30+ minutes to help with binding.
Don’t eat beans but understand these serve as a binder in this recipe.  What could substitute for mashed beans?
Very yummy easy supper. Used chopped dill pickle in the dressing. Also reduced the mayo to a couple tablespoons and used a bit of yogurt instead. Added an egg to the patty mixture.

Meh, I like my usual fish cakes — made from canned salmon — much better.  These were bland and the mashed beans (I used homemade butter beans) wound up tasting more like potatoes.  Too much starch in proportion to the fish.
I made the recipe for the tuna cakes exactly. Loved the dill dressing, but my family and I didn't enjoy the texture of the tuna cakes.
These sound great but we really don’t like dill. Can I leave it out or sub something else?
I substituted roughly chopped capers for the olives and loved it.
Can these be frozen after frying?

I made the dressing the morning of. I threw all of the ingredients into a mayo jar, that contained just the amount I needed and gave it a vigorous shake for a couple of minutes. 
I'm surprised that some people require an egg for additional binding. The recipe and the instructions as is, was sufficient. The cakes do absorb the oil though, so you'll have to refresh the oil between batches.

Great recipe.  I made a few changes, and next time probably would do a couple more.  For the wedge salad, add blue cheese or feta on top instead of the olives.  Blue cheese would make it better I think.  For the cakes, I added an egg to hold them together better and cooked them a little longer with the lid on the pan to steam them through.  Otherwise I think they would probably fall apart.  Next time I would add either grated onion, or onion powder.  They were good but just a little bland.
I made this as written and it was a hit with my kids. For my family of four, it was not quite enough. I will double it next time!
Has anyone tried baking these? Or broiling?

I usually can’t stand tuna, but I’ve been trying to eat it for health reasons. Every other tuna recipe, I can’t eat more than 2 bites, but I actually started planning when I would make this again while I was eating it! I didn’t like the ranch dressing, however, and I mixed it with some “homemade” ranch from a hidden valley packet. Together, it made some of the best ranch I’ve ever had.
Flavor was great, but the texture was not for me. I found it mushy. Won’t be making again, BUT if I were inclined to take another pass at this, wouldn’t be so thorough in smushing up the beans.

Made these tonight.  No issues with structural integrity.  I always drain the water from my tuna cans, the resulting mixture had just enough viscosity to bind.  No eggs needed.   I substituted parsley for dill, and instead of the salad and dressing, I made a Mediterranean style garbanzo beans.  I also added grated parmesan cheese for umami. We ate it with rice.  Overall, this was well received.
The cakes were a little bland. Wondering if I might add capers or even a dash of siracha next time.  Dressing was tasty

I made the whole recipe tonight and it was great! I think the dryness some people have might have to do with how drained the tuna and beans are? Mine were a bit dry but not too much and I made a mental note that next time I would add a little bean juice. I did cook my own beans from dried so I would be happy to use any of that liquid as opposed to the canned. However bean juice won’t be much of a binder if that’s what anyone wants!

Changes I made to the patties:
Added about  a tsp of Old Bay Seasoning.
Added about two tbsp Kewpie Mayonnaise.
Added about a half tsp of red chili flakes.
Changes I made to the dressing:
Added dill pickles.
I thought these were delicious, if a bit dry. Make sure not to use too much oil or they will fall apart. I will likely make these again. They were very easy to make and tasted delicious.
has anyone tried substituting part of the mayo with greek yogurt? or would egg be a better substitute since they are so fragile?
The fish cakes stayed together quite well, and were good even without the delicious dressing, which put them over the top. I did use an egg to help bind them and added some finely diced green onions.

Question:  The recipe calls for  dill leaves and tender stems.  These are fresh (which I never have around), but can dried dill weed work in this recipe, and what's the ratio or amount I can substitute?

We loved these!  Recommend reducing the amount of buttermilk to 1/4C or increasing the amount of mayonnaise to make the dressing thicker.  I didn’t have enough fresh dill so reduced the amounts to about half what the recipe called for in both the cakes and dressing but it was still fabulous,  simple and felt pretty decadent!

Liked the tuna/white bean cakes, served them with lots of lemon, will add a little diced celery next time for crunch. The combination of green olives with buttermilk and mayonnaise in the dressing didn’t work for me. I’d rather pair the tuna cakes with a traditional blue cheese and bacon wedge salad, classic and delicious.
Would these tuna cakes freeze well? I live alone and recipe is for four.
Loved it!  You definitely need all of the dressing.  Has anyone tried to blend the dressing or would that be too much olive taste?
Great meal. Wondering if a little anchovy paste might add to the depth and/or a little butter with the olive oil. Definitely keeping it in the rotation. Thank you.

I didn't find them fragile at all, not sure why but they held together quite well. No need for egg. I did use Great Northern White beans, don't know if that made any difference. Also substituted yogurt for the kefir but everyone loved it.
Dill is hard to come by…I would use finely chopped parsley, but it sounds good!

Recipe FAIL

They completely fell apart. Not just delicate - crumbled to pieces! I was able to keep some together on a griddle, but they weren't crispy AT ALL. I was very disappointed!

These were good, but not great, which makes me wonder if I missed something in the cooking of the cakes.  Mine were very "wet" so I formed the patties and let them chill in the fridge for a couple of hours.  I ended up coating them in bread crumbs to hold them together while frying.  Oddly they did end up looking like the picture!  Just found them kind of bland.  I'm going to try again and try a different tuna and also add capers.  We'll see!
Ranch dressing awesome. Use lemon juice in milk. A tad of yoghurt.
Did you skip the mayo? I'm a mayo-phobe and can't bring myself to buy it. Would it work if I did buttermilk + plain yogurt? Or just keifer without the mayo?
Works well with canned chicken breast too!
No doubt canned mackerel would be fine too.

I used Panko bread crumbs and followed the recipe exactly. Mine were not super crispy, but did have the velvety interior. Maybe my heat needed to be a bit higher. No need to add additional binder in my opinion. They held together fine when I formed them and, while delicate, didn’t fall apart when I flipped them with a spatula and fork. Made half the recipe for two of us but now wishing I had leftovers for lunch!

We have frozen dill easily available here (in Sweden) and I used it, which worked fine, but it's more compact so you need less of it. Also I will use a bit less lemon in the dressing next time. Adding a beaten egg sounds like a great idea, will try that.

The 35th time this recipe appeared on my Facebook feed, I gave it a try. 
1) While tuna, beans, and olives are pantry staples, buttermilk and $10 worth of fresh dill are not. The copy writer was stretching a bit there. 
2) Yes, the texture was good, but we also like flavor and these tuna patties were bland. I don't know where all that dill disappeared to, but it did. The dressing was nice, but just didn't cut it with my family. We found ourselves scrounging in the frig for livelier sauces.
Easy and low effort but not much of a reward. Kinda bland and does not yield a lot of food. I could have eaten them all myself. Dressing was pretty good. Nothing to write home about though.

I made them as written (only subbing Northern beans for Cannellini) and found the recipe a bit blah. I really wanted to like them. I love fish in general and i was hoping for a better flavor so I'm willing to try using salmon or mackerel and adding onion and/or capers to ramp up the taste.
So good. Followed the advice of refrigeration and breadcrumbs. I might eat this every other day!
The only change I made was adding some of the chopped olives to the cakes. They were fantastic and held together nicely while frying in a cast iron skillet.

Followed recipe except subbed the herbs I have in my garden: parsley, basil, tarragon. 
Based on notes, added an egg to the tuna mixture and refrigerated for an hour after forming patties. Dressing was tasty, but super thin. 
Family liked it, easy weeknight dinner!
So GOOD!  I added capers and it's juice as I like the added brininess.  Definitely a keeper recipe!

I liked the dressing. I normally stay away from Ranch, but this dressing was good and easily got used up. The tuna cakes held together well, but they were bland as written. They definitely need some pizazz. Capers? Heat? The tuna flavor didn’t come through which I suspect was because of the cannelini beans.

I followed the recipe but used capers instead of olives and used tuna packed in olive oil instead of water.  It was a hit with my husband who doesn’t like fish.  I chilled them for 30 minutes before frying and they didn’t fall apart.  I mashed the beans to a creamy consistency and I think that’s the key. We will be repeating this recipe!

I made the dressing with kefir and put it on the lettuce per the recipe but I don't think the olives are necessary. I ended up putting the dressing on the tuna cakes when I made them again the next night from the leftover mixture in the fridge.
I followed the recipe, and it turned out pretty awful. Texture of undercooked pancake.

Made this a month or so ago -tuna cakes were good (I did amp up the herbs and spices); green goddess dressing was delicious! Tonight I had close to a pound of leftover cooked wild salmon. Doubled the beans (puréed with a hand blender), added lots of dill and a couple minced garlic scapes and it made 8 half-cup salmon cakes/burgers. Currently freezing for future meals. Very versatile dairy free recipe!
We had canned salmon and decided to try the recipe with that instead of salmon. It was delicious as written!

Everyone was right about adding 1-2 eggs (I added 2) to create more solid and cohesive patties. I also think that next time, I may add some capers to help with the saltiness. I had to add salt more than twice for flavor enhancement.

Made the cakes per recipe and they held together just fine (no need to add eggs).  Need to be gentle with the flip, but really, no problems if you mash it together well. I used dried dill (fresh would be better) and added a few dashes of cayenne.  For the dressing, I also had to use dried dill and used yogurt instead of buttermilk (I think that worked fine).  Could experiment with capers in the cakes and/or dressing I think. Didn’t have iceberg, so served with arugula/spinach blend.  Good!

As is, I found the cakes a bit bland. So I used gluten free matzo crumbs (no real taste, just needed to be gf). I added dried minced garlic, dried minced onion and celery seed. I used the food processor to blitz the beans. The dried garlic and onion helped tighten up the mix. I let it sit in the fridge for about 20 minutes.
These are really good. I will make again. Added a squeeze of lemon to each one when plating. However, I will add an egg next time as bits and pieces fell off during cooking.

Lots of subs on the first go-round but will be back. 

- Butter beans instead of cannellini
- parsley in place of dill
- Greek yogurt subbed for buttermilk
- 2 tbsp chopped capers instead of olives
- added egg (feared butter beans were too dry)
Has anyone used an air fryer for this recipe?

Holy cow this recipe blew my socks off! The balance of the rich tuna cake with the light, lemony salad dressing was really incredible. All my children loved it, too, so this will be a new dinner staple
Can these be made in an air fryer ?

Made this for dinner tonight- it was a perfect summer dinner! I puréed the beans with my immersion blender and the patties held together perfectly. Used spring mix fresh from the garden cherry tomatoes and cucumbers. My husband hates olives, so I puréed those as well. Love the dressing!
The tuna cakes were good, but were missing something. I think maybe I'd add scallion next time and a bit of hot sauce. As for the wedge, the dressing was terrific!

I followed "Realist's" and "Cristine's" advice (below) and was pretty happy with the results.  I noticed some of the ingredients in the dressing were also in Hidden Valley Ranch dressing envelope I had in the pantry. I whipped up the Hidden Valley recipe and added the chopped olives for a pretty tasty dressing. I had Romaine but no iceberg so just made a normal salad.

Followed the directions- I think that the people who are having trouble with dryness might be those who rinsed their beans so I recommend draining and not rinsing. 
The only issue imo is that these just weren’t very tasty to me :(

I added a little mayo to the mix to bind along with scallions (because I could) and instead of mixing in the breadcrumbs, patted them on, and then chilled while I ran an errand. They weren't fragile at all when it was time to fry. Subbed capers in the dressing because that's what I had. My daughter agreed that it was delicious. And so quick to put together.

I loved this recipe and jumped on it right away. I carefully followed the instructions, but unfortunately, the result was disappointing (hence the three-star rating). The biggest problem was that they fell apart while cooking, and the taste was good but not great because they were a tad bland, and I missed a deeper flavor profile. I will try again by adding green onions, chili pepper flakes, and an egg or two to the mix.

I plan to add a Tbsp or two of the canned bean liquid to the mixture to further hydrate the bread crumbs. I'll also refrigerate the mixture for a few hours, as well.  This should enhance binding to hold the cakes together. The lemony-dill dressing was wonderful, with the olives giving that final salty and flavorful pop!

Giving it four stars because I found the dressing for the salad disappointing. The tuna cakes were excellent. I followed the recipe exactly except for two things: after forming the cakes, I put them in the fridge for a couple hours. While the formed perfectly and stayed together I thought this might help keep them together (I saw other comments about them falling apart). Just before frying, I pressed both sides in some panko. They were encrusted but had enough to give a little crunch. Perfect

Excellent use of canned tuna! I rarely go for iceberg lettuce and loved this wedge salad. I swapped in a mix of basil, cilantro, and parsley for the dill and added some finely chopped red bell papped to the tuna cakes for color.

Made to the recipe except it seemed so dry that I added a tablespoon of Greek yog at the end. The result was tasty—moist but not crispy enough and barely held together. Will chill for an hour before frying next time.

This recipe definitely needed an egg (or two) for the tuna cakes. The dressing was too thin with 1/4 lemon juice and 1/3 cup dill was way too much (it called for 1/2 cup). Not sure how to thicken up the dressing.

I saved the water I drained off the tuna and used a little at a time to get a malleable consistency for shaping the patties. Also took recipe advice and used a fish spatula and a tablespoon to move and turn over the patties. This meal got rave reviews at my house.
This bomed! Didn’t hold together. Just mashed it all up!

Really enjoyed these.  One can tuna, one of salmon from the pantry.  Didn’t get enough lemon rind so added some sumac which I liked a lot. Did think a bit bland even then so added some capers which I also liked a lot. Froze a few; they came out okay. Made a nice sandwich on toasted bread. Will definitely make again to keep on hand for quick meals.

Is there no mention anywhere of the roasting temperature? Did I miss it?  Try preheating a cast iron skillet in a 500-degree oven and roasting the butterflied bird (or, like me, leg quarters) in that. Place empty pan in cold oven. Rub oil, salt, and pepper on chicken, add a sprig of something. When heated, put chicken in and reduce heat to 400. Perfect every time. (Lest I be accused of plagiarism, credit goes to Cooks Illustrated.)
Recipe says to heat oven to 450 degrees.
Love the leg quarters idea and they are on sale frequently.
Recipe twice mentions setting oven to 450 degrees
A sharp knife will do if you lack the shears.  I don't remove the spine, I just cut one side and flatten it out.

This is always how I roast chicken, usually adding onions and carrots to the pan to roast at the same time. I find a 4-5 pound bird takes about 60 minutes at 450. My dry brine uses salt, onion powder, garlic powder, dried sage and black pepper. Overnight brining is best but I have had great results at 6 hours too.

Fabulous recipe. Ask your local butcher to spatchcock the bird for you.  Another variation my family love that results in a flavorful and moist bird: brine the spatchcocked bird in a ziploc bag in the fridge for 6-8 hours (or overnight) with buttermilk, 2-3 fresh rosemary sprigs, 2 garlic cloves, fresh pepper and 3-4 tablespoons of kosher salt. Remove bird from the bag, gently shake excess buttermilk off in sink, then place bird on the rack and follow recipe as otherwise written.
Be sure to use a high-quality, STAINLESS STEEL rack. The salt and high temperatures can leech out other metal coatings.

When I roast a spatchcocked chicken, I put it directly on top of big pile of carrots and onions in addition to aromatics. After the chicken is done, I put the pan with the vegetables back in the oven until they are ready to eat, and they are delicious.
Always looking for different ways to roast a chicken. I’ve used spatchcock many times but never thought to put it on a raised sheet pan. My new favorite way to prepare - so crisp and juicy!

The only way to cook a Chicken. From room temperature (off an overnight wet salt brine) I coat the bird in spice rub and “dry brine” on the counter for several hours. Pop  the bird in @ 500 degrees for 30 minutes then dial it back to 275. A few cups of white wine in the sheet pan for dripping gravy. Just over an hour or so for 4-5 lb bird to done.

A nice basic recipe. I like lifting the skin from breasts. Then adding herbs (dry or fresh mixed in olive oil) and minced garlic and stuffing under skin of breasts. Helps keep the breast meat moist while adding flavor.
Bird will not cook evenly if taken directly from fridge to oven.  Take it out an hour before to come closer to room temp.  You will not die, I promise
Very nice recipe but 160F too high, I take out at 140 for breast and 160 for thighs. Residual heat will move it higher for safe and really juicy meat.
Far easier than any poultry shears is pruners. Just wash the blades before and after cutting out the spine. A sharp Felco pruner goes through it like butter.

See also Melissa Clark's two recipes--one with herb butter and one with a spice and brown sugar rub. I'm short and have never succeeded in squashing the breast till it lies flat. After removing the back bone, I usually use a chef's knife to crack the wishbone and spit the tough breast cartilage. If roasting the chicken on a rack, I would also put sliced carrots and potatoes (drizzled with oil )under the rack. Why let oven heat and drippings go to waste?

Great recipe, with one caveat. Do NOT roast at 450°; preheat the oven to 400° instead, and roast a little longer. A higher temp will make a royal mess of your oven, and the results won’t be any better. On the other hand, if you enjoy cleaning your oven frequently, do as you wish.
Or if you enjoy following a recipe as written, roast at 450.
I agree that the cast iron pan is the way to go with a spatchcocked chicken. From Samin Nosrat I learned to point the legs at the back left of the oven.

I'm rather short so can never quite get the leverage to flatten the bird. My five year old grandson solved the problem for me. "Step on it, Grandma". I did, with a cloth to protect the bird. 'Out of the mouths of babes...".
Very tasty chicken.  Salt and pepper generously applied to olive oil-rubbed chicken tasted perfect.  No one could taste the lemons.  Would make again in heartbeat.  Meat was moist and tasty.
When I spatchcock, I cut the skin down the middle and peel it back before I cut out the spine. No point in wasting crispy skin!

Addition to salt and pepper: granulated garlic, granulated onion +/- a sprinkle of chili powder such as cayenne or chipotle. It's not as spicy as you think. A bed of sliced lemon can be the basis of a nice pan sauce.

Where in Florida (west of Palm Beach) can I find the 3-4 lb chicken?  Most of the birds available (whether national or store brand) are 5 lbs and up.  As I'm cooking for one, the smaller bird would work better for me.

@Alice. Try Whole  Foods if available to you.  They used to sell uncooked spatchcocked chix approx 3.5 lb, but not lately. However they still sell 3-4 lb whole uncooked chix.  Their chix and meat is generally better than typical grocery store.  I don’t buy much there, but this is worth it.

If you have the patience for it, removing the keelbone and ribs and snipping out the wishbone before cooking leaves you with all-meat, easy to carve white meat and drums, thighs and wings that are easy to remove and section. It’s not that much more work. Same as thighs- if you’re in the habit of buying sink on bone in thighs? Zipping out the bone is fast and makes for a faster cooking, easier eaten portion. Jacques Pepin is a great resource for at-home poultry butchery.
@Jaime Jacques Pepin is a great resource, period!

This is my preferred way to cook either whole chicken or turkey, and tho I've tried several types of poultry shears (and I have fairly strong hands), I nearly always reach for a sharp knife to cut out the backbone as I find it REALLY hard to get shears to go through the thicker ribs.

An alternative to a rack is to roast the chicken on a layer of vegetables that roast well - carrots, parsnips, beets, cauliflower, garlic cloves, etc - and everything is done about the same time - tasty!

Incredibly tender and delicious. I bought a 4  lbs pastured chicken at Whole Foods and did a dry rub of salt, pepper, garlic and onion powder. I let the bird sit in the fridge for about 5 hours before taking out to warm up. Cooked at 425 for about 50 minutes. I had never placed aromatics under a rack and didn’t think they would infuse much but I was surprised at how much flavor they imparted. Great way to cook a small bird.

I have cut a 6.5 lb chicken in half and it works just fine for the two of us. Freeze the other half.
I also freeze the cut out spine and save the bones ( frozen separately) from our first half.
I make chicken stock from the collection of bones from our whole chicken after we have eaten the second half. We haven’t bought chicken stock in years.

Easy and quick way to do a whole chicken. Doesn't work as well in smaller oven. Needs to be done in regular size oven. Putting chicken on bed of onions, lemons, sage, and spices is important. Chicken was moist and delicious. Cutting out the spine for spatch it is very hard with standard kitchen scissors - need heavy duty or a knife you trust.

I keep a mixture of Maldon salt at hand for steaks. 
2/3 regular and 1/3 smoked. 
Applying that after brushing on unsalted butter from Normandy… doesn’t get much better, following the rest of the recipe here.

Back in 1995, Food&Wine magazine ran a series “Cooking with Julia”. Her recipe called for a spatchcocked Cornish Hen, simply brushed with butter and salt and pepper. She broiled it until lightly browned and finished in a 350 F oven. She called it “A Simple Little Hen”. I have made it many times along with “A Soulful Chicken Soup”.

I really wanted to try spatchcocking after watching a video on it.  So I made this recipe exactly as written with the 24 hour dry brine.  I don't know if it was the spatchcocking or the dry brine but it was incredibly juicy and delicious.  I actually left it in the oven a little too long and the internal temp was 172,  above the recommended 160.  And it still turned out perfect!  The skin color was a little light, but it was still crispy and tasty.  Thanks, Sohla!!!

On dry brining: yes, salt pulls the moisture out of the bird (or other meat). But then the moisture dissolves the salt which creates a concentrated wet brine at the surface. The sodium infuses into the meat and helps denature the proteins, which tenderizes the meat. You have to dry brine for long enough to complete the process. Sometimes I quick wet brine with salt and sugar in the wet brine. The sugar increases the osmotic pressure which increases the infusion of sodium into the meat.

I've been spatchocking chicken for years based on an old Cook's Illustrated recipe.  Try lining your baking sheet with foil or parchment and put some thinly sliced Yukon potatoes underneath the rack.  All that delicious juice and fat makes for yummy potatoes. I also use a wet brine, rather than dry but dry works well too.
Where does one find a 3 to 4 pound whole chicken? Groceries and Costco on my area have standardized on 6 to 6.5 pound birds.

Free range or pasture raised chickens are typically 3.5-4 pounds. I find them at
My regular market, no need to go to Whole Foods or a speciality market.

@Bob Az if you have a good farmers market near you where the producers come to market there are usually farmers that bring chickens that are in the 3-4 pound range. Mine also sells them already spatchcock which is nice.

I’ve been seeing prepared spatchcock chickens in my local chain grocery stores lately too.  Not too expensive per pound and you’re not paying for the additional weight of the bird.  For the holidays I made stuffing that went under the rack   Was even on the pan bottom so it cooked evenly.  Just needed a little toasting after taking the bird out.

I buy a pre spatchcocked chicken because I'm lazy. Preheat convection to 375. Prep veg onion, carrots leeks etc in a bowl toss with salt and pepper and a little olive oil. Place on rack put chicken on top salt pepper and a brushing of melted butter. Roast 15 minutes at 375. Drop temp to 325 for about 50 minutes...a no brainer.

Some improvements:

1) Use a cast iron skillet. Much easier to clean afterwards and works better.

2) Place a half onion (or a two halves) under the chicken. That onion will come out deliciously caramelized and infuse the chicken with flavor.

3) Depends on the size of the chicken) but at 450 towards the end the skin will start to burn and smoke but the thermometer may say the chicken is not yet done. Do 425 instead to avoid burning the skin if you need more time.

4) throw small potatoes in!
The best way to roast a bird is to spatchcock. Cooks evenly and typically within an hour. A meal that competes favorably with any bistro.
It took longer to get up to the temperature than expected. But the breasts were extremely tender and flavorful. I definitely would make this again.

This is, hands down, the best way to quickly cook poultry. I have cooked my turkey this way for decades. I don't use oil on the skin, no need. Season well and move forward. Frankly, I have cooked a 28 lb turkey in 90 minutes, using a meat thermometer to ensure doneness. Release yourself from low and slow (AKA dry) turkey cooking!!

I have now made this nearly every week for 2 months straight. Comes out perfect every single time and I have no notes on the recipe. Dry brining the night before I plan to roast the chicken works best for me. For any very new and green cooks such as myself, Sohla's Chicken 101 cooking video on YouTube is very helpful to get one started on this recipe.
I did the dry brine the day before and made it exactly as directed with a local spatchcock-ed chicken. The result was perfect!! Crispy skin, flavorful and moist meat, great leftovers.

Rather than putting aromatics on the wire rack under the chicken, I put peeled/sliced potatoes and carrots on the sheet-pan. The chicken juices cook the vegetables beautifully (they need to be flipped after 30 minutes or so) and they are absolutely delicious.
If you have some fresh bay leaves, try adding a couple to the herb bed under the chicken--they add a lot!
The difficulty is finding a smaller bird.  Stores seem to favor the 4+ to 6-pounders.  With a household of just two, and limited fridge space, a smaller, air-chilled chicken works far better!

I’ve started dry brining my split chicken for 2 days now, finding the flavor of the chicken to be even better. I use the smaller size D’Artagnan  Green Circle chickens, the best relatively easily available.

"Exceptionally tender" is not hyperbole! This was the most tender chicken I've roasted - not sure if it was the chicken itself, the technique, or both - but either way, I'll make it this way again. It didn't take as long as I thought it would to cook - if you prepped it the evening before (and put it in fridge to dry brine), you could have dinner done in under an hour. Very simple! I did take the breastbone out (maybe that also helped cook it faster?).

We made this tonight for two, with a 3.2 lb. chicken. It was easy an delicious. Used onion, lemon, thyme, parsley and rosemary for aromatics, cooked at 450º F for 40 minutes. Temperature was more than 175º. Meat was moist and flavorful. We had NYT Radicchio Caesar Salad, Italian Roast Potatoes and some cheap red wine. We have enough of everything for tomorrow night. Can't wait!

Totally agree with Laurie. I have grilled or roasted a chicken (always Mary’s whole bird, as big as what’s available at Taylor’s or the co-op) at least 36 weeks out of every year for a decade or better. Not once did I think of using a rack over a tray - brilliance.

Did this last night. I'm a big fan of spatchcocking, and while this was my first chicken (been doing the Thanksgiving bird for years) it cooked as expected. 

But once again, regardless of how many things you shove under the skin (I used fresh sage and rosemary) and how much oil and garlic you rub on the outside, it always tastes like a baked chicken.

Often roast a well seasoned spatchcocked chicken over cubed bread. When chicken is done, remove to platter. Remove bread to bowl and reserve. On stove top, add some vinegar of your choice and heat with your favorite greens until wilted. Toss with some of the bread. Serve with the chicken.
I use the extra flavorful bread with eggs.
Bet this is from a NYT article or recipe.
For a little finesse run your thumb along both sides of the breastbone and pop it out. Use a small knife and separate the wishbone. This lets the seasoning get better contact with the breast.

I always spatchcock the chicken to roast it, but good olive oil will smoke at high temperatures. Use canola oil instead, and as someone else pointed out 400 degrees works just as well and your oven won’t get as messy.

Love this cooking method. I like to line the pan with potatoes to catch the chicken drippings while they cook. Or, taking a page from Zuni Cafe, placing large chunks of bread under the chicken halfway through the cooking to absorb the drippings, then using them as croutons for a salad.

I wish I’d read the notes before I prepared the chicken as I would have left the backbone in, only cutting along one side. Also agree the temp is way too high. I checked after 30 mins and turned down to 400. I had a small bird from Trader Joe’s, it was juicy and delicious and did finish up in under an hour. I added more seasoning and was glad for it. It’s a basic inexpensive way to cook chicken and have material for a homemade stock if that’s your thing.

I have one question for Times cooking. Where in this day and times can you find a 3 to 4 pound chicken?
I live in a "well supplied" small city out west and can't remember the last time I found a 4 pound chicken. I've quit buying whole birds.

After splitting, and seasoning the bird, I heat a cast iron pan on the stove top on high. Let it get hot. Put bird in the pan, skin side down, for 5 minutes. Then place the pan in a hot, 425 degree oven for 15-20 minutes, depending on the size of the bird. Take out the pan, flip the bird over so the skin side is up, place back in the oven for 35-40 minutes more. This will result in crispy skin, evenly cooked, delicious chicken.
Use convection oven, 350, and no need for any oil or butter just sprinkle with kosher salt....freeze backbones and use for chicken stock

Upside: Tasty chicken
Downside: Filthy oven, smokey kitchen
Agreed. Set off apartment smoke alarm 20 minutes in, even with external ventilation set to max.
I spread achiote paste all over the chicken (under skin too) and let it marinade in the fridge for a half a day. Then use the cast iron pan/oven method.

If you remove the backbone, "pope's nose," and wing tips, add them to the neck and gizzard (in the giblets package), you can make a small amount of stock, which will serve as a base for a gravy or sauce while the bird is resting. Or just add them all to your chicken bits bag in the freezer for future use in stock.

For those who can't/don't want to spatchcock the chicken themselves, the butcher at many supermarkets will do it for you at no additional cost. Also, Bell & Evans sells an already spatchcocked chicken.
Sliced ranges, lemons and onions (skin on the citrus) are all excellent under the chicken, and lovely to eat, as well.

Turkeys, no matter how large, can be spatchcoked, too.  The butcher will have to accomodate the backbone removal; but the method reduces the time spent by literally hours.  An 18 - 21 lb. turkey takes about 90 minutes (or a bit more), and with the proper butter/wine basting, there is absolutely no sacrifice in color.  Turkey emerges lovely brown to mahogany.

I did a quick version of this because I was in a rush.... so I didn't marinate, I just did the dry rub and roasted right away. I also didn't see the part about the oil (rushing!), so I didn't do that and.... It was delicious anyway!
Perfection!
Very moist, but needs a fair amount of salt. Would probably stuff some lemon slices under the skin as well next time
Wishbone? Aren’t you cracking the sternum?

Under the dry brine step it says 450°F oven. I find this exceptionally high unless quicker us needed. Otherwise, start with 450 then turn down to 325-350°F once the skin starts to brown. If it's getting too dark, tent with foil and SLOW ROAST the rest of the way.
With a weight on top--"en brique"-- it's the be-all bird
Also, it works fine without a wire rack for me, and less cleanup. I like to be able to stir the onions/carrots/etc. that I put alongside in a sheet pan.

I leave the back bone in just cutting close to it on one side and season lightly with garlic salt on the inner surface before cooking. I cook the bird at 400 degrees on roasting pan with a layer of water under the rack. The water not only provides moist heat but it captures juices that enrich the stock made with the leftover carcass. Waste not, want not.
This comes out soooooooo yummy and moist. I rubbed the added poultry seasoning under the skin like oiling a freshly washed baby and the flavor of the meat was chef's kiss awesome.

Over 30 years ago, I watched Julie Child butterfly a chicken on her program.  She started with a single slice down the backbone, and carefully carved away along the ribcage on both sides.  She 
cautioned against piercing the skin as it compromises the juices while roasting.  The trickiest part is separating the leg and wing bones from the ribs.  Laid out and covering a layer of dressing, it cooks and presents beautifully, in less time as mentioned here.  I've done the Thankgiving turkey since.

My local store now carries spatchcocked Bell & Evan’s chicken of the size mentioned here and I roast one in a 12-inch cast iron skillet, over a bed of garlic cloves slicked in a neutral oil. A 1-pint container of peeled cloves is about right. The garlic comes out confit under a mantle of rich chicken fat, drain the garlic, store it in a covered jar in the refrigerator and use it for up to a week. Both the bird and the garlic benefit greatly.

Love any and all forms of spatchcock cooking. I roast at 425° and use a Meater 2 probe (inserted in thickest part of the breast) and remove at 155° - more than adequate to kill any bacteria while retaining exceptional moisture
I used preserved lemon instead of fresh lemon because of the additional aromatics, and I built my bed with fresh shallots!
I believe that most butchers will cut the backbone out of the chicken if you want to skip this step

Doesn't need to, but it's good. Just throw it on a sheet pan in the oven and roast till brown. I make spatchcocked chicken often, and so I put all the backs in the freezer until I'm ready to make stock. Then I put them on a sheet pan with cut up veg for the stock, including onions with skin on, and roast till brown. They are a wonderful addition to the stock.

i followed this verbatim (my aromatics - onion, lemon, parsley, oregano, garlic) -- did add some sliced, small, redskin potato on top of the grate.  Best roasted chix i've ever made.  Loved the crispy skin.  Loved the short cooking time.  Only downside, my smoke alarm went off repeatedly.  That being said -- well worth it.  (and the redskin potatoes were awesome, be sure to slice them thin).  Definite 5 stars.

For those having trouble flattening the breast, it helps as noted to use a knife to remove the breastbone and wishbone.  If it is hard to disjoint the breastbone, cover the chicken with plastic wrap and use a rubber mallet from your workbench to do it.  I consider ours a kitchen tool!  I usually need it to get the bowl off the mixer stand after kneading bread dough.

Excellent. Be creative with aromatics! I used all I had on hand before we went on a trip; parsley, onion, shallots, celery, lemon, lots of dried herbs and put carrots on the rimmed sheet pan.  SO GOOD!

We use a broiler pan, line it with foil and place sliced potatoes and onions in it. The fat dips down and flavors the potatoes and onions. Sometimes we make an herb butter and work it in between the breast/thighs and skin. Simple, fast and delicious.
I put about a half can of beer in the sheet pan. Made the juiciest chicken ever!

I’d never made a spatchcock chicken before this and found it actually surprisingly easy with day-ahead planning (which I’m not good at generally). The results were wonderful!  Brined an 8-lb stuffing chicken with dry rosemary, salt and pepper for 22 hrs. When ready to roast, placed cut onions, carrots, fresh fennel, and 1/2 lg lemon in sheet pan with salt, pepper, rosemary, then the rack and olive oiled spatchcock chicken. Used auto-roast function at 400 degrees. Roasted maybe 1hr 15? Delicious!

There's no indication here about whether one roasts a spatchcocked bird entirely on one side (skin side up as pictured) or flips it once about halfway through. Also, I find that gently loosening the skin (I use the handle of a silicone spoonula) and spreading an olive-oil based mix of chopped fresh herb under the skin adds a lot of flavor to the finished product.
I dry brined my chicken for about 6 hours and followed the recipe exactly.  it came out very juicy and tasty! I will do this again!

Great way to roast a chicken, breast meat stays moist and the thigh and drums are done at the same time. I used a roasting chicken so lots of leftovers for sandwiches and soup - including my favorite Melissa Clark soup that has farro, chickpeas and Indian seasonings in it. Note:, if you think you will be using this technique fairly often, consider investing in a quality pair of poultry shears. This is the best roast chicken I have ever made. I watched the video and dry brined for 24 hours. As per the video I used an organic free range chicken and followed the recipe/video exactly. Thank You!

The chicken turned out well cooked and juicy, but lacked flavor.  Next time we'd add more salt to start and then pair it with a gravy or a spicy sauce like chimichurri.  We got out a jar of minced Giardiniera and it improved the taste.
My husband reported that cutting out the backbone wasn't hard as he had good kitchen shears.
Our chicken was on the large side ~4 lbs and it took about 1 hour to cook to temperature.
Oil started smoking at about 20 minutes of 450 and set of apartment smoke detector, even with ventilation fan set to max.

I am a lazy person who waits until the spatchcock chickens are on sale at a certain store and I buy two and freeze them. They are the best! I have a pair of my grandmother's poultry shears that my mother gave me and they must be from the 1930s or earlier. I have never used them but they are very pretty and HEAVY.

For those looking for someplace to purchase a 3-4 pound chicken, Publix sells Murray’s brand although it is sometimes not in stock. Whole Foods sells several brands that fit the bill. The cost is higher, but the meat is absolutely much better. I recently read an article comparing brands saying that Bell And Evans is the best.

Re bird size and weight: Costco size birds are raised with growth hormones etc. Try a yard-raised, air-chilled bird and you taste a deeper chicken flavor. I find the white meat of todays chickens to be virtually tasteless.
The oven temperature IS confusing.  I have to assume that it is 450 for both methods.
Fantastic, loved this!

When dry brining in the fridge, should the chicken be covered fairly tightly with plastic wrap to protect the rest of the foods in the fridge from contamination?  Or covered loosely with plastic wrap? Or not at all?  I learned the hard way not to use aluminum foil to cover the raw chicken in the fridge.
For PR: Best to leave it uncovered in the fridge. The skin will dry, making it crispy when baked. The plastic wrap will hold in moisture and make the skin less browned and possibly mushy.
Very good. Make again.
Very good. I added more maple surup
The chicken was fantastic.

This worked great. They ran out of chicken in the supermarket on Dec 23rd in the evening so I replaced it with two chicks and it worked well. I accidentally stored them in the freezer, so defrosted in the fridge for 24h first, then 6hrs dry brining. The lemon-thyme-onion based kicked in really nicely with the peppery salt on the skin. I accidentally covered only the upper part of the chicken in salt and pepper but it was still good.

I have done this a number of times and the chicken always comes out perfect. Some variations, the last one I did I dry brined with kosher salt and brushed mayonnaise over the top of the chicken.  It was the most moist chicken I ever made.  I roast at 450 and cover the bird , if needed, with aluminum foil if it's browning too fast.

The roasting method result was delicious, juicy chicken with crackly skin using the dry brine method for the chicken for 24 hours in the fridge, uncovered.  I used ghee in the "oil or clarified butter" step.  But the roasting method result also made a mess of my oven.  Good as it was, for that reason, as I clean my oven this morning I think this is probably one and done for me.

I followed this recipe and added compound butter with thyme, rosemary, and some sauteed garlic under the skin just before roasting. My chicken was 4.5 lbs and I cooked it at 425 for about an hour. It was perfect, and my date said ti was so good he nearly cried.
What are aromatics?
Usually onions, celery, carrots, herbs, garlic.  Anything that goes in a mirepoix or that you use to add flavor to a roast or stew.
The author provides specific suggestions for aromatics in the ingredient list.

Dry brining is a definite NO…. Takes moisture OUT of the bird.
BUT wet brining in a water salt , sugar and flavourings over night will ensure  moist delicious bird… .. don’t believe a real chef wrote this recipe.
I've dry brined a turkey and it turned out very moist and flavorful.  And the skin crisips nicely.  You should try it with the chicken.

I have bbq a spatchcock chicken for years.   Fifteen minutes skin side up.   Then 30 minutes skin side down with weights on the chicken.  Comes out perfect every time.   Excited to try this in the kitchen.   No one wants to stand outside in the cold!!!!

I make a brine of two tablespoons olive oil, one tablespoon salt and then add whatever herbs look the best from the garden.
Never been able to get past the word or the jump-scare appearance. Still, Sam Sifton remains the best food writer out there.

I advocate the "reverse spatchcock". A chicken has breast meat and dark meat. Breast meat is properly cooked to 155°F. Dark meat is properly cooked to 175°F. It is nearly impossible to get both right when cooking them at the same time. So, remove both breasts and the breast bone. You now have a "reverse spatchcock". Flatten and proceed with this recipe with a final temperature of 175°F. Save the breasts for another day.
The only way I cook poultry - no more dry white meat

I have been making Mark Bittman's Chicken Under a Brick for years. Spatchcocked, but also weighted, first breast side down, then up. We like it dry brined, and then with butter, herbs, garlic, whatever, under the skin. But I am totally going to try it on the rack with the aromatics underneath!

This is all too much work. Cook’s illustrated has the easiest and most foolproof method- no spatchcocking required. Preheat a heavy frying pan, preferably castiron, at 450.  Rub whole chicken with olive oil and the salt/spices. Tie back legs. Place chicken breast side up onto heated pan. Cook for 25-30 minutes until breast is ~120 and thigh is ~135. Turn off oven. Keep chicken in oven for another 30 minutes or so.Remove chicken and tent with foil and rest for 20 minutes. Make gravy with juices.
That's not a spatchcock chicken, is it?

Like another poster, I cut along just one side and flatten it. (Self-interest; arthritis in my hands makes  "snip along the backbone" a more difficult and painful task than it used to be, whether scissors or heavy knife.) Roasting the backbone along with the rest gives you more goodness to make easy stock out of, along with the picked carcass.

I've recently tried airfrying a spatchcocked chicken in my oven/airfryer. The basket is large enough to fit the whole bird but the wrong parts of the bird are under the fan so I needed to separate the legs and thighs so everything gets done close to the same time.
I used 325F and 50 mins for a 3.5 lb bird. The breasts are much thicker than the thighs so even though the fan was blowing directly on them they could have used another 5 min and maybe a 5F bump.

Here’s a much easier method - no cutting through bone! https://www.nytimes.com/2021/01/27/t-magazine/peter-som-roast-chicken-recipe-cooking.html He shows the cut at about :40 in the video. The chicken is good too - best if you marinate overnight.
Easiest way. Ask your butcher to spatchcock the chicken. Most are happy to do it with a little advance notice
Ask your butcher to spatchcock the chicken. Most of them are happy to do it with a little advance notice.
