For households of one or two, spatchcock the bird, then divide it in two.  Roast half using your recipe of choice and freeze the other half for next week.
I love a stuffed roasted bird. I'm sure some stuffing can be put under a spatchocked chicken, but is it enough to feed a family of 4?
That will depend on how much stuffing you put under the spatchcocked chicken.
Please tell me how refrigerating poultry or any food, uncovered, is safe (from a food safety perspective)?   I've always wondered this, but never asked.  Thanks in advance.

@Rita, bacteria cannot survive salt because salt draws moisture out of them, along with drying out the skin of the bird. Bacteria grows in moisture. Salt has been used as a preservative since before we have records.
I believe it is much easier to cut the bird in the “leaping frog” style, no cutting through bones required. Cooks the same as a spatchcock bird.
The only issue with this is that it results in an oven that will need to be cleaned. It makes a delicious chicken but there is no way around the mess if you cook it on an open sheet pan.
Itried this and will never go back to the old method.  The meat is moist and tender, simply perfect!
These clear and adaptable instructions revolutionized my weeknight roast chicken. I might never roast a chicken another way again.
Wonderful
This is also the best way ever to cook a turkey!
Didn’t brine. Sit on the bed of sliced onions and lemons and Rosemary didn’t have onions or would have added. Overdone at 40 minutes try 30. What is a 4 pound chicken.

The only problem with this recipe is the difference between dark meat and white meat cooking times. I remove the leg/thighs and start them12 minutes before the breast section. It may not look as pretty but it avoids the rubbery dark meat or the overcooked white meat problem.
Remove the leg/thighs and start them 12 minutes ahead of the breast portion. This avoids the underdone rubbery dark meat or the dry overdone white meat. Works like a charm.

I followed the directions exactly but I found it impossible to crack the wishbone. Maybe I’m just not strong enough? I ended up flipping the bird back over and just cutting the wishbone with my shears. Seemed to work just as well.

I make a simple stuffing of wild rice, chestnuts, a bit of old bread, and herbs to roast my spatchcoked chicken on. I stick it back in the oven to keep baking while the chicken rests to be sure it is cooked through.
I also am wondering where to find a 3-4 pound chicken.

I’ll add a plea for smaller birds — impossible to find a  three to four pound chicken, even with the flocks decimated by avian flu and ranchers trying to catch up financially. Everything is five pounds and up, regardless where we shop.

The chicken itself was delicious but I wish the recipe suggested how to handle/use the drippings. 

I used misc aromatics from my fridge under the bird - some old celery, parsley stems, half an onion - but then i felt that these got all the delicious drippings in and on them, but weren't actually things I wanted to eat!  So I threw them into the pot later with the bones, but since they had gotten a little singed, the broth now has a bit of burned flavor that I don't like.
Excellent, so moist!
Overnight dry brine 5lb spatchcock chicken still cooked in less than an hour after setting on counter half hour out of fridge; great with planked russets ala AGA Blake & bull parboil but no flour

I followed the recipe exactly (and watched the video). In my opinion it results in chicken that oddly tastes like salty chicken soup - not roast chicken. The skin becomes thin and peculiarly chicken soup-ish in taste. It did not cook evenly as promised.The entire bird is so overly salted, I found it inedible. I've made spatchcocked chicken several times and had high hopes for this recipe but, sadly, this one is not worth all the effort. I would never make it again and do not recommend it.

I put the chicken over a bed of fennel, shallots, lemons, garlic, and herbs. Preheated convection oven to 425, cooked 45 minutes. Internal temp read 180. I thought it would be totally dead but it was still great!
If you have weak or arthritic hands, cutting out the backbone with kitchen shears may be too hard.    A pair of tin snips from the hardware store makes the job much easier.

For most beaten egg dishes, like a frittata, I mix 1 tablespoon of corn starch to 3 tablespoons water into a slurry, add seasoning and aromatics and add to the beaten eggs.  It yields an excellent binder, distributes the seasonings in the mixture as well as fluffing and puffing the eggs for increased volume.
@Skyeenter Cornstarch is a highly processed food, has no nutritional value, and is not needed whatsoever. I’ve made hundreds and hundreds of frittatas over the years and they are great without it.

@michaluna

So I guess it depends on what you’re looking for — if you want something fluffier, you could try the cornstarch slurry tip. But if you want to avoid the processed food, you could skip it.

If you have a glass cooktop, do NOT give the pan a “firm tap” against the stovetop unless you want to shell out hundreds of dollars for a new glass cooktop.

Shaking the pan on a glass cooktop is likewise a bad idea.

My favorite filling, in a one to one-half ration:  fresh cup up asparagus for the main, Then 1/2 each of diced onions and red peppers, to equal the amount of asparagus.  Sauté all until veggies are just beginning to get tender and remove from heat.  Mix eggs and milk, salt & pepper in a bowl, add grated cheese (half Cheddar and half Gruyere are good) and stir into eggs.  Then place in a well buttered casserole; bake in oven at 350 until set.  Much easier than dealing with a cast iron pan.

In my experience of making a weekly frittata, there is no need to stir things. Start with sauteing onions, add the other veg. (leftovers or uncooked), once they are browned and softened, pour eggs over the lot, add some cheese, leave on the stove top for a few minutes (covered may help) then transfer to the broiler. Perfection. (Dollops of Labneh are my newest cheese addition.)
@MC what’s your favorite pan to use for this? Cast iron? Love the one pan style
I have just one question: what are leftover french fries??

@Ally
They are in the same category as leftover wine, I imagine!

After they're sautéed, I'll toss all the non-egg goodies into a large bowl with the cheese and eggs and give a good mixing. This generally ensures good distribution of ingredients. That gets put back in the pan over medium-low heat and cooked until the sides are set and small bubbles begin to form in the middle. After that I'll stick it in a 350º oven for 12-15 minutes. Perfection.
Don't tap especially if you are using a cast iron pan on a glass stove top!
I don't use a broiler. I've always flipped my frittata by inverting the skillet over a pizza pan and then sliding the frittata back into the skillet to cook/brown what was the top side.

This is the method for a Spanish tortilla.  Traditionally, it is made with thinly sliced potatoes and, for some cooks, onions.  It is equally adept at using leftovers and bits & bobs.  I favorite is ham, onions and marinated artichoke hearts.

Italians and Spaniards cook all the stuff you want to add first and then add it to the scrambled eggs in a bowl. Let it sit and the heat makes them stick together. Then clean the pan and start over to make the frittata.  Don't burn it.  When crust forms flip it.  When done it looks like a pie.  In Spain you will see Tortilla Espanola served by the slice for lunch with bread and a salad.

I make a frittata for the two of us every Friday with whatever leftovers I have from the week: in an 8" nonstick cake pan goes whatever I'm using along with 4 well-beaten eggs, and everything goes in the pre-heated air fryer for 12 minutes at 360. Golden, puffed and always delicious: not to mention super easy!
Sohla, your video lesson on cooking eggs so many different ways is fab. Sharing it with every egg lover I know. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MPqnS1K4vgw
I made this recipe this morning, as is, and loved it!

I made this night with leftover cooked veg plus sautéed onions and red peppers.  As some cooks suggested, I popped the whole business in a  casserole dish, put chucks of goat cheese on top and baked till golden brown and delicious.  No broiler. It was delicious.
I prepared this morning and thought it was delicious and easy. Using an assortment of vegetables it came together quickly. More broiling time was needed for browning.

This was delicious! I was skeptical of some aspects of the technique, but I followed the instructions exactly and my frittata came out perfectly. This will be my new go-to method! The only thing I did differently was started sautéing onions ten minutes before everything else to give them time to caramelize, then added red peppers, asparagus and halved cherry tomatoes for the recommended quicker sauté. Added a bunch of fresh chives with the eggs, tipped with cheddar and goat cheese, et viola!

With our 12-inch cast iron, 9 eggs, 1/2 t fine sea salt, a large onion caramelized in 2T olive oil, close to two cups diced chard stems, a tablespoon fresh thyme, over a half cup chunked feta, and a sprinkling of torn tarragon leaves to finish. Excellent!
I’m afraid I don’t understand what is done in the broiler and what on the stove. Can you clarify?
@Jmuscato: You turn the broiler on in Step 2, but all of the cooking is done on stovetop until Step 6--when you broil after dotting with cheese.
Hi, any thoughts about the best mix-ins if you want to serve at room temp?  Thanks.

I really like to use cherry tomatoes cut in half if I'm going to serve at room temp. Other veggies (spinach, kale, mushrooms) eg, and proteins (fam fave is bacon) are usually fine, but adding tomatoes give a room temp frittata a bit of freshness and pop!
I thought a Frittata was baked to cook...this is just scrambled eggs with mix-ins and cheese broiled on top...
Recommendations for a broiler safe nonstick skillet?

Cast iron. Every cook needs at least one and a 10 1/2 inch skillet is the perfect size for many recipes. 
Search cast iron and you'll get a gazillion choices!
ScanPan. Highly recommend.
I have one that I got for under $20 bucks at Ikea.

I have a slightly different technique, as I mix the vegetables into the beaten eggs, quickly wipe out the pan in case anything has stuck to it, add a little more oil if necessary, and then pour the egg/vegetable mixture back in. I don't usually use cheese but instead top my cooked frittata with salsa verde, zhoug, or pesto base (i.e. herbs, garlic, oil - no nuts or cheese). But I'll try this.
I love these "clean out the refrigerator" recipes!!  I just put more than 2 cups in!
In this time of climate chaos, energy conservation is necessary as well as efficiency.  Inverting the frittata instead of turning on the broiler is worth doing—especially on my induction cooktop.
This was amazing. I had leftover pork tenderloin, roasted potatoes and added bacon and tomato and Gouda, Cheddar and Feta cheese. Sooooo good!

My version, not looking for a browned end product, sauté mix-ins, mix eggs with a cup of cottage cheese, shredded cheese of choice and mix-ins. Pour in a pie pan and cook in the oven at 350° for about 30 minutes.

We were iced in and I had no frozen mixed veggies, so I used match stick carrots, red pepper, and shredded red cabbage. I had frozen peas so used those. Sautéed the raw veggies before the aromatics. Tasted better after some sesame oil and Maggie sauce. Did not care for ketchup so used some Sriracha as was all we had. Definitely comfort food on a cold icy night! Looks like some great leftovers.
When does this go into the broiler? Am I missing something?
Step 6 says to remove the pan from the heat, dot with cheese, and broil.
I was taught that adding salt to beaten eggs and leaving it before cooking caused the eggs to separate. Anyone got any science/further thoughts on that?

As a friend once suggested to me many years ago… The best made omelets are made from last night’s leftover (“everything”) pizza.      
He said “Just scrape the toppings off and you’ve got a great start.”  I think this would work for frittatas too!
How can I incorporate frozen spinach?
Best to thaw it first: place in a glass bowl, add a little water, and microwave 3-4 minutes. Drain in colander, pressing very well to get all the green liquid out. then use that.

I have sautéed it in the pan, straight from the freezer bag; the water cooks off while you sauté.  (In my case, the spinach had been in the freezer for a while, and I sprinkled with veeeery little garlic powder just in case there was a freezer flavor, lol.  Turned out fine!)

We do “clean up the refrigerator omelets and frittatas regularly.  Use spices and herbs and seasonings as appropriate for the ingredients and your taste.  We ALWAYS have a variety of cheeses, which sort of marry the ingredients.  Hot, nutritious, tasty, no waste, what’s to not like?
The farro texture was surprisingly meaty. I even did a double take, thinking it was sausage. My family assumed the same thing… delicious

I needed to cook this significantly longer than stated. Using a cast iron pan and an induction stovetop, I found that the center was solidifying faster than the edges.  Next time I would prewarm the pan in the oven if I used the cast iron again.  All in all, I would make it again. It still turned out well and the kids gobbled it up. I added the butts from whole grain bread, frozen peppers from last summer’s garden and some baby Swiss chard past it’s prime.

Great cues for putting together a "whatever you want" frittata. I used 12 eggs (for weekday leftovers), a small amt of olive oil, a number of veggies, cheese and a couple slices of leftover ham. I was worried that sauteing the mix-ins all in the same pan as the egg mix would make it stick but it didn't- came out in perfect slices.  Very quick, easy and delicious, adapts to whatever you have on hand.

Love this clean out the fridge method. And I did and it was super tasty. I did overcook the frittata somehow. Maybe cuz one of my add-ins was breadcrumbs. Or maybe cuz I let it go past the "jiggly" phase when it was on the desktop or perhaps it overcooked in the broiler phase, not sure. I'll make this again, but would watch that.
If you have neither broiler nor air fryer, how do you finish? Very hot oven?
Frittata is my go to for feeding two to four people easily for weeknights or even entertaining! My fav add in is leftover roast potatoes. Killer!

Why in the world would you run your broiler on high for up to 15 minutes while you cook the frittata on the stovetop? Simply turn it on when you get to that stage in the recipe — or, as others have suggested, simply flip the semi-hard eggs in the pan, either with a deft wrist flick or the safer inverted-plate method.

This recipe ended up being one of this holidays' favourites. I found out some confusion about stove top degrees during the process: 5 on my stove cannot be medium, because it does nothing to the eggs and 3 cannot be medium-low. I improvised somehow, replaced the chicken with Wiener sausage, and the recipe still became a success.

Having been a life long steak person (as was my mother and both grandmothers) I can pass on that most steaks (rib eye, NY strip, filet, porterhouse) should only be flipped once. Never mash the meat, never oil the pan (salt your seasoned cast iron pan with coarse salt) …. Skirt steak, hanger and the like are a but different…but on finer cuts, this is the standard. Butter, sauces, and toppings are served on the side after the steak has rested allowing the crust to fully form.

Insisting on only flipping your steak once is the single best guarantee for an overcooked steak. That's why modern recipe writers, chefs, and now home cooks don't do it that way. You risk a lot and gain little. https://www.seriouseats.com/the-food-lab-flip-your-steaks-and-burgers-multiple-times-for-better-results
@Hula frances that’s actually a myth. People live by it but you can flip steaks more than once especially when they’re very thick. It won’t cause it to be overcooked if you don’t overcook it.
We should all make a New Year Resolution to be thoughtful on when and why to comment.

I follow America’s Test Kitchen reverse sear method.  First, salt and pepper the steak, then warm it in a 265-degree oven for 10 minutes, then flip for another 10 minutes.  Then into the preheated hot cast iron skillet.  No oil, just Kosher salt.  Used to swear by 4 minutes on a side.  Recent research shows that flipping the steak every 60 - 90 seconds gets better results — the crust isn’t as thick and dense.  I have a Thermoworks instant read thermometer to check the temperature.

Obviously, there is more than one path to the perfect steak, and that’s a good thing. My version is salt the hot skillet, flip once, watch for the juice to bubble on top, double check with thermometer sideways through the end, deglaze pan with white wine, add one T of good butter, proceed with gilding the potatoes. Divine and 500 calories less.
@Karen a question.  Is it ok to deglaze a cast iron frying pan with wine.  Does it damage the finish built up in the pan?   Does it extract iron into the wine affecting the taste?   Thanks.

@Drgonzo777 no,it does not. Not for a cast iron pan. And yes that’s the best way to deglaze a pan. Also a seasoned cast-iron pan shouldn’t get anything in your food but if it does, it won’t be iron. It’s going to be the seasoning.
Never done a steak like this, it was perfectly cooked and I liked the outside browning. Thyme is delicate and I'd add more next time for a stronger presence without being overwhelming.
Followed instructions precisely - perfect! Could cut my steak with a butter knife
Get the smallest potatoes possible. Salt the water like the sea
My nit to pick is wiping out the pan before the butter baste step. That's a lot of flavor going to waste; much better to let the taters bathe in it combined with the butter, no?

Rather than dedicate 30 minutes of constant administration and flipping, I always reverse sear the beef, pork or lamb. The difference between the two outcomes are minimal to the point of questionable.
A great steak tutorial!  Had a beautifully marbled steak demoniac from a local Vermont farm that I did NOT want to over/under cook.  Perfect medium rare, enjoyed by all.
This comment from Hula Frances thoroughly refutes the recipe-how confusing!

The comment to which you refer did seem to be less than helpful insofar as it indicates that the information presented in the recipe was not taken into account.

As some other commenters point out, research has been done, better techniques now deliver superior results. Notably: 1. Flip Often (this recipe). 2. Reverse Sear. 

Oh, and always use a good digital thermometer. This recipe explains why in the head-notes.
Umm,,, skirt steak, cut from the diaphragm of the cow, is almost never as thick as rib-eye. Or almost any other steak.  I'd say that this recipe is perfect for most steaks, except for skirt!

My best steaks now come from my air fryer. Start with the best cuts. Brush with olive oil, massage in S&P, refrigerate for 2  hrs, bring to room temp, 400° in fryer, 10 minutes, flip at 8. No butter needed. Rest for 5 minutes, in or out of the fryer, off, depending on your doneness preference. Crisp char.

Low rating only because I made this with skirt steak and declare there are better uses for that cut than this recipe. The flavor was great, but the texture with all that butter and a thinner cut of meat wasn't appetizing at all.
Who has 30 minutes to cook a steak?

Another method that I have used, to great success  (from various reputable sources including the NYT and Cook's Illustrated) is; pre-heat a cast iron skillet in a 500 degree oven. Season a room temperature steak (T-bone, Porterhouse, or bone-in Ribeye) with salt and pepper. Open a window or turn on the exhaust fan! Put the steak in the skillet and close the oven door. Cook 3 to 5 minutes (depending on the kind of steak and the thickness), then flip and cook another 2 - 3 minutes. Let it rest.

I found I’d use less salt. A sprinkle or two rather than coating the steak. I’m glad I didn’t use as much as the recipe indicated. 

The cooking method did result in an amazingly tender and flavorful steak though. I’ve never employed this  frequent-flipping method and I must say I was impressed.
How many does this recipe serve?
Under ingredients:  Yield: 2 to 3 servings

I can tell if a steak is medium rare, the way I like it, just by poking the steak with my finger. It depends on the give of your poke if it's done. Then if that fails, 125F is the right temp for doneness.
Great taste, very rich!
I just applied this method to loin lamb chops with great success.  They were enjoyed with sautéed veg and potatoes fried in duck fat.  Washed down with a valpolicella.  YUM.
Super good and classic. I don't cook steak often but the instructions produced a noticable crust and great texture.

title: Roasted Salmon With Dill and Cucumber Salad Recipe
author: Sohla El-Waylly

comments.commentBody: 425 degrees for 12-15 minutes?  I beg to disagree. I followed this NYT cooking advice a few times only to be disappointed and frustrated that the salmon came out overcooked to the point of being dry and tasteless.  I suggest at least checking it after cooking for 6-8 minutes.

I preheat the oven to 400, then get my skillet hot on the stove.  Season the salmon as you like. Then add some olive oil to the pan. Sauté the salmon flesh side down for 15 seconds, just to give the flesh a little crispness. Then flip it skin side down, and immediately pop in the oven for 8 minutes.  
Crispy exterior, buttery soft interior. If you like firmer cooked salmon, give it another minute in the oven.

If the fish is already properly cooked when I take it out of the oven, wouldn't I overcook it if I stick it back under the broiler for 2-3 minutes? Or did you intend for the skin to be removed and put under the broiler? Otherwise, wouldn't it make more sense to slightly undercook the fish and then finish under the broiler to crisp the skin?
Remove the skin and place that under the broiler.

The classic rule of thumb for oven-roasting Pacific salmon is 10 minutes per inch of thickness at 450 degrees. I have to assume that would work for the Atlantic variety as well.  If using the broiler to crisp the skin for 2-3 minutes, I’d dial back the roasting time by a minute or two, as the oven will retain a lot of heat and continue to roast the fish while broiling.

The cooking time for salmon depends on whether it is wild or farmed.  Wild salmon takes about 1/3 to 1/2 the cooking time of farmed (depending on size).   This recipe must have been based on farmed salmon.
You can also quickly use a blow torch to crisp the skin

Kenji's "Dry-Brined Salmon" recipe from this site recommends a minimum of 8 hours of salting to achieve "ideal results". Is the difference the amount of salt being used? He gives a specific amount of salt to be used in his recipe and this just says "heavily salt". Can you please clarify?
Can this dry brining technique be used on all fish? Skinless fish, like a cod loin?
I have tried this technique with dry brining a few times and despite rinsing thoroughly, I find the fish comes out saltier than I like it. Should I be using less salt?
the cucumber salad deserves to be its own recipe :)
Way to salty.  Didn't salt the second time after rinsing and still to salty.  Afraid my landlord will find my mumfied corspe in the morning.
This was great! We loved this at our house. I am not a dried fruit fan, so I swapped the cranberries for some fresh pomegranate. Highly recommend - makes for a bright and flavourful side salad.
Hey all y’all. If you only want to cook salmon the way you already cook it, why even read this recipe??
This is way too salty even with rinsing. i cook salmon every week and do not find it dry ever.
Trust the recipe and process with this one.  The salad especially seems very strange but all put together delicious and they compliment each other.

I have to agree with previous commenters who questioned the time needed to cook the fish. Mine was overdone after 12 minutes and probably would have been perfect at 8 or 9 minutes. Also, I salted the fish as directed after rinsing off the brine, and the overwhelming taste was of salt rather than of salmon. If I made this again, I would not use the second dose of salt. But sadly, I won’t make it again; I have more successful methods in my repertoire.
Do you return salmon to broiler skin side up under broiler?  Seems that would cook salmon more than you want?
Wait, did I miss it? What do you do with the cucumbers, dill and cranberry mixture?
"Rinse the salmon under cool water to remove the salt... Lightly season with salt." Perhaps just tweak the dry-brine step so you don't have to rinse and re-salt
Brett - the cucumber, dill, cranberry part is just a side dish suggestion (cold salad). It doesn’t mix with the salmon unless you place them overly close on your plate. ;-)

Better cooking in a saute pan lightly oiled on medium high skin side down.  Get pan hot beforehand.   Flip to skinless side after five minutes or so.  Then watch the color change rising up side of fish to exactly your preference (mr to mw).  Lift off with spatula and skin will be crispy!  Love the dry brine idea can't wait to try...

I think Alison Roman’s recipe for salmon and herbs is a much more efficient method for cooking/crisping by broiling it for 5-7 mins . The recipe itself is simple and outstanding . After reading this recipe thoroughly and the comments I think I will stick with AR’s recipe and double and reduce her sauce / marinade which can be  refrigerated and used again when the mood strikes !
I have tried this recipe. No matter how long one washes the salmon, it remains overly salty.
why not just wipe the salt off the salmon when ready to cook instead of flushing the fish with water after all the salt is removing moisture and firming up the fish isn't it?
Does everyone eat the "brown stuff" underneath the skin? I have avoided because it seemed to have an off-putting taste?

I followed the instructions, marinating the filet for 25 minutes and rinsed it well.  It turned out to be very salty and had a hard texture, not at all like it usually turns out, whether pan or oven roasted.  I wont be trying this again.
This far too complicated and results in dry tasteless salmon.  The suggestions in feedback say it all.
You could also just pull the fish from the stove or oven when it's somewhere around 110-115. It will finish cooking as it rests. 125 degrees is the magic number if you hate overcooked salmon.

I've never cooked salmon skin up before. It makes it hard to check for doneness. I set the timer for 8 minutes and cut into it. It still seemed uncooked, so I set the timer for another two minutes. Shame on me, from the PNW, I know better!  It ended up overcooked. Nonetheless, we cooked some brown rice and had bowls with the rice, cucumber salad, and salmon. Delish! Will make again, just cooking less.
It would be helpful to specify where in the oven the rack should be. The top of the oven will be much hotter than the middle or bottom.

NYT Cooking has some wonderful recipes, but for those of us on a low/no salt diet, the recipes fail to suggest either a decent salt substitute or herbs. I miss salt, but I would rather do without than have another heart attack!

Salmon is oily enough. Lightly sprinkle greek seasoning and smashed garlic all over, roast at 425 on "airfry" if you have it for the 15-20 minutes, depending upon how pink you want it inside. I recently had a mixed citrus "dip" at a steakouse in NYC. Made my own using lime/orange/lemon rinds, butter and a pinch of sugar. Exceptional. P.S. If you can find King/Chinook salmon, undercook. You'll be glad you did.

This recipe must be for incredibly thick and fatty salmon, because my wild caught keta salmon was a hard, dense lump after only 8 minutes at that temp. Broiling it after didn’t improve anything. I’m surprised NYT writers don’t talk about different varieties of salmon, and just assume everyone is cooking the thickest and most fatty varieties.
Lovely recipe! Nice hack with with the rolling pin and the cucumbers. I found 220°C for 15min a very adequate time. The salmon came out just the way I love it.
Are the dry brining instructions to salt both the skin and meat sides of the fish?
I agree with other readers.  I cook it for Max 5 minutes and crisp the skin separately

Remove the skin, or buy it skinless, It tastes & smells fishy. Pan sear it in a hot skillet with butter until brown & crispy, about 2 minutes, flip it over & finish it in the oven for 5 minutes, ( 425 ) and it's a perfect medium that is cooked through but not dry. I prefer M/MR so 3 minutes works.

Totally different preparation so totally different taste.   I've been cooking salmon with skin on for the past decade.  It does not smell fishy and is easy to cook in a variety of ways.  This sounds interesting and easy.  I think I'll try it.
Would this work in my air fryer?
Yes. 11-12 minutes, 400F, flesh side up, no flipping.

I love Sohla and hate to be negative, but this is waaaayyyy more done than I ever want to eat salmon. I always dry brine, usually also add some cane sugar. If I want crisp skin, the start in a cold skillet method works. Or you can just broil, skin up for 8 minutes. But my favorite is to cook the skin separately. I do the meat at 275 for 15 minutes and then toast the skin on a piece of foil in the toaster oven until it is like a potato chip, which takes about 10 minutes.
At the risk of sounding like a boor, I loathe cranberries and wonder what an acceptable substitute might be? It's a texture thing/phobia, I guess...
The Washington Post has a salmon recipe to broil salmon skin side up to get it cooked and crisp. https://www.washingtonpost.com/recipes/broiled-salmon-horseradish-sauce/

Given the industrial farming of most salmon and the fact that it’s shipped to the consumer by airplane, I don’t get it, while this fish still is recommend. There are so many alternative fishes with less environmental impacts.

After I dry brined I marinated salmon in maple syrup and dash of soy for a few hours (my usual method). Rinsed off salt, towel dried before popping back into marinade, adding more maple syrup on top and cooked for about 10 minutes before popping under the broiler. Worried fish was going to be overdone but not yet crispy, so stripped off skin and returned under the broiler on its own. End result was delicious, fish perfectly done, tender and moister than usual: modify recipe to your own liking.
The fish came out perfectly! Mine was thinner so I roasted for ten and broiled for one.
The dry-brining method was so easy & far superior to standard oven baked!  425 convection for 25 minutes...salmon came out excellent flavor AND texture!!!
Don't know about others, but instructions as written gave us a completely yummy dinner.
really easy, delicious way to cook the salmon.

I have been wondering how to achieve nice crispy skin on salmon. I was excited about trying this recipe until I read the notes. I'm confused how it got a 4-star rating. Can anyone offer some insight on whether the notes tend to be overly critical/picky or do the reviews skew toward being overly positive?
Nub - best ever way to cook salmon. It was perfect, thank you!
Too much salt!!! Unhealthy....

After trying a number of methods of cooking salmon, I've come to rely on my air fryer. I prefer farm raised Atlantic salmon; center cut skin-on filets of 8 oz. I spray the screen, season the flesh side, and air fry without flipping for 11-12 minutes at 400F. Comes out perfectly each time
I cooked a 7oz piece for 5 minutes and it was perfect.

Did not work for me. Used the flavor profile successfully for a canned salmon puree for flatbread roll ups. Subbed greek yogurt for oil, left the non pureed cranberries and cucumbers to scatter on last. So good on hot day. Chives next time.

I cooked at 425 for 10 minutes, then 3 min under the broiler to crisp the skin.  Perfect.  However, the additional salt called for after the fish had been dry brined put it over the top of saltiness for me.  The cucumber dish was yummy.  I substituted sultanas for cranberries.

Would you all do yourselves a favor and use a meat thermometor?  145 degrees for salmon will be medium.  I like it rare, so 125. All of the anxious comments about cooking times for meat and fish, when a simple thermometer produces perfect results every time.
whaat if you cant get cucumbers

title: Broccoli With Sizzled Nuts and Dates Recipe
author: Sohla El-Waylly

comments.commentBody: Fine idea, except why bother with the broiler? Put the cruciforms back in the still-hot pan, and stir around while they blacken. I add a little water at the end and put a lit on to get a little steam cooking if needed. Add the other ingredients for some more heat just before serving. One pan, no waiting.

@Lisa 
Great idea!
I prefer to roast the broccoli at @425* for about 20 minutes to deepen the flavor while remaining crunchy. Also provides a larger margin of error to avoid over charring.
This nut-date topping is fantastic. I used pecans and pistachios, charred the broccoli and served it over farro. Delicious!
Roasted broccoli is much easier in an Air Fryer if you have one. 360 for 3-6 minutes depending on quantity

I have made this several times, roasting at 425F instead of broiling.  I microwave the broccoli, misted with a little water, for 2-3 minutes before roasting.  It retains its bright color and crunch but I don’t have to watch it as closely as I would have to if broiling.  Last night I used a combination of walnuts, pecans, and a few pistachios, using walnut oil instead of olive oil.  That was the best version so far.   Going to try this with cauliflower next.

@Kit
I love all of your ideas. Cauliflower would taste good too and maybe adding butternut squash or roasted sweet potatoes too.
@Kit genius move with microwaving; I debated blanching/shocking to get it cooked through beforehand, but microwave was perfect before getting some roasted edges.
What a lovely addition to the broccoli rotation. I used pecans and pepitas with the dates. Easy and delicious.

To simplify, roasted broccoli in the oven at 400F for 30 mins. Delish. Next time, will smear whipped ricotta on baked sweet potato and top with the broccoli and seasoned nuts and dates. Sprinkling of fresh lemon zest to finish. Bliss.
Awesome served over farro.  Added a little date syrup and chili oil.
Paired nicely with pork tenderloin.

This was so incredibly good! I like broccoli extra done so I roasted it at 400 for about 25ish minutes with a can of chickpeas and half a red onion. The dressing is OMG delicious. I used pistachios walnuts pepitas and pinenuts and followed the recipe after that!! I could eat this every day!
Delicious. You can air fry the broccoli to save time, but nothing beats a slow roast in the oven for taste. Used szechuan peppercorns for an extra kick!
This was a hit with my family. So much so that I re-made the nut and date topping the next morning and we have been throwing it on everything (and, I confess, eating it by the spoonful).
Expect those sizzled nuts to show up in other dishes! Breakfast oatmeal with a maple drizzle to finish? Yip. Even with all that black pepper.
You can totally broil the broccoli while toasting the nuts. Do not omit lemon juice. Mix with white beans for a full meal.

A delightful surprise - easy to prepare, delicious (used pepitas and pecans), and devoured! 

Also appreciate the tip on using our salad spinner to dry the broccoli - not something I would've tried for fear that spinning something so chunky would break it. It didn't, and worked like a charm.

I used sliced almonds, walnuts and dates., plain California were fine. More the merrier. The lemon juice should be stirred into the mixture off the heat and set aside. 8 minutes under a close hi broiler is fine.

Absolutely delicious . different and super healthy and a versatile side dish. Could also be a main dish with the addition of  tofu or chicken.
I made Spanish mackerel fillets  escabeche and it went great together.

Made this tonight, outstanding way to liven up sometimes boring broccoli. I took another poster’s advice and charred the broccoli in the same cast iron pan used for toasting the nuts, simple and delicious!

Delicious.  Used cashews and pistachios.  I took another reviewers suggestion and microwaved the broccoli 3 minutes and then roasted it in convection oven at 400 for about 10 minutes.  It was perfectly charred and just soft enough.
I used roasted mixed nuts and pumpkin seeds. I cooked the broccoli as instructed in the air fryer. Favorably received

This reminds me of the “Sunny Broccoli” deli salad of the 90’s made with raisins and sunflower seeds.
Other combinations of nuts and fruits could be interesting as well. I might try craisins for a pop of color.
Made this tonight and loved the nut and date topping! Worth repeating the recipe.

We served this on a plater surrounded by the NYT recipe "Air-Fryer Tofu" and it was beautiful and tasty. This recipe pairs nicely with other recipes because it doesn't compete with strong flavors...it is subtle but wow!
This tasted even better than it looks.  2 - 4 servings and I had to restrain myself from eating it all. Guess what I'm having for breakfast today!!!
Perfect as written for me. Browning nuts in a pan is important since you then add the dates to plump up a bit.
The topping doesn't adhere, so you'll be chasing it around your plate to try and balance it on your fork with the broccoli. It's so good tho that you won't care. Just eat it with your fingers.

Put all cooked components in a bowl and added a quarter cup of a sauce composed of olive oil, honey, Dijon, and harissa (about a tsp of the latter two). It made the flavors pop without overpowering. Guests loved it!

I (mostly) made this Saturday and loved the topping!  I used pecans & Pepitas, & it was delicious. I roasted the broccoli as I usually do, rather than broiling, & I served it atop yogurt mixed with salt, pepper, cumin, & lime (I was out of lemons).

Loooved this! The combination of nuts / dates / black pepper is excellent. I roasted my broccoli and used a mix of almonds and pecans. Served it with edamame beans, baked tofu, and chilli crisp for lunch.

Excellent!
Steamed broccoli in the MW first, then broiled about 8-10 minutes 
Used pecans and walnuts
Like the ratio but may have had less broccoli than called for
Lemon is a must
I was skeptical about this one, even though I love all the ingredients but is delicious and now it in our family rotation.

Delicious. Like several other commenters, I roasted the broccoli (25 mins @ 400F) along with, on a separate sheet pan, quartered cremini mushrooms (17 min) I’d tossed in olive oil and seasoned with sea salt. I thought the combination worked well with the date topping, which really does only need a minute to cook.

The topping is dangerous! Easy to eat it all up before it makes it on to the broccoli! I will be making it regularly and keeping it in the refrigerator! Highly recommend the European blend peppercorns from Penzey’s for that ingredient.

I used sesame and sunflower seeds with almonds. Added some creamy goat cheese crumbles and chili pepper flakes to the top. Combined with the dates and charred broccoli, the flavors are a perfect mixture of sweet, hot and salty.

I must admit that I steamed the broccoli.  Perhaps I don't know how to properly roast it, because every time I've tried, it comes out simultaneously burned and raw.  I only had walnuts, so that's what I used, along with some dates I found in the back of the pantry.  I devoured the whole thing.
Roast 425x20min
I agree with Lisa.  Just blacken the broccoli in the hot pan.  (In Texas, I like to avoid the oven if possible--too hot in the kitchen!)
This is delicious, beyond simple. I roasted the broccoli, used pecans, walnuts and sunflower seeds and made a meal of it by putting it over sweet potato glass noodles.

My broccoli hating husband absolutely devoured this. I added sesame seeds, cashews and walnuts. I realized cashews roast quickly so maybe add it a minute later than the walnuts. 
I will add few more dates and probably chop up the broccoli after cooking so i get all bits in one spoonful 🥹

Made this for Mother's Day. Was a huge hit. The nut & date topping will be a go-to for me as it's easy to make and will work well over other veggies or even salads.  I happened to have dates at home. But if out, dried cranberries or raisins would work well.

I wasn't able to get the broccoli stems crispy, even on broil high for 12 min. They were bit soft. Crowns were fine. I'll try drying it even more next time.  And I might skip the step to peel the stems.
Am I crazy or do the sizzled nuts and dates need a pinch of brown sugar and maybe even some red pepper flakes?

This was really good. I realized (as others have commented) that the nut mixture would work well with broccoli just roasted instead of broiled and I'll do that next time just because I'm more used to cooking broccoli that way. For me, seasoning with a little salt was almost too much. On the other hand I only used a little bit of pepper and it wasn't enough. Also, I amost overcooked the nuts - have to be careful there. I made this with figs because I forgot to by dates. Still good!
Vegetarian go to

everyone, i mean everyone at our dinner last night loved this recipe.  even the dog, oops, we don't have a dog.  i slowly toasted the nuts and seeds because i have burned them too many times before in other recipes.  i also took my time when adding the dates.  i will be making this recipe again and again.  i used bagged broccoli florets.  my husband doesn't like black peppercorns and so i just lightly sprinkled the broccoli with freshly ground black pepper.  it was delicious!

Toasting nuts in a toaster oven, if you have one, for about 6 to 7 minutes results in perfectly toasted and not-burned nuts.  Pan toasting requires constant attention and if you turn away for a minute you've got char.  Different toaster ovens may vary in the time needed but once you've got it down bob's-your-uncle.

For a bachelor & senior citizen, this set of instructions is very confusing. "Trim the dry end off the broccoli & peel the stem. Cut broccoli stem in half lengthwise, then pull it apart to break the head in half. Cut into individual florets, each with a long spear of stem attached." I understood none of this. The dry end? Peel the stem? Perhaps with detailed instructions like those using terminologies that I found confusing, & perhaps others did too, we could've used a video to show us the steps

I guess this is close to how I prepare broccoli anyway so it made sense to me.  Here is another way to say it:  Remove the tough end of the stem and discard it.  Peel the stem up to the crown.  Cut through the stem lengthwise but leave the crown intact; then pull apart the two stem halves to gently separate the two halves of the crown.  Cut the two half-heads lengthwise several times to make slender spears, each having some stem and crown.

The sizzled nuts & dates are so delicious! I haven’t even put them over broccoli yet, but I’ve been making knockout breakfasts of plain whole milk yogurt, sliced banana, a drizzle of honey and topped with the nuts/dates. Sounds strange, but trust me, the sweet/salty/creamy/crunch is divine! I think this topping will become as much a staple now as the homemade chili-garlic crunch!

- Can put back in the still-hot pan, stir around while they blacken. Add a little water at end w a lid on to get a little steam cooking if needed. Add other ingredients for some more heat just before serving. One pan!
- 425 for 20 min or Air Fryer @ 360 for 3-6 min
- used pecans and pepitas with the dates
not for me. too hard to eat , the nuts and dates don't stay on the fork with the broccoli.

Easy, delicious and nutritious.  I cooked the broccoli in the air fryer with 2 Tbs evoo, used a mix of walnuts, pistachios and pine nuts (based on what we already had in the house), slightly increased the dates and added a sprinkling of cayenne.  This will go into our rotation from now on.  Yum!
This is one of the best things ever if you love brassicas and dates, crunch and acid. Yeah, I roasted the broccoli. Otherwise, as written. Brilliant. Thank you, Soha!

Very special flavors.. husband raved. I doubled the nuts/ dates/ lemon. Used some Costco mixed nuts plus sunflower seeds
Due to timing issue, I added the charred broccoli into the cast iron pan with nuts- coating everything nicely and then a quick reheat…my new fav broccoli recipe!

We all loved this!  A perfect blend of nutty toastiness, char, and sweetness!  I used pistachios, slivered almonds, walnuts, pecans, sunflower seeds, black and white sesame seeds with only 2T olive oil. After the broccoli and nut blend was assembled, I sprinkled a few tablespoons of pomegranate seeds over it all which added tiny bursts of sweet/tart moisture. Delish!  And easy to make.

Roasting the broccoli first would help to get it cooked through and then finish under broiler if needed but mine usually gets toasty anyway.  I used whole raw cashews but next time I’ll break them up.  Date/nut mix was delicious but I doubt I used 2 teaspoons of pepper and it was quite peppery!  This mix was also good in some coconut curry rice we had with it and I can imagine it with a lot of other things!  You could add pasta for a meal as well.
Would this be good with olives?

Great recipe! I was skeptical of the dates, but they were really good in combination with the toasty nuts. I suspected that two tsp of peppercorns was going to be excessive so I halved it and was glad I did, but other people may be bigger peppercorn fans.
This dish was ok. The nut topping was delicious, but the preparation suggestions need work, I think.

Can Cauliflower be substituted for the Broc, using the same recipe (but I assume a longer cooking time, due to the density of the Caulis)?

How about figs, rather than dates?  Anyone try that?
This is a really interesting dish. I served it on a bed of mashed potatoes. In the future I might cut the spears into bite-sized pieces before broiling for easier serving.

I am switching to more plant-based meals and found this pretty delicious over cooked farro.  However, I wish it were a little sassier. Does anyone have any suggestions how to have the topping have a little more liquid? Thanks.
This is delicious!! Easy and I look forward to keeping it in the broccoli rotation.

I discovered that the last of my dates had been consumed so roasted some sweet potato cubes instead. Delicious! Love the toasted and seasoned nuts. What a great idea to combine with roasted broccoli. Next time I’ll try the dates.

Quite tasty. The dates and broccoli are a great combination and the nuts complement the dish well. The problem is getting the nut and date mixture to adhere to the broccoli. Basically, one has to fork a piece of broccoli and the scoop up the nut mixture but the flavors are great
I've made this a few times. Interesting recipe.  I might add a pinch of Korean pepper to the next batch.

This can easily be done in a wok, skipping the broiling, but giving a similar result. I did it in two stages with the nuts and dates first, then setting that aside to dry sauté the broccoli before recombining all. Two things to clean. Pretty good, if not identical.
An amusing aside. I totally botched cooking this recipe. Too hot broiler, nuts mixture added to broccoli. Result: incinerated veg. Live and learn
I used the nuts/seeds I had on hand - cashews, sesame seeds, pecans and pine nuts, and wound up eating half a head of a huge roasted cauliflower. This is bonkers good.
This is delicious.  I too modified how I cooked the broccoli by roasting it at 450 degrees instead of broiling. The combination of flavors and textures is perfect.

We all really liked this but the dates never really melted to make a coating on the broccoli similar to the pictures. I used a combo of pecans, pepitas and pistachios. My guests asked for the recipe so it was all good.
Amazing - the combo of textures and flavors is fantastic. Had it for dinner all by itself and was very very happy.
Absolutely delicious!

Delicious nut, date & lemon sauce. Trust the 2 teaspoons of pepper. It gives a nice kick without using capsicum. 
I added a bit of lemon zest last minute with a tiny sprinkle of parsley just before serving. 
Roast any cruciferous veg any way then top with this. Yum!

I had freshly roasted pepitas on hand so warmed the nuts gently & skipped to step 2 without the 2T. oil. Easy, interesting texture, great leftovers.
Par boil  the Broccoli and then in oven - shorter control time in oven

The date nut addition (I only had cashews) was great.  Lemon juice didn't come across as lemony, just as brightness.  I messed up the prep and made small florets with some chopped (peeled) stems and it was still great.
Everyone in my family loved this! The date/nut mixture adds such a lovely texture. I used half cashews half peanuts. Watch the nuts carefully when sauteeing, they burn quickly!

sohla did it again! this nut-date topping is incredible. we grilled our broccoli.. but why stop there? serve over salads, your favorite protein bowl, leave out the pepper and add it to yogurt or pancakes. a new favorite topping in our house!

This topping is phenomenal. Dates and broccoli really go hand-in-hand. I ended up microwaving briefly first to cook the broccoli through (instead of blanching/shocking) then roasting which I would continue to do. I will be making this regularly.
Anyone try roasting a day ahead & doing a quick reheat? I've got too much going on in the oven.
Could you make the topping a day ahead and then warm it during prep?
A big favorite at our Thanksgiving.
My first comment here because this is THAT delicious. Absolutely genius blend of flavors and textures. Yum!!
Very good and easy to make! It was a hit at Thanksgiving!
One of the best vegetable (and vegan) dishes we've ever had.  We made it exactly as written, with the nuts being a mix of cashews and pecans.  Yum, thank you Sohla El-Waylly!
The delicious topping is also excellent on roasted Brussels sprouts.
Change out the dates to slim slices of red pepper and red onion to keep the sweet vibe with less calories. Healthier, too.

Great and easy recipe! I increased the date/nut mixture a bit and added scarlet runner beans and roasted butternut squash with cumin and a little chili powder. Made a great dinner that happened to be vegan!

stats.Cook Time: 30 minutes, plus 20 minutes’ soaking and 15 minutes’ resting)

comments.commentBody:
I'm Armenian and all of my grandparents were from Turkey. We made it like this: 

First, lightly brown very thin egg noodles with butter in a saucepan (my grandmother and mother used to use an entire stick of butter, but of course, that was the 60s. Three tablespoons works just fine) - just don't let them get too dark. Add the rice, thoroughly coat in the butter and then add chicken broth to cover. Simmer covered until liquid has been absorbed. Season to taste. Fluff. Perfect. Every. Time.
Jacquie, your recipe is very close to mine. I’m GF and use GF spaghetti, broken into the butter. Cook to golden, add rice and mix into the butter, then chicken broth! Delicious.

My Armenian grandparents were also from Western Armenia, Dikranagerd, before they were driven out by the Turks. Had to add that for accuracy sake! :)
We would add shaydeh (thin egg noodles) and cook as you've described except my mom would pour in boiling water or heated chicken broth to the sauted rice and noodles. Btw, a heavy bottomed pot is essential.

@Laurig That’s the way this non Armenian (“odar”) was taught to make it by my Dikranagerd mother in law.  I still use a stick of butter to 2 cups of rice.  But I discovered a diffuser. I put that under the pot when I lower the heat.  No more rice stuck to the bottom (but that is a treat for whoever empties the pot!).

The repeated rinsing , soaking and discarding of the water in this recipe will effectively extract much of the B vitamins remaining in the white rice or that have been added to it via enrichment.  My practice with white rice is to limit the rinsing, live with the starch and keep the B vitamins.

Parching brown rice until aromatic and colored causes it to open and be fluffier, more like white rice in texture.  A neat trick for the brown rice adverse. Cook as you would normally after the toasting.

@BJ. The recipe here doesn’t call for enriched rice. In most cases, normal rice grains such as aren’t enriched and should always be rinsed before cooking. There is light traces of arsenic from the soil found in rice even in organic rice and should be rinsed off. Rinsing the rice also reduces the excessive starch in rice making the resulting cooked rice lighter and fluffier.
Can this be adapted to a rice cooker?

@Raba Zaki
Yes.  I used to do this in a small heavy pot as a side dish, or a one dish meal. When I had a rice cooker, I made it in the rice cooker.
This is the best rice I’ve ever made. I followed the recipe exactly. Thank you.

I’m Indian born in Kolkata, India. My family members cooked this dish on all the religious occasions. And we loved it all the time cause It’s great. And it’s one of the most tasty foods that you’ll ever taste.  Try it and feel the smell of heaven in your nostrils. Good luck….

I am assuming that after step 2, all ingredients can be transferred to a rice cooker.  Step 5 has detailed instructions, it seems, for those uninitiated in rice cooking.  Any thoughts on why this might not transfer to a rice cooker successfully?

My Mother, from Northern Illinois, learned to make rice this way while working in Brazil.  

Back in the States, American cooks were astonished to see her “fry the rice before boiling it”.   And all the more astonished about the fluffy outcome.  (“It’s not even the instant rice!”)

As suggested in the recipe, she also added chopped onions and minced garlic.  The rice was ready to be steamed when it had become opaque.

Ever since I left my mother's house (she relied on Uncle Ben's Instant Rice, which I hated) and began cooking for myself, I have always cooked rice like this. I just thought this was how rice was cooked. Now I am going to make myself sound fancy and say I am cooking pilaf!

Thanks for this useful recipe but would like to point out it is never advisable to use water from the hot tap for consumption as this water contains containments (even 2 Tbs from the step to take if your rice has not cooked sufficiently after 18 min).
Good tip. Never drink water from a hot tap as it might contain dissolved minerals and metals due to the heating process.
Use filtered cold water.
I do a cheater parching where I spread the dry rice on a toaster over tray and toast it then proceed as for regular stove top rice.

Living g in San Antonio TX rice is a major part of Tex Mex cooking. I received a tip from someone to place a tea towel between the pan and lid during the steaming process at the end. I get fluffy rice every time whether I toast it first or not. Have also used this technique with other grains.

Yes! I do the tea towel for steaming/resting at the end, EVERY time I cook any kind of rice, and it never fails. People always want more of the fluffy, perfect rice, whether it's a pilaf or just plain rice.

I love this recipe -- it's similar to how I cook rice, except that (I can hear the horrified intake of breath already) I don't soak. I just measure it out, plunk it in a pot, add 1 tsp of Diamond Crystal Kosher salt per cup, 1 tablespoon of good butter per cup of rice, & 1 1/2 cups boiling water per cup. I cover it & peek in at about 15 minutes, when it's usually done, & then I turn off the heat, fluff it, cover it with a tea towel & then the pot lid. 5 minutes later it's gorgeous perfection.

Rinsing enriched white rice removes the added vitamins and minerals. Do not rinse unless you have reason to believe it's dirty. I generally add onions, garlic, peppers and celery when I make a pilaf. The green makes it pretty. Cumin can also be toasted with the rice. Use imagination. I've been cooking this way for close to 60 years.

This recipe is the first time that a NYT recipe was a bust! For me, at least.  I followed the instructions for rinsing and letting sit covered in water, just 15 minutes.  Then sauteed in the butter per the instructions and added only 3 cups of chicken broth for 2 cups rice.  Even with less water, this turned into a mushy concoction.  I can't imagine what another cup of liquid would have done.  Probably turned it into a porridge. Next time, no soaking.

I would suggest that your pot that is used will be a big determiner in how much moisture is left or isn't left at the end. I have one pot, I only use for rice. It isn't a wonderful pot it just works. I try not to use it for anything else and that helps keep it "working" as my rice pot. I have bought several upgrades thinking that they would be the shiny new rice pots. They were all a disappointment. How to find the right pot? Look for one without "hot spots" but mostly try different pots.
I added some toasted broken angel hair for extra flavor and authenticity.
This is a lovely, but hardly foolproof, recipe. I find that baked rice is easier, more forgiving, and always turns out beautifully.
My mother's friend cooked rice this way, she used Uncle Ben's and it was the most delicious rice I've ever had.
Add how much water when ready to cook. I don’t see that.
Susan - re your question for how much water, which varies depending on the kind of rice you choose, see the info posted in step 3.

I have never seen a pilaf which differed from cooked rice solely by browning the rice in butter first.  My pilafs feature a flavored liquid, often broth, with spices, aromatics,  and/or vegetables.  Sometimes I throw in dried fruit like raisins, dried apricots, dried mango bits.  Been cooking for over 50 years, am not a newbie.  

Dictionary definition of pilaf isn’t as Sohla claims.  But don’t just take my word for it.

I added some more butter at the end and my spouse didn’t like it as much, though my daughter did. So 50/50. I felt like the effort didn’t result in anything more different than my rice cooker. 

Maybe I’ll try again with more salt and less butter. I also want to try it with more aromatics and extras.

I’ve been making basmati rice like this for years and always turns out well. I use 1:1 rice to water. One trick that help keep rice (and other grains cooked in water) fluffy is when you turn off heat, let it rest for 5-10 minutes before serving but put a tea towel over the top and then the lid on top of that. The towel soaks up the extra steam and prevents the rice from getting mushy from condensing steam.
I will try this method, but I wonder what the purpose of rinsing the rice is? Doesn't that make it mushy?

I use long grain rice ( basmati).
Butter is nice but parching in olive oil or ghee works fine too.
For extra flavor stir fry some whole cumin seed and a big black cardamom with the onions and garlic before parching the rice.
Because parching gets the cooking process going I prefer to use 1.5 cups of water for every cup of rice. Also live to add greens like chopped coriander and/or thinly sliced scallions to the finished product. 
For a veg  ‘biryani’ just mix in frozen peas and carrots.

I just learned a similar recipe but where one does not rinse first. The explanation was that the starches that are not rinsed, help to give the rice a nuttiness and keep it from clumping. Any thoughts?

We made this with Carnaroli rice (an Arborio-type Italian rice often used in risottos), and it didn't turn out creamy, as some have suggested; the grains were softer, but separate and only very slightly sticky. Very delicious with the addition of shallots with the rice while parching, coriander stems laid on top while cooking, and chicken broth as the cooking liquid.
For those who want to know the amount of water, try reading step 3 from start to finish. It says it right there
Liquid needs to be just about twice the amt. of rice - maybe a tad less. I use the same container to measure the liquid after filling it with rice.
managed to be both gluggy and undercooked!
Go back, cook some more until rice is just the way you like it. Always helps to keep the lid on the pan.
My rice was soggy. Even after resting. And it needed much more salt.
Turn up the heat with the lid on the pan until liquid is absorbed - add salt before or after to your liking.

I use a container, cup or glass filled with long-stem rice.  The liquid I add is just about twice the size of the rice container. Ex: If I use one cup rice, I will add one cup water and one cup broth. I add a Tbsp. butter and a few squirts of olive oil, to the pan plus salt.  I put a lid on the pan and cook on high until rice comes to a full, long boil. Then I turn off the heat, leave the lid on the pan and let it set until liquid is absorbed.  Fluff with a fork.  Works perfectly every time!
I followed the recipe using a rice cooker, and adding the butter up front made a big difference.  It was delicious.
can you use arborio rice?

Hi, John  It states in the description that this Pilaf can be made with almost any grain, fat, or liquid, it is supremely versatile.  So I think Arborio rice would work, but this is only my assumption based on how versatile the recipe is claimed to be.
You will likely get something closer to a risotto than a pilaf by using arborio rice. Creamy as opposed to fluffy.

@Raba Zaki & Gonzalo.  Yes this can be cooked in a rice cooker or even in an Instant Pot - I’ve done it many times.  IMPORTANT: Use the same amount of water/liquid as you would be cooking normal white rice when using the electrical cookers; otherwise the rice will be mushy. However oftentimes I find that cooking pilaf rice in rice cookers tend to reduce the fluffiness of the rice.
Would basmati or jasmine work here?
Yes, either would work - I have used both.
How much water should be added after browning?
The recipe outlines the amount of water per the type of rice you are using - short, medium or long grain.
Buttered rice is one of my ultimate comfort foods from childhood. Thank you for encouraging me to make it with this recipe!
Could you use olive oil or canola instead to parch the rice (for vegans or cholesterol restrictions)?
Add how much water????
Read Step 3. All of Step 3.

If you don’t want sticky rice that clumps together, just use (Uncle) Ben’s and skip all the rinsing and soaking rigamarole. You can sauté it beforehand and cook it in chicken broth for delicious perfection.

Any pilaf variation will taste better and smell divine with the addition of a sprig of curry leaves. Temper the leaves in the butter before adding the rice. This is a "no-fail, go-to" herb in all Sri Lankan kitchens.
What kind of rice? Basmati, Jasmine, Arborio, Valencia......
Add allspice to Jacquie’s description and it’s my grandma’s recipe from karamanmaras turkey!
I never have rinsed rice. I routinely toast rice before I add the water. What do rinsing and soaking do?

I was married to an Armenian whose family cooked pilaf exactly as Jacquie directs. Simple, delicious, gets the job done. 

In the nineties, I adapted the microwave recipe on the Carolina rice bag to do this in Corningware. Unfortunately, microwaves today are higher-powered, and the Carolina rice method no longer works in the microwave.

I do not rinse rice, or certainly not more than one light rinse. I happen to like sticky, starchy rice, and I want to leave the nutrients intact, also. One brand of rice I use specifically says not to rinse the rice at all, to retain flavor and vitamins.

Step 3:  Add the water.  What water?  All of the water has been drained, and there is no water listed in the ingredients.  Does this mean I should just add the amount of water I would normally add when making rice?
Read Step 3 for specific types of rice.
I make rice like this using sesame oil instead of butter.  I do it with onion and garlic starting on the stovetop and finish it in the oven so no chance of sticking.

Somewhere along the way I picked up a recipe for turmeric rice that was probably based on this.  Basically just add high-quality turmeric (or a few varieties if you have them on hand, like the blue turmeric from Burlap and Barrel) -- about 1 heaping teaspoon per cup of rice -- and toast it fast in the butter before putting in the rice.  Very good.

This is just how I make a Pilaf. I like more salt, but that’s a taste preference, and I use half butter and half olive oil for the fat. For one cup of rice, try sautéing 1 Tb diced shallot and 1 Tb diced celery in the fat before adding the rice. Microwave a pinch of saffron threads in 2 C water and allow to steep while preparing rice, then add to pot and simmer partially covered on low heat until water is absorbed. Turn off heat and rest about 10 min. Enjoy!

I echo Jacquie Tellalian's comment. Probably every Armenian girl learns to make this dish when she is tall enough to reach the stove to take over the stirring and keep the egg noodles from browning.  My grandmother taught that the rice was ready to add broth when it appeared clear and slightly "pink."

This is a horrid recipe that I would put in  the league that  includes phrases such as "cook until done", "add seasoning for flavor", or the ever popular "cook onions until caramelized, five minutes". If people are having difficulty with rice then most likely their issue is amount of water they are starting with. Having a variable amount of water in a recipe for every type of rice on earth is not helpful, nor is cooking by ear.

I really appreciate Sohla-El-Waylly ‘s rice advice and made the butter pilaf last night. As a rice obsessed person (I started making rice by myself at age 5) I was skeptical to give up my beloved rice cooker. I’m VERY particular about cooking rice since I eat it almost every day. I stepped out of my comfort zone and I’m very happy I did! Goodbye rice cooker, hello All Clad stainless pot!
Perfect rice! Followed recipe exactly; served with Shrimp Etouffee and green salad
If making rice leftover  porridge with stock, add peas, pecorino, pepper, lemon zest and juice.
Only if you like peas, pecorino, pepper and lemon.
I made this tonight and it was delicious. It made a lot so next time I would halve the recipe.
How much water?
The recipe is missing the amount of water (or broth) to add when cooking the rice.
"Add the water". How much water?
Re: amount of water. Read the recipe!
For the dairy averse, would olive oil work?

I prefer adding olive oil to the water instead of butter. I bring the covered water to a boil, & add the rice. I recover & drop heat to low & cook until all the water is evaporated. Never had a problem with watery rice or it being undercooked.
Step 3 "add the water".  Is it me or am I missing how much water to add?
I just love these vids, Sohla! Love love love.
I followed the directions exactly.  No better than when I don’t rinse.  Even the butter didn’t seem to add anything

Delicious.  I followed the recipe but had a 16 oz bag of frozen mixed veggies and it was perfect.  The addition of ketchup at the end seals the deal.  I'll be making this on repeat.  I have a family of four and it was the perfect amount with enough leftovers for a couple of servings (lunch tomorrow, yum!!!).

NEVER rinse cooked pasta! OMG, I can't believe people do this. 

First, it's unnecessary: it's just been in boiling water; why are you rinsing water with water?

Second, you're rinsing off the starch, which is an essential pasta ingredient, not only for taste, but so that any sauce or condiment you put on it will adhere to it. 

And never just put cooked pasta aside with no oil on it, or keep it separate from whatever sauce you're using. It will turn into a gloppy, gluey mess.

Rinsing the cooked noodles is a normal step in preparing Chinese noodle dishes, where you *don't* want the starch from the noodles to gum up the dish and you *do* want the noodles to be cool before adding to the wok/pan for later stir-fry.
@Lisa Simeone that’s a very one-sided view.  Different people and cultures cook noodle dishes differently.   Remember from where pasta originates, it wasn’t italian.
Rinsing pasta can be appropriate under certain conditions. In this recipe, the pasta is rinsed to cool it down and stop it from cooking further.

The pasta is still cooking when you turn off the heat, when you carry it to the sink to dump it into the colander, when you set it down afterwards. So just do all of that a little sooner. I still say never rinse pasta.

The choice to rinse or not rinse your pasta depends on your ultimate goal. If your goal is to help emulsify any fats into a sauce, then, yes, do not rinse your pasta. Leave it wet, even reserving the starchy water to aid in the formation of the sauce.

If your goal is to substitute pantry-staple spaghetti for fresh lo-men noodles, then you should rinse the pasta. This dish should be loose, the noodles separate.

yum my Bengali mom makes this too! she never rinses the noodles. she also sautés some green chilies with the ginger and garlic, and also adds cut and lightly steamed potatoes with the other veggies. mushrooms taste so good in this!
Sohla!  This was/is fabulous!  Only change I made was using Heinz Chili Sauce instead of Ketchup.  Keep these old family recipe coming!!
This is a great recipe! How rare to come across this kind of fusion to find it so delicious and adaptable. Add a jalapeño, chopped celery, sliced mushrooms. Don’t stint on the ketchup!

Expanded the content with 16 oz spaghetti, 2 lbs of chicken thighs and a 28 oz bag of frozen mixed vegetables. Also sauteed a large spanish onion, 8 cloves of garlic and one third of a cup of finely minced fresh ginger. No honey or ketchup. Added chili crunch at the end. This fed four very hungry people with seconds for all.

Yes. You DO want to rinse the spaghetti after it is cooked to make this dish. For folks who say "never" do this, please watch the video of Sohla making this. You'll find it item 3 in the 4 dish demo (@ 15:30). My 100% Italian heritage hubby would usually be in the "never" camp but for a dish like this, even he drops the objection. My brother who loves Asian flavors and also ketchup would "thumbs up" this combo. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9JhOI1vLHSo&t=959s
I made this with olive oil and the finished product was very tasty but too oily.  I will make this again but will cut the oil slightly or try a different fat.
Used a bag of rainbow slaw and a bag of extra fine egg noodles
This was great and very cost effective. Will try this with rice next time.

I thought this was just ok. I like very flavorful dishes and had to amp up the flavors in this dish with a bit of chile crisp, sesame oil, and more soy sauce and rice vinegar. Probably won’t make again.
Added some hoison sauce and sweet chili sauce and it was very good. No other subs
I saw this on the cbs morning show and I was excited to try it and i loved how easy it was to make and how tasty it was.  Definitely will be on my go to last minute dinner recipe.
Reminds me of growing up eating Cocola noodles in Bangladesh in the evenings for nasta. Definitely a nostalgia bomb flavor wise!
What a great dish to add to the weeknight cooking list.  Made as directed - ketchup at the end made the dish!  Excited to eat the leftovers for lunch tomorrow.

I really appreciated that this was relatively inexpensive to put together.  I followed the ingredient list and instructions as written. It was a filling meal that took me about 40 minutes to get to the table. My impression was that this was a wonderful dish for children. I striped it with ketchup as pictured and the hit of sweet tomatoey-ness definitely helped. If I made it again, I’d double the aromatics and the sauce ingredients and also add some chili garlic oil or chili crisp with the final mix. I served this with a fresh fruit salad and warm bakery rolls. We all felt healthy and full after dinner; happily, it was immediately followed by chocolate cake to negate that effect.
This was so good. The recipe was written in a very genuine way. It tasted great, also after microwaving it after the fridge. Wish there were more recipes like this. Ordinary people stuff.
Ok so I've accidently made a dish just like this , exceptions being no ginger,sesame or catsup,it is regular now.I will add the missing ingredients called for in this version next time,
Easy to tweak to liking
Do the frozen vegetables cook in 3 to 5 minutes that is specified here? I assume not but of course I could be wrong.

In all the “To Rinse Or Not To Rinse” back and forth, why did no one mention the assault rinsing pasta is to drain lines?  Scoop the pasta into an ice bath if you want to cool it down.  But you should never throw that starchy muck down your drains.
@Robert Keilitz that muck is going down the pipes one way or another.

This was delicious! Big hit with the kids. I had pre-cut chicken breast for stir frying so I tossed that with salt, pepper, and cornstarch before stir frying. Otherwise followed exactly except I used a half bag of coleslaw mix and some frozen corn for the veggies. And added ketchup with the other sauce ingredients. Drizzled with a little sesame oil. So good!
Skip the ketchup and grab the sriracha. Jackpot.
This is very good and easy but the dish needed more sauce.  Maybe my problem was using a thin pasta, as this is what I had.  Next time I will try regular spaghetti and increase the sauce by half.

I recommend watching the video. It answers the questions that are here and likely will be here, plus it's entertaining.
- there's a ton of sugar in these recipes, that's the point
- use whatever mix-ins you want
- imitation vanilla is used with the other extracts to get "that nostalgic packaged cookie vibe"
- rolling the dough in mix-ins yields prettier cookies
- the dough is formulated around being mixed and baked. Making the dough ahead is likely to dry it out and yield less chewy cookies
Made these with weed butter. Got pretty high so I liked them.

No imitation vanilla in this household and no plans to buy…would one substitute with the same quantity of pure vanilla extract…or reduce it a bit?  Asking for adult kids who love anything sprinkles. Thanks.

I found this, hope it’s helpful:
Imitation Vanilla Substitutes - Use 1 teaspoon of vanilla extract for every 2 teaspoons of imitation vanilla

To substitute use half of the pure vanilla extract
@Christine B. Yes, ew, no to imitation vanilla, I was thinking when I make it I have vanilla sugar (Heilala is the brand, very good) and I’d use that for the sugar and then the pure vanilla, etc.
Watch the video -Sohla is so lovable and funny. My favorite line: (reference to her husband who doesn't like these cookies) "He doesn't understand joy". Can't wait to bake these, joyfully!!
@Hillary from Durham great video. She is so funny
@Hillary from Durham same here! She’s so funny and fun!

Be careful with the timing!  I made the full-size cookies with a number 40 scoop, and they only took 12 minutes to cook. The ones that I baked for 16 minutes came out brown, crisp and almost too hard to bite. Anyway, so easy to make I made another batch. I mixed up the second recipe in my stand mixer with the paddle, on low. Easier than by hand.

Grandkids were over last night, and they thought they were great. Ten seconds in the microwave before eating will also soften these up.
I am obsessed with these!!! Fun to make and fun to eat. The perfect cookie. Mine took only 12 minutes to bake, but they might have been on the smaller side.
Wondering if these cookies can be successfully frozen?
I froze a few as a test and it was a success! Once fully thawed cookies were still wonderful! They didn't lose their chewy/ crunchy texture.

My cookies were perfectly golden after an 11.5 minute bake.  To speed the shaping process I popped the cookie batter from the scoop, rolled the dome portion in the m&m and sprinkle mixture (leaving the bottom of the cookie plain) then placed and pressed on the sheet pan. I omitted the almond extract due to a tree nut allergy.  The cookies were chewy when warm then light and quite crisp upon cooling.

Made these today but didn’t have imitation vanilla at home. I upped the real vanilla to 2 teaspoons and the cookies were delicious. I thought 12 minutes might be too long so I sat and watched them. It was spot on. I weighed each one to make sure they’d bake evenly. If yours are smaller or larger than 35 grams you’ll need to adjust the cooking time.
Thank you! I don't have a scoop but wanted to make them perfect. This helps.

these are PERFECT!! i used christmas sprinkles and holiday mini m&ms- they are so cute and festive. DO NOT cut the sugar content in half like some of these fools!! if you are finding the recipe to be too sweet- use a baking scale. i measured 3/4 cup granulated sugar but when i weighed it, it was 30g too heavy! so i adjusted to 150g. suggested baking time is far too long- we did 10 min and ended up with 10 giant chewy cookies. perfect with a glass of COW’S milk per sohla’s tip :)

i will not add the sprinkles to the dough next time around . .   they bleed color and make the finished cookie a tad ugly ( unlike those pictured ) . . . just gonna use them on the tops 

will cut back a bit on the salt, and up the flour by a couple T.
Don’t bother with all of the comments saying 350.  the cookie wants to bake hot and fast and that low temp makes it too cool and you will end up with a crunchy cookie in the end. 12 minutes at 375.
Erin is correct!!
I had difficulty getting the toppings rolled onto the cookie dough. Too dry! I read all the comments and watched the video but no other bakers commented on this issue.
I sprinkled the mini m&ms on top to get them to show.   the sprinkles stuck well for me
Cookies were too salty and ended up very hard, wouldn’t make again

Mine were perfectly salted with Diamond brand kosher salt and still had great chewiness with about 10-11 minutes in the oven at 375. I wonder if you used something other than diamond kosher which might explain the saltiness?

I love this recipe and am with the other cooks who called it "the perfect cookie." I'm a horrible baker, but these made me feel and look like a star! And I LOVE that the dough doesn't have to chill before baking. So fast!! I didn't have imitation vanilla on hand, so I used vanilla paste in its place. Don't skip the final roll in the sprinkles before baking. My boyfriend wanted some with fewer sprinkles, so he made me do some without the roll. They look so sad and naked.
Under cook these slightly! I didn’t let them get a golden edge and they were still a little crunchier than I would have liked and would have benefited from a minute less in the oven.

As others have noted, the suggested baking time was far too long. I set for the minimum of 14 minutes but should have checked sooner - the first batch was quite burned and crisp. I froze the rest of the dough and will check after 10 minutes when I got to bake those.

So fun for Pride month! Kids loved them. I see any instruction to melt butter as a requirement to brown it.  I did that here and 1. I came up short 2 cookies doing everything by weight; 2. 14 mins was way too much baking time; 3. Cookies were delicious!
