I let my batter sit, per the instructions, and had no problem with sprinkle color bleeding. I used Wilton Sprinkled/Colored Jimmies.
If ever there was a school bake sale recipe, this is it. Kids always pick this over all else.
This was a great option for getting rid of a lot of my kid's Halloween candy! The video I'd very helpful.  These cookies are meant to be extremely sweet.
My batch were hard at 14 minutes. I wish have followed reviews that said 12 but I was distracted……following the recipe.
I made exactly as written for a pride party. I was worried they would taste like food coloring and canuba wax but people inhaled them! Definitely a crowd pleaser
These are just plain amazing! mine came out a bit to crunchy after 12 minutes, so I would bake for a little less than that.

My changes (which I was pleased with):
-Sugar cut in half
-Used table salt, cut in half since it’s lower volume than kosher
-Also baked at 350 for 12-14 minutes.

I had trouble rolling the dough into balls, and then wasnt able to roll them in the candy because they were too dry. I watched the video and didn't rest the dough - I may try weighing the flour next time, maybe I had too much?
I had this same problem and added an extra egg the second time - worked perfectly.

This is my favorite kind of cookie, crispy on the edges, chewy in the middle, made in one bowl, and endlessly customizable. Per other's recommendations, I cut the salt by a bit since I wasn't using diamond crystal, and baked the cookies for 11-12 minutes. I also made mine slightly larger at 45g each.
My cookies were delish but too crispy.  Did that happen to anyone else?  I want to make these again, so I want to learn what changes to make.  Thx!
@Hollymine too and I was disappointed by that. I think creaming the butter & sugar til fluffy, rather than letting it sit for 30 and stirring twice, might be the key to softer cookies.
@Holly I should have trusted my instincts and baking experience to pull these cookies at 10 minutes. They came out crispier than I wanted, not terrible, but not the chewy cookies I was expecting.

The Lisa Frank of cookies! These were the number one pick of all the cookies I made. Follow the instructions  as written, make them all the same size and don’t overtake.  They are the perfect blend of chewy, crunchy, colorful sweetness.
I can not wait to try these. They will add a perfect pop of color to our cookie boxes this year. Thank you Sohla!
For those of us with an allergy to almonds…should we substitute or just skip?
You should know about imitation extracts if you have nut allergies! Of course, do your research before ingesting anything you're potentially allergic to.

Ememem - My partner has a nut allergy and I’ve found the McCormick Culinary imitation almond extract to work really well in baked goods. It gives that wonderful amaretto flavor I missed all the years I didn’t think to look for imitation.
There is no cookie more PARTY than these! So pretty, fun, and delicious. I can’t wait to make them again. I needed to adjust the baking time to 10 mins— mine browned in that quickly.
I love that--'there is no cookie more PARTY than these!--tells me all I need to know about these little gems.
Where should I look for "large" rainbow sprinkles? Is there a recommended brand?
A sugar bomb, as advertised. So delicious! My cookies took about 12-14 minutes. Huge payoff for minimal work and mess.

Might have been my oven, but 14 minutes was way too long to cook these.  I made other recipes and the timing was right, just not this one. I was definitely in the 9-10 minute range to avoid hard and overdone cookies.  Maybe try a test one or two before a full batch? When i worked out the right timing, they were delicious.
Followed the recco for 350 oven at ten minutes… got the perfect, soft, chewy cookie. Definitely do the 30 minute rest, too!

Could I brown the butter or would that affect the outcome? 
These cookies are very good, but they need that something extra. As written I would give them a 4/5. 
 I weighed all ingredients, opted not to do the 30 min rest and baked for 10-12 minutes.

Imitation vanilla is both more economical and far more sustainable/environmentally friendly, and in a recipe like this, the lightly artificial flavor is the entire point. Save the real stuff for custards and sauces only and make the world a better place while you're at it.

Don't make the mistake I made with the "let this mixture sit for 30 minutes" step! That's at **room temperature**, not in the fridge. You will be sad if you put it in the fridge. You will be more satisfied if you leave it on the counter.

Made by my adult daughter.  She reported that the dough was crumbly and she needed to add more butter to form cookies.  Also, they hardened prematurely.  (Followed recipe.) She suggested it might be better to experiment with adding sprinkles and m&ms to a favorite sugar cookie recipe instead. Won’t make again.

Thank you for mentioning vegan butter as an alternative in the recipe. We are not a vegan household, but we are strictly dairy free due to an allergy. A note to others who might be making these cookies for loved ones in the same boat: watch your mix-ins! M&Ms aren't vegan and contain milk.

These cookies are easily over-baked! The problem with the direction about how long to bake them is that the dough has a deep colored tone to it before you bake it, so looking for a "barely golden edge" is futile -- the dough isn't pale enough to show a golden edge. The directions should say: Bake for 14-15 minutes (I did 16 and it was TOO long); the cookies won't change color but will have a pliable edge when you take them out. I sadly over-baked mine.

9 mins in the oven and 1-2 on the cookie sheet before a cooling rack gave me the chewy cookie I wanted! Any more made the cookies super hard (I think the cookie itself doesn’t have a lot of fat and more sugar). If I were to make them again, I’d also focus less on the mix ins and make sure my dough balls were completely rolled in the candy, less sickly sweet and get the texture and look!
Kids are very excited about these, but I did have to take them out at 10 minutes and would probably cut the salt a bit- I'm a salt wimp, but it is a great, super fun recipe :)

-Mine baked for 11 minutes. MAYBE even 10, at 12 they were BURNT
-i added pretzels in addition to MnMs/sprinkles, worked really well
You must watch the video. So entertaining and really helped me understand the steps of the recipe and made me buy the app for a year lol. We ate these cookies up in 24 short hours!
The cookies surely will last longer than 3 days!
I added some chopped mini pretzels because I was craving a little more salt and crunch. So good.
Used a sprinkle mix with larger shapes - big pools of melty sugar once baked. Stick with the waxy, rod shaped sprinkles.

I also found the mix quite dry and I had a hard time rolling into balls as they came apart.  Just scooped and flattened.  Also shortened the cooking time.  Omitted the almond extract due to allergies but upped the vanilla.  Everyone loved them.

Well, too salty and too cake like. Let me stress, I expected that with baking powder in the dough, they would be more cakey than crisp.  Twelve minutes baking time was pretty spot on but again...these were cakey (not chewy and yes I let the dough rest for half an hour, stirring twice)...and my God salty. An 18 year old cookie expert in my home tasted one and said to my husband "why are these cookies so salty?".
Glad to see it wasn't just me thinking that they are too salty. If I make again I will definately cut the salt in half!
These were fun to make and a festive treat for a solstice party, but ultimately were too sweet for my taste. Did have nice chew, though. Be careful not to over bake!

I am the type of person that the recipe writer talks about, who always cuts sugar in half in recipes. I have preschooler who loves rainbow everything and sprinkles but she doesn’t need a giant amount of sugar. I did that here and don’t regret it at all! Sweetness level just right considering the outside is coated with more sugar. I used extra crunchy sprinkles and it tasted like the entire outside was a candy shell. Heartily recommend cutting sugar in half to all who are debating it!
delicious cookies!! They make the most delectable ice cream sandwiches ever!! Used choc chip cookie dough ice cream and I was in Heaven!!!

These were tasty and easy to make. I took the advice from other commenters and baked them for 12 minutes and it was good but I'll probably try 11 minutes next time because they are still a bit on the crunchier side.

These were highly disappointing. Baked them for a party and felt I had to bake something else last minute to bring instead. While they look colorful and cute, they are hard as rocks, even when shortening the cooking time. Followed the recipe/video to a T but just lacking. Huge bummer.

These cookies were delicious on day 1, but did not keep well unfortunately. By day 3 they were hard and inedible, I tossed the rest. I would only make them if you can make them fresh and know the whole batch will be eaten!

As Sohla says in the video, these are best eaten the same day. If you want to keep them past that, she counsels, store them in the freezer and temper them in the oven just before eating.
BTW, her videos are a treat. I watch them even when I'm not that interested in the recipe.

With the first batch I followed the recipe and was having a really tough time mixing in the sprinkles and m&ms. The dough got tough and the cookies turn out pretty hard…they were also a bit salty. The second batch I halved the salt and did 1 and 1/3 cup flour instead of 1 and 2/3 cup. The dough was more buttery and everything mixed in and stuck on much better. The cookies were chewy and delicious. Second times a charm!

Now, that's a cookie! Sweeter than sweet. I'm sure kids would love these; I know I do, as I really like sweet cookies. It takes a lot of sprinkles to coat the outsides and I ran out of tiny M&Ms, but these are really great. Make them the way Sohla says; don't try to make them "healthy".

major fail for me. maybe I misunderstood the directions, but I rolled the discs on all sides in the m&m sprinkle mixture, and all of the m&ms that were on the bottom of the disc touching the pan burned to a crisp. I even baked the cookies for less time than stated and the cookies were incinerated on the bottom and had to toss them all. a shame because they look so pretty in the picture :(

I thought I followed the directions to a tee and the cookies came out as hard a rock- but obviously I must have overcooked them ( and if so only by 3 to 4 minutes). Way too much sugar for me. I’m sure sea gulls at beach will enjoy them. Will stick to my tried and true recipes handed down in my family.

Well at the last minute I relented on my vow to use 1/2 sugar and i regret. Kids hopefully like this (little,  sweet-toothed kids) but I found them too sweet as is. Used dark brown and maybe would’ve been less cloyingly so with light (also prettier). 
I had to use more than double the sprinkles and m&ms to get coated. Maybe that didn’t help.. 
so much sugar couldn’t even taste the imitation vanilla!
Baked 10, rested 2 on sheet - perfect consistency!

Did anyone try these without the almond extract or with a different extract. Im sure they are still delicious but was curious about other bakers experience. I have a couple of friends with nut allergies.

I have a severe nut allergy to all nuts and I used Maraschino cherry liquid, which was recommended as a substitution. I'm also allergic to stone fruits (cherries), but when cooked alters the pectin or something and I can handle it fine.

These cookies are extraordinarily as advertised... a true candied sugary confection and just as stated pure "joy" to make and eat!

This is a good recipe, in concept, but I think it needs some adjusting. I had to add a decent amount more flour than the 200g to get the dough to a state it could be portioned and rolled. Also, the cookies were well done (but not burned) after the 14 minute minimum for baking.
Gonna try again soon, addressing those issues

I had made the required amount of everything, and the cookies were put on two trays. The first tray came out amazingly! But as I was waiting for the first batch to come out, I let the second batch just sit there, for she said “Do NOT put cookies in for cooling!” I needed to put the second batch in the fridge, for the butter had melted too much in the second batch, and got burned. I think the recipe needs to be tweeked

Made these tonight with 4 T of "Just Egg" instead of an egg. Next time would use just 3 T; I think the texture was a little too runny. I accidentally added an extra 1/4 t of salt so they were definitely salty, but in the finished version, kind of addictive. I ended up cooking mine for just 7 minutes-- any longer and they were too dark.

I made these the night before my kiddo's birthday and sent him to school with the batch. These are officially my go-to birthday cookies! Absolutely delicious with tiny celebrations in each bite! I found sprinkles with hearts and stars and it made them even more fun.
I thought the batter was really dry.  It was falling apart when I tried to roll them.  Also, I baked the first batch according to the recipe and they were almost burnt.

I unfortunately did not like these cookies. They weren't overtly sweet (which I was surprised) but I baked them at the minimum time range and they cooled down hard as crackers, lol. My oven was at temperature, maybe running a little lower. They do sound interesting, though! They looked great in the video. I like that this recipe is not trying to be over-the-top, and I enjoy the idea of a mix-and-bake cookie.

I have three daughters and they all love this recipe
its so easy that they can do it themselves!
I love how its not a TOO sweet depending on your mood 
over all its a 10 out of 10
These are delicious. I did… lower the sugar amounts (don’t hate me). Lowered to 130g and 90g. Freeze beautifully, defrost in 20 minutes. Make it!!!
This is a wonderful recipe to make with a group of kids. I scooped the dough, they rolled the balls and rolled in sprinkles. So much fun!  Cookies are sweet and perfect.

Definitely put the batter in the refrigerator for a bit to firm it up before molding and rolling in the sprinkles. This keeps the cookies from spreading out too much when cooking and they form neat circles.

I made a mash up of these with Yossy Arefi’s malted sugar cookies and they were amazing.  Basically used the malted sugar cookies as the base (using half brown and Half white sugar) and the extracts and mix ins from this recipe. Yum! Baked 10-12 min at 350.

Love these fun cookies. Yes, they are very sweet, but I would think that would be obvious from the sprinkles & M&Ms. If you want some sort of fancy, nuanced, sweet/savory cookie, find another recipe.
     These are EASY, fast, and yummy! Followed the recipe exactly, including the 30 min suggested rest time (before adding flour). I agree with everyone else about the cook time - I baked my first batch for 14 mins (way too long). Second tray baked for about 11 mins & were perfect!
I watched the video, read the comments & followed the recipe & these cookies turned out perfectly! I made 8 dozen for a cookie exchange. They’re fun, colorful, tasty & easy!

Fun, festive, and super easy to make! I ran out of time to prepare something fussier for our neighborhood cookie exchange so I made these and they were a hit. Love the rolling step since it makes much prettier cookies!

These were the worst cookies I ever made.  They are teeth curdling sweet with a bitter chemical aftertaste, presumably from the imitation vanilla extract.  No matter how cute they are, I found them inedible.

These cookies make the best ice cream sandwiches!! I use cookie dough ice cream and people go nuts! 
They also freeze exceptionally well.

I am not clear on why there are 2 bacthes of M&M's.  
1/2 cup of M&M's left intact for the batter, then added 1/4 cup M&M's crushed with sprinkles to edcorate cookies before baking,  Di I get that right??
Made exactly as directed without the 30 min wait time. They were absolutely perfect. If they’re misshapen it’s super easy to shape them when they come out. A keeper!

For being so sweet, these are actually pretty good! My 4-yo daughter picked this recipe out and my wife and I were pleasantly surprised. 

Only change I’d suggest is baking at 350 for 14 mins. The video claims that if you like them soft bake them as the recipe calls for, but our oven made them too crispy to eat at 16mins@375. Other than that, great and would do again
I rested the batter for 30 minutes as recommended and it was so loose and sticky that it was difficult to scoop and roll in the toppings.
Delicious! I baked them for 12 on the second batch, 14 made them a little too hard for me.
O.M.G. - the moment I saw the picture I knew I had to have these. Just baked two batches because we were so excited to get a taste. Will these keep until Christmas Day if we pop them in the freezer?
She states in the video that they should be frozen if you aren’t eating them within a few days.

Someone asked about freezing these. I freeze everything and these seem to take it quite well.
They are not my favorite but I’m expecting the kids will love them.
They WERE super easy and quick to make and add really fun color. For Christmas, next time I’d just use red and green sprinkles.

375 in oven browned the cookies far too much by minute 11. Lowered the temp to 350 and they still browned quite a bit by minute 14.  Not sure what temp they should cook at and for how long.. These are tasty, but just never felt I got the consistency of the final product right for the recipe. Not sure I would make these again.

I baked mine for 15 minutes and when they cooled for a good 20 minutes they got really hard. Would it be better to reduce cooking time or adjust the butter to flour ratio? I felt my dough was not ‘soft’ so perhaps that was my issue. Any suggestions welcome because the flavors are great.
Hi! Did you let the dough rest for 30 mins as suggested? I did and I thought the texture was excellent. I wonder if that might make a difference for you.
Wondering if this could be made with pure butter (cream and salt) Unsalted butter has undefined “natural flavors” added that I can’t eat. Thanks
I almost always bake with salted butter because it's what I keep on hand and I just like it better. I made these with salted butter and just decreased the salt to a pinch. They were great.

These were quick and easy in addition to being delicious. Great party cookie; they were a big hit. I made them as instructed but didn't have imitation vanilla so just used more pure vanilla. I don't love almond extract so debated omitting but was glad I didn't. It adds a little something extra but they don't taste "almond-y". No issues with the rainbow sprinkles bleeding. Will make again this season for sure!

Followed directions with exception of using all natural vanilla, as that is what I had.  Fourteen minutes and they were ready at 375.  I just returned from our local rec center potluck and they were a big hit.  I saw one man with three in his hand!
Will make again to give out to neighbors. Thanks for a fun and tasty cookie.

These cookies are everything Sohla promised. Sugarbombs, nostalgic and fun to make. I think the less time in the oven, the better the chew of the cookie is (as others stated they can become hard fast!) 9 minutes in, with 2 mins on the sheet was good. If you are highly sensitive to the sugar content, I would do less mix ins and focus on coating the entire ball with candy. Makes for great texture, look without sacrificing the dough’s intention.
Followed the recipe to a T and they were over baked at 14 mins. Probably should’ve done 12 mins.

These cookies were delicious, but ended up VERY flat. I recommend upping the flour until it yields a texture that is more dough-like. The flavor is perfect however. If you don’t have imitation vanilla, just make do with the recommended around of real vanilla. Any mix-ins work well!

Christina Tosi of Milk Bar fame always uses clear vanilla in her bakes. It gives the the flavor of a real bakery made product.  I keep my Madagascar vanilla for recipes where vanilla should really shine. These are super fun and festive!

Agree with others—14-16 minutes is too long to achieve the pale color in the photo. I used the 35-gram weight to portion and 12 minutes resulted in browned crispy cookies (overdone) with 10 minutes being about right.

OMG I love these exactly as they are! I over baked them a bit the first time. We suffered the pain of eating them immediately and I’m baking another batch today! 
Thank you for my new favorite I-Want-a-Cookie-Right-NOW recipe. Forget Christmas - I’ll be baking these all year!

Made this as written — grateful for a use for the imitation vanilla my husband bought by accident some time ago. Delicious and fun! Maybe I’m too generous, but I ended up using twice as many mini M&Ms as called for during the rolling phase. Thirteen minutes of bake time worked for my cookie scoop.
These cookies are delicious and fun to make. I didn't have an imitation vanilla,  so I just left it out. Crispy and chewy- the perfect cookie!

Wow!  This cookie base is outstanding!  I had so much fun making it and my teenage and her friends went wild!  What I like best about this cookie recipe is that you can incorporate many different combinations, the “flair “ (mm/sprinkles) could be anything.  Next time I’m going to let my imagination run wild!   Thank you NYT- making this recipe truly brought joy into my world and put smiles on my other people’s faces!

As many have said, Sohla explains all in the NYT Cooking YouTube video. I was initially put off by the imitation vanilla and multiple extracts but there are reasons for each based on the flavours she was looking to recreate. Just give it a try. I made these with Smarties (for non-Canadians, I don't mean the tiny sour sugar disks no one likes, but a chocolate candy very similar to M&Ms). They are so fun. They spread a lot so definitely be mindful of spacing on the pans.

I followed the instructions exactly (including weighing each cookie before rolling in sprinkles!) and I have a thermometer for my oven, and I definitely overdid the first batch at 14 min, I found it to be closer to 12 min.
Do not over bake—they won’t look done but they are. These babies spread out, so give ‘em some room on the baking sheet.
The commenter who suggested freezing the rolled balls and then baking was spot,on. Far less fussy. I used ancho chili powder rather than cayenne and they were great.
These cookies are easy to make, fun festive cookies that are delicious.

I was disappointed with these. Either the bake time is too long or the temp is too high, or both! 
They weren’t really worth it to me to try again though because I’m not a huge fan of cookies with candy in them anyway. 
But you’ve been warned- watch them closely in the oven and take them out before you think they’re done. They brown further while cooling which means they won’t be as soft. This isn’t the kind of cookie you want to be hard and crunchy.

Only altitude adjustments (we live at 7500’) was to skimp on baking powder and baking soda.  Used stand mixer with paddle attachment on low (thank you, Meg Greer).  12 min at 375 in convection oven, rotating at 6 min.  Perfect.

The 69 and 70 year olds who have sampled these for me absolutely love them. Sized down to 25 grams for a better holiday box size. When rolling and dunking in sprinkles, the cookie feels exactly like a scallop. And they’re so FUN and FESTIVE!
Fun for A but taste like candy

My kids went absolutely wild for these and kept saying things like “I’m in candy heaven!” They can’t wait to make them again. When we do, I’ll probably add another tablespoon or two of flour because they were thinner than I was expecting. Definitely keep an eye on these as they can get overbaked quickly. .
These were pretty and tasted good, but were way too hard. I did let them sit for a bit to let the sugar dissolve, but there was still zero chewiness. Don’t think I’ll make them again.

These are delicious cookies - a sweet treat. And gorgeous. If dough gets too warm they’ll spread so be aware. They are also very large - visually a delight but practically may want to make them smaller and adjust cooking time.

These cookies were a huge hit, even when made vegan and gluten free with King Arthur GF flour, Just Egg and Imperial veg. oil baking sticks. I did cut 1/4 cup of granulated sugar and skipped the imitation vanilla (didn't have any). Even the non-vegan and gluten loving friends and family gobbled these up.
Hi! what did you substitute for the m&m’s? Can’t find vegan minis.

Watch the video, it's cute and funny! :D I love that these are named Lisa Frank cookies. You can totally get away with regular M&Ms, maybe even just roll them in the rainbow sprinkles but do rainbow sprinkles and regular M&Ms inside, that's what I did! Turned out great and were a family hit.

I baked these with red and green M&Ms and similar sprinkles for a Christmas theme. I baked my first dozen for 15 minutes and found them overdone. I followed other commenter recommendations for the 2nd dozen and baked them for 12 minutes. Much better!

I made these for my Christmas cookie boxes for my friends with kids who complain my cookies are too adult for them.     I added about 3/4 of Cristina Tosi's corn flake crunch and it gave a nice salty crunch that breaks up the sweetness a bit.
After carefully weighing each dough disk to 35grams, the recipe yielded 20 cookies.

People have mentioned “the video” - there’s no link in the recipe, I guess that was when it was in a Times article?  I followed the directions as best as I could, although my cookies ended up ½ to ¾ “ thick.  Using two tablespoons per cookie, I got SIXTEEN, and they were huge, like 6” in diameter.  Next time, one tablespoon per cookie.  I also didn’t get the inference that sprinkles and m&m’s went on the top, it says to roll the cookies after you’ve made them discs.  Perfect kid cookie though!
Substituted the imitation vanilla with a little more normal vanilla and almond extract, turned out lovely. We also used gluten free cup for cup flour, and had lovely results.
I followed the recipe but took the advice of others and took them out around between 10-12 and they were perfectly chewy. Any longer and they would I’ll have been over done.

The video is very useful. My cookies only needed 12 minutes to bake & I worried that I had over baked them as they were more crunchy than chewy the first day. However, these cookies get better a few days later & are perfectly crispy on the edges & chewy in the center. I did not have problems with the sprinkles bleeding on the cookie.
The FUN factor in making these cookies is over the top!!

I made these with 2t real vanilla and no imitation extract.  Accidentally switched the sugar quantities so mine were brown sugar heavy.  Best baking outcome across 3 batches was 12 mins in oven, 2 mins on sheet, then to a rack.  I have a gas oven, for what it's worth.

They are really sweet and pretty one note but my Secret Santa (who is a self described maximalist) loved them.
I’m planning to make these with gluten free flour. Any tips?

Just baked these. They’re easy, fast, and fun. Make sure to give them the space on the cookie sheet the recipe suggests; they really do spread as they bake! Trust fingertip touch and appearance over time; my Thermador oven took 18 minutes for two sheets, and I rotated them on the shelf about 12 minutes in. And don’t skimp on the sprinkles to roll in. You will use and want the whole half cup for rolling with the mini M&M’s!

For
Anyone maybe wanting to know, I veganized these pretty successfully by just replacing the egg with a flax egg, the butter with vegan butter (as the recipe does allow), and adding to extra tablespoons of flour to compensate for the flax egg.

Also, why are we hating so much on imitation vanilla?  It’s fantastic.
I couldn't buy imitation vanilla in my large local grocery store.

Thank you thank you these cookies really deliver a delicious memory. Spot on with all the extracts combined to perfection. Kids love love love em. Crispy edges and softer centers with a sweet vanilla warmth in every bite. Nostalgia in a cookie form.

I was able to make these vegan by subbing the egg for Bob’s Red Mill Egg Replacer. They came out delicious! 

I did not chill the butter/sugar mixture for 30 minutes but the cookies still came out chewy. I think I’ll try chilling the mixture next time to see how they compare.

I agree with other commenters: bake for 11-12 minutes. That’s the sweet spot for getting perfectly baked cookies.
In the video, Sohla says to pop it in the fridge for an hour or two before forming the balls, but the recipe omits this step. Any thoughts/helpful experience on whether or not to chill this cookie?
Don't put it in the fridge. She says that will dry it out.
Thank you McKlem. I did end up chilling them the first time because my dough was so crumbly before doing so, and because she mentioned it in the video. I didn't do it the second time around.
These were really fun to make, although they came out a little crisp. I baked them for 10 minutes, but I will probably cut that back next time. (And there will definitely be a next time!)
Great taste and look! If you’re not planning to do exactly 35 grams per cookie dough ball and closer to two tablespoons, you’ll need to double cooking time.

Made using 2-1/2 t real vanilla.   Needed more sprinkles & M&Ms for rolling the cookies.   Per other comments, 12-14 minutes cooking time is more than adequate.  I’d say remove the cookies before they start looking cooked because they get hard when cooled.   Wonder if this could use a bit more butter?
Love the fun look and delicious taste of these but also agree that the bake time should be 11-12 min. My batch baked to the full time was rock hard.

These were such a quick and easy recipe, I swapped out for king Arthur’s measure for measure gluten free blend and these were the first cookie recipe I have found that was still able to produce a flat, chewy/crisp cookie while using a gf blend! Perhaps 14 mins a bit long for a bottom line, I found that mine were ever so slightly over…. Great recipe that will probably become my standard now for a “chocolate chip/mixin” recipe

Be careful with the sprinkles that you use. Mine melted, ran off the cookie and blackened on contact with the cookie sheet. I learned that there are different types of sprinkles. Not all are meant for baking.
Because the butter is melted the dough is very warm when finished. It does need chilling. I needed a little more flour to make it firm enough to roll.

The recipe says to form the dough  into balls and flatten into disc's before coating them in the M&M and sprinkle mixture but it doesn't seem that Sohla does that in the video. I flattened mine and baked for 14 minutes. They were too crispy and not chewy. Will attempt again.

What a fun recipe and delicious! I couldn’t find mini M Ms so I used regular sized in red and green and got red and green jimmies. Very festive! As for the imitation vanilla naysayers, I paid $0.99 for a bottle in Target. Read about imitation vanilla sometime. It’s not as horrible as you may think. Highly recommend this recipe!

Our family loves these cookies. Our only changes are to skip the almond and imitation vanilla extracts, upping the regular vanilla to compensate, and baking on the shorter side like other commenters suggest. With an adult eye on the oven use, my kids can otherwise make these cookies themselves. That alone is worth an extra star. My kids love the video too and crack up at "he doesn't understand joy."
35 g is not the same as 2 Tbsp!! gotta bake them for like 5 min less than it says
This is also just a killer dough for if you want to make fast and easy chocolate chip cookies!! It's so versatile and delicious.

I baked them first with no mods except for time in oven (13 mins). I made a 2nd batch, this time I excluded the almond extract and replaced about half (60 grams) of the mini M&M's with Guittard Extra Semi Sweet chips (55%). I kept the sprinkles and imitation vanilla but I did not roll the cookies in extra M&M's or sprinkles. I reduced the temp and baked them at 350 for 15 mins. They turned out great! Still sweet but little more balanced. Not hard/crunchy but just the right crisp/chewy texture.

I have to preface this by saying cookies are not my forte.  Because of this, I watched the video.  I weighed my ingredients, and weighed the cookie balls.  After baking for 9 minutes they were still raw.  At 10 minutes they were brown, and I took them out. After cooling, they were as hard as a rock. I wouldn’t bother to make them again.

SO good. I made as written except didn’t have imitation vanilla so I upped the regular vanilla. They were easy, came out beautiful, and once cooled are pleasantly crispy (kind of like a Tate’s but a little bit chewier). Absolutely will be repeating this recipe and trying different mix-ins!

as usual, sohla does a brilliant job of walking us through her recipe.  i added less minis to the batter because i didn't have enough but didn't change one other thing about them.  it took a total of 12 minutes for me. they are gloriously playful and colorful and just the right foil for all the more sophisticated cookies out there. thank you, sohla.
These are RIDICULOUSLY GOOD. They are (mostly) going to my nearest neighbors in the goodie bag I do most Christmases.  IF we can stop ourselves from eating them. Thanks for the recipe.

The first time I made these my dough was very crumbly, even though I followed the recipe to the letter. It didn't look like the dough in the video, and I'm not sure why. These still were very tasty. The second time I added a second egg so the dough would be easier to work with and it worked perfectly and were delicious. Only thing I can think is perhaps the eggs I used were smaller than the ones Sohla used? Anyway,  I love these and will definitely make them again.
Great recipe! Instead of rolling the entire cookie in toppings, I just dipped the tops in the sprinkles and pressed 5 mini m&ms on top. Baked at 10 minutes for crisp and chewy perfection!
Delicious taste but I'm so grateful for the readers who mentioned that the baking time was closer to 12 minutes.  The cookies were a tad too dark after only 12 minutes.  Beware!!

These are a great, nostalgia-inducing cookie - which Sohla explains she was trying to accomplish. I think the almond extract is what brings that "artificial" box cake/cookies vibe - so don't skip that unless you need to. I think these may be my new go-to quick cookie as I usually have the ingredients and the bright colors make everyone excited to eat them. I used Great Value sprinkles and mini M&M's and had no color bleeding issues.
Agree with the need to reduce cook time. We added nuts after multiple suggestions by enthralled participants, and they were a great addition. Will be a fixture at gay pride next year.
Had to reduce the time on these in my oven to like 9-10 minutes even - but they were good and super fun- hoping I won't forget this next time!
I’m mad about how good these are. I did half mini m&ms and half chopped up Heath bar and it did not disappoint.
I hate that I made these cookies - they are so dang delicious & easy to make. I made them for the holidays and they were a total hit. No modifications needed, but also recommend watching the video.

After making these a second time-
I will take out at 9 min in a 350 convection oven. They will not look done on top but they are. Bottoms were brown.
Absolutely chill the dough about 30 min.I have never met a cookie dough that can’t be chilled or that chilling dried out. Wrap it in plastic wrap if you’re worried.
I did take out 1/4 c of sugar from the recipe with no affect on taste and far less spread that the first batch.
I weighed the flour the 2nd time and it was perfect. No extra needed.
Good and cute but very sweet! I need to bake a couple minutes less!

I love these cookies, but also had trouble with the baking time as others have mentioned too. I baked for 13 minutes and they turned out very crispy (think Tate’s). I will bake another batch and start checking at 11 minutes.

maybe I made then smaller than suggested but needed much less cooking time, 9 minutes tops.  otherwise great.








=

I love Sohla but the predominant flavor of these cookies is sweet. I couldn’t really detect much chocolate flavor from the mini M&Ms. If I did make these again I would use peanut butter M&Ms for flavor. 14 minutes at 375F was perfect.

3/4 tsp orange extract
Use tbsp scoop
Bake for 11 min

Yum!  Made 2x so far. Second time added mini-marshmallows and black pepper potato chips with the mini m&ms and rainbow sprinkles. Very fun combo!  Formed 36g flattened balls baked at 375 for 15 minutes.

Will it cookie cake? Yes! Made this dough exactly as instructed and pressed it into a standard 10 inch cake pan. I used a round of parchment and lots of butter so it wouldn't stick. I pressed extra sprinkles and m&ms into the top since I couldn't really roll it. Decorated with white icing. It's giving Great American Cookie Co. Took a while to bake (30 min or so?) and got a little over cooked at the edge while waiting for the center to finish up. Next time, I'll divide the dough into 2 pans.

This is the sixth cookie from cookie week that I’ve made.  I’m so glad at least one of those recipes is super quick and easy!  I read all the comments about the reduced bake time. You won’t believe it—mine only baked for TEN minutes! Go figure.  Just keep an eye on your batch and I’m sure they’ll be fine.

This cookie is divine. The only change I made was using pecans instead of sprinkles because I don’t like sprinkles. My cookies were perfect at 12 minutes. I just ate the first warm one that came out of the oven and I had to go to another room to keep from eating two or three more.  The saving grace of a cookie this sweet is that I can’t eat more than one or two at a time although they are addictive. The video was very helpful.
It should say that it should look like appel sauce not wet sand
Loved these! Used 1 tsp cake batter flavoring instead of the imitation vanilla and almond to give them a more birthday cake vibe.

I’ve already made a different variation of this recipe using Sohla’s cookbook (the corn chip/marshmallow/chocolate ones, also great!) but these really shined. Yeah, they’re super sweet, that’s the point! These taste like something I’d be served at a birthday party as a kid and I loved them. I only got 12 cookies out of the batter but they were pretty large. The only change I made was using my homemade vanilla extract for both extracts since I didn’t wanna buy imitation just for this one recipe.

I made these cookies exactly as the instructions and video describe and they were chewy and crunchy and beautiful and people couldn't believe I made them! One batch was a few minutes more done than the other and the slightly underdone cookies were my favorite. I served them the same day although the next day they were still chewy too.

I actually had a similar experience to many other reviewers. I baked the cookies for 16 minutes and they came out way too hard to bite into. I lowered the cooking time to 14 and they were a bit softer, but still had quite the chew to them. I don't know if I would make these again, but if I did I would try to lower the baking time to see if that would help.
I loved these! Crispy and colorful and baked up fast. Subbed out imitation vanilla bec I just couldn’t find it.

I used Almond flour in these to make them gluten free. I tried subbing 1 to 1, but the dough seemed a little soft, so i added more until it became more of a stiff cookie dough texture. I didn’t measure, but probably added another quarter cup or a bit more than that. Tasted great! Cookies probably a bit thinner than they otherwise would have been, but not a bad substitution.
i love the way you make this cookie with m&m's and sprinkles i just love it !!
Definitely need to chill for 30 minutes before forming the cookies or they will spread too much. Also, I baked for ten minutes and they were done. Any longer and they would be burnt.

I didn’t see & watch the video until I was too far in.  Wish I had seen it.  I did refrigerate the dough for a couple of hours and that wasn’t good.  Had to go down to 13 minutes on cooking time, and they were still too hard.  Still delicious though!!  This saved the day for my wife for her office cookie exchange.
super sweet- not surprised as the sugar content is crazy high- but a nice sense memory taste and for a holiday treat, why the heck not....had one with my morning coffee and do not feel worse for it
I thought it was really hard to add on the extra toppings, but it was overall a very simple recipe! They also taste delicous! I reccomend.
its hard 2 get the extra toppings on, but they taste delicious so its WORTH IT! I reccomend watching the video first. They are super easy overall, and I'd make them again.
11 minutes in a convection oven at 375 is perfect. Halve the amount of sprinkles to roll in. I had lots left over.
Too much salt, didn’t enjoy this recipe

Wonderfully sweet and delicious with such a nostalgic flavor and satisfyingly chewy texture! My first round of cookies burned very quickly so if your oven runs a little hot, I'd recommend lowering both the temperature and the baking time (mine only took 11-12 minutes at 360 but every oven is different).
375 for 13 minutes yielded a brittle over-cooked cookie in the first batch. Dropped to 350 for first 5 minutes then 375 (to get crisp edges) for 4 final minutes. Perfect.

This is the absolute best recipe for kids, and brings joy to them cooking. We've used any and every kind of mix-in and it always turns out perfectly. We don't use imitation vanilla and just go heavy on the real stuff. My daughter loves to pick out what goes in and at this point she probably could make these cookies herself. We love adding dried coconut! We use a stand mixer but that works just fine.
These were delicious
These were delicious. I did not use the clear vanilla, just added more pure extract. Do not over-bake unless you like really crunchy cookies. Everyone loved them.

I loved these cookies. Usually I’m a “not too sweet” person but these were so fun to make, look at and eat. And I love having this as a base recipe for spontaneous bakes bc you don’t need to wait for butter and eggs to reach room temp. I’d like to try it with salty mix-ins like pretzels and potato chips.

I made a batch of these yesterday. Agree with 11 minute cooking time. Because I've had mixed results making cookies with melted butter, I used half room temp and half melted (because I already had some at room temp). I decreased the granulated sugar by 1/4 cup, decreased Diamond salt to 1 scant tsp (only because I watch my sodium intake).
Additions: mini M&Ms, peanuts, chopped rolos, a handful of potato chips. I did not roll them in toppings. Appearance is fine and they are delicious!

Really loved this recipe. It wasn't a firm enough dough to roll into the sprinkles, but an ice-cream scoop then dip into the sprinkles worked fine. I feel like a touch more vanilla would really bring that flavour out a little more, but otherwise perfection. Such a fun biscuit to have for parties.

So...I had so many issues with this recipe. My batter looked nothing like the video, my cookies were too small and I used the wrong size scoop, and my toppings would not go on as easily as demonstrated. But, when I did get it right, the cookies had this lovely crisp edge, and were amazingly chewy in the middle. It took me a couple takes, but once you master the recipe, it is magical.
I actually couldn't *find* imitation vanilla at my local grocery, so used extra vanilla (2 t total), extra almond extract (1/2 t total), and rum extract (1/4 t).  Cookies were delicious.
I’m not sure if it was my fault, but I followed the recipe to a tee I think. But the dough came out very crumbly… I just added some milk to it and it cooked well though.
Used 2 tsp vanilla extract and omitted the other 2.  Excellent

Made this recipe exactly as written, and I weighed all the ingredients, but was disappointed in the results. I opted for dark brown sugar and unsalted butter (Tillamook brand.) My cookies spread thinner than pictured, turned out much darker, and were already overbaked at the suggested 14 minute mark. The sprinkles and M&Ms also sank into the dough during baking, dulling the colors.

I wouldn't make this again, but if I did, I'd use light brown sugar and check them at 10 minutes.
@Matthew S I used 1/2 light and 1/2 dark and cookies have no height to them. All my leavenings are working, so not sure why they’re so flat. They don’t look like the picture!

These cookies are so joyful! They are tasty and whimsical - and can't we all use more whimsy? I subbed in Christmas M&Ms and sprinkles to add to my holiday cookie collection and replaced almond extract with Fiori di Sicilia because it's delicious and what I had on hand.

This cookie has entered into my heavy rotation.  It’s 1) super easy without a mixer if you don’t have one and 2) super nostalgic with the sprinkles and clear vanilla extract evoking your childhood birthday cakes.    I’ve found I’ve needed slightly more sprinkles/m and m’s for rolling, maybe about 50g extra on both ingredients.
Be very, very careful when baking. I baked as directed on got browned, hard cookies. With the sprinkles especially they are very hard when overdone. They spread out quite a bit on the pan.
I was contemplating using peanut butter m&m minis has anyone ever tried those or similar?

These are great to make with kids. They love all the colors and you don’t have to be too precise when measuring all the fun add-ins. The kids loved eating them too ;-) Great recipe to teach/practice baking with the littles.

I’m giving these 4 stars instead of 5 because I have this underlying issue with sprinkles; like jello and Cool-whip, they aren’t really food. Neither is imitation vanilla, so I used double the real thing. But the cookies are good and the flavor is great.

Used King Arthur 1:1 GF flour.  Simple Truth sprinkles with natural dye (not quite as vibrant colors).  These came out very crisp but were well received.  Next year will make with red, green and white sprinkles!
Be careful not to use too much almond extract. I would even cut it in half. Overall, great texture and fun to make!
I make these for my Christmas cookie boxes every year and I add cashews along with gems(indian m&ms) and they are truly loved for their cute appearance and delicious taste.

Perhaps I have the palette of a 12 year old but these are the best cookies ever. Froze the baked batch and took them out one at a time to eat- still perfect once thawed, especially if thrown in the oven for a couple of minutes.
The dough was really dry for me, so I added a bit more melted butter. Still quite crumbly once I started rolling them in my hands, but they tasted great and were a big hit.

The first batch we baked was too crispy. In order to make these taste like the Insomnia Cookies confetti cookie, need to bake at 350 for only about 12 minutes. we left out the chocolate, again to try and re-create the Insomnia Cookies confetti cookie.  Turned out great.

omitted m&ms (didnt have any), upped the amount of regular vanilla by 1/2 teaspoon (2teaspoons total) using vanilla bean paste instead, and baked for 12 minutes as suggested in comments. they came out perfect and delicious!
They turn crispy when cool, not soft and chewy like I’d expected. Pretty basic. Not my favorite.

I made these with my 7 yr old. Honestly they aren’t really my thing but I respect them for what they are. They tastes like a sugar fueled, 90’s Lisa Frank fever dream. My son was delighted. They did spread a lot in the oven and went from being almost done to almost too done very quickly. There was a nice chew when they were still warm but as they cooled they were crisp all the way through. If it were up to me I probably wouldn’t make them again, but I’m sure that my son will insist.
Way too hot and for too long. 190° for 14 mins resulted in some burnt/all hard cookies. 190° for 12mins was better, and next time I'll try 180° for 12 mins. Tasted and looked great, otherwise.

I made this for lunch today and enjoyed it! It’s easy and offers textural interest thanks to the bread crumbs, crunchy veggies, etc. Grating a tomato by hand is so satisfying. Looking forward to making it again tomorrow (I saved half the dressing and crumbs to make another salad).

Very easy to prepare and eat. I used turkey pepperoni slices and forgot to add 3 tablespoons of EVOO and used small mozzarella balls and it still came out great. I will used this as my go-to don't feel like cooking meals.
This salad is very good. So much flavor. I made extra of the pepperoni breadcrumb mixture and put it on top of the Chile Crisp Fettucine recipe from the NYT. Incredible.

Have made this a couple of times and love it. Since I’m always looking for ways to make things easier and cut down on mess, I’ve found that “sheetpanning”(new word) the bread and pepperoni together with a little oil (on parchment so you don’t have to wash the sheet pan) works very well. I only cook these items, then toss with everything else.
I really enjoy adding to a large tortilla with hot pepper hoagie spread and maybe a little ranch for a filling “pizza” wrap that’s great for a more filling lunch.

This was a good Sunday night dinner on a humid day. It was delicious and quick, we enjoyed all of the different textures. I would halve the breadcrumbs next time and maybe add a little vinegar to the dressing, the brine in my pepperoncini wasn’t punchy enough.
Really tasty, and very simple.  As my girlfriend said, it tastes and smells like a pizza joint in Jersey.

Made for dinner. Kept pepperoni slices whole and lightly fried to remove some of the fat   add something crispy. I did not do the whole thing w/the pepperoni crumbs but instead toasted some baguette and seasoned those for crunchy croutons. For the dressing, grater was in the dishwasher, so stick blended chopped tomato, small clove garlic, a couple pepperoncini, the pepperoncini vinegar and olive oil. (No sugar.) Halved a few cherry tomatoes, few grinds of pepper, some torn basil, and tossed.

Some twists that improved this:
• Rather than grating the tomato(es), slice into bite-sized wedges, drizzle with EVOO, salt, and a bit of red wine vinegar. Marinate 15+ mins as you prep the other things, and they'll leave a wonderful umami juice you can use as the base for the dressing. The pieces of tomato can now be used in the salad as an added element.
• Put a few slices of good bread in the blender to make your own bread crumbs. They toast up more easily than panko and taste better too.
This is excellent! I used 15 cherry tomatoes and blended w immersion blender for dressing. Also added halved tomatoes to the salad.

I made this for brunch today as part of my summer project to make all 100 easy summer recipes. This is the first time I ever grated a tomato and the result was satisfying. I'd make this again. My definition of easy doesn't include two sautes and two gratings and 4 different vessels to clean. Lucky my dishwasher is working. Paired it with the NYT cold Buttermilk cucumber soup that sat in the fridge overnight. Lots of complex flavors and textures to start the day.
This really does taste like pizza!! Delish, no notes.
Rarely are there decent tomatoes where I live, perhaps august and September, period. What is an acceptable substitution for the beefsteak? Tomato paste?

This is such a delicious recipe and very adaptable. I didn’t have a tomato on hand so I added some acid and sweet umami by adding about 2tbs balsamic to the dressing. I also used marinated bocconcini with a little of the oil which was so good. Really you can put pepperoni breadcrumbs on a stack of dryer sheets and they’d be delicious, but they make this salad exceptional. This recipe is literally everything I want in a salad: crunchy, flavorful, salty, and perfectly balanced.

Yum! I liked the interest of the grated tomato; I’ll have to remember that for other salads. I added chopped yellow pepper but otherwise made as described. Easy and filling enough for dinner. Will make again!
Double the amount of pepperoni you crisp, and toss the extras into the salad, while they are still warm.  One of the easiest and best recipes ever from NYT Cooking!

As I worked on this salad, I listened to NPR, and guess who they were interviewing on Jen White's show?! Sohla!  This salad is fun and a treat as something different.  I used up some cherry tomatoes since I didn't have a beefsteak.  Even missing the purple onion, this is savory and tasty.  I bet I could also substitute cabbage (a favorite of Sohla's) for the romaine!

Made this for dinner last night and it was great! For the Super Bowl, I’m contributing some of the food for 50+ teenagers. I told them I’d bring “walking tacos” but these flavors will really improve the idea!
Back in the 1980s, in Bon Appetite I found a recipe of pizza salad that built it in a pre baked pizza crust. Lightly dressed lettuce. Pepperoni, mozzarella balls, etc. delicious

Do not make the mistake I made and assume it will have enough flavor without plenty of salt.  I expected the pepperoni, oregano and garlic powder to do the heavy lifting but....no.  Also thought the panko to pepperoni ratio was off- will probably add salt and subtract panko next time.

Really tastes like a pepperoni pizza! I thought 3/4c was too much breadcrumbs. I would use 1/4c less next time and double the pepperoncini and black olives. Be careful with adding salt to the pepperoni and bread crumb mixture because most pepperoni is already very salty!
I liked this, but the breadcrumbs turned to utter mush as soon as they made contact with the very wet dressing. I imagine something more like a crouton would hold up better.

The pepperoni crisps and bread crumb additions are stellar and give the salad a pizza flavor. However I thought the tomato dressing was lackluster. It needs something more.  I even used a ripe from the garden brandy wine tomato and it was still missing something. Maybe some Italian herbs?

Delicious! Made this as a giant salad for my whole
family. All 4 kids loved it which rarely happens. And most of my kids don’t even like pizza! Like how it’s full of pizza flavors without the heaviness of cheese.
My husband said this is the best salad he’s ever had.

I take leftover pizza, slice it into small pieces with my pizza cutter, add whatever salad dressing and greens and voila - a pizza salad. The olives and pepperocini are a nice addition and I always have those available.
Wish I had seen this last summer when I was getting fabulous Beefsteak Tomatoes in my garden.
Thought my husband would like it. And he did. But if I'm going to eat anything with this much oil, it might as well be real pizza.

We made this salad twice, first following the directions as written and then using croutons from some ciabatta in place of panko. Seasoned the croutons with the garlic powder, oregano, salt, and some red pepper flakes and added a bit more olive oil to the dressing. The result was a bit more like panzanella. Love the idea of pepperoni crumbs for eggs or pasta, but for this salad croutons in a tomato vinaigrette will be our version of a fun and flavorful salad.
Terrific. Will make again.

Could use couscous instead of breadcrumbs.  Needs more garlic. Fresh oregano use mozzarella don’t substitute.  Use fresh ripe tomato…maybe more than one or throw in cherry tomatoes.  Maybe more less crispy pepperoni?
I didn't have pepperoni but did have plant based hot Italian sausage sliced into thin rounds as a substitute.
Solid recipe with room for lots of modifications! I lightly sauteed the red onion and then the grated tomato for a more mild taste to both. Was wonderful!
This was great as written, but with the leftovers, I threw a few spoonfuls of crushed Calabrian chiles in with the dressing and it really amped up the flavor.

This is genius. I love the grated tomato in the dressing (rather than in pieces) and of course crispy pepperoni strips and pepperoni flavored breadcrumbs. I think this would be good as a pasta salad too. Plenty spicy without extra red pepper flakes but my pepperoni was on the spicy side.

Made 2x. 1st time, followed the recipe. Pepperoni was more burnt than crisped by sautéing in oil, so the 2nd time, I just sliced it up and added it as is. Much better, and cut out 2 Tbsp. of the oil as well. The other change I made the 2nd time was to use GF panko breadcrumbs (guest with celiac). They were excellent!  Did take a few extra minutes to brown. No one could tell they were GF.  Tomato dressing is excellent as is.  Just like with pizza, you can add or subtract based on preferences.

Too much oregano!!! If I make this again I will use at least half the amount of oregano and will not fry the pepperoni—that seemed to really make the flavor of the really good pepperoni I bought disappear. The crunch of the breadcrumbs is plenty of crunch. Did I buy super potent oregano or what? We couldn’t even finish our salads.
I hate sliced black olives. Would kalamatas work?
It was alright but Mark didn’t like it that much
Absolutely delicious. We did not sauté the pepperoni nor did we add breadcrumbs, and it was still extremely good.
Such a fun and delicious salad! Make this asap

I’ve never left a comment ever- but safe to say this may be the recipe I’ve made the most of the entire repertoire of NYT recipes. My husband devours it and it’s great for two for dinner with no leftovers!
10/10 salad, perfect for a Super Bowl Sunday starter. Used banana peppers instead of pepperoncinis, turkey pepperoni, and gluten free Panko.
I made this as written & it’s fantastic. It’s pretty low effort considering there are a few steps. I think it needs parmigiano in the crumbs to make it the real pizza experience. Recommended!

This salad was delicious. I substituted burrata for the fresh mozzarella and it combined with the dressing in a lovely way. Did not use any sugar in the dressing, it was not needed for the flavor. Will definitely make again.

There are enough sardines in the sea to feed the world, and we should be eating more of them. Quality counts--most grocery-store sardines come from Morocco and are industrially processed. Those from Spain and Portugal are generally better quality. A separate note: Italians would never put six garlic cloves in puttanesca. We brown a couple cloves in the oil, then remove them so as not to overpower the other flavors.

@Max Alexander, Rome Leave a few sardines for the dolphins, please. Oh, and everything else that feeds on them. Aim for multiple days each week focusing vegetarian. This is every creatures world.Thanks.

@Max Alexander, Rome we recently went to a Portuguese store at the base of Cape cod, where they specialize in sardines from all over the world… Bought about seven tins of them… Now I know what we can do with some  of them!

@Max Alexander, Rome

Perhaps you wouldn’t add six garlic cloves. I definitely would, did, and am happy!

I love this general technique and use it often, but a warning: careful when you smash those tomatoes! They're little juice-bombs and will happily make a mess of you and your stove and anything else within spitting distance. I pierce each one with a knife before tossing them into the pan to mitigate their explosiveness.
@Su No mess with with tomatoes!

I love this recipe.  A huge hit.  My only modification was that I let the tomatoes sauté in the oil a bit longer than the recipe calls for before I added the pasta water.  Otherwise, I think the tomatoes sort of boil instead of fry.  NB: As with all dishes that cook the pasta along with the other ingredients, for a few moments it seems like you've added too much water and are inadvertently making a soup. Don't despair. A moment later the pasta absorbs everything and all is well.

Made this with no fresh ‘maters as we didn’t have- but we had lovely sardines. Instead, I used a little Raos marinara, tomato paste properly caramelized, and diced tomatoes, drained, plus all other ingredients. The oil from the sardines was wonderful. Will make this following the recipe next time. The time estimate of 40 minutes was right on the mark. Everyone loved it.
@karoma What do you mean by “properly caramelized?”
@Anne Add the tomato paste to the pan early in the process and saute it, smashing it into the bottom of the pan a bit, until it turns a darker red.

Wow, this was FABULOUS!! When you’re craving that salty-fishy-OOOMPH-on-your-palate-dish, make this. Followed directions pretty exactly, using dry-cured black olives (which added to the ooomph factor), and Portugese sardines thanks to another commenter’s advice. A whole small tin of anchovies. Only needed about 3 cups pasta water. Easy to keep most of the ingredients on hand. Will make again and again!

If these ingredients have too much salt/sodium for you, try rinsing the capers and olives and omitting the added salt. Drain well before adding to the pan. This helps remove the extra sodium without having to omit any of the tasty ingredients.
@BJ I would not add anchovies to this (although I haven’t yet cooked it but know and cook anchovies in other recipes).   Sardines capers and olives enough IMHO.

Let's be clear, when they say black olives, it typically means kalamatas (or something similar), not mild black olives that you'd use in Mexican dishes or are ubiquitous on pizza. Substituting with these mild olives is not an option. If you don't have the right ones, wait until you do to make the dish.

@The Cannoli,

Green can work too. I used amazingly good Gordals to a fine result. But agreed: no bland black olives!

I love pasta with tuna and love sardines but was a little leery of sardine pasta.  It was surprisingly delicious.  I halved the recipe for the two of us and improvised by using a 14 oz can of cherry tomatoes (didn’t have fresh) plus some tomato paste.  I halved everything except I used the full amount of anchovies. I only needed to add a splash of the pasta cooking water at the end.  It was an easy, delightful meal.
Simply outstanding - tremendous flavor. I followed the recipe and was rewarded with a meal everyone raved about.

Tasty! This dish would generally be a hard no for my husband so I left out the olives, he dealt with the capers and said it is a make again dish! We are lower sodium so I did not salt the pasta water (I know I know) and I did use an entire can of anchovies and did not use any seasoning salt. Satisfying and bright. Definitely needs the spice without the olives though, gotta have some punch.

This was pretty good. I added some freshly chopped oregano when adding the tomatoes to the olive oil mixture and cooked the tomatoes without adding water. Doing this the tomatoes will burst out of their skins and caramelize a little so the flavors are better and you don't get splattered trying to smash them up in water. I think the flavor of the dish would be improved by then adding white wine instead of pasta water so I will try that next time.
@Jane S Wait! You'd substitute white wine for 2 cups or more of the pasta water? I like wine in recipes as much as the next person, but I urge others NOT to follow that suggestion!

This is a fantastic, flavorful weeknight meal that will be part of our regular rotation.

Added 1 tsp of oregano along with the chile flakes for extra flavor, and some baby spinach to make this a one pot balanced meal. There are lots of briny flavors, but we found the sauce still benefited from a 1/2 tsp of kosher salt and lots of fresh black pepper.

Really nice dish.  I'm not someone who would normally be interested in a recipe with sardine in the name but was looking for any excuse to use up the massive quantity of cherry tomatoes coming out of the garden now.  Made  exactly as written and was pleasantly surprised--just lovely.
Really needed a spritz of lemon to finish it off, but with that, made a great pantry meal!

I have always loved puttanesca and this recipe hits all those happy places on my tongue!  Instead of parsley, I lined a bowl with some baby arugula and served the hot puttanesca and pasta right on top of it, so it would wilt and lend its peppery flavor to the dish.  The only thing I had to leave out was the black olives (drat, didn't have any).  No matter, it was delicious anyway, and will make again!
interesting technique to smash the tomatoes! I went for 1/2 tsp red pepper flakes, probably could have been 1tsp. all other measurements worked for me.

Very delicious. Have made several times and the best was today.  The differences from prior were 1) I used only the oil in the sardine can, which was the right amount for my taste; 2) halved the cherry tomatoes and sauteed a bit for adding the reserved pasta water and 3) added a few glugs of passata.  Boil the pasta, drain, then use the same pot for the sauce for less mess,  Living in Portugal I am spoiled for great quality tinned sardines for €2-3, so thrilled to have such a good recipe.
Benn adding good quality tuna in olive oil to puttanesca for years but never thought of sardines.  Will try that next time.

Absolutely loved this recipe! We had everything we needed in the pantry or fresh from the garden. Parents: this recipe is child’s play. My daughter has been helping cook it with me since she was 3, and now at nearly 7 she can do it mostly by herself. Our only regular addition is a bit of thinly sliced fresh basil when we plate it up. Such a pleasure!
I make a similar dish frequently - but use grape tomatoes (sweeter) and sardines in lemon with olive oil. Delish and easy!

This is SO good. And so flexible. We had jarred tomato sauce from the summer and this seemed a good use for them and it was, together with some higher end tinned sardines and anchovy paste, since that's what I had. Fast, simple and better than you can imagine.

I've got to admit that I've never eaten canned sardines and was skeptical, but my wife lobbied for this recipe, and... it was delicious! Complex, deeply flavorful, aromatic, and healthy. I imagined that I was eating this in a trattoria in Sicily  Followed recipe as described, and it turned out fabulous. And yes, don't worry about undercooking the pasta - it softens up with the tomato sauce.  Ciao bella!
This was excellent. Made exactly as written.
Made this a while back. Stuck to the recipe and it was great. Use good quality tinned fish. Added parmesan, even though I know that's often omitted with fish.
In lieu of black olives, I used kalamata.  They added a wonderful more complex flavor to a terrific dish.
Great alternative for a regular puttanesca. Economical, fast, nutritious and delicious!
I used some passata as I hadn’t any fresh tomatoes… we loved it!

Honestly, after eating this two nights now, I think I'd prefer it without the sardines. I find it fishy. (I like fish.) I'm not UNHAPPY eating it, but I won't make it again, at least not with sardines included.

Excellent recipe, allows for a lot of substitutions and additions, for example more sardines and added mushrooms.  Didn't have enough cherry tomatoes so used canned crushed tomatoes...tho fresh would definitely taste better.  Also didn't have spaghetti noodles, but fusilli is not a terrible option.
We really liked this, including the kids. Our three year-old had seconds and kept demanding more sardine pieces.
Beware! I love seafood but this recipe was way too fishy for my taste. I would only recommend this for the biggest of tinned fish aficionados

Every Puttanesca I’ve ever prepared came from the oven. Mix everything but the pasta on a baking sheet. I also add three small onions sliced as thin as I can to melt into the sauce during baking. 190c/375f for 30 minutes. Meanwhile cook pasta al dente, drain and hold back 1/2 - 1 cup pasta water. Return pasta and finished sauce from oven into pot. Sowly add pasta water while stirring to emulsify sauce and thus allowing it to cling to the pasta. Enjoy with a drizzle of EVOO and freshly grated Parmesan. For a vegan version simply switch anchovies and sardines with 2 tablespoons or more of Miso paste and omit Parmesan.

New hit meal at our house. I prepared as directed. More or everything would not hurt it! Tasty and nourishing. Served with Italian bread, roasted red peppers and mozzarella on the side but it needed nothing additional.
I like puttanesca, and I like sardines, but the combination just didn't work for me.  I'll stick with good canned tuna when I want fish in my puttanesca.

We have been using white anchovies in many dishes that call for anchovies---they are delicious, less intensely salty than other anchovies & add protein to any dish.  They are a bit hard to find, but worth the effort.

I made this sauce to go over gnocchi that failed, so I added it to angel hair with the sardines and it was tasty. I love sardines and don’t understand people turning their noses up to them. They are delicious and so good for you and sustainable. As others mentioned, the only thing lacking for me was a bit of brightness and lemon zest would do it. I did drain/rinse the capers and olives to reduce salt. I would make again.

OMG! This is delicious. Tell them it's tuna. Wow I used a tin of sardines a tin of mackerel and a tin of anchovies. Everyone was a member of the clean plate club! Hahaha! Serve w some warm soft bread,
so good.
OMG this was so good. I used a tin of Anchovies, I mean seriously 4anchovie fillets! a tin of sardines and a tin of mackerel. Delicious! Yep watch those tomatoes they spray everywhere! Lesson learned

Love this recipe -- and I've been making a version of this for ages, since I was a student, as it's also budget-friendly. I like things a tad less salty than many, and this recipe has a lot of salty ingredients.  I love the olives, and use anchovy paste, which allows me to adjust the salt level. I've also added fresh herbs, like oregano or rosemary. A great recipe for those who say they don't like sardines!
Delicious!  My market did not have black olives or decent parsley. I subbed in green olives and watercress.  Otherwise, I followed to the letter.  What a big hit at my house.
I feel like green olives would have been better in this
Absolutely delish, great marriage of flavors. A squeeze of lemon really brings the flavors together.

A nice variation:
Instead of bucatini, sauté gnocchi until lightly browned.
Cut the cherry tomatoes in half.
Thinly slice a Meyer lemon and halve or quarter the slices
After step 2, add the tomatoes and Meyer lemon. (Skip pasta water) 
Sauté for about 5 minutes until the tomatoes have softened. No need to mash them.
Add capers, olives, and sardines and stir for a minute or so.
Add the crispy gnocchi and garnish with parsley.
Delicious either way; bucatini w/ water, or crispy gnocchi.

Delicious!  Made with 15 oz canned tomatoes since I didn't have fresh on hand.  Only needed 2 c of pasta water.  As someone else said, trust the process, have faith when adding the water and the pasta.  Would like more tomatoes.  Next time may add fresh and canned.  Could use more capers.  Goes in the rotation as a great pantry meal.
We love love love sardines and are so glad to have this recipe, as we love love love pasta puttanesca, too!  THANK YOU!

I followed this recipe exactly, but left out the sardines. I know, I know! But I wasn't sure how my son would feel about them. I doubled the anchovies to compensate and it was delicious! Everyone agreed they would like to have it again!
This was really tasty, but too liquidy—judge the juiciness of your tomatoes before adding the water, because 2 cups was a lot for mine.
This was fabulous! I feared it was going to be too fishy, but it wasn't at all. Just plain delicious with an incredibly punchy flavor. This one will be entering the rotation!

This was outstanding. The fishiness of sardines is balanced by the other flavors and develops into a restaurant quality pasta.  I had some leftover spinach which worked well when tossed in at the end. I would echo the warning about smashing the tomatoes, however.  I burned myself slightly when one exploded.

This was a fabulous meal. I didn't have anchovies, but it still worked, though i did season it accordingly. Didn't have long pasta, but worked well with Orechiette which had a good shape to match the olives. At first i worried it was too watery with 2 cups of pasta water, but it really thickened up. Added baby spinach with the sardines, olives etc like another reader and it made for a truly fantastic one pot meal. And easy clean up too.

Really loved this recipe! I added in sautéed onions, 
oregano, basil, crushed tomatoes vs cherry tomatoes and put a little lemon juice on top when served. I didn’t use the leftover water at all. Very simple and delicious! Can’t wait to make it again!
This is one of my favorite dishes. Sometimes i use tuna in olive oil instead. But sardines are probably more healthy and tasty.

Loved this...very easy, very satisfying.  I agree with other comments, because there are so few ingredients....they should all be too quality, especially sardines and tomatoes.  I will definitely make again.

Potato masher on the tomatoes? It worked for me.

A bit too much pasta water to reduce to a sauce; I'd try less next time.

A traditional puttanesca might need more subtle garlic handling, but this is a robust dish that shouts its flavors. I hardly noticed the garlic.
A little red wine added an extra layer to the flavor of the sauce. And I agree with others that some coarse salt elevated everything.

Cooked pasta at same time. Pan roasted the tomatoes with oils, garlic, red pepper and anchovies. , deglazed with red wine, added the undercooked pasta with the pasta water to the pan to finish it off. Was fabulous.
Anchovy paste is helpful if you don't want to have leftover anchovies in your fridge.  Also, a splash of vodka in the sauce towards the end of cooking elevates the flavor.
Loved it!

I so wanted to love this.  I adore Chicken Puttanesca (over mashed potatoes) so I thought this would be a slam-dunk, but it was too. much. I used Portuguese sardines and followed the recipe exactly. I did appreciate the construction of the recipe and how the sauce all came together at the end after continuously stirring the pasta but if it's possible, this was too much umami and too much fishiness. A total bomb at my house.

Wow! I am so surprised by this recipe. I’ve made puttanesca many times before and adding sardines never occurred to me. I wasn’t even sure I would like sardines, but I LOVE this recipe. Very easy to make. I was able pull this together in an hour.
I would just use canned tomatoes next time. Didn’t care for the tomato skins. A good way to get your family to eat some healthy fishes.
Pasta purists may faint, but this easily works as a one-pot dish …
I was out of sardines and substituted canned smoked trout. Fantastic.
I was very sad to see that the can of anchovies filets I used was actually whole  anchovies, so I missed out on that lovely flavor. That being said, we really liked the finished dish.
Read many comments beforehand. Added entire tin of anchovies. Loved it!!! Will try splash of fresh lemon juice next. Keeper recipe.
Amazing flavour and so easy to prepare. Will be preparing this again and again.
This elevates my already beloved Puttanesca to a whole new level. One of the best recipes I’ve found so far in NYTimes cooking, and that means a lot.
Very tasty. Converted an “I don’t like sardines” brother into I really like this!
Also: Could people PLEASE stop saying "I made the recipe exactly--except for...." Once you change even one thing, let alone half a dozen, you have NOT made the recipe "exactly."

@Guy William Molnar
why not? sometimes, you find interesting improvements - I for one added a little grated lemon, very good ☺️

Used Kalamata olives, but I like a briny flavor and could see green olives being a nice addition. Served with fresh grated parmesan but it definitely works well without. I don't think I boiled the pasta long enough and didn't end up with enough pasta water but made it work.

Sweet Jesus this is delicious!!!! I had a can of sardines in tomato sauce, so I used that and one can of plain sardines. I only had one pint of grape tomatoes, but I had an open jar of tomato sauce in the fridge, so I threw maybe 1/3 of a cup of sauce in with the tomatoes when they broke down. I used Kalamata olives. I also usually heavily salt my pasta water, but I only lightly salted it seeing how much of it I needed to use in the recipe. I ended up using just over 3 cups of pasta water. Fabulous!!!
It is SOOOOOOOOOO good!
Yum! My husband doesn’t love fishy pastas but he gobbled this up. Me and my friend were also very happy with the dish. Thanks for this delicious, fast and healthy recipe!
You will fall in love with sardines and anchovies after this dish. So good!!

This recipe is in our regular rotation now. We love it. I thought I would share my adjustments because I really botched this the first time I made it, juggling the timing of the pasta with the sautéing of the garlic, burning the garlic, having to start over, ugh. I am generally slow chopping and measuring ingredients for any recipe, so I always figure on a much longer cooking time.

As I am boiling the water for the pasta, I am chopping and measuring the ingredients and condiments for a half-recipe. I generally do more than 1/2 of the goods other than the pasta, because we like all that good stuff. I cook the pasta for half the time the package indicates for al dente — in the range of four to five minutes. When it is done, I collect 1-1/4 cup of the pasta water (a little more than needed, to be safe) before dumping the half-cooked pasta in a colander in the sink. Yes, it sits there cooling and sticking together, but it comes out fine when thrown into the developing sauce. Reducing the initial pasta boiling takes all the time pressure off. I cut the little tomatoes in half for quicker reducing, and I throw the red-pepper flakes in with the water instead of into the oil, which fills the whole place with acrid vapor. It’s perfectly spicy without putting yourself through that. Last night I used chopped arugula in place of parsley and it turned out nicely.
Fabulous. I added some shredded spinach and it gave it a little more color.

This was a big pot of deliciousness!  Made according to recipe, except used canned tomatoes and a bit of tomato paste in lieu of cherry tomatoes.  We served the hot pasta over a bed of arugula, which wilted.  This is a great way to add fatty fish with bones in into our diets,

Yum! Everyone in my family loved it. I made it without the tomatoes, and added some white wine in place of them to give it some liquid and sweetness. Used the full tin of anchovies because why waste. Green olives instead of black because it’s what I had. Bow tie pasta, same reason. I sprinkled toasted pine nuts on top to serve. So good! Not as fishy as I would have thought. One note, the sardine oil splattered a lot when adding it, as the olive oil had already heated. I will add them in quicker succession next time, which there certainly will be!
@Sally Lou I mixed them both together before adding them!
Made to the recipe. Boy, my family just did not like it. Too bad because it sounded great.
