made twice - my notes:

-cook tomatoes down til soft, then add 1/4 the recommended pasta water and cook down
-lots of pepper 
- add a squirt of lemon. top with parm

it was way better when I made it pop the way I wanted it to. also the more you eat sardines the more your body wants them in the winter imo

Have had to ration myself to eating this once a week for fear of family getting bored of it. I at least double garlic, anchovy, olives. And 1.5x capers. Basil and/or fennel work well instead of parsley. Easy, insanely delicious, omega 3. What’s not to love?!

Good recipe- everyone loved it.
Will make again!
Not sure where I went wrong, as I followed the recipe to at T but I found it bland and watery.
We decided that we like this without the sardines. There was a nice briny salty umami from the anchovies, etc, and for us, the sardines made it fishy.
What anchovies? The recipe does not call for anchovies. Skip anchovies, add sardines.
BJ, perhaps read the recipe again.

I saved the garlic cloves from the "Garlicky Chicken Thighs with Scallions and Lime" recipe (Discard them? Please.) and used 1 15-oz can of diced tomatoes, 1 12-oz can of tomato paste, and about 3/4 c of tomato salsa instead of the cherry tomatoes (all of the supermarket tomatoes around here taste like nothing). I would use about 1 c of salsa next time. If, like me, you're not a big caper fan, 2 heaping T is plenty. I did not add any oregano or wine, but I will next time.

I thought that this needed a little more zing. Canned cherry tomatoes and the addition of some sun-dried tomatoes was helpful, also some chopped fresh oregano. Cooking the pasta in the sauce was a good touch.
Surprised you don't include brown rice pasta in this recipe. We like its texture better than whole wheat pasta!
They didn't recommend whole wheat pasta.

I love this recipe! I can't wait to try it with my garden tomatoes this summer. Used canned diced tomatoes and it was great! But my house stinks a bit from the sardines and anchovies. It was worth it.

Nice and satisfying dish ! It was a hit with chili loving husband and our chili hater child.
Such good value for money ! Will make again.

I added a dash of crushed fennel seeds to the tomato sauce - made from 2 bland winter tomatoes and a little bit canned tomatoes.

I made this the other night.  I used brown rice noodles since I am gluten free.  I made the mistake of adding the sardines with their oil in the beginning with the anchovies and garlic.  It still came out delicious, even though the sardines were mostly just small bits rather than huge chunks.  Will definitely make again.
Halved the recipe to serve two persons. Delicious.

Very tasty. I was skeptical about putting so much water into the tomatoes, but with time, the sauce came together. Added very little water to the pasta as I stirred it into the sauce. Love that the olives, sardines, capers and parsley are added at the very end, not cooked down. The oil from the canned sardines ensures a nice umami throughout.
This is a super easy dinner with very little prep! I recommend a lot more anchovies (I used 2 tins). It made the sauce rich and flavorful.

Added 1/2 of a peeled chopped granny Smith apple as a garnish -sardines and granny Smith go well together, but didn't work here.  Next time I'll try some golden raisins.  The idea is sweet to balance the pungency.

I increased the veggie quotient by adding a bunch of broccoli rabe, making it a single pot meal. Could do spinach or arugula. I cooked the rabe in the pasta pot while cooking sauce in the Dutch oven, then added it while plating. 
I used the whole anchovies tin, to avoid left overs, and it tasted great - didn’t add any salt. 
Tried sautéing/caramelizing cherry tomatoes before adding the pasta water, but I don’t think I had the heat high enough.
Rich delicious meal, ends up 6 - 8 portions.

I like to riff based on what I have. I only had a few cherry tomatoes so I added some leftover jarred vindaloo sauce. I used berbere and Aleppo red pepper instead of flakes and that really made things special. It took a while for the water to burn off since there wasn’t much tomato to work with but it’s no worse for wear. In fact I’m a bit obsessed and might try to do it this way again! Went light on sardines and added goat cheese. Love!

This was a good addition to our weeknight rotation, although I’m a bigger fan of NYT’s lemony chickpea pasta. Next time I’ll try reducing the amount of pasta and serving crusty bread to soak up the sauce. Everyone seemed to comment on the sodium level, so I didn’t salt the pasta water - next time I won’t omit this step as I did have to add some salt at the end.
I had everything in my pantry except only 1 can of sardines so I doubled the anchovies. This is a marvelous dinner that comes together easily and quickly. It’s now on the “repeat” list!
I have an allergy to tomatoes but like the look of this recipe. What might be a good substitute for the tomatoes?
Made as directed and it was fabulous.
Puttanesca is a flavorful and easy dish. I usually add a dozen little necks but I’ll try sardines next time.
This was so good! Halved the recipe, let the tomatoes cook a little longer in the oil,  but otherwise followed it exactly. We loved every bite. It's a keeper! Served with a little parmesan on top.
Do pierce and sauté tomatoes before adding pasta water so they neither explode nor steam. Double anchovies. Reserve some parsley for garnish. Fabulous!
If one is stuck with winter store bought cherry tomatoes adding a tablespoon of date molasses to the sauce will compensate for the lack of sweetness in the tomatoes.
Great. Easy to make, good for a weeknight (though next time I'll do the prep in advance.) Big hit. This will go into the rotation.
Divine.  Used a little less garlic, and subbed green peppercorns for the capers -- added a perfect kick.  Highly recommend, will make this regularly.
quite good but i felt like it would have been a lot better with breadcrumbs! also could use more anchovies.

If using a GF Pasta (Trader Joe's Tagliatelle) only cook for 5 minutes & start sauce same time as pasta. Next time, will use more olives & not make mistake of adding sardines with anchovies (only the oil). Still, loved it!  Topped w/handful arugula dressed with lemon juice & olive oil to add brightness.  So fast and easy.
I’m making this tonight for guests and I wanted to know if I’m supposed to use Kalamata olives. Someone said yes, but why didn’t the recipe call for them? Thanks!
Yummy. Cut recipe in half but for some reason took me a while to boil out water. Omitted olives
Excellent. I wouldn't change anything, although I might have been a bit heavyhanded with the garlic.
😋😋😋😋😋a definite 5 YUMMIES EZ, Tasty, appropriate meal for this cold late Fall night. Perfect as written Thank You

This recipe is easy and delicious! I will make often. I splurged and bought the expensive handmade pasta at the grocery store (Tortello if you’re in Chicago) and it soaked up the sauce really well. The other ingredients are inexpensive, so the splurge is definitely worth it.
This is so easy and absolutely delicious.  Essentially a two pot meal.  Clean up was a breeze.

Made pretty much as written. Happened to have sardines in tomato sauce so used that. Went perfectly. 
All the ingredients blended perfectly. Delicious and will definitely make again.

I also used sardines in tomato sauce, and liked this very much. Actually I doubled the sardines and cherry tomatoes, and that made for a lovely gooey, saucy dish. Also added more red pepper flakes at the table. I would say it was flamboyant!
Easy to cook , we just loved this great and very tasty dish!
This is a fantastic creation!  My whole family loved it.  It's a delightful way to eat more of the 'lowly' sardine!

Made it as directed and it was good, but the balance was off--next time I will double the most or all of the sauce ingredients. But please help me with the timing; I must be missing something. The recipe calls for starting the sauce when the pasta is almost done, but from the oil hitting the pan to adding the pasta to the sauce is a minimum of 12 minutes. I started the sauce early and still had pasta sitting and waiting long enough to turn gluey, and I had to rinse it thoroughly with hot water.

@Guy William Molnar
they say this so you have cooking water ready for the sauce I think - but if you do it, you should definitely drain them when they are cooked (and keep some of the water)

@Guy William Molnar
(actually it is mentioned in the recipe)
Wowsa! Really good. So easy.
Amazing recipe, thank you!! definitely the best way to use canned sardines

Fortunately my menfolk like sardines, so this was a hit and will work for a weeknight. I happened to have grape tomatoes on hand, which worked pretty well. As for the pasta, I opted for thick spaghetti. Don't skip the fresh parsley!

My advice: go easy on adding the pasta water, maybe limiting yourself to half a cup at a time and only adding more when the existing water has been completely absorbed.  Otherwise, you end up with sardine/anchovy/cherry tomato soup.

Some commenters found the dish too salty. I, on the other hand, didn't find it salty enough, despite adding more anchovies than the recipe called for and not rinsing the capers. I suppose tastes and ingredients differ.
If you like canned sardines, this is a great dish!! I added green olives and pickled sweet peppers from the garden for extra tang.
Sauce never fully came together for me. I added some acid in the form of wine and lemon to the sauce because I found it a bit cloying.

Delicious and easy dish that comes together quickly. Tho I do use fewer garlic, only 2 large cloves, and remove them after browning. I’m eating more sustainable fishes and this fits into my repertoire nicely.
Delicious!!

We really enjoyed this, though we used tinned mackerel instead of sardines and omitted the anchovies (we didn't have any).  It didn't taste fishy this way.  Next time we'll try it as suggested and I'm sure it will great, extra fishy or otherwise
This was a "crowd pleaser" but the next time I make it, I'll add plumped golden raisins.
DELICIOUS!  This was a simple but amazing dinner during a snowstorm!  Comfort food at its best.  With homemade bread.  A little fresh parm.... SO GOOD!  Will make again!
Phenomenally delicious!   Even my partner who said he would never eat sardines, snarfed it down.

Everyone needs a fuss-free pantry pasta in their repertoire, and this beauty has become mine. I usually make a half-portion with chickpea noodles and that winds up being enough for 3-4 meals. The use of sardines (or any canned fish) makes this a flexible and affordable option for all, there's barely any chopping, and the rest of the ingredients are pantry staples that effortlessly amp up the flavour and take this into wow territory every time. Easy to adapt to include additional veggies, too.

Easy, tasty, satisfying homey dinner for two: one can of sardines, what was left of a box of Sugo, one fresh tomato, and the rest of the ingredients.  I poached the garlic in oil in the microwave then smooshed it in.  Added bread crumb topping (garlicky sourdough)

Maybe I'm a purist, but I prefer either traditional puttanesca or pasta with sardines and capers (ala Mark Bittman's NYT recipe). This hybrid didn't do it for me. The sardines were mostly lost, while the other flavors were less punchy than a typical puttanesca.

Did not have fresh tomatoes so I used two cans of Mutti baby Roma. Worked well. No anchovies so could be missing out on flavor. Next time. Had some arugula and threw in at the end. This is an excellent pantry items meal. Quick and tasty

I made this as written, the only change I made was 8 ounces of chickpea pasta. With that change, 2 cups of water gave us more liquid. However, my husband did not mind at all, especially since he had some wonderful bread to help sop up the extra sauce. But next time I would maybe cut back a little on the water for 8 ounces of pasta. A great dish with very good flavors.
Looks super salty which I don't love. Any suggested modifications?
I’m sure a good recipe, and made as directed, my family found this unpleasantly fishy.
I used the oil but next time I’d ditch the sardines. Just too fishy.

Okay, so we walked into this thinking what the heck, we have left over pizza, I had bought some sardines from Costco, and I wanted to try something different. This is awesome and not good at the same time at least for my pallet. I am going to try again, halve the sardines, add some kielbasa, and a bit of good parm, I will update you. The recipe is as promised so even though I didn’t love it, I am giving it 4 stars. 

Oh, I used an immersion blender and I liked how that worked for smoothing the sauce.
This was unpleasantly fishy. Did not like it at all.
I feel like there wasn’t quite enough sauce for the pasta, I might halve the amount of pasta next time
This is delicious. Made as written but added some sundries tomatoes. Quick and very tasty. Used my sardines in lemon brought from my last trip to Portugal.

I love this recipe, but FYI it's not for those who aren't into tinned seafood already. Also, I cook down the smashed tomato sauce an additional 15 minutes or so, makes it nice and thick and more "saucey."
Two cups of water was too much; I would start with one.  Good recipe!
Which kind of black olives do you all recommend?
I followed this recipe to a t and it did not disappointment. The flavors melded perfectly. It was filling and nutritious!

This was so delicious! I added some thinly sliced onions (against the grain, super thin half moons) with the garlic because I had some that needed using up. I also used some leftover passata as I did not have any tomatoes, and just let it cook down a bit before adding the pasta and cooking water. Fantastic quick, tasty pantry meal.

Delicious! Used 10 oz fettuccine, needed about 3 cups pasta water. I’ve made this twice - 2nd time I accidentally bought water-packed sardines, so added EVOO to compensate. Definitely best with oil-packed. A whole small tin of anchovies with their oil works great.
@Rachel Yes! The sardine oil comes to life when sauteeing the aromatics!
This is excellent - flavorful and not at all difficult. I used whole wheat spaghetti to increase the fiber content and it was delicious.
I used anchovy paste instead of whole anchovy filets. Canned tomato because it’s January. Really nice!

This is the second time I made this great recipe. I wish the cherry tomatoes were easier to skin! That was a bit of a pain. I added chopped basil, chives in addition to the parsley. And just pressed the garlic in with a press. Yum
@jillene Since the recipe did not call for skinning, I just smashed then right on the pot when the softened!
This was delicious! Very unctuous. Didn’t have cherry tomatoes but a can of diced tomatoes and less pasta water worked fine and made it a pantry meal. Used Castelvetrano olives.
This was really tasty and so easy to make. Definitely great for a weekday meal. The only change I made is I was out of parsley so I just substituted it for dill.

During the process I was worried it was going to end up too soupy and bland, but it worked out ok! (Did no have anchovies, though). 

Next time would make half the recipe, as in the middle of cooking I had to switch to a larger pot! Now have tasty pasta for the weekend! 😀

This dish was so incredibly salty that I had to stop eating even though I was still hungry. I feel I’ve gotten a month’s worth of sodium in one meal. I was looking forward to trying a new protein, but this dish was not it. Maybe without the capers it would have been okay? Who knows. I had to throw it out.

Outstanding dish!  I try to make a new recipe exactly as written the first time - and have often wished I had added the ingredients or omitted some I thought of while making it.  Not this one! It is delicious as is and I will be adding this to our regular rotation. I am ordering more sardines today!

Wow this was amazing! I didn’t have olives but had everything else in the house so gave it a whirl. I used Miracle Noodles so didn’t have pasta water; added a splash of vermouth instead. Didn’t change anything else and it was fantastic.

I didn't have the black olives that I wanted so I used giant caper berries instead. They are the size of olives and add a lovely lemony hint.  They worked very well and I will definitely use them when I make this again with the proper olives as well!!!

A perfect dressing for this salad is (clutch your pearls everyone, there is a bottled dressing suggestion ahead) is a half and half mix of Thousand Island dressing and a hot as you like it salsa.  You're welcome.

My mother used to make a taco salad with Thousand Island dressing.  I don't particularly like Thousand Island on a green salad, but it worked with the taco seasoning on the meat.  Never thought of mixing it with salsa, but it sounds good.
While this is not a healthy meal, Fritos and Doritos are terrible junk food - so high in salt they’re almost inedible. Try a good quality blue corn chip like Bearitos.
How about using Quest chips?

@Kan
The description says, “ You can use any corn chip for this recipe…but opt for Fritos if you want that authentic taste.” It was created by Charles Elmer Doolin, a cofounder of the Frito Company at his Disney Land restaurant, so of course he used Fritos.

This is the quintessential Seventh-day Adventist meal and is referred to as "haystacks." I prefer mine with homemade guacamole, black beans (or chili beans, or both), salsa, a blend of romaine and spinach, cheese, sour cream, cilantro, and Chili Cheese Fritos.
@Matthew That’s right!!! My brother still makes “haystacks”  for parties or any gathering over 5 people. We love them

1/2 sour cream and 1/2 salsa (herdez please) and a squirt of lime all blended together is the salad dressing for this at our house!
Also any Mexican cheese instead of cheddar, and soy curls for the veg version.

I skip the meat and season warmed pinto beans with cumin.  I add it to romaine lettuce, shredded cheese, avocado, red onion, tomatoes, tortilla chips and (horrors) Catalina dressing.  It's a go to for a quick, meatless, summer meal....been loving it since the 80's.

There’s a Girl Scout version of this recipe called “Walking Tacos.”  Start with a small individual bag of Fritos, add cooked taco meat and whatever additions you like: beans, onion, tomato, avocado, lettuce, etc. Clean up was easier and the kids loved it.

I use a stainless dough blender or potato masher to breakup ground beef when cooking. Also, browning half a pound at a time (while lengthening prep time) allows for more pan contact and better browning.
Add fresh off the cob corn that’s slightly cooked.

homemade guajillo tomatillo salsa. I toast dried guajillo chilies, dried Chile Morita, and dried Chile Pasillo and 2 garlic cloves in a pan with a tbsp canola oil. Then I put six tomatillos and one sliced white onion in the pan until bruised and soft. Last, place all in a blender, with two tablespoons white vinegar, and two tablespoons water. It beats Pace Picante store bought brands ("New York City!")
Skip the corn chip base and stick with crumbled on top to make it a bit healthier anyways, I uses red salsa and sour cream as a dressing. And sub Juanitas corn chips- any Oregonian will agree!!

To crumble ground meat, first add a few tablespoons water to pan and then add meat. Stir around vigorously when water evaporates the meat will be loose and will brown easily. Also, the leaner the meat is the harder to break up.
When I was growing up we did this with Doritos.
It's the summer version of a Frito pie. You can just put everything on the table and let folks assemble their own. I like a nice dose of pickled jalapeños on top.

I grew up eating this in the 80s. Our version came from the back of a french dressing bottle. Need to add the dressing to take this up a notch, as well as Ranch style beans (rinsed) instead of plain beans. No avacado or sour cream needed. It’s the best!!

I will make just about anything Sohla El-Waylly tells me to. I loved the method she used to brown the meat, it worked well and I plan to do that any time I have to brown ground beef. The flavors were perfect, and reminded me, as many other cooks, of the taco salad of my youth. 

I followed the recipe as written, and though I may make some changes for my own taste preferences, it is a solid recipe as written.
Forget the Fritos, serve it piled on a mound of white rice, and you have Okinawan Taco Rice. So good!
The Ta-Cup was one of my favorite things about going to Disneyland as a kid. I think it helped to shape and elevate my immature palate. Thank you for eliciting some fond memories.

As a Texas girl, a taco salad is one of my favorites! I use ground turkey for my meat seasoned with home made taco seasoning. Salad must also include: cilantro, beans, corn, and avocado or guac. The rest depends on what I have in my refrigerator. Dressing: part Catalina dressing, part salsa and lime juice. If you can start with a margarita, all the better!

SO GOOD! I agree that cilantro would be a good addition (but I'm colombian and cilantro and lime are like salt for us). Jalapeños and jalapeño sauce of course were a requisite. Corn kernels would also work... there is no ruining this recipe, and I did not feel guilt over it. We love you, Sohla <3
Growing up, my father would make this once a week with Doritos and Kraft French dressing. I don’t know why that dressing worked with this combination, but it did!
A lime juice vinegarette with a few tablespoons of a good salsa works, too, and it's not as sweet as French or Catalina.
We used ground turkey and swapped out pinto beans for refried beans. Guacamole and pico de gallo replaced the avocado and tomato. Delicious!

FABULOUS recipe!!!  Sooo delicious.
Over the years, have tried a few different taco salad recipes, this one is the absolute best.

This is exactly what it appears to be, haha. An extremely solid taco salad! It was really popular and the fam requested that it show up again. I used coconut oil so I could really crank the heat when browning.
Let cool after cooking and pulse a few times in your food processor

Wow! This has been a Go-To for me for over 60 years. Grew up in SoCal but never connected this with Disney (about 15 miles away).  The only differences from my version are that I break meat up in pan during frying, rather than doing so afterwards...and serve it with my own fried corn tortillas on the side.

This was lunch every Thursday at my high school!  We called it haystacks, always with Fritos, but it was the vegetarian version (sans beef).  It was my favorite meal!  Yumm.  Too, I never knew about the Disney connection.
My mom made a version of this growing up but added a can of Old el Paso enchilada sauce to the mix!

I’ve had this as a staple for years, but can’t stand Fritos. Local tortilla chips (California) are way better. Also add a lot of cilantro, but I realize people differ.
The fun of making this an easy group dish is that the ingredients can be prepared and assembled, and each person can “build their own”
Homemade Catalina or Western dressing is delightful on this salad.
Has anyone tried this with plant-based meat? Curious how it tastes.

This won't appeal to everyone but we made it with crumbled baked marinated tofu - followed the rest of of the recipe.  It was delish.  
Next time I will try it with the suggestion of Thousand Island dressing and salsa!

In the summer in the Midwest, it's easy to find "Walking Tacos." I just had one at a graduation party. We built our own (in the open chip bag) so each guest could add meat, mushrooms, lettuce, cheese, sour cream, pickled jalapenos as she wished. A delicious version for a crowd.

I only eat veggie, so yes Susan, any “fake meat” works perfectly. I also up the chili powder and don’t use oregano (love it but not in tacos). I dump a can of petite diced tomatoes (unsalted) and let it simmer for a while, too. Garlic is a must as well! Roasted corn and any kind of beans are delicious—I’m partial to pintos. Fritos are not my choice (whatever people like is what they should enjoy!), but the generic grocery store tortilla stuff is great!
Love these comments, that most don’t disparage but take it seriously, reminisce about it, etc. Thank you. ❤️
Shredded cabbage vs lettuce

My husband grew up with a version of this and makes it to this day. No pinto beans - he uses both kidney beans and garbanzo beans. No Fritos - any tortilla chip will do. His go-to dressing is Kraft Catalina. We jazz it up with chopped up red peppers and sliced black olives. Yummy.

Made this for lunch today, and it was a big hit.  The Fritos make the dish, and we all agreed we wanted to try it with chili cheese Fritos next time.  You can tweak this to your own taste, adding scallions, sweet peppers, olives, etc.  It would good with some chopped cilantro, too.  This served four with a lot of left overs!

Quick and easy vegetarian version for hot summer nights. Chopped iceberg lettuce. Drained and rinsed canned black beans. Defrosted frozen corn. Diced tomatoes. Shredded cheese. Avocado cubes. Sliced black olives.  Crumbled tortilla chips. Toss everything in a big bowl with Aldi's chipotle ranch dressing. Serve with a side of extra chips and salsa.

I love fritos but when they're put in a dish like this all I taste is salt and chemicals. Take the extra 5 minutes and fry some stale tortilla cut into strips in a little oil.  You get a much nicer corn taste  and a better texture.
Made with Impossible plant-based meat and it was delicious!

Olive oil for browning beef please! Pico de gallo, fresh cilantro, and a lime crema make it more authentic to a taco, as do high quality tortilla chips, also made with good oil, at least in our kitchen. Fresh lime for serving a must!
Yes, pico de gallo!

The things you miss when you grew up in San Antonio but have lived in Europe for 20+ years: Middle-school Frito. Though it needs saying that an Avocado was beyond exotic in Texas in those days. I had to import the Fritos through a friend in Mexico City. Well worth the trouble for primal comfort food.

Excellent! Wasn’t sure if the layering order was important but I did it as stated (I may follow recipes too literally the 1st time!). We had leftovers, so for lunch the next day I did corn chips on bottom, then lettuce, then the meat and toppings on top of lettuce (crumbled chips on top), and that worked best to get some of each in every bite. Will make this again and again.

Made this salad with suggestions from others: added red bell pepper, corn, avocado, pickled red onions, and the best ingredient - left over bruschetta mix. Also added a bit of salsa verde and used a bit of greek yogurt instead of sour cream. Crowd pleaser for sure!

This was fine but we found the seasoning underwhelming. If we make it again, we'll add much more seasoning to the meat and beans. We did like the addition of the beans to the meat mixture. My husband's family always served this with Catalina dressing
This is a dry version of the Frito Pie, we walked around with made IN the lunch size frito bag at fiestas in Santa Fe. And the origins, while debatable where, definitely predate this one
Seemed dry when I made it. If I did it again I would add a can of diced tomatoes after the meat is browned. Spices also need to be taken up a notch.

The beans should not be added to the meat mixture.  The beans should be the bottom layer, topped by the seasoned meat and the other toppings, each forming a separate layer. Fritos are spread around the edge of the beans and on top of the whole stack.

For better browning of  ground beef ... to 1 pound of meat:
Stir a quarter to a half teaspoon of baking soda into one tablespoon of water. Mix into beef.  Let it sit for five minutes. And you are good to go.
Use in any recipe calling for browning ground beef.

Made this last week, and introduced the YA offspring to an OG taco salad. It was a hit. Echoing others' comments, I think this recipe calls out for a dressing, and I vote for ancient Catalina or French. Without the dressing, the balance of ingredients -- the Fritos, the fairly dry beef and bean mix, the cheddar cheese, the sour cream -- tilts the mouthfeel too far toward the dry/sticky end of the spectrum. A dressing, especially if you spike it with some hot sauce.
Other additions include pepita seeds, jicama, black or red beans, red onions, jalapenos, and/or corn.  It tastes wonderful to mix equal parts salsa with ranch dressing!

Works really well with ground turkey as well. You can also do a vegetarian version with adding black beans along with the pintos and using non dairy cheese and sour cream. My family likes both versions.
I tossed the greens, tomatoes, and cheese with juice from half a lime and some red wine vinegar and thought that really kicked it up a notch. Crushed Fritos on top add a great crunch to every bite.

Making this tonight— a vegetarian tofu version.  As many have noted already, this was a staple dinner in high rotation when I was growing up in the 70s/80s.  Ours always had Catalina dressing.  Tonight’s version will have a homemade Catalina dressing to keep the tradition alive!

This was such a fun throw-back to my childhood!   I made as written but used ground turkey.  My mother used to make with taco seasoning mix.  Felt much more grown up using my own spices.  As others have noted, fresh salsa and hot sauce were perfect additions, as was the squeeze of lime.  Thanks for this!!

There were not enough spices in the "taco seasoning" in this recipe including, most notably, paprika, garlic powder and onion powder. Is much better with the addition of these. Also needed red onions and where is the Catalina dressing?
I’m honestly a little ashamed how often I make this. And I look forward to it every time.
I use shredded cabbage vs lettuce. Easy to make dressing: mayo, ketchup, mustard (basically thousand Island). Important to have meat/beans hot when tossing so cheese melts.
Very good; I used turkey meat to keep it healthier.

My only tip is not to forget the lime. This is the taste of childhood in every possible good way. The first time I had this was at the 1982 World's Fair in Knoxville, Tennessee, when I was a kid. My mom quickly made her own version of it and this NYT-featured version tastes so much like hers. What a great comfort, this comfort food is.
Skip the chips and spend five minutes frying stale tortilla strips. Less salty, more corn taste and they stay crispy longer.

Here in NM it's just called a Frito Pie. We raised $ at schools instead of cupcakes.
My FAVORITE is at the Plaza Cafe [santa fe plaza].....it's called the Upside down frito pie
cause they put the fritos on the top!!!! more crunch!!!
I used ground turkey instead of beef and guacamole instead of avocado.  Kept the recipe exactly the same otherwise.  It turned out fabulous!  This one is definitely going into the dinner rotation.
I grew up in the 1980s with this on the solid home dinner rotation.  Mom always served with Catalina dressing.  And that is how I shall serve or this Christmas—with homemade Catalina.
I added thin slice zucchini and fresh corn. Went with the salsa/sour cream dressing.

I wonder if anyone knows a good way to crumble up ground beef? …it takes forever to crumble with a wooden spoon. But maybe I’m just lazy

Also, I like to make guacamole, and for a kick, I make a guajillo Chile sauce with roasted tomatillos, onions, jalapeños, and guajillo chiles, as well as toasted ancho and pasilla chiles. I put them all I a blender with white vinegar and some water. They add a nice touch that imo beats the pace picante store bought versions (“New York city!”)
does anyone know a better way to crumble ground beef than using a wooden spoon? It always takes me forever.

Jay, I put ground meat into hot oil/skillet, let it sit to brown and then turn it over. Then bring in the potato masher. You can crumble the meat evenly. I use a potato masher all the time, for breaking down split pea soup, etc.
My mom used a round potato masher/ ricer— once the meat is broken into large clumps, rub the meat with the masher in small circles around the pan. I think it works great.
A wooden sauté spatula is a bit more efficient than a spoon. OXO makes a nice one.
A Zulay meat chopper works well for ground beef!

I got this recipe from a friend in the 80’s….it is delicious but a bit different.  Tomatoes, shredded lettuce, shredded cheese, crumbled Doritos, Western dressing along with the cooked ground beef, spices and drained beans.
I use a pastry cutter to mix and crumble the meat.
I've made this salad for years with salt free corn chips and bottled Kraft Green Goddess dressing - really gives it a zing.
We used to make this in the '70s.  It had Doritos and the dressing was Catalina.  Funny, we were jus.t talking about this yesterday
Made mostly as directed minus Sour Cream and fried my own tortilla strips. Delicious!

Bland. Not enough flavor in the seasoning mixture. I tried to amp it up but at the point I did, the beans started to disintegrate and the mixture became mushy. In all it was just ok. Kids ate it so that was good. I would just use a powdered mix for the meat adding the onion part. Good time saver and kid proof. Make sure to use really fresh crispy lettuce.
I always add extra spice too and add some powered chipotle, cayenne, smoked paprika.

This was a terrific recipe. I made as directed in the recipe with two small exceptions. My avocado wasn’t ripe enough so I added some pickled jalapeños. I also thin the sour cream so make it glide off the spoon easily. The hot sauce I used was Valentina’s extra hot. I’m careful only to dot it about sparingly. I will definitely be making again. The recipe doesn’t state what kind of oregano, so I used the Italian oregano that I had. I may try Mexican oregano the next time just to experiment.
At least double the seasoning recommended or buy a taco seasoning pack to go with it. Not very flavorful as written but relatively easy to throw together.
Really good!  Made it exactly per the recipe.  It does not need any additional dressing.

We have been living on "Old School Taco Salad"  for the last couple hot days. We use Penzys bold taco seasoning w beef (sometimes we use Morning Star chorizo crumbles for beef). Pickle red onion, jalapeno, radishes. We add corn, red pepper, cilantro, duritos (oft just tortilla chips), taco sauce, sometimes quinoa pilaf, doctored up canned beans, oft baby spinach w romaine. It's a summer favorite for sure esp when it's hot and you can eat leftovers for several days.
Made as directed, found the seasoning underwhelming. Added more salt, cumin, and chili powder. Hot sauce helped... and it was great. Used Doritos "taco flavor" instead of Fritos.
To take this dish to a new more tasty level, I put the chips, meat mixture and the cheese in the oven to warm prior to adding the other toppings.  Makes it all delicious.

This recipe would be improved by using greener, healthier lettuce, like romaine. For years I pondered what sort of salad dressing to use that would complement taco salad and I finally figured it out. This is more technique than recipe: use half sour cream, half mayonnaise, (amounts to be determined by how many servings you are making), adding smokey paprika and sriracha to taste. It’s a game changer; try it!

Instead of beans I used an ear of fresh corn as I didn’t have beans.  Followed recipe pretty much as written but used morning stars vegetarian crumbles. Perfect for a summers supper. Will make again for sure.  With beans?  Maybe, although the early corn was awfully good.  Yes I know, not as nutritious as the beans.
Delicious and satisfying salad. Will definitely make again.  Added cilantro.
We used Impossible meat, romaine and tortilla chips as substitutions. So good!

I typically cook 1.5 lbs of taco meat(minus the beans). For the first night it's beef tacos. For the next night it's basically this version of taco salad, with the exception that I simply drain and wash the beans in a colander and add to the salad. It's a great way to cover two dinners with little effort. My go to dressing? Catalina dressing all day long.
Use tortilla chips, taco seasoning, cholula sauce and salsa
Made as is this is pretty bland. Definitely needs pickled jalapenos, guacamole and salsa but then most things would taste OK with all of that added!

I made this as written, and it came out really bland, like it was missing something. I added an envelope of Sazon Goya, and it immediately improved it. Was a nice changeup from my usual Taco Tuesdays. I presented it buffet style and let everyone make their salad how they wanted it. Had some pickled jalapenos and additional salsa on hand as options as well.

It's been so hot,  I used sliced iceberg ad the main ingredient and added others as flavorings. Delish! Agree with other comments that more seasoning (cumin, oregano, chili) is needed but that's easy enough to doctor. So refreshing on a hot day.

Pleasantly easy weeknight dinner. Don’t skimp on the line juice, it’s the only thing that really brightens, and be generous with the salt in the beef. 

I added about a third of a cup of water to better accommodate the simmer step. Served with chopped spinach instead of iceberg. I’ll make again with quick pickled red onions on top.
Great salad... I made chips from corn tortillas.
Kraft Catalina dressing, yes the red stuff, is another popular dressing for this salad. As shocking as that may sound, it's really good.
This brings back memories. When I was at Ohio State, "Texas straw hat" was everyone's favorite meal!

This was delicious! The perfect quick, easy warm weather dinner. We rarely have Fritos in the house and my husband ate nearly the whole bag before I had planned the dinner for later in the week! We added some Red Duck taco sauce on the top. Making it again this week!

This is what I grew up calling Haystacks, and my family still loves it half a century later. It’s best with Fritos, which I love, really, It’s still good with any sort of taco chip. You don’t even need the beef. (My family always used canned Vegeburger in a soupy mixture with smashed pinto beans (not refried). You can start with lettuce, then add pinto or black beans, avocado cubes, tomato cubes, fresh salsa, grated cheddar cheese, sour cream if you like. This cuts the calories (and flavor).

Refried beans and cheese are all the protein you need for this. Guacamole instead of avocado. Romaine instead of iceberg for nutrition sake. Sliced olives. We have this on the couple of too hot to cook nights we have every summer. Eaten outdoors of course! Enjoyed since a spring quarter party at UCSB in 1976!
Catalina dressing and finely crushed Doritos

We loved this. We live in Spain where chili powder is quite mild so upped to 1 1/3 tablespoon. And them did a whole tablespoon of cumin. Also, black pepper and about 1/4 teaspoon of cayenne. But the meat cooking method is great.
In case anyone was wondering this works really well with impossible meat
Excellent as written. Happened to have cilantro so popped some on top!

Bland, bland, bland. Came here looking for a good DIY taco seasoning mix (to avoid all the salt/chemicals). Ended up adding a lot more chili powder plus some smoked paprika and some tomato paste just to get it to taste like something. I'll keep looking.

Swapped the ground beef for tofu crumbles & added a can of black beans to further amp up the protein. Also added fresh chopped jalapeños to the garnishments to take the spice to the next level. Delicioso!
Quick easy meal, especially to use up some drying tortillas….  Toasted them in the oven to crisp them.  Mashed up kidney instead of beef to make this a pantry meal.  Would recommend
Easy and good for. Quick dinner. Next time I will probably add some cayenne pepper or use jalapeño pinto beans for a bit more kick.
Delicious and easy to make

I’ve been enjoying this all my life (though without lime or garlic) and thought I knew everything about the history of Fritos. Born and raised in Dallas, I also am a lifelong consumer of Frito pie. But I had not heard of Ta-Cups. Thanks.
pretty good salad but i used chicken instead of the beef (mistake)
Excellent!  Made exactly per the recipe and it was so good.  Definitely a keeper. - may add a little more heat next time (jalapenos?).

Just served this to our collection of two teenagers and two adults. Loved it. We used quality tortilla chips instead of Fritos and added a little salsa, so  no need for salad dressing. Don’t forget the squirt of lime! I will say this: it would take four huge appetites to consume all of that hamburger and bean mixture in one go. We have enough left over for four smallish luncheon salads tomorrow.
We do ours with chopped romaine, and a quick delicious dressing is 1/3 taco sauce (your preferred spice level), 1/3 ketchup and 1/3 mayo.
Oh yes, also I do one with leftover chicken, pref dark meat. Thin tortilla chips vs Fritos.
I grew up eating this, and my mom’s version was very similar. I still make it a lot, but have added diced cucumber and red bell peppers to mine.  They’re a nice fresh addition!
My mother always used tortilla chips but…

Really good. As my wife finds beef hard on her I used a good quality ground pork with fat in it and used that. Skipped the avocado as neither of us are fans and used corn chips. Easy and taste was very rewarding.
Instead of Fritos, heat a super size corn tortilla in a hot pan, place it on the plate, and then spoon on the meat mixture and other ingredients. We prefer this method to using the unhealthy Fritos.
I made this with Impossible meat and added cilantro and fresh corn. It was delicious!
Save yourself some dishes and time and just dump the can of beans in. I strained mine and measured the juice, which came out to slightly over a half cup.

Diabetic? Looking for old school comfort food? To make this blood-sugar friendly, omit the corn chips. Keep cheese/cream moderate for heart-health balance.
Then add extra veggies or seeds/nuts to bring back crunch and lift the nutrient profile.

Delicious. I got a skeptical look from my son when I said we were having taco salad for dinner, but he loved it.  The bean/meat mixture was enough for five servings. Worried about health aspects?  All you need is a small a,punt of Fritos for plenty of crunch. We used about 2 oz for two salads. Skip the avocado and use low fat sour cream. Delish!

I am an experienced cook & was very disappointed in this recipe. It is unnecessarily messy and under seasoned and under spiced. Also needs chopped chilies. Use real corn not corn chips, except for some crunch on top.
I haven’t made this recipe but watched the short video showing how it’s made. Did anyone else see a bug crawling in the lettuce or am I hallucinating
@MP saw bug for sure
made a salad dressing from another recipe which it needed; simple avocado oil, 1/4c lime juice, 1/2 t of chili powder, sprinkles of dried oregano and cumin....was just right.

I subbed lentils for the beans because I don’t love the texture of beans and I can’t detect lentils and it was great. I love recipes like this because they’re so easy to modify to your liking or what you have in your pantry! It was delicious.

Love this recipie - and Sohla! I serve with tortilla chips and Fritos to have options. Also, you can add any veggies you like - I add diced red peppers and black olives. I use salsa as the dressing and pretend it’s healthier. I also like to make it more salad than chips - I’m watching my figure. I love that it uses some pantry spices instead of taco seasoning mix. I’ve also found you can add the same amount of water if you don’t want to use the bean liquid; you may just need to reduce a few minutes more.

title: Quinoa and Broccoli Spoon Salad Recipe
author: Sohla El-Waylly

comments.commentBody: Although this salad sounds great (with the exception of the cheddar, which I cannot eat) raw broccoli is not appealing. I always briefly parboil chopped broccoli then plunge in ice water to stop the cooking if I plan to use it in a salad. Not only does this improve the flavor, it brightens the color to a very pretty and palatable bright green.
i agree - raw broccoli doesn't sound pleasant to eat.
Briefly parboil? How brief?
@Sue omg! This was my first thought upon landing on this recipe

A note on raw broccoli: Melissa Clark notes in her recipe for garlic broccoli salad with sesame dressing that the vinegar « cooks » the raw broccoli, kind of like happens with the fish in ceviche. I’ve tried it and it’s really good; it’s not like plain raw broccoli at all. I recommend that all the skeptics try it.

@sally agreed. I have a sensitive stomach— raw veggies cause problems. The dressing in this salad does indeed ‘cook’ (break down) the broccoli.

I love this salad and have been evangelizing about it for a while. Friends w similar issues can eat this without problems. (I do not parboil the broccoli).
Followed recipe exactly and it was perfect.  So glad I did not follow advice to parboil the broccoli. This is really good.
Instead of eating raw broccoli, I drop it into boiling water for a few minutes ( 3 to 5).

Even though I’m not a fan of raw broccoli, I gave this recipe a try. I used toasted walnuts because I didn’t have any pecans. It was absolutely delicious! I will absolutely make this again. I grated the cheddar because it’s easier than chopping. Extra sharp cheddar adds a very special taste to this dish.

Chop the broccoli finely and let it macerate in the dressing a few minutes as you prep the remaining ingredients. I was skeptical at the outset but the raw broccoli worked great and the result was delicious. I did hold back some of the quinoa because the recipe as written makes such a large quantity. This means mine was even more broccoli forward. I cooked the quinoa in the Instant Pot, which also made the whole process easier.

This recipe is very similar to one from Bon Appetit magazine that uses almonds, dates and scallions with cheddar and it’s easy to riff on the combinations based on what you have on hand (nuts, dried fruit, cheese type.) The raw broccoli makes the salad texturally, try it before you decide to parboil.  The warm quinoa and the dressing soften the broccoli and the salad keeps well, it’s delicious with a fried egg on top!
Delicious! Also nice to add some heat (finely chopped chili I had around) - and some herbs (a tiny bit of mint and parsley in my  case).

This was delicious! Bright flavor and very satisfying. The only change I made to the recipe was to squeeze the juice from the whole lemon which probably helps soften the broccoli and adds extra tasty tang to this delightful salad.
Followed the recipe exactly, and it's delicious. Which is good because the recipe should have specified that it serves 4 to 6 distance swimmers during an Olympic training camp.

Can any other Gen-Xers make this salad without singing "She's chop'in broccoli, chop'in brocco-li, chop'in brocco-la, chop'in brocco-laa-aa, she's chop'in brocco-lay, chop'in brocco-lay?" 

Every time. Can't help it.
@Tim H Dammit! You just had to go there—and take us with you on this earworm!

This recipe totally delivered. I decided to try it because I had everything in the recipe already. And I was surprised that I didn't need to change anything - no tweaking necessary. Really delicious. And it wasn't like eating raw broccoli. It's such a complex mix that nothing stands out and everything blends well.
This salad would be excellent with the Unexpected Cheddar from Trader Joe’s.

Really delicious, and certain to become a weekday lunch favorite.  I was pleasantly surprised with how quickly the broccoli 'cured' in the dressing.  I soaked the quinoa overnight (first time for me) and it turned out nice and fluffy with none of the bitter/soapy taste of past quinoas.  I didn't have cheese and so added some green onion to bump up the savoriness of the dish.
I bet this would be fantastic with wild rice subbed for quinoa!

I have made this for the last 9 months almost weekly. If you use the raw broccoli, it keeps for a week, allowing me to make it on Sunday and parse it out into individual containers for worktime lunch. Hold off on the dressing until the morning you take it, though - dressing soaks into the broccoli, and you can't taste it if applied a day in advance. For more grain taste, I use barley rather than quinoa. Orzo and pasta don't hold up for 4 days, so orzo can be used if eating in 48 hours.
I made this tonight and it was delicious. Used dried blueberries because did not have cranberries and they were great. Such a bright tasty salad on a cold snowy January day.

This salad is like a party in your mouth. I added more apple (1 1/2 apples) and cranberries (3/4 cup) as I liked the way the fruit played against all that broccoli. I could only find broccoli crowns, so I used about 1 1/4 pounds. (Do not be tempted to cook the broccoli; it's crunchy and perfect just marinated in that fabulous dressing.) I thought it needed a bit more dressing, so I made 1 1/2 times the vinaigrette recipe and added a bit more lemon juice. More of Sohla's spoon salads, please!

This turned out great. But I wish that recipe writers would say what the trimmed and finished proportions of the ingredients should be. I had broccoli crowns instead of a large bunch of broccoli with stems. So, as I discovered, 1-1/2 pounds of finely chopped broccoli was WAY too much in proportion to the dressing amount. I used 1 pound of it instead. It would be much more helpful to tell us the AMOUNT of chopped broccoli recommended in the ingredient list, to get the proportions right.
Delicious! Used sour dried cherries instead of cranberries, and manchego instead of cheddar. Used what I had in the pantry.

Excellent but a bit sweet as written, so I scale back the honey.

FYI backpackers: This makes a fantastic, calorie-dense cold-soak meal. Dehydrate quinoa, broccoli & apple; mix with nuts & cranberries; add dry mustard, crystallized lemon, sumac (in lieu of vinegar) salt & pepper; portion dry ingredients into baggies. To eat, soak in 1 c. water for 45 mins. (a Talenti gelato jar is great), stir & add oil & cheese (Babybel—or aged cheddar is OK on trail a while). My favorite John Muir Trail meal!

Delicious! Followed others advice and didn’t cook the broccoli, and added extra lemon juice. This is so much better than similar broccoli salads from the grocery store deli. I did keep the pecans separate to add to individual servings, since all nuts tend to get soggy or gummy after sitting in a salad for awhile.
This was a recipe I didnt cook, but was served by a friend this week and insured me to subscribe  to the cooking app. Delicious!
Addendum: perfect amount of dressing (to our taste) is 1.5X that given in the recipe.  2X is a little too much (but still delicious!).
Agree 100%.

One could blanch the broccoli in the boiling water and, after removing the vegetable, use the boiling water for the quinoa. Italians do this when making a variety of vegetable and pasta dishes. I'd cool the broccoli somewhat in the ice water, and drain it well before adding it to the dressing.
Raw-broccoli skeptics: be brave. Chop it small. This is great as written!

I didn't have quinoa on hand but my husband buys those I cup Minute Rice thinks and happened to have two of mixed Brown Rice-Quinoa. Zapped in microwave and left to cool while chopping up the rest. Came out great. Would maybe crumble some crispy bacon on top next time.

The three of us spent the whole meal talking about why we didn’t love it. First and foremost, it was too vinegary, and I had already cut the vinegar from 3 Tablespoons to 2. The rest of the recipe I made as is. 
The ingredients didn’t seem to go together properly. They just didn’t fit or blend or complement each other. Just not our favorite.
@Vinegary totally agree. It just doesn’t work.

I followed this exactly; it was hearty and delicious. It makes a generous amount! It keeps well in the refrigerator for several days. One recommendation: if you aren't' going to consume it all at one meal, I suggest doubling the dressing, put half of the dressing aside in the refrigerator, then proceed with the recipe as written. The next day the salad is quite dry and needs more dressing; you can stir in the reserved extra batch of dressing the next day (or second day).

Really good! Was looking for recipes for TG sides that are a bit healthier. This is perfect! I made everything as is except I didn’t rinse the quinoa because I forgot to do so before putting it away the day before making the rest of the recipe. Didn’t seem to matter. Fun fact: recently learned that eating broccoli with mustard increases health benefits of broccoli so tastes extra good knowing I’m doing good by my body!

@Teresa that fact about mustard seed and broccoli was so interesting. I looked it up, turns out it is restorative to cooked broccoli, as it puts back in what the cooking process takes out. Glad that I know about this now, thanks!

I thought this recipe looked great and so I decided to make a double recipe for a potluck, following the directions exactly. Big mistake! The combination of flavors really didn't work for me, especially the sharp cheddar cheddar with the lemony dressing. Nobody else liked it either, although this is a crowd that readily embraces adventurous vegetarian fare. All the other potluck contributions were gobbled up, but not mine! It's going into the compost pile. What a waste of good ingredients!
I'm thinking I'd prefer to use feta cheese.

Winner winner, salad for dinner!!  Loved this recipe, made it exactly as written with the exception of subbing Gruyere for cheddar because that’s what I had on hand.  

I did NOT parboil the broccoli, but added some extra dressing to ensure the broccoli “cooked’ thoroughly.  

Got rave reviews from dinner guests.  Just ate some leftovers for lunch on a bed of arugula and topped with a boiled egg and some pan-fried halloumi.  Delicious!

Five stars for giving me exactly the flavor I wanted from reading the ingredient list. Lots of flavors and textures that mix wonderfully. I wish I'd paid more attention to the comments about doubling the dressing though--the amounts given are for people who like very lightly dressed salads. I like heavy dressing, so next time I'll double it. But there WILL be a next time.
Subbed Pom seeds for apples cuz at last minute apples were so sad looking! Fab recipe!

This recipe rocks. I wasn't sure how it would be on a winter day, but it offered a change that is refreshing in the midst of winter. Conversely, I'd make this in an instant in summer as well.

The broccoli chopped finely, including peeled stem was beautifully tender. I second the comment to let macerate while preparing the rest of the ingredients to add. It's going to Snowshoe Camp next week!
this is exactly what I think. Finely chop the stems, and let them macerate in the honey vinegar dressing. This is a really, really good salad.

I was skeptical of this salad - looked like a ‘healthy’ salad I see at the deli counter of the local supermarket. But it was actually very tasty, if a bit chewy. I made exactly as written - glad I did not cook the broccoli as some notes suggested - the dressing broke it down enough. Make sure to cut the broccoli even smaller than you think. Eating again now for breakfast - it’s a little addictive.

I cook quinoa with a 2:1 ratio of water to grain, always turns our perfectly with no need to drain and less chance to overcook. It's done when the water's 95% absorbed and there's a sheen of water at the bottom of the pot. Fork the quinoa out of the pot into a large bowl to cool.

This dish just planted itself into heavy rotation at our house. I can see easy variations in the future depending upon what is available. For the most part, I made the recipe as described. I didn’t have Dijon, so I used a small amount of Yellow Mustard and a bit more black pepper and salt to compensate. Another thing… I did not rinse the Quinoa. I never do that part. I loved mixing the warm quinoa with the cooler other ingredients. One extra bit… I topped the bowl with some steamed beets. YUM!
Great recipe - I blanched the broccoli first because I prefer it on the softer side. Works well with sliced almonds too.

This was a big hit, but I did make a few changes. After cooking the quinoa, it seemed like too much. I reserved a cup for another meal. Had walnuts on hand, so used those instead of pecans. Husband hates cranberries, so I added raisins. Also made another batch of the dressing to serve later. All in all, a big hit!

Followed the recipe except used walnuts for pecans and dried sour cherries for cranberries. Loved this salad. I chopped the broccoli into small bits and after just 30 minutes blending flavours, it was really, really good.

Made to spec except used walnuts bc I didn’t feel like going out to buy pecans. It was tasty, but not the flavor explosion I expected from the comments. I ended up making a third more dressing and added that and it helped. Next time instead I would use half the quinoa and leave dressing as is. Used a nice very sharp cheddar. Wish it had a little more flavor. Maybe I’ll try adding scallions if I make again. PS this recipe makes A LOT! I would halve for my little family next time.

Just made this and want to confirm - do NOT be afraid of the raw brocc! It stays crisp but isn't woody, and soaks up the dressing so nicely. I substituted with vegetable oil in the dressing bc I was out of EVOO, subbed roasted salted pistachios instead of walnuts and farro instead of quinoa because it just seemed yummier, and can confirm that it was. TRY THIS. IT YUM.
Prepared as written, except for the cranberries.  I only had golden raisins on hand.  It was delicious and easy to make.  The broccoli was great raw.  It gives it a nice texture.

I made this as written and it was so-so.
Then it became asparagus season.  We grow our own and were inundated.
So I made this with chopped asparagus and did not mix in cheese but topped with Feta.
It was fabulous!!

I made a half recipe exactly as written and I'm really glad I didn't waste any more good broccoli, pecans or cheddar cheese.  This salad had no taste.  Maybe I chopped the elements too small, but my salad looks pretty much like the salad in the picture.

Made this following the recipe exactly and didn't love it. The raw broccoli was totally fine - I really did chop it finely. The dressing was just okay but in general I wanted the salad to be a lot more tart and bright. Did not love the cheddar cheese or apple. I think if I were to do something similar to this, I would leave out the honey and sub in feta and leave out the apple. Would probably also add way more lemon. Cranberries added the right amount of tart sweetness that I was looking for.

For true blue vegetable lovers, I'd recommend cutting quinoa to 3/4 or even 1/2 of a cup -- I'm missing some of the broccoli flavor I got before I added the quinoa. 

However, everyone (no matter their palate) should trust Sohla on the raw broccoli; I normally find raw broccoli, as on a veggie plate, dry to the point of being flavorless, but peeling the thick skin and chopping finely completely transforms the texture and flavor. Do not parboil, I promise you won't regret it!

This was terrific. Cheated and hit some of the broccoli in the Food Professor because spoon sizing all that broccoli was a pain in the butt. I kind of thought a little more cranberry, pecans and apple would have been nice, but definitely not necessary. Also thought maybe 25% more dressing would have been cool, but my lovely wife disagreed. Will absolutely make again.

Made this for our Super Bowl party.  Perfectly adequate, but everyone agreed it was a bit bland.  It did have a nice lemon flavor, & perhaps would be good to balance out a spicier dish in a menu.  Notes:  do not parboil the broccoli.  The recipe calls for the broccoli to be finely chopped, and it does not taste too firm raw.  I used a good cheddar, but it didn't add much taste given the amount of quinoa.  This will serve more than 6 since people will only want a small portion.
Double the dressing!
Delicious, easy, and toddler approved. I put the apples and broccoli in the food processor and did not toast the pecans because I don't have the time or the energy 🤣

I love all these items individually, but together in this salad, it was a bit underwhelming. I should say, I'm not a huge fan of dishes that lean towards the sweeter side (I love spice, salt, acid) and so perhaps being deeply satisfied with this particular dish was going to be hard to achieve. I'm not sure I'd make it again, but if I did, I think I'd go with others' suggestions to use feta and maybe use less apple.
I had exactly the same experience, this salad was not punchy enough for me as written.

I recommend doubling up on the vinaigrette if you’re making this the day ahead. Add the amount the recipe calls for as the “marinade” and then add some to the salad shortly before serving. It’s loses its vibrancy of flavor/acidic notes in the fridge. The extra dressing freshens it up and adds some moisture back. Also, added the apple and the chopped cheese the day of serving as well in order to avoid slimy cheese and soggy apples.
The mustard flavor sort of took over, I thought.  I wished I would have put in a little less.

Delicious! Made as written. Unlike other grains, quinoa is a complete protein, so this salad is a good vegetarian main course and will serve 4 to 6 as stated. But if you are serving it as a side dish, this will yield at least 12 servings.

Nice summer lunch or light dinner. Whirred broccoli in food processor - light pulses to get it small. Subbed in what I had - dried cherries and parmesan for the cranberries and cheddar.  Upped the lemon and ACV slightly.   Tasty.  Could see it working well with crunchy, firm red grapes. Feta'd be a nice alternative too.
Made as directed.  (I like raw broccoli.) It’s good but I found the dressing too sweet.  Next time I would omit the honey.  I don’t care for any sweeteners in salad dressing.

This was so amazing and delicious when it was freshly prepared but it was not as good just a couple of hours later and did not keep well at all in the fridge. Would recommend to eat quickly or maybe make it but keep the dressing separate/i.e. don’t mix the dressing into the whole batch but just add the dressing to each serving as you go.

This recipe is wonderful! I agree with other commenters that the broccoli, which I finally diced, was "cooked" and softened by the acidity of the vinaigrette. I didn't have cheddar cheese so grated Manchego cheese into the salad mixture, which I found to be a tasty substitution. This truly is a wonderful salad, especially in the winter months, and really, a complete meal.

This is a little bit putsy if you’re lazy like me, but it is soooo well worth it. The depth of flavor is incredible.  I serve it slightly warmed. The only change I made is I pulsed the broccoli until it was almost riced, which is just a personal preference.

This makes a LOT; well more than 6 servings unless this is a main course for big eaters.  It is a tad sweet; I added the juice of the other half of the lemon and a splash more apple cider vinegar. Used roasted salted pecans. Used manchego instead of cheddar. All in all, quite delicious.  Would probably use less honey next time.
I agree with the note about the raw broccoli - finely chopped raw broccoli the way the recipe intended is best. This was a good recipe, including the sharp cheddar cheese. I shall make again!
Huge salad, next time make half. Overall good but cut back on the honey next time, also I didn't add the cranberries (too sweet already). Add the nuts and/or cheese per serving for variety.

I will admit I was skeptical about the raw broccoli but I followed some other tips and let it macerate for a few and I have to admit, winner winner chicken dinner!! Absolutely delicious, crisp, and pairs well with just about anything. Since I was shredding the cheese I did the same for the apple and it blended really nicely. Definitely adding this to the rotation. Thank you!
This has become a staple. The honey mustard dressing is great with broccoli. I usually use lentils in place of quinoa. Works well with any dried fruit, nut, and legume /grain.

Endless variations on this recipe have been my favorite spring, summer, fall food for years. I like it with roasted broccoli florets, raw slivered broccoli stalks, feta cheese, tofu, lemon/oil vinagrette, lemon poppyseed dressing, various nuts, whatever fresh fruit is lingering in the fridge, etc. The constant is the quinoa and cranberries. The tartness of the cranberries balances out whatever sweet ingredients happen to be there. Also makes a great sandwich if stuffed into pita bread.

You can get shredded broccoli stems that look like slaw at Trader Joes or other groceries. I find it's not as hard on my stomach as the broccoli crowns.
I've made a salad like this for years but used farro which is nuttier and chewier.

I have made this several times with no changes to the recipe.  I may fudge the amount of broccoli and/or quinoa depending on what I have, but this recipe is awesome.  I use maple syrup if honey is not available.  Still awesome.  It is part of my meal rotation.  There is no need to parboil or blanch the broccoli.  The apple, cheddar and dried cranberries give the flavor depth and variety.  I use Trader Joe's sweet and spicy pecans (my pantry is NEVER out of it!). Again, awesome!

This recipe is fast, easy, uses ingredients common to kitchens, and is very nutritious.  Good protein source, a "complete" meal in one bowl, plus it tastes delicious.  I followed the recipe, but I held back adding some of the cooked quinoa - just seemed like too much.  Next time I will use 1/2 cup dry quinoa rather than 1 cup.  The crunch from raw broccoli, apple, and pecans complemented the softer texture of the cheese and quinoa.
Darn good as written. Makes a lot, more like 8-10 servings for meat-and-two meals. Would be good as a base for a salad topped with smoke salmon or shredded chicken.
I made this with bulger.  It's marinading in the fridge now for tonight's supper. Tasted the dressing, while prepping the veges.  It is outstanding!.

Made this last night exactly as written but used the farro option instead of the quinoa. It was delicious! I definitely vote for using raw broccoli. It added a lovely crunch. Love the combination of textures and flavors!
Great summer lunch. Used kohlrabi instead of apple which worked well. Cut the honey in half and the balance worked for me.
1.5 dressing for single recipe.
Agreed that about 1.5x dressing is needed. Agreed that the raw broccoli cooking in the dressing is perfect! Left out cheese as we are dairy free—still awesome!
This salad makes a delicious meal. I almost doubled the sauce, and that really brought out the flavor. I live alone and was surprised at how much it made- I will be eating it all week!

Definitely a keeper! I subbed in pan-fried halloumi, dried cherries, and roasted almonds because that’s what I had on hand. Love this salad and excited to eat it for WFH lunches for the next few days.

Chopping broccoli releases cancer-fighting phytochemicals; chop and stop to maximize this chemical reaction.  Perfect recipe for this! For me, broccoli crowns are more easily digested.  If broccoli gives you issues, avoid the big stem.  I love this recipe!

This was delicious and well-received.  My only note: perhaps I'm slow in the kitchen, but this took me substantially longer to prepare than the 30 minutes it estimates (found this recipe in their list of "quick vegetarian meals").

This is just lovely. Used my food processor to finely chop the broccoli using the pulse function because my small dicing knife skills are not the best! Subbed the dried cranberries with chopped Medjool dates. Simple, quick, healthy and delish? Checks all my boxes!

Like, well …. I didn’t feel like
going to the grocery
store ( oh dear)
so instead of peas, I used celery 
(Because that’s what I had )
and I didn’t have lettuce, so I used cabbage, oh and also…. Instead of
dried berries
I found dried ants in
the pantry, but other these
changes… oh wait…..
for the dressing, was out of olive oil,
So subbed some
Maple syrup….
Turned out
Spectacular!!

@Sky Island Brilliant. This captures exactly how I feel about reading reviews when people list the 96 things they did differently and talked about how much they loved the recipe and will be making it again.
I cook like this, too.........

I like the combination of ingredients for a salad, but I agree with others that the recipe as written makes a bland, uninteresting dish. After reading comments, I used 3/4 c quinoa, doubled the dressing, and reserved half of it to add before serving. It was still lacking, so I added another lemon, half a scallion, and more pepper, which helped. 

I will try it again with 1/2 c quinoa, triple the dressing, two lemons, green onions, and other spices as needed.

As someone who really needs a recipe, I have found the encouragement from the description and others to use this as a "base" to be absolutely true! I've used whatever grain, cheese, nut, or dried fruit I've had around, including feta/walnuts/raisins instead of what is listed (though always sticking to the dressing and the broccoli). The 30 min prep time is accurate and amazing for lunch prep for the week, highly recommend!

This salad is delicious! Sometimes I omit the quinoa if I another carb on the menu. I also have begun shredding the cheddar to sprinkle on top instead of mixing it in.  The cheese can get a little mucky. It’s great the next day especially if the cheese is added as a topping. I have also used dried Michigan cherries instead of cranberries. I make this recipe frequently! It travels well and is great for picnics.

@Carol
I can attest to this salad’s deliciousness!

I love this recipe and make it 2-4 times a month. It makes a great lunch for work the next day!

I love to add more lemon juice, and put some grilled chicken in it for extra protein.

Enjoy!
This would have been so much better without the cheddar, or with feta instead. Wish I tasted before adding the cheese.

This is delicious! Tastes so fresh. The secret is to take the time to finely chop the broccoli.  Just enough dressing to add all the flavor but doesn't overwhelm the freshness of the salad. Great as a stand alone or a side dish. I'm taking to our next office pitch-in!
Roasted the pecans and finely chopped the cheese, but didn’t mix in. Let guests top with them on their own.

My tastebuds and stomach were at odds on this salad. I really enjoyed the taste but my stomach did not agree with the raw broccoli. Next time, I will just use the florets of the broccoli. Maybe I didn't get peel off enough of the woody part of the stem when I made this the first time? I will trust that when the food goddesses, Sohla and Melissa Clark, say that the broccoli does not need to be parboiled, they are correct.
Really, really good with wild rice rather than quinoa.
Wonderful! I only had slivered almonds, and they worked well. Next time I will try Bleu cheese or goat cheese.
I accidentally made this with three cups of quinoa instead of one and it is great. I imagine that if I had only used added one cup it would have been too dressing-forward.

This is a remarkable dish that allows for so many swaps. I almost exclusively use Farro rather than quinoa and it holds up so well over a few days, which is all we get as we eat it nonstop! No cheddar? Try crumbled Feta, or add some sunflower seeds. So many ways to use a few of those items in your pantry. When you know a recipe by heart, it’s a keeper!
Next time I will less aggressively salt the quinoa water.

Made/brought ingredients to a friends house to make this salad. Instant hit and was asked for the recipe immediately. Agree with other comments that the raw broccoli makes the salad! However if you don’t like that, parboiled broccoli I’m sure works for people with texture preferences.

Subbed farro for quinoa, walnuts for pecans, and hard cotija for cheddar as they were what I had on hand, and it was all just great. Definitely do not parboil the broccoli--as others have said, it macerates nicely and quickly in the vinegarette. This recipe also makes A LOT of food. Luckily, it is delicious and healthy and was very easy to make.
Really delicious. Makes enough for a small army if using as a side.
Do wish your recipes would be for smaller portions, as for 1-2 persons.  Always a challenge to have to half/quarter amounts.  Discourages one from trying the recipes.

Thought this was pretty good especially when the quinoa is still warm - took other folks’ advice and left the broccoli raw. It was fine since it was diced up small! Felt healthy and fiber forward. I doubled the dressing and that helped (also a good way to use up two already-zested lemons), probably used about 1.5x and saved the remaining 0.5x for leftovers. It does make a lot!

This just didn't come together as we anticipated. The cheddar was definitely "lost" in the finished product, the pecans also vanished. And, way too much quinoa for the amount of dressing and combination of other ingredients. Even keeping at least half the quinoa back and doubling the dressing didn't quite work for the anticipated consistency. Feta and toasted slivered almonds might be better choices. That said, we probably won't do a repeat on this one (part of it is still in the refrigerator).

Love this. Usually don’t go for uncooked broccoli, but trusted the recipe and it was amazing. Made as written. It doesn’t taste like uncooked broccoli. It tastes bright and sweet and tangy and crunchy and definitely eat w a spoon because u will want to shovel this in your mouth. AND u can feel good about yourself for eating something healthy. Trust the recipe.
I oftentimes pick new recipes to try based upon ingredients that I already like, and so tried this recipe.  Sooo disappointed, it lacked flavor.

This salad delivered, and then some. Used what I had knocking around the fridge - super mature Gouda instead of cheddar, raisins instead of cranberries and Dutch mustard instead of Dijon. Turned out a straight up banger. Served it with hot olive bread, hummus and a fruit salad as a bit of a Saturday-picky-bits dinner. The whole family loved it - and that includes teenagers. This is gonna go in heavy rotation. Might try adding chickpeas or lentils as well next time.

This was amazing. I did use the whole lemon in the dressing. The dressing was drinkable it was so delicious. Note if you don’t want to heat the kitchen the quinoa can be cooked in the instant pot in 1 minute with a natural release.
This makes an absurd amount of food, FYI.
I found the dressing to be too sweet and agree with the other reviewer that raw broccoli isn’t great, and maybe a quick blanch would help.
I will make this as written because I trust Sohla. Then I’ll make a riff substituting feta and chopped dates for the cheddar and cranberries.
I’m not a fan of raw broccoli, but this dish is very delicious! I used toasted walnuts instead of pecans and a very sharp cheddar. So delicious! Grate the cheese. It’s easier than dicing.
Yum! I followed the instructions but was hesitant to serve it all combined. I ate it combined and love it!

I followed the directions and ingredients exactly except for the cheddar, as we are vegans. The salad is very good! I'm sure it will improve with the mellowing of the raw broccoli as the hours pass. I am going to add a few more cranberries and pecans. I I think that will kick it up even more.

I enjoyed this although did not add cranberries and parboiled the broccoli. I also used almonds instead of pecans as that’s what I had. Next time I’d add chili flakes for heat. I think it was a bit too heavily dressed and wish I hadn’t added all the dressing

This salad was fabulous. I did use walnuts instead of pecans because that's what I had. I also don't like raw broccoli, but the dressing made it seem less "raw." I just loved it, and my guests did too.

I had to make more dressing and add it to the salad. It absorbed all the flavor after the first couple of hours, and the tang and thrill was gone.The broccoli didn't soften up. Perhaps blanching briefly is the key. I would advise doubling the dressing and adding more as needed, because the recipe makes a huge amount of salad.

Having now made this, I am 100% team cheddar + raw broccoli. The cheddar pairs classically with all of these ingredients (think broccoli/cheddar soup; cheddar + apple; cheddar + nuts/honey/dried fruit). But it's also the texture that works really well--the little creamy-chewy cheddar bits contrast nicely with the crunchy broccoli/apples/nuts. I had some plain and some smoked cheddar, and so used both. Also gave a few chops to my bizarrely huge dried cranberries to get them in line. 5 stars!
I'd suggest that you let the quinoa cool before adding the cheese so it doesn't melt.

I make a raw broccoli salad not dissimilar to this and it’s lovely particularly as I’m not too keen on cooked broccoli as a whole!
But raw it’s great,I do however soak for a little while dried cranberries-as we can’t get fresh in NZ,in earl grey tea.about 5 mins and they have a slightly different flavour which always gets great comments.
But I’ll make this version tonight as we’ve beautiful broccoli in our summer garden.
Thanks Melissa for another great recipe

I made this for lunch today and thought it was great! Quite healthy, but the nuts, cranberries, and Cheddar all give it enough richness to make you excited about eating it. One addition I might try for the leftovers: some scallions!

Excellent! No need to cook the broccoli.   And I will try some of the other grains in future salads. I just cubed the cheese, quite small , very easy, and it doesn't leave a grater to clean up.
Also, a good dish to take when you are headed out to a friend who is making dinner, or  for a potluck.

We made this for dinner last night and thoroughly enjoyed it. Like some of the other comments, I was skeptical about the raw broccoli but it wasn't a problem for me at all. We added roasted hazelnuts instead of the pecans and it was a great addition. Also next time I make this salad I will make a little more dressing. It seemed a little dry with the amount the recipe calls for.

Made this for dinner tonight and it is GREAT.  I made a half recipe which was still a lot of salad.  I used raw broccoli and made sure to cut it into small pieces.  I mixed the raw broccoli with the dressing and let it sit for an hour or so before finishing with the other ingredients.  Subbed dried cherries for the cranberries.  Otherwise, followed the recipe.  It is a keeper!! Everyone loved it.

Delicious! I LOVE broccoli and will make this again! Next time I will chop the apple and cheddar more on the chunky side rather than fine. Recipe as written makes more than 4-6 servings in my opinion.
Great idea for a healthy salad - dressing underwhelms, unfortunately. Kept tweaking with extra acid and salt. Good - not great.

Holy Stromboli!  Made per recipe & had enough for a small army.  Very good first day.  Looking forward to seeing how it tastes 2nd day & 3rd since we'll have it that long.  Not so much a fan of raw broccoli but this was intriguing & with enough good cook's notes (thanks to those who left them) to try. Keeper to make again, although cut by 1/2 if for just four.  Would be good dish to take along when asked to bring veg or salad. I think dry cherries or white raisins could sub for cranberries.
