You could try it- it depends on your taste preferences. I like salads to be really tart rather than sweet (this salad as is wasn't tart enough for me), so I wouldn't sub, but if that isn't your taste preference, and you like sweet rather than tart, it would probably be fine.
Make it a day ahead. The broccoli needs to marinate and “cook” in the vinegar and lemon.

I salted the water aggressively as was instructed and the quinoa came out way too salty.  An exact measurement would be a helpful guideline. Unfortunately I used quinoa that was passed the expiration date and it was too hard! All the other flavors and textures were good, even with subbing tart cherries and a different cheese. For the recipe ingredients. I will try this again with less salt and fresh quinoa!
Perfect amount of dressing (to our taste) is 1.5X that given in the recipe.  2X is a little too much (but still delicious!)

This is such a good dish, bright & flavorful! Follow the recipe and don’t blanche the broccoli. I used farro as I thought that matched the size of the other elements a bit better than quinoa.  I skipped the cheddar as I didn’t have any,  I thought it was so delicious and I’ll make it again this week
Delicious! I added a marinated cooked and chopped chicken breast to it that I had in the fridge and it was even better!
I omitted the quinoa.

This is outstanding!  Lots of chopping, but so many healthy and tasty ingredients, it's my go to potluck dish and I always bring copies of the recipe to share knowing I'll be asked!  Have made it many times.
This was good! I steamed the broccoli and used goat cheese crumbles instead of cheddar. Also think it would taste better with just 2 Tbsp of Dijon. Will make it again!

Shelley…yes, I made this today with farro. 
It was delicious.

LOVED THIS!  Easy and healthy.
Made with what was on hand - bag of frozen broccoli (steamed in bag), no apple, no dried berries, barley instead of quinoa.  Made the vinaigrette as directed.  Absolutely delicious.

I love this salad. I had something similar in an Indian restaurant in London. That version did not include quinoa and the dressing was more mint than mustard but the general idea of raw broccoli “cured” in a vinaigrette with nuts and dried fruit - a fresh chili is nice too - is pure crunchy delightful deliciousness. I like it equally well with it without the cheese addition so I think you can suit your own tastes and diet preferences there. Make on Sunday night and have a healthy lunch all week!
Delicious as written! It makes a ton of food too. I halved the recipe with success. Perfect as a make-ahead lunch for the week following all the rich Thanksgiving foods.

Very nice recipe. I think it needs a bit more of the lovely dressing...about 1.5x more. Zingy cranberries are nice, but dried cherries are also really nice in this salad. Also delicious without the cheese.
I suggest reserving some of the dressing and adding it as needed at the end.

My husband and I loved this salad! Made it on a Thursday night when we knew we should have a vegetable. I am so glad I made it exactly as written. Just make sure you use a nice cheddar that’s been aged a bit. I had to supplement with a softer cheddar (someone had been snacking on the reserve cheddar) and the softer cheddar pieces don’t hold their form as well. Regardless I loved the cubed cheddar, it felt so special.
Just checking - is it 1 cup of quinoa before cooking or after?
Followed exactly except subbed sun dried tomatoes for the cranberries because that's what was available, and it worked nicely. Finely chopped broccoli did not taste or feel raw at all.

The second time that I made this recipe I doubled the dressing, chopped three smallish apples, and used extra-old sharp white cheddar. Chose a box of Casbah quinoa which cooks in two cups of water without all the palaver of the sieve in the recipe that I followed the first time. I always use dark maple syrup in place of honey in salad dressings which suits this recipe very well. It's a great recipe for a potluck. A keeper, for sure.
Took to a potluck and no one liked it. Just a bad combo of flavors. Raw broccoli was too pungent.

Please answer: The "1 cup quinoa, rinsed" implies the quinoa is a COOKED cup of (rinsed) quinoa. True or not? Those who said this recipe makes more than expected &/or that dressing / other ingredient ratios were off maybe measured the one cup of quinoa before cooking? It would be great to know the intention before I ruin my first attempt at making this.

I agree that it isn't clear (at least to me)! I made this today and used 1 cup uncooked quinoa, which yielded a LOT. So I dumped a hefty amount into the salad and saved some for possible use some other day/way.
I substitute short grain brown rice for the quinoa, and toast slivered almonds instead of pecans. It's delicious, and can't imagine it made any other way!
I cooked this as is except to add some chopped scallions to enhance the flavor. It makes a lot - at least 10-12 side servings.
This is the kind of recipe that you can either make as written or use as a springboard to create your very own version(s). Fun, delicious, and nutritious. Perfect for my dietary needs.
I am always looking for healthy travel  food and this is it!

I am a fan of everything that goes into this salad but together they make a mess of conflicting flavors bound together with honey mustard dressing which is cloying.

The salad would be better without the mustard dressing and few less ingredients: a simple 50/50 mix of seasoned rice vinegar and extra virgin olive oil would not mask any of the flavors but add some acidity and it so needs green or red onions to perk it up.  The cheese just gilds this pig’s breakfast.
I really thought this was a horrible recipe. I didn’t like the raw broccoli or the cheddar.
I made it exactly as written and it’s incredible! Going into rotation!
I used farrow instead of quinoa and it turned out great, and substituted almonds for pecans because we have a nut allergy, which was decent but did add a crunch

We had high hopes for this recipes, but unfortunately, It really disappointed. The flavors don’t meld, and it took almost an hour to prep (not so simple). We thought that it might improve overnight, but it’s just a bland combination of ingredients. I wouldn’t recommend this one.
This salad is delicious! Followed the recipe exactly and it did not disappoint. It came out much larger than I expected, and had to share the wealth with friends.
Used red quinoa.
Oh no!!  What does "season aggressively with salt" mean?  I'm a numbers girl....give me info in teaspoons or tablespoons!  Any suggestions on actual amount of salt?
To your taste. Honestly, as in all cooking. To your taste.

I made this and didn’t have cranberries, but subbed a few chopped dates. I also added some celery because my apple lacked the crunch that I hoped it would have. Regardless, this salad was excellent and I hope it becomes a regular staple in our house. It feels fun but still healthy. Thanks, Sohla!
This didn’t do it for me, sadly. It sounds great, no crazy ingredients. Unfortunately, something is missing. It made so much and I’m thinking about tossing it! Any recs on adding flavor? Dressing?

Delicious, but I thought there was too little dressing for these proportions, and I found the Dijon overpowering. I had even added more olive oil and lemon juice than the recipe called for. Next time I'll up both of those and use less mustard. Otherwise, quite yummy.
I added sunflower seeds because I didn’t have pecans.

This was tasty but needed a bit more brightness. I think a bit more dressing would do it. I increased the pecans and will add more apple to the leftovers. I did not add cheese. I like a salty thing in salads, but not cheese.
Great salad with lots of protein with the quinoa, pecans and cheddar. Use very sharp cheddar and don’t cut too small. Blanched the broccoli for a few minutes. Would do that again.
This was quite delicious. I only used the broccoli crowns not the stem. Used feta, walnuts and a mustard vinaigrette that I had on hand. I halved the recipe but it still made easily enough for four.

Stuck to the recipe. It makes a lot more than 6 servings and would be a good addition to a neighborhood pot luck (I'd guess enough for 20 to have a 3/4 cup serving). Not especially memorable, but pleasant enough to make once a year. I used Irish sharp cheddar & a Pink Lady apple.
Really yummy. Probably didn’t need 2tbs of honey as well as the apple and cranberry unless you like it really sweet.
This recipe is absolutely delightful, and I could eat it every day. Great for company! I agree that the raw broccoli is “cooked” by the lemon juice, and is not tough or bitter.

I felt that the proportions were way off. I used about half of the sauce ingredients. Cut back on the broccoli and oil. I substituted raisins for cranberries. Also cooked the broccoli. Makes for a good quick meal

Fantastic salad! I followed the recipe as written, allowed it to sit for 3 hours in the refrigerator, and both my partner and I were bowled over how flavorful it was - we ate the entire thing! I did make a little extra vinaigrette for mine, but my better half said his was perfect as is. I might try with toasted almonds on the next go-round. This will be on a regular rotation from now on.

This was absolutely delicious! I’m not a raw broccoli fan, but the flavors from the other ingredients really mellowed the strong flavor of broccoli. It’s also the first time I tried quinoa. I used a multicolored variety which made the salad look even more colorful!  Will make this many times.
Delicious, fresh, bright flavours for a bleak winter day. I subbed golden raisins for cranberries and added a grated shallot to the dressing. Will definitely make again!

I made this as written, although I cut it in half as we are just two people. The one exception was leaving out the cheese. It just didn’t seem to be worth the extra calories for me. My broccoli was chopped smaller than the photo and I agree, don’t par boil it. It was crunchy and delicious.

This recipe is delicious, and I look forward to making it for potluck dinners.  I was a bit suspicious of the raw broccoli, but in this recipe it doesn’t taste “raw.”  I even used organic ingredients.
It is 20 degrees today, I made the quinoa in a larger pot than necessary, then added all ingredients while the quinoa was hot, served lukewarm, what a difference it made on a cold january day!
Delicious! I used roasted broccoli and almonds instead of pecans. The salad had plenty of crunch from the apples and nuts, and was very flavorful.
I made this tonight and it was delicious. I changed the lemon to a lime because I didn’t have a lemon. Reduced the apple cider vinegar to 1 tablespoons. Reduced the quinoa to 1/2 cup.
Crunch, bursts of flavors with each bite , balanced , delicious and filling! What’s not to love about this salad?! Definitely adding this to the rotation.

So I'm doing a pantry challenge for the next two months and adapted this salad to what I has in the house and wanted to use up as well as just preferences. I roasted the broccoli at high heat and think this made a delicious salad even more so. I also subbed walnuts that I also toasted and dried cherries for the cranberries. I also threw in the rest of a jar of a lemony garlic salad dressing I had in the fridge as well as the dressing recipe. I can't stop eating this! Great texture and flavors!

Made as directed except I steamed the broccoli for 1 minute first (my digestive tract says no thank you to raw broccoli for some reason), used Pecorino/Romano cheese since I had no cheddar, and use Pistachios since I had no pecans.  Still an outstanding combination of flavors and textures, will definitely keep on my revolving list!
Filled recipe exactly and it was delish!  The broccoli gets “cooked” by the vinegar. I made and stored overnight and then ate. Perfect balance of sweet and tangy.
Followed recipe exactly, let broccoli marinade for about 1/2 hour and texture was perfect.
This was very tasting and filling, good for lunches for work. 1 pound of broccoli was sufficient.
GREAT salad- used candied pecans, oranges for vinaigrette (I didn’t have lemons) and skipped the cheese.
Great recipe. I’ve made it a couple of times. Now I leave out the quinoa or any grain because it soaked up the dressing. Fantastic without it! I also sub feta cheese.

Stellar salad; feels both indulgent and virtuous. Made subs based on fridge contents and allergy considerations: 1/2 kale and 1/2 broccoli, dried blueberries, toasted walnuts, and hemp hearts instead of quinoa -- this last one is great because they're thrown in as is, making prep even easier.
Delicious flavor but I found rinsing the quinoa after cooking it made it a bit mushy.  I’d leave out that step next time.  Made with manchego cheese.

I was suspicious when I came across this recipe but when my wife lobbied me to make it I gave in. I ran out of quinoa sono used buckwheat groats instead and was pleasantly surprised. It’s like gourmet reinvented picnic fare. I could make a whole meal out of this.
I zapped the broccoli in the microwave for 2 minutes before it went in the dressing. Just to take the edge off. Really tasty! I’ll cut the broccoli smaller next time.

This was REALLY good. The dressing is amazing.  I made a few modifications.....I microwaved the broccoli for a minute or two because I don't like raw broccoli.  I left out the cheese and cranberries, but I'll add them in next time, and there will be a next time!  But even with these changes, it was terrific.

This is crazy good…I’ve made it twice in the past week. The broccoli does not taste raw at all. It’s delicious and all of the different textures and tastes are fantastic together. I didn’t have cheddar so I subbed large shavings of Parmesan cheese which was also quite tasty (to me, anyway). Keeps well for a few days though the nuts get a little soft.

This was excellent! By a happy mistake I put in the warm quinoa - cooked it per instructions on container and didn't rinse under cold water - but that actually cooked the broccoli a tiny bit.  Will make this again and again.
Delicious! Perfect combination of textures and flavors.

I followed the recipe exactly and it was perfect. Love the broccoli raw…because it’s finely chopped, it’s a perfect crunch; in my opinion, cooking it beforehand, as others have suggested, would have made it mushy. The dressing is delish! I look forward to experimenting with different ingredients!
Delicious!

This was a near miss -- the grain was good with the broccoli (used medium bulgur), as were the other ingredients and the dressing, but the totality was not very good. Clark's broccoli salad with garlic and sesame is much better.
The first NYtimes recipe I found lacking. No one in my family would eat salad. Added 3/4 cup of mayo with about 1/4 cup of sugar…..then we all liked it.

Made a few substitutions but still turned out great. Couscous and candied walnuts are great, and a quick broccoli boil made it easier to chew. Paired with a salmon fillet and it was a great weeknight dinner.

This is delicious! The only changes I made were using sugar-free cranberries and very slightly steaming the broccoli before chopping it.  The dressing is excellent and very flavorful. I'm on the GoLo diet and this has it all:  protein, carbs, vegetables and fat.
This makes a ton! It would probably be fine to make a half recipe. It can certainly serve as the main course.

I'm so into this.  This recipe makes a ton of perfect lunch food for days.

I've made it twice exactly as written except for paring the zest off and then cutting it into tiny chunks instead of grating it; the lemon taste is just a bit more aggressive this way.
Raw broccoli is not my thing, so I roasted it before adding it.

I just made this for lunch and it was delicious. Just trust the raw broccoli -- it works (I already knew this because there is a raw broccoli salad from the WholeFoods deli I like called "broccoli crunch salad"). The dressing is amazing and the mix of textures is perfect. I didn't have pecans, so I used almonds. I'm thinking this would be a great salad to bring to lunch for work. Give this s try!

As others have noted, this was yummy.  I had to halve the recipe because it's just me.  I happened to have Unexpected Cheddar from Trader Joe's.  As someone suggested, it was wonderful.  The uncooked broccoli was fine.
I followed the recipe exactly except I added more dressing. This is fantastic!
This is delicious. Followed the recipe except used peanuts instead of pecans because I am allergic to tree nuts. I will grate the cheese next time like someone suggested.

Unlike some other NYTimes recipes, I think this made more than 4-6 servings. It has a lot of steps, but is easy to make - especially with a food processor - and very tasty. I decreased the mustard a bit, added more apple because I like it and toasted walnuts because that's what I had on hand (and I like them with apples). I'm waiting on the cheese, both because I like the taste as is and because I don't want it to get mushy in the leftovers. I might try adding some tomorrow.
Elaine made for lunch.  Huge recipe - make 1/2.  Use a prairie breeze” cheese - not sharp cheddar.
Made as written, and DARN good!!
We enjoy this exactly as written.
Delicious. Uses the Trader Joe’s Aleppo Pepper honey mustard vinaigrette and feta cheese. A , will be making again.

I loved this until I added the quinoa. Would make again and omit that entirely — the flavor of the broccoli, nuts, cheddar, cranberries and tangy dressing is very nice and the quinoa just seems to add unneeded bulk
Followed the exact recipe and it is delicious! It’s flavorful, satisfying, and wholesome —the perfect meal. The raw broccoli was just fine for me.

This is a salad I'm actually excited to eat! It has lots of great flavor (the cheese is very important here), though peeling the broccoli can be time-consuming. However, I wouldn't consider it a whole meal. Even after eating a whole bowl, I had to supplement with something else. I may add more quinoa next time.

Made for second time. I increased the nuts both times but otherwise, made as outlined. I like how you can use it as a base and add things for variation each time you eat a bowl. I added two fried egg on top for a meal (runny yoke), added mashed avocado, and another time added thick balsamic vinegar w/ fried egg for a punch up of flavor. All delicious.
Even hubby likes it! Sunflower seeds was only variation.  Winner!
I didn't make any improvements in the recipe. It was a hit with the family. Next time I won't make any improvements either!

BIG success for me! I enjoy the different textures and flavors of all the components. Kind of like a party in my mouth. :-)  I did make a teensy change: I doubled the dressing. As several others have remarked, I’m not a super big fan of raw broccoli, and it was a lot of broccoli when all chopped up. So with the extra dressing as it sat while I prepped and mixed in the rest, the broccoli was softer. Score another one for NYT Food! Thanks!
I made this exactly according to the recipe and we really liked it. Even my husband who is not usually a broccoli fan enjoyed it.
Delicious even if you cook the broccoli. Husband said no raw broccoli so we blanched it.   Also dividing in half still gives enough for two with a bit of leftovers
So so good!!  I made a few swaps - maple syrup (about 1 1/2 TBS) instead of honey, sliced almonds instead of pecans, and farro instead of quinoa…..I will DEFINITELY be making this dish all summer.
Super delicious!
Made exactly as described, subbing toasted almonds for pecans ( because it was what I had).  Surprising how quickly the dressing "cooked" the raw broccoli!  Excellent!

The method for cooking quinoa didn't work for me. After rinsing, I drained in sieve, pushed down a little to extract liquid, and even rolled it up in a kitchen towel to catch more excess liquid. It still had a waterlogged flavor (and I used a lot of salt in the boiling liquid). Added to the salad, it made the whole salad waterlogged tasting. Next time I'll cook quinoa a couple hours in advance the usual way, fluff it, and then let it cool with the lid off.

I don't think raw broccoli is a problem - ever. I chop raw broccoli into green salads for crunch and a bit of a bite on a regular basis. I eat the stem after peeling off the tough outer part and my dogs like it too. So to me this issue with raw broccoli seems odd. We eat raw carrots,  cucumbers, radishes and celery to name a few, so why is broccoli a problem?  BTW, this salad was the bomb!

I made a bit extra and wish I'd made even more dressing, although it marinates well over time. Unsweetened cranberries were an excellent choice here, keeping everything nicely balanced and hearty. Delicious.
Made this as written and wouldn't make it again. It was a weird combination of textures and flavors that just didn't work. Alas, it was quite visually unappealing as well.
Delicious and fully worthy of making again with no changes!
Made this for Cam and Gabe. Makes a ton! Good for potluck.
This was quite delicious as-is. No edits made! Others are correct that the acid “cooks” the broccoli so it does not eat raw.
Delicious, used a green apple and followed recipe exactly.
Delicious, makes A LOT. Made as suggested. If cheese is salty, leave out salt in dressing.

Not a fan of cooked broccoli so I was happy to leave it raw. I didn't have pecans so used walnuts. I would perhaps use a little less quinoa next time, although my head of broccoli was small so that might have been the issue. All in all loved this recipe.
No honey or sugar, added chopped red cabbage, no cheddar, used feta instead
Very good. I think Sue's comment will help next time.

I didn’t enjoy this until the next day, in my opinion it needed to sit and mellow out. Now I’ll probably make this regularly! Tip: cut up broccoli into tiny florets. I subbed feta for cheddar (it’s all I had) and it came out fine.
Nice combination of ingredients. I always cook/parboil broccoli after having a bad episode of food poisoning from raw broccoli. The dressing is delicious.
Absolutely delicious! Hearty and filling, paired beautifully with oven roasted salmon!

This was only ok in our house.  “Well, it’s healthy..” was the faint praise from my foodie husband. Adult children took small servings to be polite.  I made it exactly as directed, but added a bit more dressing when it seemed too bland for my tastes.  That really didn’t help much.
I used Ali Saigle recipe (https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1024861-quinoa) for cooking the Quinoa and that made the salad even more delicious, adding a delicate nutty flavor to it.

When I was at the grocery store and went past the deli counter I realized, YUCK, the salad I'm about to make looks a lot like the gloopy broccoli & cheese salad they sell there that has zero appeal. Still, I made it anyway, equating mass-produced anything to be far inferior to homemade. While the ingredients weren't exactly the same as the store-made salad, this just didn't work for me, my husband, or sister. I cut the vinegar to 1 tbsp, as I'm not a huge fan, but it just didn't work. Pass.
Endlessly adaptable!
Good idea but not great. Lightly steam broccoli. Too sweet. Use less quinoa and consider toasting it

I think the recipe as written was fine but somewhat bland. The next day I added crispy bacon and it was super. (Figured it was already not the healthiest dish given all the cheese, so why not?) Also, next time I make this I’ll try toasting the quinoa as one reviewer suggested.
I don’t know. I really wanted to like this but it was just bland. I know I used a little more broccoli than the recipe but I also increased the dressing and even added some chili sauce. Not a fan.
Not that delicious.  With so many stars I was expecting more flavor.

DELICIOUS, make it just as written. Easy to substitute if you wish later (I prefer dates to dried cranberries) but trust that this dressing brings it all together beautifully.  Even better the next day.

I bought a bag of broccoli florets and steamed them in bag for a couple mins just to soften them. Easy and they were already cut up. I didn’t cook them through just a little bit. Otherwise recipe exactly as written. Delicious!!!

Made this as written (with 50% sugar dried cranberries - we don’t notice a difference) and pulled all but the cooked quinoa together. Perfection! Served the quinoa on the side to meet family preferences. A delicious combination all around that will be made again. Benefitted from the juice of the full lemon.
Really good. I skipped the cheese.
Wonderful, bright flavor. Ir was a hit.

So fresh and tangy! I don’t generally care for raw broccoli as some others commented here. But the dressing truly softens it. In fact, I doubled the dressing for extra flavor. I didn’t have dried cranberries on hand but I always have a couple bags of frozen and so I diced up about 2/3 cup and tossed those in. I also took the time to toast my pecans. This is definitely going to be on repeat and on the potluck list, as well.
I thought this was delicious but then I again, I added bacon…. So my results are highly skewed
made as written, perfectly delicious!
Wonderful salad!  I used pomegranates instead of the dried cranberries, added a nice crunch and flavor.

This is one of the yummiest salads I've ever made. It's a lot of chopping but worth the effort. Way too much quinoa  tho - could probably half that and despite everyone's skepticism(including my husband's) the uncooked broccoli  is the right call.
Do you really have to rinse the cooked quinoa? I don’t have a sieve and wondered if it was necessary and why?

I accidentally measured 3tbsps of apple cider vinegar, but it tasted great to me so would probably do that on purpose. Letting the broccoli marinate in the dressing while prepping the other ingredients took the raw edge off of it for me. I did cut it up pretty small, which might have helped. I liked the cheddar, but am skeptical of how well it will hold up in the dressing after a day or two.

Made this as is, with the broccoli raw. It turned out great. chopped dried cherries, in lieu of cranberries, which worked well. Made it without cheese and it was excellent then added some TJ‘s unexpected cheddar. I’m sure this would also be great with Parmesan. Sending this round to all my friends who cook and like to eat healthy.
When does the zest come in?
Made this with farro instead of quinoa. Used my food processor to chop the apples and broccoli. Easy and absolutely delicious.
So very good!! Made as written.

One of my pet peeves is recipes with instructions to “salt to taste”.  Salting a dish is so important. A good recipe gives the reader a sense of how much salt is reasonable and he/she then can adjust to use more or less the amount.  This recipe is probably the worst “season aggressively with salt”?!!!  What the heck?  NYT, please please request your recipe developers to add specific measurements for salt.  Something like “1 teaspoon of Kosher salt or to taste” would be very helpful.  This is why I like Ina Garten’s recipes.  She gives specific measurements for everything including salt. 

Now on to the recipe itself, I like raw broccoli and quinoa and all ingredients used here.  However, I found in combination it’s only okay, not spectacular.  Id make it again but I’d leave out the cheese and just add it when I feel like it.

made this today and it exceeded expectations. definilyy don’t boil broccoli- just chop it up and it gives a great crunch. added sweet potatoe and that tied everything together for me and made it a hearty meal! i took out nuts and cranberries because i don’t like them and it worked perfectly
Delicious.  I can eat this for lunch for days and days.  I think it is much prettier with red quinoa!
I added juice from 1 1/2 lemons and I’m not sorry.

I was mistrustful of using raw broccoli but followed advise from a commenter and glad I did.  It was fabulous 
I was also mistrustful of using 3 Tbs of Dijon so used 1 Tbs. Next time I’ll go up to 1 1/2 Tbs.

This is fab! Subbed pomegranate molasses for the honey but otherwise followed the recipe (broccoli does not need to be cooked). A good use for the bags and bags of pecans in my freezer from my mom’s tree in Texas.

I made this following the recipe. Everyone loved it. Based on several comments, I almost parboiled the broccoli but decided not to. I'm glad I didn't. Next time, I would add a little less quinoa to enjoy a little more flavor from the dressing.
I can't eat dairy and have not found a good dairy-free alternative to sharp cheddar. Anyone here found something?

Excellent! Made as written except I substituted farro because I'm not a big fan of quinoa. I live alone, but made the entire recipe since it will keep well and I'll add other proteins to it for variety.

Yes!  Had to use a mix of cauliflower and broccoli and cilantro instead of parsley and feta vs cheddar to avoid last minute market run.  Still awesome!  For leftovers we keep adding more cheese (cheddar this time) and cranberries.  It’s the dressing that pulls it together.  Went liberal with the lemons.
Quite nice. Used what I jad i the hose which meant Havarti instead of cheddar.  Think the cheddar would add more interest. Generous with the pecans and cranberries'. Will be perfect summertime dish.
I added some thinly sliced red onions and let them marinate in the dressing while I got the rest of the ingredients together. They added nice flavor and texture!

recipe is a keeper. I made it as written, then tasted it and decided to double the dressing, which was a good idea. I used red quinoa, because that’s what I had, and I included the cubed cheddar, but it would have been great without it. To the commenters worried about raw broccoli - trust the process. You won’t be sorry. I served this last night alongside a roast chicken and my husband said, “this would be great itself in a wrap for lunch,” so that’s what we will do with the leftovers.

Great, easy salad. I tossed it and let it sit for an hour so the broccoli could soak in the vinegar a bit. It was perfect for a weeknight meal. 

Notes: I only used 1/2 cup of pecan bits and wish I had done 3/4. I wish I had used slightly less than the prescribed amount of water for the quinoa. I don't love a ton of dressing and this was the perfect amount for me. I didn't add cranberries because I don't like them.
I’ve made this a few times. It’s super satisfying and makes me look forward to lunches for the week! My only change is that I add a couple heaping spoonfuls of chili crisp to the dressing. So good!!

So good and so simple! Agree with everyone - leave broc raw like the recipe says. Chop it in small pieces, and the vinegar lemon juice dressing will soften it up nicely. I didn’t have dried cranberries on hand so I used raisins instead, and I left out the mustard because I’m not a fan. Also left out the cheese - seems unnecessary for a salad like this, but I might add it if/when I make this for a potluck.
love  raw broccoli@Sue

I dislike raw broccoli but it was really good in this salad. I marinated the chopped broccoli in the dressing a few hours ahead. I used bulgur instead of quinoa and that worked well. I think this would be an excellent Thanksgiving side for vegetarians.
This was shocking it's so good. I want to eat it every day. It lasts in the fridge for a week. Not quite as good as the first day, but still yummy
It was the oddly paired ingredients that drew me to try this recipe.  Wow!  Just delicious.  Will add to my favorite ways to sneak broccoli into my diet.

This recipe is a staple in my kitchen, although I only make mine with Farro rather than quinoa. Everyone who eats it raves about it, it’s easy to make, substitutions are plentiful, allowing me to use what I have in my fridge or pantry, if necessary. I can’t find anything to complain about!
I’m terribly allergic to mustard so miss out on wonderful vinaigrettes like this one. What can I substitute?

The salad makes 6 servings, with 455 calories per serving, with 25 grams of fat and 6 grams of saturated fat per serving; so fat is about 49.5% of total calories.

Has anyone tried it without the olive oil and cheddar cheese? From the ingredients, it looks like it would still be very tasty, and much lower in fat. And I liked the suggestion to blanche the broccoli first.
@Dr. J Yes. Cheddar is not necessary. I also made the quinoa crispy & added at the last minute. Just don’t leave out the lemon - it is key. Delicious
Quite delicious!  I did not have quinoa, but I did have some leftover brown rice. It was satisfying with the brocolli salad.
Used hot honey in the dressing.  YUM!!!

I was skeptical of the raw broccoli but I bought some very fresh from the farmer’s market and chopped it finely as if I was dicing onions. It was tender and really didn’t need cooking. Absolutely delicious and a great potluck or picnic dish!
I left out the cheddar & did not miss it. I also made the quinoa crispy in the oven & added it just before serving. This salad is delicious. It keeps well so you can enjoy it for days.
I added some celery and substituted brussels sprouts for broccoli and it was quite good.

Super yummy - a great bring to a potluck or picnic as it makes  a lot.  However, since finely chopped broccoli is NOT one  of the ingredients, beware - it will take 30 minutes to clean, peel and finley chop them for step 3.  NYT timing is really off here, taking you way beyond the 30 minutes prep time listed.
Sure, add as much stuff as possible till the taste of broccoli.

I used the Trader Joe’s farro- added about 2 cups cooked. Was great and made it hearty and more appealing to my spouse. 
This is excellent, balanced with the juice from the lemon at the end, and so much black pepper. Yum.
I always know Melissa Clark recipes are going to be good. Deep flavor, varied texture and nice acidity in this without needing much time to prepare. Plus it makes lots of leftovers for lunches.

I happened to make this recipe this week but used frozen broccoli because that is what I had.

The dressing was a little sweet for me and I reduced the honey by half but overall doubled the dressing.

Because I am on a very low sodium diet, I used low sodium mustard, did not add any additional salt, and left out the cheese.

Very good recipe.

I love this.  Easy and delicious.  I made it as per the recipe, other than adding in some diced orange pepper I needed to use up.  Wouldn't change a thing!  And I agree that parboiling the broccoli isn't needed here.  The broccoli is cut small and the dressing softens it up already so it's not as crunchy as if you were just eating raw broccoli.  

Will definitely make this again.

I made this using wheat berries instead of quinoa and feta instead of cheddar. It was a hit. The dressing completely tenderized the broccoli. It was a hit and I will definitely make it on a regular basis.
Feta! Yes, I was hesitant about cheddar myself.
Thoughts on switching out broccoli for Brussel sprouts? It is what I have on hand.

I make something similar with quinoa, black beans, red bell pepper, corn, parsley, and red onion. Of course you can mix it up by adding or substituting garbanzos or whatever sounds good.

Pro tip: If you make it (or this NYT recipe) WITHOUT any kind of dressing, it keeps a long time in the fridge. Just add when eating. Yes, as this recipe notes, with the vinaigrette it gets better for a while, but it also greatly reduces its shelf life (stated at 3 days). The one I make can go almost 2 weeks.

I didn’t have enough broccoli and added some shaved Brussels sprouts and sugar snap peas that I had on hand. Used crumbled goat cheese. It was delicious and I thought even better the next day. A keeper for sure.

I was skeptical of this salad, but oh my goodness it is delicious! All of the flavors go well together and the broccoli softens from the citrus and apple cider vinegar. The pecans add a crunchy texture and nutty flavor. It also made more than 4-6 servings, so now I have more to eat throughout the week!

Regardless of preparation, I've never found quinoa to be edible. It has a nasty taste that no amount of rinsing seems to wash away. Many things that people turn their noses up have been delicious in my experience, but not this. The idea of substituting wild rice sounds good and I'll give that a shot.
Delicious. I made it as directed and will make it again. And again.
Definitely no need to Blanche the broccoli, crisp and crunch is what this salad is all about

Delicious and riffable. Used goat cheese bc I had it (but definitely want to try cheddar). Raw broccoli taste was too much for my taste, even with dressing and after sitting a day or two. Next time I'll put broccoli in the strainer and drain the grain over it, or drop it in the pot just before draining. The quinoa kind of disappeared - farro or bulgar might work better here. I'll also try unsweetened dried tart cherries - I love them as an alternative to sugar-laden dried cranberries.

Additional notes... As others noted, the recipe makes a LOT - I've had it 4 days in a row and still have probably 1/3 of it left. Next time I'll halve it (or make it for a group). As written, dressing amount is just enough. If you prefer a "dressier" salad, you might want to make a little extra. Also, the pecans get soft in the fridge. If you're likely to have leftovers, hold the pecans to top individual servings so you don't lose the great crunch and toasty flavor.

Made it today. As others have mentioned, I also had 1.5 lbs of broccoli florets.... After I faithfully chopped it all up -  the resulting amount was ... frightening.. Still, I followed the dressing recipe exactly, thought it was inadequate for the mountain of broccoli... Added just about 2/3 of quinoa... Mixed in all the other ingredients except nut.. I was expecting some Miracle to occur ...
As I had expected, the resulting mix was pedestrian.. I've added a bit of garam masala to perk it up..
Meh. It has too many ingredients and together they yield a salad struggling between sweet & salty. It reminded me of the gloppy hippie salads one person always brings to a potluck. Next!
Try with twice the lemon and half the honey
best. cheese. ever. Great call @Em !
This is amazing. I made as directed ( added a small shallot, cuz why not). Family loved it, not as many leftovers as hoped for.

This is truly fantastic! I made it exactly as written, although I was momentarily daunted by 
"season aggressively with salt," so I decided to start "timidly" with a rounded half-teaspoon, which was fine to my taste. I might suggest changing the number of servings to 6-8, especially if you serve this alongside something else. That said, this salad is soooo good that second helpings are likely.

Really good salad! Served it at brunch along with a heavy egg dish. Our guests loved it. Appreciate that it’s naturally gluten free. I’m not a fan of raw broccoli but this works. We made it yesterday and chilled overnight. Perfect!

Blanch the broccoli stems! I used Cabot gouda and chopped dates in it since that's what I had and YUM. Great lunch but also great side dish with some pan-seared pork chops & a little extra mustard vinaigrette.
Fabulous salad!!
I did not think this was going to be that good based on the ingredients but OMG, you really should try this. It is absolutely delicious. 5*
Too much going on in this salad. And not enough pay off in taste. I ended up throwing it out. And that was a lot of food wasted!

I was a little doubtful when I decided to try this but we really liked it.  I cut the quinoa back to 3/4 cup and used a bit less broccoli but it still made a big bowlful. Had it with dinner and several lunches
When I read this recipe, I thought “Gosh, these things shouldn’t go together.” Made the dish and proved myself correct.

I’ve made this recipe a few times and it doesn’t fail. Colby Jack worked perfectly this time. This dish is comfort food to me and it’s a brilliant alternative whenever I’m craving something “nasty” (like fast food). It somehow amuses the child in me —who’s always loved broccoli— and this makes me want to cook it for my nephew.

This salad is bright, fresh, and satisfying. I reduced the olive oil by 1 tablespoon, and used juice from whole lemon. Because I chopped the broccoli and apple in small pieces, the fully mixed salad fit nicely on the spoon. Salad is perfect for the warm weather.

Delicious. I used buckwheat groats instead of quinoa and feta cheese instead of cheddar. The broccoli was softened (by the sauce?), didn't seem raw. The whole family loved it including the septuagenarians and the toddler. It's now on our weekly rotation.
Add chick peas

Half the recipe was the right amount for 2 people and to have left overs. It took 45 minutes to make. It took longest to trim/cut the broccoli into little bit size pieces. No need to chop the broccoli after that.
Much better using asparagus and Feta cheese
Made exactly as written. Wanted to like it - I like each of the ingredients and I liked the dressing but together it just didn't seem to work for me or my husband.

This is a winner! I missed the note about chopping the broccoli finely but even with larger pieces I would not recommend parboiling as some other reviewers have suggested. Made a few subs based on what I had on hand - farro instead of quinoa, almonds instead of pecans and I left out the cheese to make it dairy free. Super delicious and I can tell it's going to be even better tomorrow!

A bowl of utter bliss! Seriously, the best raw veggie salad I have ever made, and I've made a lot. I cut the amount of quinoa (half a cup was all I found in the pantry), and honestly, I think I'll do it that way next time, too. Perfect. Also, next time, I think I might omit the cheese. Didn't really seem necessary.
I omitted the cheese and used currants instead of cranberries and short grain brown rice instead of quinoa. Delicious!  The raw broccoli and apple give this salad a great texture.

I too was scared of raw broccoli so I roasted it a bit. I also left out the cheese because it's everywhere and I'm trying to eat less. And I cut back on some of the quinoa. Liked it better than I thought I would and will try it with the raw broccoli next time. Definitely will also play around with it. The dressing is really good.
A bit fussy for us with all the fine chopping, which indeed came out nice, but next time we’ll probably be more lax about that part and I’m sure it’ll still be delicious.

What is this amazingness?!?!  Made the recipe without substitutions and as directed. Highly recommend.  It makes a big portion so can feed for days or great for a gathering.  I see many seasons in this salad...might even be amazing with some crunchy bacon but that could just take it over the edge.

Y’all give the raw broccoli a shot. It makes the texture of the entire salad.
I only had the florets so it was a bit annoying to get rid of the rough skin. I would add more lemon next time
Love this recipe! I use the dressing for everything now
This is delicious and makes a lot!

I made this with 1 1/2 # of broccoli as recommended, and it was enough servings for at least 12 people. I doubled the dressing, and it was still too dry. I think maybe a parboil on the broccoli could help...

Late to the party but wanted to chime in to agree with everyone who said that this was scrumptious, that you can sub nuts, that you do not need to cook the broccoli, and that it took longer than the time suggested. All in all, a revelation of a salad!
Delicious! I left out the cheddar cheese - not needed.

This was a great recipe.  In the rotation now. With the exception of fish and eggs, my diet looks more like that of plant-based.  I added chopped chick peas (I'm not a big chick pea lover, hence I chop them up and mix in!) for added protein and used more pecans and dried cranberries ...because I just like them!  Thanks for a great recipe.

Delicious as is.  We made it into a main dish salad by adding 2 tins of drained, rinsed and dried black beans.  Next time I add beans I will double the dressing.  Without cheese (or honey for strict vegans), it is delicious vegan meal.

Delicious!   Hubby agreed.   I didn’t have any apples, so substituted a few dried apricots for sweetness, and a bunch of chopped up French breakfast radishes for crunch.  Also, since it is Springtime, I included some chopped up garlic chives!   Agree it is too much g quinoa, so used the extra later.  Nice flavors and varying textures make this a very interesting salad!
This was so good. We all loved it. I ended up using barley because that’s what I had and it worked out great. Will definitely keep this one around.
Excellent! Made this for a potluck at Jitendra & Ellen's; everyone liked it. Don't double the recipe; it's too hard to mix that much quinoa and broccoli. It makes enough to serve 8-10 as is.
I really enjoyed this, but it needs to be eaten pretty quickly. It makes a lot so be prepared for that.

I’ve made this twice now because I want to love it, but the combination of ingredients just didn’t work for me. It was weird and just okay. It results in a huge quantity, which we likely won’t finish.

I made as directed last year, loved it and reviewed it, but tonight I tried a different version of my own. With yellow zucchini fresh from the garden instead of broccoli, quartered, sliced, grilled in grill pan and added to the warm quinoa. Omitted apple, nuts and cheese. Added julienned basil, halved cherry tomatoes, and sliced Klamath olives. For the dressing, omitted honey and used balsamic instead of apple cider vinegar. Very Mediterranean and a nice riff!
I really enjoyed this. Unfortunately I chopped the ingredients a little too finely to really taste any one specifically, but the texture is lovely with a nice chew.  It makes A LOT!

I seem to be sensitive to quinoa so I substituted freekeh for the quinoa. Otherwise followed the recipe exactly. Served some to my sister and she loved it too—this is now in regular rotation at our homes. Excellent recipe!
Just curious about how or why the apple doesn’t brown if the salad is made in advance or kept as leftovers. Thanks!
@Stephanie it’s the acidity of lemon juice and vinegar that helps the apple to maintain its original color
This salad slaps. It’s going into my regular rotation. Good with or without the quinoa. Otherwise, don’t change a thing.
This was great! I only had frozen broccoli so used that, and it was too wet (my fault). Definitely making this again with raw broccoli. Used buckwheat, really good! Farro would be great as well.
Delicious! Easy on the lemon juice and vinegar
I have made this several times.  I agree with other contributors -- 1.5 x the dressing is perfect.  I have made it with and without the cheddar and I prefer it without.
We roasted the broccoli first.

I've made this salad (without cheddar) several times. The quinoa and broccoli soak up the dressing, making it rather dry by the next day.  I am now doubling the dressing recipe, and keeping half of it in a separate glass container to add to the salad as needed.

I made this a couple of weeks ago and it was delicious. I thought the recipe called for way too much mustard but my husband insisted on trying it as written and it turned out he was right.  The flavors meld together and none of the individual flavors overwhelms the others. I would note that this recipe makes a lot of salad,-8 standard side servings at least. The leftovers are just as great as when originally served, but for the two of us, it took days to finish it off.
Great salad and I will be making it again.  Thank you.

I am confused. The recipe indicates to finally chop the broccoli, apple, and cheese but the picture shows larger chunks of broccoli and what it looks like small cubes of cheese. These ingredients do not look like they were finally chopped. Did everyone finally chop or did you do as shown in the picture?
I made this recipe as instructed and I genuinely could not taste anything other than mustard. It completely overwhelmed every ingredient. Never again

I love this salad and make it on the regular (though I'm often too lazy to zest the lemon). Some twists I enjoy to mix it up are replacing the quinoa with buckwheat kasha and using dried blueberries instead of cranberries

Wonderful kid salad. Dubliner cheddar works perfectly as it blends well with the Granny Smith Apple in aesthetics. A white cheddar prevents this from reminding/tasting/looking like melted velveeta on broccoli.
Absolutely delicious. My partner made it a couple weeks ago and I’m glad she did. This is a really good salad for hot summers and it’s surprising how refreshing the dish is.
Does anyone have a quantity of chopped broccoli in cups instead of weight they recommend?

2nd time making - so good! I cut the quinoa a bit and add extra lemon.  Otherwise made as per recipe which is very unusual for me :-).  I let the broccoli “cook” in the dressing while making other items - it’s cooked without the stove! Also we add lentils on the side to this salad for extra protein.
simple, healthy, delicious. Thank you!
Really really good, and yes a bit addictive. Perfect for work week meal prep. Made as written.
Chop broccoli more finely than picture -- almost into sand. Don't need to par-cook the broccoli this way. Finely diced apples, cheese, nuts until almost quinoa size.

Wow!  Better than I expected.  The dressing is great and the salad is yum.  Agree with others that the broccoli doesn't have to be cooked in advance.  In fact, I think that would detract from it.  Chop up fine and it softens in the dressing.
Typically I'm not a fan of the pungent flavor of raw broccoli, but the vinegar, lemon & mustard collectively neutralize the intensity of it, and the salad as a whole is delicious.
I made this salad today, lightly blanching the broccoli and substituting blue cheese for the cheddar. I also added dried tarragon to the dressing. YUM!

This is absolutely delicious. The mustardy dressing really makes the dish. I followed the recipe exactly, with the exception of the suggested swap of farro for the quinoa. It was a very welcome change after the heavy dishes over the holidays.

It’s New Years Day and I saw this recipe when I wondering what to do with little bit food on the fridge and a long day of putting away Christmas decorations, the tree etc. I had to make some adjustments and substitutions but it was still yummy.
I had half the amount of broccoli between regular and broccolini so I halved the recipe, substituted walnuts for pecans, and used more apple for the cranberries.
My partner wanted “protein” so I thawed frozen shrimp, sautéed it in a bit of lemon infused olive oil  and we had that on the side. 
Made the dressing first and let the chopped up raw broccoli marinate while I did the rest - yum. I also didn’t  cool the quinoa or mix it in - we just scooped some onto our plates and and put the cold salad on top. It was really good like that
Next time I’m going to try it with feta
Added to the rotation as a fav!
@Em that's exactly the cheddar I paired it with! quite yummy
Borrowing from the Brits, “unimprovable”.

This was really good.  I reserved a little of the dressing and brushed it on some salmon before roasting, then served the salmon and salad together.  

The salad was a little bit sweeter than I typically prefer.  Next time I’ll probably reduce the honey a bit.  Overall really good though.

Tried this last night and wasn't a fan. I should have read the comments on parboiling the broccoli as the raw flavor was overwhelming. There was no flavor to the quinoa at all and I felt there wasn't enough dressing for the salad. I added more dried cranberries, oil and the rest of the lemon later which helped. I think maybe a champagne vinegar would be better than apple cider vinegar. My largest complaint isn't related to flavor at all. Finely chopping the broccoli makes a huge mess!
Loved this. I  blanched the broccoli, used honey crisp apple and sharpe white cheddar. The salad makes a hefty amount.
What a great chopped salad! I ate it as a light dinner and loved it! I definitely wouldn’t recommend parboiling the broccoli, as the crunchiness and crispiness are key in this recipe.

Great taste! I used a food processor to chop most of the ingredients and next time I will chop it by hand because it chopped the ingredients too finely and I would like to have more of a crunch when I eat it. I also added chopped bacon and it was delicious!

I find the recipe good as is! No need for parboiling the broccoli, if you chop it fine enough, the vinegar is enough. I substituted farro for quinoa, but I’d probably reduce it down to half a cup of grain next time. Also used golden raisins and toasted walnuts because that’s what I had on hand instead, and these were fine too. Good recipe for meal prepping for office lunches. Thinking of maybe adding bacon or thin pork belly or shredded chicken for more protein next time too.
Great dressing! I didn't mind the raw broccoli, but overall the flavors didn't work for me.

4 ounces was plenty of cheddar for me. I doubled the dressing (because I think it needed it), and doubled the apple (because I wanted it). Soooo satisfying, and great to have in the fridge. Don't worry about the broccoli being raw!
I also followed the recipe as is and found it to be very delicious. The texture of the raw broccoli was perfect after marinating in the dressing for a little bit.
This salad is so good, you can take it anywhere and know you are coming home with an empty bowl.
next time I make this I'll half the mustard, it was making my nose run and eyes water as it was just too much. yum otherwise though!
Less honey , quinoa and less mustard

Made as written once with the cheddar, then another time substituted 4oz of goat cheese. Even my goat cheese skeptical family members much preferred it the latter way - we found that the cheddar, even good sharp cheddar, got lost and the recipe really benefitted from goat cheese's creamy tang.

I love this salad.  I do steam the broccoli briefly before chopping in the food processor.  I double the apple and increase the cheese, cutting it in small cubes (the sharpest cheddar you can find). And my small mighty secret:  add 1 capful of vanilla extract to the dressing. You will not regret it.

Quite fast you have a delicious meal, this is a keeper. Perfect for a potluck too. Instead of cranberry I used dried cherry, it was delicious! A perfect Mariahs between ingrideants. Also added a small can of lentils and instead of cheddar I used a matured Gouda. Was afraid of using raw broccoli but I am so happy I tried it!
This is so yummy! Made it without the apple due to an allergy, otherwise as written.

Great to make ahead for lunch all week. I used dried apricots instead of craberries because that's what I had on hand. Will be fun to play around with different nut, fruit, cheese and grain combinations.

At first, I thought this recipe was too much chopping, but all the flavours worked so well together. I especially loved the addition of the cheese, and yes, the broccoli doesn't taste raw. I have no idea how the transformation happens!

Tasty, but took me well over an hour to make, not 30 minutes as stated in the recipe. Lots of cleanup time. I steamed the broccoli slightly, toasted and chopped the pecans, used pepper jack cheese, as I had no cheddar, and added a small amount of chopped chicken breast to add more protein and make this a main dish salad. I liked this method of cooking quinoa, which I've never tried before. Worked well. The recipe made a huge amount!
This is FANTASTIC on Day 2. But know, lotta chopping. I only made half the recipe and the chopping was such I wasn’t sure if I’d ever make it again, but Day 2 persuaded me. Made exactly as written.

I switched out the quinoa for bulgur partially because I had that on hand and also because my people don’t love quinoa. Chopping things properly is the key to this recipe. It was delightful with a grilled salmon on the patio. Thank you!
Note for future: add more lemon to taste, depending on how much broccoli you had time to chop up!

I made this and it was delicious.
I didn’t  have enough broccoli  so I added chopped curly kale . 
I used a little over half of the called for quinoa as I prefer the greens to the grains.
I will make again.
Perfect. I used broccolini, the only change. Everyone loved it.
This was not great.  Way too much!!!!  And not all that delicious. Made 4/26

Make the recipe as written. It is that good!!!!! And the acids break down the broccoli so it's not too hard on your tummy. Tastes best on day 2. We leave out the cranberries because I don't like them... but other than that, we make it as written!
Yummy, used what I had on hand too. Used 5T honey mustard as I was out of Dijon.

I make this on repeat. It is delicious. My modifications: no honey, add small chopped shallot. Definitely use crispy quinoa - cook it then crisp it in over @ 400. Add when served. Do NOT forget the lemon. Save some dressing to add as the week goes on; add crispy quinoa. This salad lasts in the fridge. Delish!
Pretty good! Easy, light Sunday supper. I like the crunchy raw broccoli.

This is great - making it on repeat to have a veggie snack option in the fridge. I didn't get around to toasting and adding pecans the first time and still loved it. Lasts all week in the fridge. We used dried cherries because it's what we had and now I'll always use them for this recipe. I might try adding some white beans next time.

Great leftovers for lunch as well. I add cheese as we go, and sometimes not at all, as I do limited dairy. Lactose intolerant but love cheese! I have made it twice once with pstachios and dates as I didn't have the correct nuts and dried fruit.

Made this with faro as we were unexpectedly out of quinoa. And I left out the cheese since my husband can’t have it. Wonderful! I agree with the earlier commenter that mentioned the dressing “cooking” the broccoli. It was perfect!

I am an outlier, I didn't care for the recipe. Too much cheese (which I would reduce by at least 75%), not enough apples or brightness (tartness). On paper it should work but, it didn't for me.  My wife also agrees with me (and she has a better palate than I do.).
I substituted farro for quinoa, toasted slivered almonds for pecans, dried cherries for the cranberries and sherry vinegar for the apple cider vinegar.  Very tasty !
@Elizabeth thanks for the idea of using dried cherries!  I love dried cranberries (and this recipe), but variety is nice!
We love this broccoli salad so much. We make it almost exactly as directed (swap raisins for cranberries) and it is perfect.

I made the recipe exactly as is.  My teenagers all consumed it without complaint. A winner!
The one thing I might add is I thought it tasted the best on the first day.  On the second day the individual ingredients became indistinguishable taste wise.
Salad so good it reminded me it is life worth living after all.
Rinse the Quinoa after you cook it?  That seems weird.

I've made this multiple times, both exactly as written an with variations (Jarlsberg instead of cheddar,  cashews and/or almonds instead of walnuts, rice or orzo instead of quinoa). All fantastic. Two tips: cut the broccoli and get the sauce on it first so it begins to soften while you're prepping the rest, and cut everything quite small so you can get some of all of it on your spoon. Great recipe!

I wondered about the raw broccoli, but kept faith and cut it into pretty small pieces and tiny florets and it was delicious. Absolutely no need to cook the broccoli - unless you prefer it that way. I loved the textures and flavors; it was full of fresh and bright flavor. Loved!
Made this exactly as written with Trader Joe's Unexpected Cheddar but eliminated the quinoa. Delicious as a side dish; easily fed many more than the 4 to 6 servings suggested.

My mom made this with couscous and added shredded rotisserie chicken for a succulent non-vegetarian version.  The broccoli was beautifully "cooked" after the salad marinated for several hours in the fridge.
can I use frozen broccoli and hust microwave it for a couple of minutes ? its all i have at home

I like the simplicity and adaptability of this recipe. I cut the honey down to one T or so, and subbed applesauce for 4 T of the oil. I added a touch extra to make up for the moisture lost by cutting back the honey. Turned it tasty and plenty sweet for me.

I followed this exactly and I am an experienced cook. These look good but have little to no taste. Its not sweet, you can't taste the peanut butter. The salt on top pushes it away from sweet to... neutral. Perhaps this is for "special" diets. It feels like food-by-committee. Just don't. Really.

@American in Paris yes, so I’m looking forward to making these for my toddler, as well as myself as I’m in post partum recovery in need for something quick, high fiber and no dairy due to infant dairy sensitive. We’re very “special.” Wouldn’t touch these otherwise.
@American in Paris I felt the same and I’m an experienced cook, too. Only my dog likes them.

@American in Paris same here! I was so confused how all these ingredients that sound like they should go well together just turned out so blah. And the texture is not great either - I think it should’ve had some baking powder.
Chocolate chips made a great addition..and when do they not!

@Miranda agreed!!
I added chocolate chips, shredded coconut, and a third banana. Delish.

Followed recipe, used almond butter in place of peanut butter. No overarching flavor, even the banana gets lost somehow. So bland I looked over the ingredient list several times to make sure I added everything.

Agree with comments these are too salty. Recommend halving the salt, or even less. Like the texture and found them reasonably tasty. I'll make these again, but with less salt and perhaps adding raisins or dried cranberries.

I took to heart many of the comments that these are quite bland, with no discernible flavor profile. So I doubled all the spices, used about 5 tablespoons of honey and used toasted hazelnuts on top. Still, after all that extra love given, these are godawful and should never be made! This recipe should be binned, just like the batch I made.

Reduce olive oil to 2 Tablespoons 
      .Apple sauce  4.5 Tablespoons 
Reduce honey to 1 Tablespoons
I ignored the negative reviews and made them anyway.  They just don't taste good.  Even with less salt and added coconut I thought I could beat it, but no.
The amount of salt in my opinion ruined this recipe for me.  Couldn't eat it.  I would add half or a quarter of what the recipe called for.

This was so good we made another batch three days after the first one! Added a tablespoon of chia seeds to amp up the protein. I don’t get the other comments about too much salt, bland, etc. This recipe is legit.

Use bananas that are so ripe they're almost liquid in the peel (takes about a week). Reduce honey and oil to 3 tablespoons. Only 1/2 cup of almond butter. Add a scoop of protein powder. Triple the spices and vanilla (use pumpkin spice instead of plain cinnamon). Add plenty of nuts.

This gets you the perfect "healthy" cake base. I make this weekly with a variety of flavor variations (apple coffee cake, brownie chocolate chip, carrot cake, pear tart, ect.)

I found the cook time on this long so I’ll make it again as breakfast cookies.  I agree with what other people said that they’re bland, I would add some raisins, craisins, and/or chocolate chips next time.
Based on other reviews I skipped the salt. I love this recipe. Easy, tasty, perfect for a breakfast bar.) If you’re looking for something else, try a pastry.)

Made these the minute I saw the recipe--great fit with what I needed to use up!. Halfway in, I realized I had lost my 8X8 baking pan so made in a round pan and cut it into wedges.  Was it good? We gobbled the first two pieces, so yes.
These were super disappointing. Was hoping for something a bit more flavourful with so many expensive ingredients.
It’s just amazing how tasteless these were!  The flavor of each ingredient canceled each other out. I won’t bother trying it again.

After reading all of the other comments, I took a chance and made some adjustments. I substituted 4 tbsp of Greek yogurt for 4 of the tbsp of olive oil and used a neutral oil for the other 2 tbsp instead of olive. I cut the honey and salt in half and mixed the walnuts into the batter. Overall it turned out well.

Thank you to those suggesting to cut back the salt. I only added 1/2 tsp and it seemed perfect. 

Besides that I reduced the olive oil to 1/4 cup (my bananas were large rather than medium) and added raisins and chocolate chips and chopped walnuts (rather than on top) to the batter. I used peanut butter for the nut butter. And it’s true that they don’t taste overwhelmingly like bananas or peanut butter, but maybe that’s not a bad thing! The flavors just blend together for something unexpected and more subtle.

My whole family really liked these. I took them out of the oven 3 hours ago and they are almost gone. I thought they were in fact “fudgey” and sweet and healthier than a lot of other bar or cake recipes. I will definitely make them again.

Omg I made this based on the four star rating before I read the reviews. Wish I had bothered to read the reviews. I am usually forgiving of “okay” recipes and eat them anyways but this is bad. Something is very wrong with this recipe. I threw the whole pan out and am annoyed that I wasted all those ingredients. Oh, I did sub maple syrup for part of the honey because I ran out of honey…so if the honey was key to making this work…well, I don’t know.
@Ama right?? How does this have 4 stars?!
Nope, I used honey. Just bland, not worth all the tasty ingredients
I should have read the reviews - these are so bad. They’re salty and tasteless. My husband asked if I accidentally plastic into them. I refuse to waste food so I will eat them… resentfully.
@Emily I doused them with maple syrup and choked them down of the next few days because, like you, I didn’t want the food to go to waste. But, never again will I make this recipe.

@Emily I too don’t waste food I make. I chopped the bars, froze them in a pot (they seperate frozen) and added a piece or two to porridge (oatmeal) in the morning. As they’re frozen, cook porridge 2 minutes less and increase liquid by 1/4 cup, add more spices (1/8tsp good pinch), 1/2tsp sugar, pinch of salt or salted natural peanut butter to flavour extra liquid. I enjoy “bulked out” porridge, because I like bold flavour but I’m indulgent when it comes to breakfast

This is the second time Ive made these bars, I encourage all of you who may have read the negative reviews to give them a go.  We love these, perfect for a ski day snack or with a cup of coffee.  Used 1/4 cup honey, dash of maple syrup, one egg, coconut oil instead of olive,  1.5 tsp Morton salt, 1T cinnamon, 1/4 tsp nutmeg, and a good shake of shredded coconut.  Double the oats to soak up the extra batter, and added a good amount of ground flax seed as well.  Sprinkled pecans on top, no salt.
Well this is hardly the recipe, is it? As is, they're pretty bad.

@Feeding 5 

I appreciate the effort in the modification friend, but if you have to completely rewrite the recipe to make it palatable then it’s a terrible recipe. 

That being said your modifications do sound like an improvement. 

If enough people try it and comment maybe it’ll get bumped to the top of the reviews and save a lot of people the disaster of cooking this recipe as written.

These are just awful. They aren’t sweet at all. The banana flavor is lost, the peanut butter doesn’t come through, the salt is way too heavy. The recipe says they should be fudgey, but instead they are a fall apart crumbling mess. Don’t bother trying this recipe.

Other commenters call these “bland” but I would call them perfect for kids.  We made a few modifications based on preference and availability of ingredients :

- used 3 T of honey instead of 6
- 1 tsp Morton salt
- 1 1/2 cup oats (we used quick oats)
- 2 1/2 tsp cinnamon
- Added 2/3 cup flour (try whole wheat next time)
- Added chocolate chips (a must!)
- Skipped the nuts and extra salt topping

Perfect kid breakfast bars

I made these bars according to the recipe; the bars turned out well, but need the maximum baking time or a little more.  I also made these bars with an egg substitute for a vegan family member and the results were the same!  My celiac husband, who is not a big fan of baked goods really enjoys these  bars!

I used almond butter and overripe bananas. I thought these were good as a gluten free option for someone who is avoiding gluten and comes over often. I am a little surprised by the disappointing reviews. Regarding saltiness, it is important to note that Morton kosher salt is used in a lesser amount than Diamond Crystal. I don’t know if that was why some had an issue with the bars being too salty or not. Using Morton, I went with 1.5 tsp of salt. These are a breakfast bar, so they are not super sweet and they really aren’t intended as dessert despite their appearance. I would make them again for sure.

I had some overly ripe bananas and was looking for something other than banana bread to make - I got more than halfway through the recipe and then starting reading the comments and boy was I worried. I doubted myself and hastily chopped up some dark chocolate to salvage it. I didn’t need to! I used almond butter and chopped almonds and left out cinnamon. Otherwise I made the recipe word for word and it was great. Even my husband who is a hard sell on healthy things exclaimed how good it was. The things I think really matter - very ripe bananas, rolled oats, and a dry nut butter. It came out with a dense bake like texture and was neither too salty nor too sweet. My almond butter was 100% almonds and almost chalky in texture because I had it upside down in cabinet and I didn’t mix the oil in knowing there was a considerable amount of olive oil in the recipe. I would 100% make this again - the adults loved it and the kid loved it, doesn’t even need the chocolate!

I read the reviews and made adjustments to deepen the flavours (reduce salt, replace honey with maple syrup and dark muscovado sugar, use a spice blend of cinnamon/cloves/nutmeg to bring out banana flavour - see Stella Parks banana bread, mixed 50/50 salted almond butter with unsalted tahini for more flavour?, added pulsed oats (quick oats) because I had a wet batter, no surprise with no flour!!). In the end this recipe is deeply problematic, nothing can fix it flavour or texture. JUST TERRIBLE

I used almond butter, cut back on salt because of reviews, 1 tspn, added 1/2 cup almond flour super fine, just curious, and semi sweet chocolate chips. It is a great recipe, easy to make, healthier alternative to a banana muffin. It’s moist, packs a protein punch and great snack to hold you up in between meals :-). Pour batter into 24 little muffins. If you want more calories and sweetness haha throw a crumble on top. Thanks for all the great reviews, super helpful

I very rarely leave reviews even though NYT cooking is my go to site.     I made these based on the ingredients and that I am living on a boat in the south pacific and have too many bananas almost all the time.  I was shocked by all the negative reviews.   These are great and good for you and a nice pick me up in the middle of the day.  I cut them into 16 squares and find it is a much better size.   I did cut down on the honey a bit but otherwise - made them as written.    Thank you Sohia
I wanted to love these. Everything that goes into them is something I love. The bake seems good, and they smell great. But the taste is… strange, and definitely not good. Disappointing.

I read through the comments just after baking these bars and grew very nervous as I read through reviews remarking on their blandness and saltiness.  I tasted one and they're exactly what I was expecting them to be - a not too sweet, nutty breakfast bar.  I could still taste the nut butter and banana, it all blended really well. I did take into account to reduce the salt (use Morton Kosher not Diamond) and the 15 minute wait time for the eggs to work their magic with the banana.

I would bet most people made these without a scale. I measured out all the ingredients following how many g or ml indicated, and it seemed way off from the cup, tbsp, tsp amounts. I followed the recipe exactly and it came out great, full of flavor!

These are not great. Super oily. Lots of honey — there’s got to be a better choice. Was waiting for that ‘fudgey’ texture, but none. Don’t normally use salt but this time I’m looking for it. Two banans is abt 212 g.

I don't know what all the hullabaloo is over this recipe. I made them and they're really good.  They do have a 'fudgy' texture and they are 'satisfying', as described. If you want to cut down on the salt go-ahead, I might do next time but there is nothing overpowering in this recipe and I didn't find them bland at all. It really hit the spot and I will absolutely make it again.

delicious and super filling. you people swapping out the oil are crazy. they have a great texture and sweetness. they taste like a combo of banana bread and salty peanut butter. so perfect for taking to work if trying to avoid packet snack food.

The minute I took it out of the oven, it burst with flavor. The walnuts are crunchy and not overly sweet. It's a keeper and I like that it is loaded with nut protein. I won't tweak or change anything.

I made these and they were tasty, but I made some changes. 

I didn’t see all salt comments before make mine, but luckily I forgot to add the salt to my batter! My peanut butter was plenty salty, so that took care of things. I am guessing the issues with the salt level relate to the type of salt people are using (the author uses Diamond Kosher with is MUCH lighter than other salts), and nut butters vary significantly on saltiness. So, my recommendation is to just use salted peanut butter. I did put Maldon on top. 

I made three other variations that: I complete eliminated the olive oil. It just seemed superfluous to me with all the peanut butter fat. I am guessing it is adding a strong flavor that, as some cooks noted, overwhelmed the peanut butter and banana flavors.  I also used maple syrup (100g) instead of honey and used both cinnamon and ginger.

These aren’t huge changes, but they probably improved the flavor significantly. Texture is great—no meed for the extra fat from the olive oil.
Thank you. I will sub the eggs with a vegan alternative.

I absolutely adore Sohla and have employed the egg-banana method in other recipes. That said, this recipe should be flagged for removal. These were awful and I will not make them again. I’d also say they’re “squidgy” rather than “fudgy.” My preschooler and husband were both displeased, so these became compost.

The finished product was something that maybe you tried from a blogger in the early aughts who doesn’t test recipes but had an idea turn out once and then tried to remember the steps.
Terrible recipe.  It tastes like a lightly sweetened, bland, oily, salty mess.  Do not try to adapt the recipe, make something else.

Perhaps it was the ingredients I used or the fact that I like food that is less sweet, but these came out great for me. Here’s what might have done the trick:
• I only have quick oats on hand, so they probably picked up moisture pretty quickly and blended nicer.
• Teddie peanut butter with ground flax seeds. I can see how a lightweight sugary peanut butter would disappear here
• three bananas, so probably an extra quarter or half cup to what’s written
• I don’t measure cinnamon, I eyeball it and go a little heavy.

This is the third time I've made this recipe. The first time I put salt in as directed but didn't recognize that my nut butter was also salted so it came out way salty. 

Last two times I've doubled it and used quick cooking oats and a 9 x 13 glass baking dish. It comes out more like a granola bar and it's the perfect cooking time at 35 minutes. 

I've used a mixture of almond and PB, natural and with soybean oil, and it's very forgiving. Add more spices for taste.

I’ve made these a few times and I always put a lot of walnuts, and chocolate chips. This time I added pepitas and flax seed meal as well, I’ve used agave instead of honey and it’s great, these are solid, but I always use Celtic sea salt so they aren’t too salty.

there’s so much hate for this one that I had to try it out to see for myself. I thought it was good and as described.  Made as written except avocado oil instead of olive oil. Used Diamond crystal salt and the 2 teaspoons were fine. 2 teaspoons each cinnamon and vanilla-how could it possibly be bland? Not sure why there were so many complaints. I used almond butter too.

I see a lot of negative comments here, but I actually like the recipe. However, next time I would doctor it up with a lot more ingredients to make it much more of a hearty bar. I’m going to try adding more oats, some seeds, raisins, and maybe some flax. I cut the salt down to 3/4 of the original recipe since the peanut butter is already so salty and it was just fine.
I usually love the recipes from the NYT. But this one is  unfortunately terrible, even with chocolate chips.
These are WAY too salty.
Agree with the comments that these are way, way, WAY too salty. And we used unsalted peanut butter. Nest time, we’ll cut the salt as other’s have suggested.

These bars were easy and fun to make. I finally read the comments once they were in the oven and realized I probably shouldn’t have wasted my time. Things I noticed, they tasted a little different than what I expected once they were out and cooled a little. I cut them and put them in the fridge. Had one cold this morning and they were better. I think next time if there is a next time, I will add 1/2 c flour and some chocolate chips. The recipe definitely needs more substance. Also, I had substituted applesauce for the eggs. Next time I will do that and add more to replace the olive oil to not waste my good olive oil in a cakey thing where there’s not even enough to add flavor. Also would skip the honey since mine comes from our bees and is a treat here at our house and I would use brown sugar. So basically, I am making it more of a cake situation and who doesn’t love cake for breakfast!
We agree with all of the bland and salty comments.  They went right in the trash bin.  This recipe should be removed.
Made this with very ripe bananas, good quality peanut butter, olive oil, etc and really no flavor! And kind of mushy. I wouldn’t really call them bars.

I'm not an experienced baker so I followed the recipe exactly. All of my ingredients were good. These are awful tasting! Not sure what the problem is, but the recipe went immediately into the recycle bin.

These are excellent! Would be very adaptable. I only used 1/2 tsp of salt in the recipe and eliminated the flaked salt on topping. These bars would be great for winter hike, snow shoe or ski activity.

January 15, 2025.  I used 3 peanut oil, and 1 tablespoon safflower oil. Cut too 1/2 t kosher salt (may not even add that much next time), added 1/2 c. sugar and 3 T molassas (no brown sugar but thta would be nice).  I think this is best made night before and served cool!  I will make it again.  Probably change it up each time..maybe add fresh cranberries halved?
I also added a yummy neufatchal cream cheese/yogurt/milk/vanilla frosting I found on the 'net.  Family ate it!
