1T date molasses, 1-2T honey
0.3 cup extra oats
2.5 bananas
Add handful of raisins and walnuts in the batter
Added walnuts & sliced almonds on top
Spices: cinnamon   cardamom   nutmeg (very little)   pumpkin pie mix

I was looking for a baked breakfast that wasn't too sweet, but this actually turned out a little bit too sweet for me.
I made the recipe close to written (subbed maple syrup for honey), but my bananas were WARM (about 50°C - I needed to defrost them). I can see why it'd fail to sweeten if your kitchen/eggs are too cold. Try microwaving the bananas to lukewarm before mixing in the eggs.

I got halfway through the recipe before reading the comments and unfortunately there was no going back. I decided to make these with the following changes: no salt, 3medium bananas, 3 Tbsp. Chia seeds, 1/4 cup of coconut flour, 1/4 cup unsweetened shredded coconut, two cups oats, and 1 Tbsp. Unsweetened Cocoa powder. I didnt add any nuts since im making these for my 1.5 yo daughter. Theyre delicious and i’ll be making them again. :)

After reading the comments, I made some modifications. I added raisins, and chopped crystallized ginger. I also cut way back on the salt. I added a little extra cinnamon, some cardamom, extra vanilla, and peanuts to the top. It really helped add some flavor. Not bad for something with no flour.

I liked this recipe- I read the comments while it was baking and got nervous, but I liked that they were a bit more salty than your usual baked oatmeal square. The banana adds moisture, more than flavour- and mine has a strong peanut butter flavour. Perhaps helpful that I used a high in sugar peanut butter. Not sure if they’re in the regular rotation, but it definitely wasn’t a waste and enjoyed. No subs needed and baking time was true for my oven.
I followed recipe exactly and it’s way too salty but with that being said, I still wouldn’t like them with less salt. Sorry but this is a no for me!
@Dawn how much and what kind of salt did you use? I see the recipe offers two options for kosher vs table salt.
This is a clean recipe but lacks in flavor.  I would try again and consider adding some chocolate chips and possibly coconut to add some depth and contrast.
These are...just wrong.  The lack of a leavening agent leaves them raw in both texture and flavor.
Is there eggless version on this ??

Well, I'll make this again for sure! If you can accept it as a breakfast cake instead of a dessert treat, it fills the bill nicely. 

My changes, after reading comments: reduced the honey by 2 tablespoons, halved the salt, increased the rolled oats to 1 1/4 cups, and added 1/4 cups of whole wheat flour. Used almond butter.

It took 45 minutes in a convection over to test dry in the center, but I'm at 4600 feet elevation, so maybe that's why it needed more time in the oven.
Too salty
How can I make this vegan?
Swapped dark brown sugar for the honey and they were very flavorful - texture like a brownie. Whole family enjoyed for breakfast.
so blah tasting, I threw the whole thing away
Had some bananas I needed to use up - can confirm like others that these are not tasty! Find another way to use up those bananas.
Not for me, sticky and not in a good way. I agree they looked nice when cooked but who cares about how they look if the taste is not there. Going back to banana bread.
Whoops. Forgot to say I reduced the salt to 1/2 teaspoon regular salt and grated some rock salt over the top...
Too salty!

I do not recommend this recipe. 
Way too salty and bland flavors.

I don’t understand all of the negative reviews here; these were excellent. Subtle spices and a nice spongey/fudge-like consistency. The only changes I made were using just one teaspoon of salt (which was the perfect amount), and I lost count of the tablespoons of honey so it may have only been five. I also didn’t measure out the weight of the bananas and may have ended up with a little extra from two regular size bananas. Next time I might try adding some shredded coconut or a little more oats, but it doesn’t really need any changes. I have been enjoying them for breakfast all week.
Yikes. These are so bad. How do they have four stars? What a waist of ingredients. Entirely too salty. All I could taste was the salt and peanut butter.
Followed the recipe, except didn’t add the extra  salt on top and I added 3/4 cup chocolate chips. I love the flavor and didn’t expect the texture to be like a moist cake. Absolutely delicious.
These have no flavor, don’t bother.
I think they are great.  I’ve made with almond butter and then I did half pb & half almond, I also added hemp seeds which made it a little more cake like.  Overall, I love them.

This is an easy-to-make breakfast bar. I used butter instead of olive oil, crunchy peanut butter & no salt. It's quite dense and sweet. It would be a good snack before or after exercise, or pack for hiking. I would like to adjust it so that there is more protein. Currently, it's slightly oily. However, it's very tasty and filling.
They're not bad. I didn't add as much salt and added shredded coconut. I actually kind of like them.
I made it first with peanut butter, and then with almond butter. MUCH better with almond butter. I found it too sweet and, well, peanut buttery with Jif.
Could this work with cashew butter?

Sadly I have to agree something is wrong here. Perhaps the walnut can’t withstand the heat for that long? It was barely done inside but also burnt tasting; all ingredients were fresh so I don’t believe it was quality control.

These worked out for us! Added a third banana and halved the salt but otherwise followed the recipe and the texture and taste were good. I used the weight measurements for the ingredients, not sure if that played a role in why this worked for us versus the commenters who had a bad time with this recipe.

These are fine, not great not bad. I left out all salt since I don’t like much to begin with. Added chocolate chips which is great. As people said, this just lacked a strong flavor. Nothing shone through. I’ll eat what I made but won’t make it again.
This was not my favorite recipe - granted I believe I undercooked it slightly but the flavor was not great to me. I couldn't taste the PB at all - they were just super blah.

I almost didn’t make these because of the negative  comments. I’m glad I decided to take a chance on this recipe! I used natural peanut butter and 1 tsp of fine salt. I forgot the flaky salt.

I made sure not to skip the step of letting the eggs and bananas rest after mixing. These bars were rich, moist, and just salty and sweet enough. The peanut butter flavor really comes through.

Reading the comments on bland, and realising I was using natural peanut butter, I added the salt and half a tsp of baking powder to the banana egg mix before letting it sit. Not bland at all, and I can definitely cut down on the honey next time.

Good way to use up the random half packets of nuts I have lying around
Just awful. Only flavor was salt and I didn't even use as much as the recipe called for. Threw the whole thing away. Not even sure how there is a 4 star rating.

If you regularly cook from sketchy recipe blogs, you’ll like these! If you have a modicum of skill or taste, you’ll think these are shockingly bad. I’m in the latter camp. Love Sohla and many of her recipes, so my cognitive dissonance is almost as unpleasant as these disgusting bars.

I think the flavor and texture here is actually pretty good (I used natural crunchy peanut butter and overripe bananas that had been frozen and defrosted). They are so so salty, though. I thought it was my error - that I had accidentally used 2 tbsp’s of salt instead of tsp’s, but no. Made it again triple checking I was using the right measuring spoon and they’re still so salty. 

I’ll try one more time with half or a quarter of the called for salt…
Perfect recipe - don’t listen to the haters. Use ripe bananas and follow the recipe exactly by weight. The sprinkle a little sugar on top along with the salt because we deserve nice things.

Unfortunately I did not read the comments before I made. Love the ingredients so figured what could be bad. Followed the recipe and agree it was tasteless!! The salt even made it worse. 
Wouldn't bother doctoring. Just don't make!

I’ve made these several times and really enjoy them. Some things I’ve learned:

-using almond butter will yield a LOT less flavor
-account for the sodium in your peanut butter brand and adjust salt accordingly 
-only use 4-5 tbsp of honey 

I love these for breakfast with yogurt!

Made these at the beginning of the week and read comments while they were in the oven and thought I’d made a grave error. I have enjoyed having something easy to grab that’s low sugar and high fiber for breakfast. I even pop it in the microwave for 10 seconds before consuming. I did measure by weight and used Teddie salted creamy peanut butter. I also used quick oats, as that’s all I had on hand. I don’t think they’re bland or waste of ingredients as many have stated. I don’t know if they’re a staple for me yet, but could be! Was a nice way to use up some ripe bananas that wasn’t banana bread or freezing them for smoothies.
Tasteless. Would not make again.

Sorry I didn’t read the reviews before making these. When the smell of them baking nauseated me I realized they were not going to be good. Way too salty. Kind of a slimy texture and the taste is really unappealing. Very nutritious ingredients but they just don’t go well together.
There’s no nice way to say this… but these were so gross. I took one bite and immediately threw the whole thing away. I should have just made regular banana bread.
read the comments and thought "how bad could it be?" it's not terrible...but not great? even after halving the salt, the top flavor is...salt. wouldn't make this again.
I find if I cook the elderly bananas a bit before hand for any banana bread type recipes, I don't have an issue with too much moisture in the finished product.
We thought they were very yummy!

Had read the comments prior but gave it a shot. There's little flavor, if any. Each ingredient seems to cancel out the other and you are left eating texture. Echoing others, I hate to waste thus am dripping maple syrup and pairing with a hot cup of dark coffee.
These are delicious and have lots of great stuff in them.  Flaky salt on top is an important part of the recipe!

After reading the comments I almost decided not to make these, but I am glad I did! I followed the measurements and instructions (with the exception of the vanilla and cinnamon--I just eyeballed those) and they turned out well. I like the banana flavor and the crunch from the walnuts. The peanut butter flavor is subtle, but present. I think chocolate chips would be great in this as well. I'm really don't know why it turned out so differently for others!
We LOVE these!  Followed the recipe exactly, except for the walnuts on top.  Used almonds instead.  I will say the key is the Kosher salt and using the weight measurements (thank you for those!).

We enjoyed these and will make again. Met needs to use overripe bananas and reduce refined sugar intake. It’s not dessert, but IS a satisfying, dense kind of bar good for breakfast or after workout. Makes me think of some protein bars I like - The ones that don’t pretend to be a candy bar…. 

I had accidentally bought almond butter with honey (Justin’s), so reduced additional honey a bit to account for. And my bananas didn’t weigh in quite as much as called for. Didn’t taste them specify, but I’m sure added some sweetness. 

If ur looking for a sweet treat you won’t like these, but if looking for a healthy snack, these are good!
I agree this is too salty but those who are finding it way too salty might want to check the ingredients of their nut butter.

Just made this and it's beautiful! Yes, bake twice. Do not skip the longer drying step: that will generate the support necessary to hold the frangipane on the top. I would suggest slicing the sides of the dough off with a very sharp knife so the whole thing puffs up evenly. Mine was more pillow shaped bc I didn't do this.
I doubled the frangipane and I have no regrets

If you make the frangipane in advance and store it in the fridge as directed, make sure you bring it to room temperature before spreading it on the puff pastry.  The cooked puff pastry is quite delicate and it tore easily while spreading the filling. This was not much to look at, but at least it was tasty.

So, so impressive looking and delicious. I inadvertently browned the butter, but used it anyway. Yummy goodness. Other than browning the butter, I followed the recipe and served my family flaky almond love. One caveat: the recipe says serves 12. It serves 4. Or maybe only 1, if you don’t want to share.
Haha, kinda agree, 4-5 generous servings

A traditional almond croissant is fully baked, split, filled with frangipane/almond cream, then baked again.

Frangipane goes in the middle AND on top. It shouldn’t burn. The frangipane has to bake through and will prevent the pastry from burning at the same time.

This recipe needs a visual or better guidance on how long to prebake the puff pastry. I did as instructed, all-butter pastry, 10 minutes at 425, then 22 at 350. At that point it was puffy with an even dark golden color. When I later went to split and fill it, it had the consistency of a cracker. It was so dry and crisp; it shattered as I cut it. The final product was edible but not croissant-like; edges tasted singed. Also, 1 tsp kosher salt was too much; I’d use 1/2 if making again.
Try hazelnut spread, jam, marmalade, lemon curd…this is versatile recipe.
I wondered, since my husband is allergic to almonds. Thanks!
Absolutely delicious, but you MUST double the filling! Trust me, you'll want it. I found it easier to spread by using an offset spatula.

The following changes, also recommended by other users are a no-brainer:

- Make 50% more filling
- Definitely cut the pastry in eight before the pre-bake (it will be significantly easier to slice the pieces in half. Try re-freezing the rolled out sheet for 30 to 45 minutes before cutting to prevent edge compression
- Use a pastry bag to fill the inside and top of the pastry - so much easier! The "strips" you're pastry bag dispenses will blend together and flatten once baked.
I loved this recipe! I followed it exactly and it worked perfectly. I used the recommendation to cut the ends of the puff pastry prior to cooking (thanks for the tip!).

You want almond flour that is blanched, not bleached. This means it’s made from blanched almonds, with the skins removed so it’s a uniform light color. If it’s made from unblanched almonds, it’s usually labeled almond meal and is speckled brown in color. Blanched almonds, finely ground in the food processor, are a fine substitute.
The second time we made this, we added some chopped dark chocolate on top of the filling spread inside and on top of the outside and it was even more amazing!!

I cheated. I needed to feed hungry bellies quickly and still wanted to do this recipe. So I want to the market around the corner who makes good-enough plain croissants. Bought a few then went home to start at Step 2. In about 45 min (including trip to the store) I had delicious half-homemade almond croissants. Everyone loved it. 

If you’ve got a need for speed, this hack can work nicely.
@coco genius! Thanks for the tip. Could work in a pinch.

The pastry is baked first, filled and baked again? Seems like puff pastry would burn on the second bake. I know from using puff pastry sheets they bake and burn easily. Why not use two sheets and bake with the filling?
Confusing recipe. I agree. What? Cook twice?
The frangipane filling spread edge-to-edge prevents the outer edges from burning. The ratio of pastry to filling would be wrong if you used two sheets.

Julia, I just finished the first bake; my pastry puffed up to about three inches, so one sheet really is what you need.  I was worried that it would be difficult to slice a large, thin sheet, but it is no longer thin.

The first time I made this I followed the recipe exactly and, though it turned out perfectly, it was too sweet and too salty.  I've made it again today, reducing the sugar to 1/4 cup and the kosher salt by half.  Much better!

I cut the puff pastry into 8 pieces and shortened the baking time. Double frangipane. And used a high quality puff pastry. 
When I made it as one big square, it was so dry and crumbly that slicing it made a big mess.
It's very good. Kudos to Gardner for suggesting to trim the edges before baking. I substituted ¼ teaspoon of table salt for the Kosher salt. Next time I'll skip the flaky salt on top.
Can we get the ingredient proportions for the 2 variations shown on the video, please? (Great video btw)

I left the puff pastry in the fridge overnight to thaw, but even so, when I unfolded it, it broke/tore into three pieces.  I didn’t try to mend them, did the first bake and put two away in an air tight container. I proceeded with only one of them, and it made a perfect amount for a small treat for our family of four.  Made the second one a couple days later (warmed the frangipan for about 8 seconds in the microwave first) and it was just as yummy.  The third will be a hostess gift today.

I was concerned after reading comments but went ahead. Glad I did - delicious. No issues with crumbling. Made sure my butter was soft, I chilled overnight - 12 hours, used a sharp knife. Sliced easily and baked to perfection. They were attractive and delicious. (I also used volume measurements and didn’t adjust anything)

I trimmed the edges of the pastry sheet and cut the sheet into four equal-sized pieces before the first baking. Much easier to handle, cut, and serve. I cut each piece in half and put the top half top down and spread that with filling. Then I put the bottom half top down on that and spread the rest of the filling on the top layer of pastry, which started as the bottom. Made for uniform looks. Delicious, easy to make in advance. It's in regular rotation here.

I did not prick holes in the puff pastry before baking and it rose to high in the oven. This made it difficult to spread the frangipane on top and it collapsed. If you’re not used to working with puff pastry, suggest pricking a few holes with a fork before baking to avoid this from happening.

I made this today as written. It’s is fabulous. Just take the slicing slow with a good serrated knife. All in all, it is very forgiving and if it over-flakes in a spot, who cares. The same is evident in the photo. My favorite kind of rustic dessert. Don’t skip the salt on top if salty-sweet is your jam. Thanks, Sohla, for another winner!

Are the recipe amounts for the chocolate hazelnut version from the video listed anywhere? I'd like to try that one but not seeing the recipe. The classic almond came out perfect...what a treat. Thanks!

I used about 2 cups of hazelnuts and the same amounts of sugar, butter, etc. as the almond recipe. I did have to add a little water (scant quarter cup) to the hazelnut paste because it was too thick to easily spread.
For anyone that struggled halving the puffed pastry, cutting it width wise into thirds really made filleting the pastry much easier once cooked.

This is delicious but VERY salty. I’m making it again tomorrow (it’s that delicious!) but will use salted butter and omit all salt in the recipe. Otherwise, I would suggest using unsalted butter, 1/4 tsp. table salt, and omit the kosher and flake salts.
I used rum instead of vanilla and it ended up having a really strong rum flavor that I didn’t enjoy. Go for the vanilla option unless you’re a big rum ball flavor fan!

It was the height of the holidays and the store was out of puff pastry sheets, but they did have individual shells in packs of six. I pre-baked as directed in the recipe, then carefully removed the center "plug" added the filling, closed it up, any extra filling on top and "Bob's your Uncle" It worked great!

I like the idea to cut before the first bake. That was my problem: flakes everywhere!! Cut while warm? Running into the almonds creates a problem too. The extra salt over top was — over the top. Try again! Like the  reviewer who used shells!

This has quickly become one of the most requested desserts/breakfast treats I’ve ever made for the handful of mouths I delight with my kitchen’s creations. 
I agree - double the recipe for the filling and use a heavy hand with the powdered sugar! Mini chocolate chips are a great addition to sprinkle inside the two layers before the second bake!

I toasted the almond flour in a skillet for a couple minutes to deepen the flavor; watched it closely and let it cool completely. I also used brown sugar instead of white granulated for a flavor boost… not sure it makes a difference. 
I used salted butter, the full amount of kosher salt recommended, and a slight sprinkle of Maldon salt on top — really nice recipe! Wasn’t exactly like breakfast in France but will be pretty enough on my (North Carolina) brunch table. :)

Fabulous. It would probably have been better had i used almond extract instead of vanilla, but I didn’t realize I was out of it until I started. I, too, doubled the filling but did not double the salt and sugar. With the confectioners sprinkled on top at the end, it was fine. I used Bob’s Red Mill almond flour. I’m pretty sure it wasn’t bleached. I made the filling first and it sat out until the pastry was ready and it was easy to spread.

Have to wonder how many times web chefs have to make something until it's ready to show. I followed directions. It looked right but collapsed in minutes, before slicing/filling. Dufour says that means it's underbaked. Baked some more, top blackened. Collapsed again & more as I sliced/topped it. Looked nothing like hers. Tasted great -- used mac & pine nuts along with almonds inside & out, & orange marmalade inside. Note to self: do not eat while sitting in recliner; add more marmalade next time.

Really easy recipe! Was in a hurry and wanted a smaller amount for a quick breakfast treat…Instead of splitting the pastry, I rolled it thin, added the almond mixture, folded over the puff pastry and baked it all in one shot.  Used vanilla instead of rum, and omitted the confectioners sugar and added salt.  Delicious!  Some of the mixture oozed out and made a yummy cookie.  Will definitely be my go to for a quick breakfast pastry.   Looking forward to trying it with apples and walnuts.

This was so easy and so good. My puppy thought so too, as she grabbed a piece off the counter and happily trotted on in to where we were eating, carrying it in her mouth, and then stopping, looking at us and slowly realizing that wasn't the best plan. :)

Pretty much just followed the directions, except that instead of dark rum I used bourbon that I'd soaked vanilla beans in for two months.

I made one of these yesterday and another today.  I follow the recipes to the letter.  However, as written, IT'S WAY TOO SALTY!  And this from someone and friends who shared it like salt.
One suggested leaving out the salt all together and I did.  What an improvement!

My family loved this!  Made it for Christmas morning and again for New Year’s brunch.

The second time, I added slices of ripe pears and crisp apples on top of the almond paste in the middle. Terrific.

I also tried trimming the edges of the puff pastry before the first bake.  The pastry puffed more evenly, although not as high.  It was easier to cut, too.

All in all, this will be in the repertoire.
It’s just that easy! Hahaha you’re so cute, you’re my new favorite. I CAN’T WAIT to do this for Mother’s Day! Thanks so much.

Made this, but with a mix of pistachios and almonds (2:1 ratio), and all in a food processor. First ground the nuts really finely, then added the sugar, butter until creamy and blended in eggs and vanilla and almond extracts, to form a smooth paste. Much easier and faster. And it was delicious!
Mine came out too flaky and almost burned. I think the bake times are too long. I might try again with adjusted bake times.

Tried this with houseguests, they couldn't believe how fun, delicious, and interactive. Especially loved the video, this recipe is an instant go-to. Thanks Sohla! On the downside, it took me an extra twenty minutes to fall asleep because I had variations running through my head.

This turned out just like the picture and was delicious!  It is somewhat time-consuming, so  I recommend at least baking the puff pastry beforehand.  I made almond flour by putting whole almonds in boiling water for 1 minute, then ice water til cooled, and slipping off the skins.  Then I put them in my food processor until the almonds were ground to sand-like-sized particles.  It took about 2 cups of whole almonds for the recipe.  Spread can be made early, just wait to add the egg.

I have this in the oven right now on its second, almond-filled bake. I have no doubt it’s going to be delicious but geez, did anyone else have trouble spreading the filling on the top with the amount given? I will most likely bake this again and will definitely double the filling recipe.

I made this for my wife for Mother’s Day, and it was a hit!  Having satiated my sweet tooth with an abundance of almond croissants over many years, I felt that I had the upper hand of experience to deliver the goods. The secret ingredient however was the buttery and delectable Dufour puff pastry from Bronx, NY. I followed the package instructions and convection oven baked at 350 F, cooled, divided in two, spread the filling and top with frangipane, assembled the top, sprinkled a small scattering of Maldon flaked salt and copiously covered with sliced almonds. The second bake again at 350 F took about 20 minutes. The result was absolutely spectacular, rivaling the best almond croissants I’ve ever had in Montreal! Best of all, I made my wife happy! A keeper for sure. Enjoy!

This was fabulous, although mine certainly did not look as beautiful as the one in the picture! The layer of puff pastry was very crackly and hard to separate neatly. I will add that I also thought there was too much salt. Half as much would have been plenty, and yes, I used Diamond kosher salt.
Doubled the frangipane filling as others suggested and cut into 16 pieces for a brunch. Otherwise,  didn’t change anything. The filling and pastry together were divine. It got rave reviews!
Would love the recipe for the hazelnut filling Sohla does in the accompanying video.

Using the same Dufour puff pastry shown in the video, I found that baking it at the higher temp initially just led to a burned top. Baking at 350 for 25 minutes  led to a nicely browned, fully baked puff pastry.

After watching the "how to make" video, I realized the Pepperidge Farm puff pastry I had to use (live in a rural area-only frozen puff pastry available) was way smaller in size and would not bake up nearly as thick as the one used in the video.  I used both PF sheets, stacked them and baked them.  Supper easy to split in half like a bagel!  I doubled the Frangipane.
You can’t say “puff pastry any size” and then expect the filling to be the right amount. I used a 14-inch puff pastry—probably 14X10”—and should have doubled the filling recipe at least.
THIS is the most amazing recipe! Favorite party trick going forward... can't wait to try the ham and cheese for bowl games!
Five stars because giving something one star for semantics is silly. Also it’s good and easy.

This was a success using tips from previous posts.
I used a sheet of Dufour puff pastry (14 oz.)
I did not prick/dock, and baked till dark golden
I had/used slivered almonds, ground, and made 1½ of the frangipane, which I piped on.
I used ¼ tsp. of salt in the filling, none on top.
After the first bake, the pastry was thick enough to slice easily. The top layer did collapse a bit in the process, but I don't think that was a bad thing.

I made this as directed and it was wonderful!  I did not have almond flour but did have almonds, so I blanched one cup of almonds for a minute, removed the skins and then pulverized them in a food processor until the pieces were similar to sand.  Other than that I followed the directions.

Amazing! Doubled the filling and added 70% dark chocolate inside, it balances the frangipane filling perfectly. Will divide into 12 pieces next time, 8 was too large. 10/10 would make again to fill a crowd, I’m thinking Boxing Day brunch.
Could one use pistachio cream instead of almonds? I have a jar and don’t know what to do with it.

I made two of these for a brunch today and they were delicious! I followed the recipe as written. I’d recommend cutting into 9 (or less)pieces instead of 12. All that cutting destroyed the delicate pastry. Also, when spreading the frangipane over the cut pastry, I found that pressing down slightly with the spatula and not spreading back and forth helped keep the pastry from shredding in the process. Definitely a keeper!

I came across this recipe and thought I would make it right NOW IF I had all the ingredients. I found one piece of puff pastry in an opened box in the back of my freezer. It’s probably 5 years old. Also found an ancient bag of almond flour, the kind the recipe says not to use. Amazingly, a tiny bottle of almond extract. My puff pastry sheet seemed smaller than the one in the video, so I made the frangipane and it was the perfect amount. The puff pastry was barely defrosted. My almonds were in an opened package I found that were mixed with dried cranberries—-it was a salad topping.  I baked it for a little less time. This thing is delicious. I will admit I’ve eaten half of it myself. I can’t wait to make it again with fresher ingredients!
Dufour, Dufour, Dufour! Pricey but worth it

Awesome.  Use a quality all butter puff pastry such as Dufeur or Publix Greenwise brand.  Trader Joe's brand is seasonal.  Definitely cut the puff pastry before baking the first time, made it easier to cut in half and fill.   Watch the baking and adjust the time to prevent overbaking.  Added a tiny bit of almond extract to the frangipane.  Next time I will warm the frangipane to make it easier to spread inside and on top of the pastry, and add some shaved dark chocolate on the inside.
You have to split the baked dough.
Sounds delicious and so pleased to have metric measurements included. I’m assuming that almond flour is what we in the UK call ground almonds, ie very finely ground almond meal, not actual flour?
Yes, same thing.

I'm probably going to be making this, sounds nice, but I'm aware that the marzipan mix will be very stiff - and as a pp has already noted, it tears up the delicate pastry if used from the fridge. Thinking it'll be like trying to spread cold butter on fresh white bread?  I routinely warm butter in the microwave for 10 secs to get it to a spreadable consistency so the bread doesn't tear. Maybe doing this will work for this too.

I already have a bag of almond flour (I assume not bleached) that I rarely use, so I don’t want to buy a bag of bleached (assuming I could even find it).  If it makes the frangipane too stiff, as the note warns, what would be a good way to loosen it?
Is there a reason that one has to mix by hand, rather than with a hand or stand mixer on low?
Roughly what dimensions was your uncooked puff pastry before you baked it? Ours (I'm in BC, Canada) comes in small-ish blocks and needs to be rolled out.

Yes.
Some of the recipes I use frozen puff with ask that it be rolled flatter.
I didn't do this, and it's puffed to about three inches.

With only the top-view photo, I can't tell if that's what's wanted here.
I wonder if you could use Solo brand almond filling, which I love.

This is delicious! I followed the recipe exactly. Baked the puff yesterday, then pulled the frangipane together, spread it and baked it this morning. It makes a dramatic entrance! So perfect with coffee or tea.

I did as directed and the first round of baking had the pastry coming out light brown. I was able to cut easily and fill, but putting it back in the oven, the edges were too crisp and burnt to eat a second time, and I cut the baking time in half to 10 minutes. The inside was delish, just the edges were not edible because too crispy. Maybe turn the temp down from the start.

So insanely delicious. I was skeptical having pre baked the puff pastry and the filling. I thought it would turn into a crumbling mess but the filling/topping are magical and turned a crusty pillow of pastry into a soft and delicious breakfast that was devoured in an instant. I did have to warm up the filling as mine was straight from the fridge but 20 seconds in the microwave was perfect. 
This will be a staple for brunches and holiday breakfasts.
I realized that it might be easier - and would save more buttery flakes of crust - if I sliced the cooled puff pastry into 3rds, where the fold line was. The horizontal cutting was much easier.
Easy to make, however, it is very salty for those who are sensitive to that taste.  I will nearly eliminate the salt the next time.  The croissant was best after sitting, i.e., on the second day.
I could only find the puff pastry cups and it worked beautifully! Might have even been less messy because there was no reason to cut before serving

Don’t give up! The puff pastry had an uneven puff on first bake, making it hard to slice “bagel style”. We put almond filling on the top, layered the sad pieces on top, and flipped back upright. The pastry leveled out on second bake. A beautiful finish and all’s right in the kitchen. For inexperienced pastry baker, I wasn’t expecting the weird shaped first bake to even into a lovely finished product.

This tasted fabulous, but aspects of the recipe were challenging. For one thing I found it almost impossible to cut the pastry without it crumbling, especially the top, let alone spread a filling on it. Also I felt like the filling was a bit skimpy in quantity, given that it was supposed to be spread edge to edge. However I had just made some almond paste, so I added some of that to fill in the gaps. In any case the result wasn't gorgeous, but it was absolutely delicious.

Would be nice to have some extra filling for the top. After using 2/3 on the inside, it was a stretch to, well, stretch the filling to cover enough of the top. Also need to let us non-pastry cooks (speaking for myself) know that the puff pastry is going to collapse and flake as you spread more on top. The picture shows this perfectly with thin slices of the finished product.

This was incredible. All the stores were out of puff pastry - but it turns out it's no harder to make puff pastry than pie dough. When I sliced the baked pastry in half, it was a little too moist on the inside so I popped it, open-faced, into the over for 10 min and it was perfect. Filled it, baked it, devoured it. YUM! Can't wait to experiment with other fillings like Nutella...

The recipe works well and  yields an impressively tasty and visually appealing return on a minimal time and effort investment. The amount of salt in the filling is a little excessive and I would cut back to a half teaspoon next time.
Double the amount of filling --> twice as good :-)
Found Trader Joe's butter puff pastry to get unpleasantly dark at these temperatures. Also, next time will almost refreeze it after rolling pastry sheet, and cut into 8 pcs. for easier handling.

A simple recipe that yielded delicious results, but could use a little adjusting. I would make 1.5x the filling next time -- I found the recipe as-is only barely covered the top and middle. I would also bake just slightly less than 'deeply browned' on the first pass - my puff pastry grew shattery after the second bake and although delicious, was a total mess to eat. Nevertheless, dead easy to put together and even suboptimal results were tasty.

Much easier to buy in a pastry shop.  Our first try with splitting the first baked pastry (ha ha "like a bagel") was a total disaster. Just a big bubble flake on top. I was forced to lay on a new layer of puff pastry over the Frangipane, and bake for another 20 minutes.  The result was almost edible. The amount of salt in the Frangipane is way too much, basically ruining it for me and the people to which I served our Frankenstein version of this recipe.

I've made this 4 times.  I don't add any salt, and after the first time I skipped the powdered sugar.  Wonderful recipe, so easy, and 
 appreciated by everyone whose eaten it.
Doubled the filling.  Heaven.
Sohla made variations (chocolate hazelnut and ham & cheese) in a video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wvt0TkhO1CI

The language in the recipe is a bit confusing, but there is a very helpful video of this on NYTimes Cooking Youtube channel, including two variations - not sure why it is not linked in the recipe: https://youtu.be/wvt0TkhO1CI

Just made this: it's delicious and fairly easy to make. I thawed the puff pastry on the counter at RT, by inverting it as Sohla showed in the video, and it didn't break at the seam. In terms of ingredients, I followed other people's advice and doubled the almond paste, while cutting the sugar in half, i.e. 1/2 cup sugar, 1 1/2 cups almond flour, 2 eggs etc. I added a very thin layer of nutella under the almond paste, and sprinkled some shaved dark chocolate on top. Sweet enough for my taste!
My six year old asked, “can you make this everyday?” Enough said. It was beautiful, easy, and yummy.

This was amazing. It looked like a professional pastry chef made it and it tasted better. Really easy too. I am a convert to puff pastry. Crispy, soft, sweet, salty, crunchy and aromatic. What a flavor bomb. I will use this at my next fancy dinner party for sure.

I'll add to the chorus of those saying the filling can be doubled! My friends went wild for these things -- they were very popular and so delicious. I also used a mixture of rum and vanilla in the frangipane and was NOT sorry!

Easily adapted to accommodate vegans. Delicious and beautiful! Used individual puff pastry shells (accidentally vegan) and made the filling vegan with Miyokos and Just Egg. Served with fresh raspberries. A lovely dessert.
Could you use fresh berries or preserves for the filling or add them to it?

If using two pastry sheets (Pepperidge Farm 17oz. Pkg.) doubling the filling recipe seemed to be necessary. The recipe specified “any” size puff pastry sheet, and I was not sure if one or two would be enough, but I am a a novice baker.

This recipe is dead simple, crispy/chewy buttery almond deliciousness. Watch the video if the instructions don't make sense. Made 2x frangiapanne and used about 1.5 in the croissant - will find a good use for the yummy remainder. TJ's all butter puff pastry (which I had in freezer from the holidays) puffed beautifully. Though it cracked when sliced horizontally, the filling glued the pastry back together on final bake and no one knew or cared.
Impressive results for so little effort, great for brunches or out of town guests.
This is similar to a Galette des Rois, which has almond frangipane sandwiched between two layers of puff pastry, but is baked only once. Why is this baked twice?
Can this be baked and filled, baked again and stored for consumption later in the day or the next morning?
Absolutely. I baked this is the early morning, kept it at room temperature for a few hours and then warmed it again for about ten mins at 300 and it was perfect.

Our raspberry patch is overflowing this year. I added ripe, falling-apart berries along with the frangipane filling the last time I made this, and it was transcendent! It was also so pretty on the plate!

Use all-butter puff pastry, if you can find it. The difference is profound—enough so that I may try to make my own next time. Pepperidge Farm puff pastry just doesn’t do justice to this charming recipe.
This was the perfect thing to make with friends. Very little equipment and concentration needed so we could chat and drink tea and then have a beautiful and impressive and delicious snack by the end.

Loved this even without doubling the frangipane. Thanks to Joe for the suggestion of using a piping bag - I think we would have destroyed the puff pastry had we tried to spread it. Skipped the finishing salt and thought it turned out perfect.

The comment about using a pastry bag was brilliant.   I used a plastic bag with a corner cut out.

I cut off the top horizontally, but I fear I cut too high.  A good bit of the "flesh" of the top became part of the bottom, leaving a hollowed out very thin shell on top.  I cut the top flesh from the bottom, set it aside, put the frangipane on the bottom, and then manually placed the flesh of the top over the bottom and then place the "top shell" on top.

Will use more frangipane next time.
Has anyone tried a savory filling like ham and cheese?
She does a ham and cheese variation on the video.
What is a good brand of all butter puff pastry.  I think I will have to order it from somewhere as all we have in our stores in town is Pepperidge Farm.
Dufour puff pastry is amazing.
I found all-butter PP at Trader Joe's. The sheets are small, so rather than one large sheet, I made two and cut each into four pieces.
Could you please provide the measurements for the Hazelnut Chocolate variation? Looks fabulous!
My puff pastry puffed in one corner but the rest stayed flat and cooked solid like hardtack. It was thawed in the fridge and went straight into the 425 oven.
Followed the recipe exactly, used Pepperidge Farm puff pastry. The trick is to thaw slowly before you unfold it. The video really helped, this was so easy to make!

Made for Xmas brunch-very easy and festive. Family loved it. I agree w/others comments to reduce salt--and doubled the filling.
Also made the ham and cheese variation.
Thanks for this festive fun recipe
Doubled the frangipane, could probably 1.5 it? Would add a little almond liqueur to filling too.

Made this last year for a New Year's brunch that we've decided to make a tradition! It's on the menu again by unanimous consent! I followed the recipe as is and it was great. Professional chefs in attendance said they wouldn't change it.

I used a store bought puff pastry (store brand) since they didn’t have anything else, it came out terrible! A BIG cracker! I knew it immediately while it was baking, next time I’m going to search for a good brand of puff pastry!
Very good, easy.  Would leave out the salt on top, halve the salt in the filling.
How do I get the quantities for the chocolate hazelnut variation that Sohla shows in the video?

My friend and I made two versions of this recipe and hosted a breakfast for some other friends - we  ALL loved this giant croissant.  We made the almond croissant according to the recipe and it was fabulous.  I decided to try a different filling. I baked the croissant and filled it with a hommade vanilla custard and strawberry jam.  I didn't do the second baking, obiously.  We can't wait to try the chocolate version.  One piece of advice though is to use the all-butter puff pastry.

The first time I made this the puff pastry was too high and crackly and it was too salty. The second time I halved the salt, doubled the frangipane, and spread it evenly over the two halves of puff pastry and sprinkled with almonds, then baked them as separate pieces. They are more almond squares than croissants, but far easier to cut, eat, and store. I will make them like this from now on. They are so delicious.

After eyeing, rereading this recipe, the comments and watching the video for months (obsessive much, yes), I finally made it yesterday. Previous tips, especially trimming edges (Gardner, thank you!) were key in my case. My Dufours pastry had sat in the freezer for a year+ and I couldn’t find a best by date on the packaging. When I opened it, the edges were a bit dry, so trimming was particularly relevant. Even though my pastry’s age was suboptimal, loved the results and will make this again.
I baked it for 30 minutes after turning oven down to 350 degrees.  Used all butter puff pastry.
This was wonderful. Made double the frangipane, which was a bit much for the sheet of Pepperidge Farm puff I used, but it was still terrific. Stayed very good for a few days.

I have made these a lot in the past year.  Recently, I decided to cut the sheet into before baking, trimming each side of 8 pieces.  The result gives a totally different taste, level of crispness and height to the tart.    I understand the reason for the giant tart idea, but now I prefer cutting up the sheet. I made a hazelnut Nutella tart ( go easy on the Nutella) and the almond which I love.  But can’t wait to try other variations!

I made this for Mother's Day and made the filling the day before as suggested but it didn't work out.  The filling was too thick and hard to spread.  I would recommend more liquid (a second egg?) and letting it get to room temp before trying to spread it.

I also found that the first bake time for the puff pastry was way too long. Even cutting it down, mine ended up overly browned. I doubled the filling because it was too salty (I did miss the note about kosher salt). Tasty ingredients save the day, but I would take a different approach if I actually tried this again.

What size puff pastry is this recipe for? I grabbed the only one available at my store, but looking at the video it appears to be half size? Is there a standard size for puff pastry or does it come in multiple sizes?
@Question About Puff Pastry she used Dufour? Con be bought at WF TJ etc!
How can I substitute almond paste in the frangipane? I think I can just look up a recipe for frangipane and use that. But ideas are welcome
Family loved this! But too salty. Next time 1/4 tsp salt & no flakes on top. Keep an eye on your puff pastry-ours browned much quicker than recommended bake time.

DELICIOUS. Made it for Christmas breakfast and again for New Year's. Would suggest bumping the frangipane up by at least 50%, or double for almond lovers. I trimmed and scored the pastry the first time, and didn't the second - I don't think it's necessary.

And as always, it's helpful to watch the video.
Super tasty! I would just add a bit less salt next time.
So easy & delicious! I’ve made this recipe once a week since I discovered it. It works with gluten free puff pastry and stays fresh for a few days. My family loves it.
Fantastic crowd pleaser and "easy to pull off"as advertised. Sohla El-Waylly, well done!

I wish the exact ingredient list including amounts were listed for the chocolate hazelnut and ham and cheese variations.  They look so good but I need to know how much hazelnuts to buy and if I need to buy both raw and roasted hazelnuts and how much ham and cheese to use.

The recipe says that any puff pastry will do but unfortunately the one I purchased did not work for this recipe. Like another commenter, I followed the instructions but my pastry barely puffed and hardened to the consistency of a cracker well before the cooking time was done (10 minutes at 425 and another 20 at 350). The almond filling however was delicious so I smeared it on some halved day-old croissants, baked them for 25 minutes (along with the sliced almonds) and enjoyed the results.
Great and easy. Loved by all on Mother's Day. I look forward to making this again.

My partner had to be at the airport at 4AM on Mothers Day, so I legit whipped this faux croissant up in less than an hour! Divided it up into two cute boxes and had a wonderful addition to the hanging baskets I had bought my Mom and MIL.

Great recipe, though color guideposts would be hugely beneficial. 

Thanks!

This was great! Already shared with a few other New York Times cooking aficionados. Made this for a brunch birthday party and was met with rave reviews. Cooked the puff pastry and mixed up the frangipane a day before assembling and baking (yes you bake it twice - like traditional almond croissants!), and it was super easy and delicious! 10/10
Has anyone made this with a pumpkin filling/topping?

I’ve made this several times and love it. I use gf pastry (Simply Wise in Australia) and use one sheet that I cut into 4 before the first bake. It’s such an easy and delicious way to once again be able to enjoy a pastry that I’ve missed for years!

This is kind of a better idea than it is a recipe or result. I feel a little foolish for thinking it would taste like something more than puff pastry with frangipane (which in the recipes defense is exactly what this is) but it didn’t. The texture was just off for me. Too flaky and messy and a pretty mid flavor. There’s also not enough cues to know how long to do the first bake. I think you could read this recipe and have equal chance of an under or over baked result. Maybe doubling the filling as everyone says is the move but honestly, I’d never make this again. It just made me want an actual croissant. And yes, I used the good all butter puff. Just an underwhelming result for something so pretty in the photo!

My comment is not about the recipe itself (though it seems like a wonderful idea and I look forward to trying it). My comment is about the way the salt measurement is expressed. Why on earth would you give measurements for different kinds of coarse salt, which yes, we know vary in their crystal size, but not one for regular table salt? The whole business about salt has become so very complicated lately, with what seems to me as a snobbery against table salt in cooking. WHY?
Absolutely delicious! I used the Dufour pastry dough which made this even better.
Has anyone tried this recipe using unbaked, frozen puff pastry instead of the pre-baked? Wouldn’t the puff pastry puff no matter what the filling?
This recipe already calls for unbaked, frozen puff pastry. You bake it twice, once as a full sheet, next after splitting it in half horizontally and spreading the filling inside and on top.
I think after slicing the cooked pastry, you flip them so the browned side is on the inside. it shouldnt burn then, maybe.

No, that is incorrect. The filling goes on the inside, (not on the browned bottom), and the top is placed over the filling, browned side up, and then spread with the remaining third of the frangipane.

Okay, holiday baking pals, I'm hoping to tah-dah! the family with this one on Christmas morning, but I have questions. In Step 3, do we spread frangipane on the "outer" halves of the pastry (the upper part of the lid and the underside of the bottom part), or do we spread on the "inner" half of one (exposed only after splitting) and the "outer" part of the other? I'm crossing my fingers for a video showing Sohla's method.
Spread two-thirds of the frangipane on the inside of the bottom half. Then cover with the top half and spred the remaining third on top of the puff pastry.
do you mean one thin sheet, or one pack? one sheet seems really wrong!
puff pastry, not phyllo
One package that already has the butter between the sheets.
The recipe says one sheet.

So no filling on the bottom half at all? 
“Flip the top of the pastry over, then spread about two-thirds of the almond filling evenly across the bottom of it, taking care to spread it all the way to the edges.”
Looks like you spread filling on the underside of the top piece, so it's like the peanut butter in a sandwich when you flip it back onto the bottom piece.
Or spread on the top of the bottom half. You want layers of (on the bottom) pastry, almond filling, pastry, almond filling (on top).
Is there a workaround if I don't have almond flour?
If you have blanched almonds, grind them with the sugar in a food processor.  This will prevent them from becoming greasy.  Or use other nuts and do the same.
No almond flour here. What can I use instead??  Ground almonds??

I have some almond flour that I rarely use, so if it’s not bleached, which I assume it’s not, I really don’t want to buy I bleached (assuming I could even find it).  So what would be a good way to loosen the frangipane if it makes it too stiff as the note says?

You're looking for "blanched" not "bleached" almond flour.  If you can't see small bits of brown in your flour, then the almonds were blanched (that is, the skins were removed). You would have to add more liquid of some sort.
How well would this turn out with a blitz puff pastry rather than a store bought frozen puff pastry?
Blanched almond flour is not available in my area - possible substitutions?
At what temperature is the second bake?
Step 3: 350 degrees
How thick should the puff pastry be before putting it in to bake? Here I can only get puff pastry in a block that has to be rolled out before baking.
Can I just use a can of Solo Almond filling instead of making the frangipane?  Anyone?
probably but it won't taste like the recipe version
Can this be made with chocolate and, if it can, what kind?
I would recommend getting the Pastry sheet. They smell delicious and I'll see how everyone likes them. A little too dark for my liking but nothing powdered sugar can't fix. Merry Christmas!

I don't understand this direction in Step 3. How can you rotate the baking sheet without spilling the topping?
"Bake until the nuts are toasted and the topping is brown, rotating the baking sheet once during baking, 20 to 25 minutes."
You just turn the pan slowly, like a record.
Rotate horizontally, so that the side of the pan that is closest to the back of the oven ends up closest to the front of the oven. Do this slowly and carefully, and nothing will spill.

It was ok. Not too much effort to do as I was baking it whilst doing other Xmas prep which is good, but the puff pastry on the bottom ended up a bit dark and hard for my taste. Frangiapane is always tasty but I wouldn't bother cooking this again

Judy - I made it with chocolate chips on 1/3 of the of the pastry puff and put the almond mixture on the other 2/3. I put sliced almonds only on the chocolate side top, with the almond mixture and almonds on the other topside. But sides were delicious!
What a great recipe!
Has anybody tried this recipe with commercially available gluten-free puff pastry?
Just tried with gluten free and it is a half inch tall after a full bake. Don't recommend.

I followed the instructions but trying to split it length-wise, it just kept flaking and breaking. Finally cut horizontally and used half in top. Baking it now. Could you double the filling and use two sheets? I might try that next time.

This turned out lovely! Dufour pastry box said 325 on convection, so when it was time to lower the temp I lowered to 325 — when the oven hit that temp, I turned on convection. Puffy, sturdy with a nice fluffy inside.

Followed recipe but did cut off a tiny bit of the edge all the way around as suggested.  Also, popped the once cooked pastry in the fridge for about an hour before slicing in half.   Used some sea salt I got in Hawaii for the flaky salt on top.  Delicious!!
My husband requested this for Christmas morning and it did not disappointed. Groans of happiness were heard all around the table. It was surprisingly easy to make just as written. Highly recommend!

Sohla for the win! Loved this. If you haven't seen "The Big Brunch" be sure to check it out. It's a lovely show (think "British Baking" in tone). Sohla is the tough love judge of the three and you'll definitely learn a thing or two.
Dumb question:  What size baking sheet is this for?  I’m assuming half-size, but I want to make this for a quarter-size sheet.
depends how big your puff pastry is
We made homemade puff pastry (King Arthur recipe) and separated dough, baking on two different pans. Watch time in oven; ours would have burned. It’s delicious. Not two sweet. A Christmas keeper.
Amazing!  We made it for Christmas morning.  It was beautiful, smelled amazing and was absolutely delicious!

This is awesome, easy and fun

It took much longer to brown than specified, but was worth the wait
I’m in the midst of baking this. My puff pastry is about five inches high. I did not poke holes in the pastry dough….was I supposed to!? I’m still forging ahead but wondering how this will turn out!?

It turned out great. It was easy to cut in half since it was so high.  Once I put it back in the oven with the frangipane inside and on top and started baking it again, the middle sink in so it ended up looking more like the picture. Everyone enjoyed it and it really is very easy to make. I will definitely make it again!

I think of this as more a mille feuille than a croissant, which is a yeasted pastry. I decided to add raspberries to the interior and serve it as Christmas dessert. My puff pastry developed a steeply slanted top which broke under the weight of the frangipane, so I ended up mixing frangipane and puff pastry crumbs and hoping the almonds would hide the problem. Tasted good, but wasn’t very pretty! Next time I might make my own puff pastry, even though it’s so much work.

A Christmas miracle. Agree with comments about groans of happiness and fun to make. (Make your own puff pastry? Your call.) I made the first bake of store-bought pastry and the frangipane two days ahead of time, assembled and final bake day of. It is beautiful and scrumptious.
Made exactly as is, came out perfect and was a huge hit. Any imperfections in baking/slicing/filling didn’t matter at all, one of the easiest showstoppers I’ve ever made!

I baked as directed and ended up with a pillow. I should've known that puff pastry needs to be pierced in some way. If you don't, you end up with a big shattered mess when you try to spread the topping on.

I scanned the recipe, rather than read carefully, and that led to a time crunch. I see now how easy it is to partially bake, and even fully bake in advance.  The crispness lasts, so this recipe is fully dependable if cooking for an event.

My husband called it "Christmas in your mouth"!
Truth be told, i did put all the filling in the middle and did and egg wash on the top.  Really good, but if you do that, there's a good chance that when you cut the pastry into pieces some of the filling will run out, making this easier eaten with a fork.

I made it with organic gluten free almond flour, the only kind I could find. It did become rather thick and hard to spread. I heated it up a little to soften it a bit, but it helped only a little. Next time I’ll try adding more melted butter.

If you ask me, you can easily double the amount of filling in the recipe.
Super delicious! The only sub I made was using monk fruit sugar instead of granulated, and it tasted great!

This tasted fine, but trying to cut the baked pastry in half while not letting it crumble to pieces is nearly impossible, as is trying to spread the thick frangipani on the brittle pastry. In this respect this was, well, a recipe for disaster.
Really good and very easy!  My future edits:  Will use 1/8 of the filling and much less brushed on top.  Other than that, delicious.
This was incredibly easy and very tasty.  Made it Christmas morning (did the frangipane 2 days earlier, then let it come to room temperature before trying to spread it).  I wouldn't change a thing.

Making this for the second time. I love the texture and taste. One question, however. When the oven temperature is reduced from 425 to 350, does the pastry puff stay in the oven or should it come out so it doesn’t get too brown?
It looks like it stays in for another 20-30 minutes. The 425 is to brown the outside and get it puffed, and then the additional time at 350 for the first bake is to dry out the inside!
This turned out so well. I made a half batch of the puff pastry from Baking with Julia.
Very easy to put together. Quite delicious, will definitely make again. I agree with a previous comment on reducing the salt in the filling, next time I will add 1/2 tsp.

Made as directed but my puff pastry (TJs all butter) was square and not the oblong the photo shows.  Given the smaller size, there was too much filling for my taste.  Next time I’ll roll it out a bit longer if the pastry will permit.  Or I’ll try with PF pastry.  It was, as previously mentioned, a pillow.  Nonetheless, it was delicious and I’ll play around with the concept.
One word: Amazing
Made this with phyllo dough, because that’s what I had. Worked well, but would recommend brushing top and sides with melted butter before first bake, and sides before second bake.
One more note...would dispense with the salt on top. This amazing piece owns "sweet." Don't recall any salty flavor from any such croissant in France...
Just made this this morning and it is amazing! No changes needed whatsoever (except you’re going to wish you made two!) Loved by all!
I followed the recipe with two exceptions — I used vegan puff pastry and I doubled the filling recipe, so I’d have plenty, plus leftovers (which I baked alongside the pastry in a ramekin). Delicious!

I couldn't get puff Pastry sheets and subbed puff Pastry Shells. Per shell packaging, I did NOT thaw prior to 1st baking. Apart from that, followed all recipe  instructions. DELICIOUS and pretty as individual filled shells.

Follow directions exactly. It is incredibly easy AND delicious. One family member asked me to double the filling next time; I won't. It's good as is.

I followed the recipe.. 10 min at 425, then 20 at 350..baked beautifully...however it flaked like crazy which puff pastry likes to do.. doesn't make a lot of sense spreading the paste on something that wants to fall apart.. wondering if it would've been smart to underbake it somewhat and then do the spread? After all it's not like it's a traditional croissant dough recipe..

In the end, I scattered the crumbs all the way to the edges and on top, along with the sliced almonds. Because I suspected that it would be messy to eat, I cut it into smaller pieces for serving. It was delicious, but messy.
Forgot to mention that I agree that 1t. kosher salt is too much. And I love salt!
I followed directions exactly as Ash did. Mine was a mess. Tasty. A little to brown and shattered. We ate of course but it should state light brown.

My sheet was folded in thirds and it definitely caused the pastry to form in three distinct pieces which broke on slicing. It was also difficult to spread the frangipane on the domed tops. Will try rolling the creases before the first bake next time.
This was so easy I was afraid I wasn't doing it right, and so good I'll make it again and again.  Extremely well-received by whole family!
This is a great recipe, but 1 1/2 teaspoons of almond extract is too much!  It is a very strong extract, and half that amount would have been better.
So easy, so delicious!!!  It is so easy to put together in the morning for breakfast.  My family devoured it!
Thinking I will roll the pastry a bit to get rid of the trifold lines on top.  Doubled the filling and it was too much. Yummy still but 1.5 times would be better. Was amazingly good and easy.

Made this exactly as written except for the sprinkle of salt and powdered sugar at the end. I did trim the edges of the puff pastry as suggested and did not roll or press the dough prior to baking since that wasn’t mentioned. Ended up with a square pastry rather than rectangular but it easily cut into eight pieces. Next time I may reduce the sugar in the filling by half to see if we still love it when it’s less sweet. Impressive finished product for very little effort!
1/2 the sugar!
Followed the recipe exactly, turned out excellent, though it was a bit too salty for my taste.

I would use a lot less flaky salt on top than you think it needs. Store-bought puff pastry (Pepperidge Farm) is already salty, and so is the almond filling. I sprinkled chocolate chips over the almond filling before replacing the baked pastry top. Tasted great (except for the excess salt).

Like somebody else, I, too, was in trepidation, but no need, it came out superbly and easily and was a huge success w. my guests. I did make 1 1/2 times the filling and was v. glad I did. Don't skimp on it!
PS: b.t.w., as a vegan, I made it with Myoko's butter and with "Just Egg". Worked perfectly.
This was divine.  And easy.  Don't overthink it.  Read directions and BAKE! It's not that hard.
This is a genius idea. I’ve made it twice. Fantastic on a lazy Sunday with a cup of hot coffee.
So good!  Next time, will trim the edges very slightly prior to baking to ensure an even puff. I did double the filling.  I used slivered almonds on top which gave more of a crunch. Love it!

Fantastic! I did use an all-butter puff pastry and extended the initial baking time by an extra 7 minutes. Great for a brunch party.

I made this morning but I didn't take out my puff pastry until the day of. I let it sit on the counter for 20-30 minutes (per package directions) and it unfolded just fine. This recipe was easy to make, tasty...just messy to eat!

Made the chocolate hazelnut version(see video), would probably cut the sugar or maybe I had too much chocolate, is that a thing?  Was delicious, love how easy it was definitely going to make fancy puff pastry sandwiches
Too salty as directed. Will reduce salt by half next time

I wanted to try this but where do I buy the right kind of puff pastry? I went to three different grocery stores: the first two said they didn't know what it is. The third one was trying to be helpful and got me a roll of "fillo dough" which were thin sheets of flour separated by thin sheets of papers. After taking the papers out and baking the rest for 10 minutes, they came out like sheets of dry, almost burnt papers. Edible but not useful for my purpose. HELP!

Yeah, fillo dough is NOT puff pastry. Any major supermarket should have at least Pepperidge Farm puff pastry in the freezer -- look maybe in the frozen rolls or desserts section -- but PF is made with oil, not butter, though if that's all you can get it will work just fine. If you're lucky enough to have a Trader Joe's or Whole Foods, they usually have butter puff pastry, and not only will they know what it is, but also WF (mine at least) has the Dufour brand she uses in the video.
Also, if PF is all you can get, the taste will be fine with all the filling and you won't be comparing it with butter. And WF charges $13 for Dufour while PF is half the cost.

Dufour All-Butter frozen puff pastry. Accept no substitutes (although I hear that Trader Joe's is good too, and probably half the price). Dufour is probably available in a specialty or gourmet store in the dessert freezer. If you're in New York, OF COURSE it's at Kalustyan's. It's also at Garden of Eden, Whole Foods and Westside Market.

My mother made this for Easter breakfast. This makes it hard to weigh in because she is known for eschewing specifics in recipes,but she swears up and down she followed it exactly…it was INEDIBLY SALTY. We narrowed it down to a combo of possibly too salty puff pastry and the flaky salt sprinkled on top. It was painful. Also the puff pastry was browned and cracker-dry as baked per recipe direction. When it crumbled and sank into the finished bake, it was greasy.
1 teaspoon of salt is excessive.  I'd cut it to 1/4 or 1/2 at most.
Make sure filling is in middle of rows, save enough for topping

This recipe is a keeper. Delicious, flaky pastry that looks amazing and tastes as good as any pastry I’ve purchased from the top bakeries in the Bay Area. It’s seriously impressive. I did double the frangipane, because why not? Go big or go home, right? Enjoy!
The cooking guide video is helpful and very fun.

I made this with a leftover sheet of puff pastry (about  8.6oz), so mine was on the smaller side. Although the center collapsed under the weight of the frangipane and almonds during the second bake, the error was masked after being sliced and coated with powdered sugar. The frangipane is very good, and I’d like to incorporate it into other baked goods.

I think the instruction “continue baking until dry, crisp and deeply browned” is off. I baked both times as directed - on the short end of baking time - stopping at browned (slightly more than golden). The finished pastry was overly crisp/dry and crumbled apart when cutting it into pieces and trying to eat it. Texturally it did not resemble a croissant at all, more like a cracker. Flavor wise I found it to be too sweet (though I normally love the sweetness of almond croissants).

Definitely use all butter puff pastry, used Pepperidge Farm because it’s what I had and it didn’t puff enough. Who knew there was no butter in it?! Don’t cook until dark brown, just golden as in video. I doubled the filling, it’s rich with double filling and will feed more with smaller pieces. Next time I’ll try all butter puff pastry (TJs or Dufour from WFs) and just single filling to compare. Despite overdone pastry it was really delicious.

Made for Sunday brunch this morning. "One of the best pastries I've ever eaten," from the taste tester. I feel like doubling the frangiapane would be smart (for one sheet of Pep Farm), Very flaky and not too sweet. Super easy. Impressive, Delicious. Also going to try with Nutella and play with a chocolate version. Definintely a keeper!
I made the ham and cheese version and it was so good! Easy to make and perfect for brunch. I found the same puff pastry Sohla used and it was delicious. Can’t wait to try the other versions!

Absolutely delicious, have been meaning to make this for a while and so glad I did. I used amaretto instead of rum and it was excellent. Also used 25g less sugar and 25g more almond flour, and very happy with the result. There's just about enough frangipane to pastry ratio, but if feeling indulgent, I'd double the frangipane as that's the best bit. I've put half the pastry made up with frangipane (pre-second-bake) in the freezer, in the hope I can bake from frozen next time for guests.

Very, very special. Beautiful and delicious for company, perfect for brunch
Doctored it a bit to our tastes-

Doubled the filling so I could have more on the top

Made this for brunch this morning using Dufour puff pastry. The almond filling is delicious. Baking times are spot on.
Too sweet. Use less sugar and sprinkle chocolate chips inside.

Followed recipe exactly - I’m a novice baker and the video was super helpful. Results were delicious. Leftovers were excellent the next day. Maybe would cut a few minutes of baking time

Came out beautiful but I recommend not using Pepperidge farm puff pastry. Flaky and no flavor. Splurge with a nicer option if you can!
Trader Joe's is a great option.

Made this today for a family brunch and it was a major hit, everyone loved it. Followed the recipe and only change was I spread about 2 T Nutella (warmed a little) on top of the frangipane. So easy and so impressive.

Wasn’t as fluffy as I would have liked… but my husband loved it!

Delicious! And also: double the frangipane.
This is a winner!
This is delicious and very easy!
Difficult to eat, but pretty yummy! Very sweet, I feel like it would almost be better as a dessert than a breakfast. I used unblanched almond flour and had no issues spreading it.

It really helped to see how this—and the variations—were put together!
So easy and tasty! Needs extra filling, though, especially if you use a larger sheet of puff pastry. Next time I’ll double that part of the recipe.
Loved it !

Like a cross between a Galette des rois and an almond croissant. 
Used Dufour and an extra egg white i had laying around. Lemon zest in follow, grand marnier would be good too.

Just made this on Christmas morning.  As she recommended in her video I made 2- one the almond as written here and also a ham and cheese. Both were excellent.  Makes a very special and elegant breakfast for family. I also took her idea to make the puff pastry in advance so this was a super easy option that’s looks fabulous and professional. I will definitely make this again.  My only regret is that I didn’t make her chocolate hazelnut version- that’s next!!

On a lark, I made a savory version of this, barely related to the printed recipe. For the filling, I made a sausage gravy (basically a béchamel with sausage instead of butter), with cheddar cheese on top of that and some sliced scallions on that. The top got some more gravy (be light here - ours got weighed down from using too much  sausage gravy on top) and some more grated cheddar, and hit it with scallion greens when it comes out. Get the ok from your cardiologist and enjoy.
Be careful with the flaky salt, it can ruin the overall flavor. If I make this again, I will not use the rum but the vanilla alternative instead. It was much too harsh for a breakfast type pastry.
The video shows two variations but there doesn't seem to be a recipe for the chocolate variation. What quantity of hazelnuts, chopped chocolate, and cocoa?

One star because it is labled "croissant."
Croissants are named for their historical crescent shape.
They are not rectangular slabs, even if they are a buttery, flaky, viennoiserie pastry.
It may taste good, but find a name appropriate to its shape. Perhaps pan almond.

Can this be made without that one egg? What can be substituted for that egg without ruining the texture/taste?

My wife has a severe egg allergy, and it seems every pastry recipe calls for one or more eggs to be used. I have to ignore them all, but I love almond croissants...
I haven't made this recipe but a vegan replacement for egg is one tablespoon ground flax seed mixed with three tablespoons water. I don't see any reason why this wouldn't work in this recipe.
Will make tonight. Love your style, Sohla.

This recipe is fine. It didn’t make me want to stop buying almond croissants and make this instead. It was messy to eat and finicky to finish - more work than happiness I felt with the finished product. My husband, son & myself ate it and it was tasty but not special for us.
No sea salt needed as a topping.
Meh.  Texture and flavor are a little off.  Too crunchy and sweet ...
I made this for Christmas day.  It is wonderful!  I did not change anything!  It looked beautiful and tasted wonderful.

Visually, turned out just like the pictures and was not difficult to assemble.  It could be the puff pastry I used, but it was dry and unappetizing.  The filling flavor was similar to a bakery almond croissant, but the consistency was too loose.  Of the 3 people that tried, all agreed it wasn't worth making again.
Mine pf puffed up too high so the almond paste ran off to the sides but still delicious

This was fabulous. I was surprised how it got devoured by a morning breakfast group. When cooking I got concerned as puff pastry stayed flat on one end and I thought it was not going to work. But I managed to cut through it, despite its thinness in a few places. Even though it tore in places, it all came together on the second bake and was amazing.
Not easy to find a sheet as large as shown. I suppose you could use two smaller sheets.

Made this for a NYE party and it was a hit. Who doesn't want a fancy breakfast snack at midnight with your champers? I couldn't find the fancy butter-only pastry so I used a big brand one. I made one recipe of filling and stretched it to fit both of the shells in the package. The frangipane is so flavorful that it did not matter. Still tasted delicious. Will definitely be taking this to parties in the future. It's a fast way to make new friends. And, it took only about 15 minutes of effort. Win!
I creamed 8 oz of cream cheese with 4 oz unmelted butter and kept the rest of the filling ingredients the same. The result isn’t a “frangipane,” but it’s a tasty cheesecake-esque riff.
Made as written. Liked it— would have loved if the filling were doubled. Will do that next time.
Delicious but too salty! Even without the salt sprinkled on top. Next time, I would cut the salt in half. But it’s such a great premise and I’m looking forward to doing more with puff pastry!
Impressive for brunch, but low effort!

I made 2 of these, one the standard almond because it's my favorite flavor. The other was the ham and cheese she describes in the video. I used Dukes maple bourbon mustard, sliced baby swiss (because that's what I had), and sprinkled sesame seeds on top. Can't wait to try them. I used Pepperidge Farm puff pastry, and it seems much smaller than the one you used. Can you tell me which brand you used?
@Made To Take To A Reception I believe she used Dufour ? Can be bought at WF, TJ etc!

Has anyone out there refrigerated their assembled croissant, and baked it in the morning?  If so, how did it come out?
Thank you
Just wondering if you or anyone ended up trying this and if it worked?

I tried this and the pastry crumbled (admittedly I didn't use the best pastry but the good stuff is hard to find). I then started over went with puff pastry on the bottom, frangipane, puff pastry on top, frangipane and almonds, then bake.  It was delicious.

Excellent recipe Sohla! Made the Almond and ham & cheese; they were loved by all. Found Dufour puff pastry for most Thanksgiving weekend guests and also tried to use Schar gluten-free pastry but it never puffed.. Wanted to write to tell others, this didn't work with frozen GF puff but was loved by everyone else.
Didn’t turn out super pretty BUT was delicious. Crowd pleaser at Christmas

Is it necessary to wait until the cooked puff pastry is cooled before cutting it in half? Also, does anyone have tips for how to spread the frangipane on the top layer without causing it to turn into a crusty mess?

Have made this several times, and it always delights guests. Get the butter puff pastry if you can. (I learned from another reader that Trader Joe's sells it during the holidays--I now stock up when it's available!) We had a bumper crop of raspberries in our garden last summer. I froze a bunch and like to add some to the filling. Makes a good thing even better!
May I ask why we are calling this a croissant? It is clearly not crescent shaped. Why not simply call it an almond pastry?

I followed this recipe as written and served it at brunch.
Everyone loved it and had seconds.
How long does it keep.  Can you let it cool and cut it on the next day?  Thanks.
Could this be made egg-free with flaxseed meal?
Sooooooooooo goood!!

Question: Has anyone tried to substitute pistachio cream for frangipane?  I’m looking for suggestions for how to adapt the ingredients, wondering if the cream might be sufficient without adding anything else.

Fabulous!  After coming back from Italy I was craving croissants.  I will use less salt next time and skip the salt on top.  Also trying to work out high altitude cooking.  I think I might have to cook it a little longer the first time at a little lower temperature.  Anyone else have a suggestion for the high altitude?  After I cut the top off, the bottom was still a little mushy.  All said and done it was a delicious first try.  Cannot wait to try it again and perfect it a little more.

Has anyone tried to make this at high altitude using Dufour puffed pastry?  I have tried to bake it correctly the first time and then I tried to increase the temperature and less time the second time.  However, both times the pastry was still damp in the middle but the top was brown and it also collapsed.    Any other suggestions?
I used a teaspoon of salt while doubling the filling and found the filling too salty.
Made vegan by replacing egg with 1/4 cup applesauce, used dairy free butter and pastry, and it came out beautifully. Huge hit and easy for a party, definitely making again!

@Ruth no one is being snobby about salt at all. Diamond Crystal salt is the 'standard' used by chefs & recipe developers around the world; if you look at Morton Kosher  Salt you will see the sodium content is vastly different from Diamond Crystal Kosher salt.
