Baking refrigerated dough for 12 min (vs 10 min) yielded a more crinkly cookie that was still chewy. 10 min created a more doughy cookie.
These are my new all time favorite cookie!! Bold and spicy and they don't get stale. Great chewy texture. My daughter made a second batch right after the first because we ate them so fast.
I did them at 375F for 10 minutes, slamming the tray halfway through. They spread better than at 350 and still retained the crispy/chewy texture.
Subbed ground ginger for fresh and they still turned out perfect
Make sure butter is fully solidified before continuing. Liquid butter will make cookies spread
Does anyone have a gluten free suggestion for this recipe?

These are amazing! Be forewarned—I used a very small saucepan to brown the butter and once I added the ginger & nutmeg it bubbled over furiously. So opt for the bigger pan. And I used blackstrap molasses as that’s all I had and they are awesome. Best ginger cookie I’ve ever made. Intrigued by the brown butter step and all the confectioners sugar but the result is magic. Thanks, Sohla!

Pretty good. I made as directed, but added 1/4 tsp of clove and 1/4 tsp cayenne pepper. This made a very spicy cookie! I also poured the hot browned butter from the pan to the mixing bowl of the KitchenAid before adding the spices. 

The requirement to use confectioners' sugar is a bit fussy. I wonder if they'd turn out using standard granulated sugar?
I am no expert, but I suspect that granulated would melt and result in a very loose crumb. Syrup or a concentrated sugar solution might avoid that?

@Aaron K.
“Pretty good”? Is the understatement of the decade :)) !  These are amazing.  I read elsewhere that regular granulated sugar would melt and spread more, being thinner, would not be chewy in center. Frequent bakers often have confectioners sugar on hand.

I've made this recipe a few times and they are always so good. This Christmas my sister was in town and so I made them vegan. I subbed a 1/4 cup apple sauce for the egg and Miyoko's vegan butter and the cookies ended up just as flavorful and fluffy as the original version, they were a hit! The vegan butter also browned really well I was surprised.
I made these with butter and they were not chewy at all, tasty but very crisp. I substituted a vegetable oil spread the second time, nice and chewy on the inside, crisp edges.

These cookies are amazing. I followed the recipe exactly except that i reduced the sugar to 200 grams and that was perfect for me. I also got a little lazy and didn't make the coating, instead rolling them in a little turbinado sugar. I also made them smaller so I'd get more cookies out of the batch. For the smaller size, I found a slightly lower oven temperature (360) for about 8 minutes worked well. Regarding the fresh ginger, two pieces that were about 2" each got me to 50 grams.

My family and friends said these were the best cookies they ever had! We all love ginger!
I appreciated the notes on the different kosher salt measurements to use for different brands…
I will always bake this when having family or friends over or going somewhere when I need to bring something!
Delicious, despite the fact that I substituted canola oil for butter as we have a genetic cholesterol problem.

I'm perennially in search of a recipe that recreates a treasured childhood memory: chewy ginger molasses cookies from a mall coffee chain Paradise Bakery. This recipe nailed the flavor but mine came out dry and airy. I followed recipe to the T. What am I doing wrong? Baking at sea level in a gas oven.

Listen to the comments… these stick together. An inch isn’t enough. I did mine from frozen like the note says and they still spread! I measured to the gram, no substitutions. I have a full sized baking sheet. They all fit with 1.5” clearance. Next time I would bake in 2 batches. I got 4 pretty cookies. The other 22 are all square edges and ugly. Not winning the Christmas party this year for sure. 

They are delicious though! The spice is warming and perfect salty sweetness. Not too harsh. Cozy.

Made these vegan for my kid with milk and egg allergies and they were soooo good! Used Miyokos brand unsalted vegan butter (contains nuts) and Bobs Red Mill egg replacer. The vegan butter browns nicely, but it doesn’t fizzle out the same way real butter does, so no need to wait for that cue. An it took closer to an hour for the butter mixture to set back up in the refrigerator before adding the other ingredients. We all loved them, and you’d never know they were vegan!

Same as a previous commenter, mine looked nothing like the photo.  They were much lighter in color and puffed up.  So flavorful though! I followed directions to a T except using Alessi Coarse Sea Salt instead of Diamond. I was looking forward to a darker, flat cookie but am no less satisfied; they are delicous! Thanks to others' hints as to watching out for spices bubbling up in butter.  Also, I'd never heard of banging the cookie pan, researched it and tried it.  Still puffy cookies.
Adore these cookies!  I think the key to their intense yet smooth flavor is blooming the spices in the hot browned butter.
If I cut these out with a gingerbread man cutter, will they make adorable puffy Michelin Men or blobs? Asking for a friend...

For my fellow dairy-free bakers - I knew dairy free butter wouldn't brown, so I improvised! I ended up just mixing the ginger and spices with the butter and carrying on like a regular cookie. Nope. Super greasy and flat, but still a great flavor. So I salvaged by warming up dough and adding more flour - until it was barely sticky. Then re-chilled and baked. MUCH better results - not exactly chewy, more like crisp on the outside and pillowy on the inside. Super delicious!

I tried a gluten-free version, using half almond flour and half oat flour. Cookies were extremely runny/spready at first (maybe because I didn’t let the butter cool enough, based on other comments) and also were very quickly getting bubbly & lacey @ 350. To fix, I used *very* small scoops of dough (about 1 tbsp, 2+ in apart) and I turned the oven down to 300 — they cooked slowly, and instead of the chewy centers, this resulted in a beautifully crispy, light melt-in-your-mouth texture. So good!

These were fantastic. Mine came out completely deflated so were named Flat Stanley cookies. Not sure what happened though I did not have the right amount of powdered sugar and had to add a bit of granulated. As one commenter pointed out, they were pretty buttery for this type of cookie. Not sure why the note says they are best eaten on the day of baking. Three days later, mine are still extremely yummy.

SUCH a good recipe. I ended up accidentally purchasing about half the ginger required for this and it turned out completely find and totally ginger-y enough. Also made 12 cookies instead of 24 (they were 2.4oz each) and they were a great size. Everyone was asking for the recipe!
Made these twice w gluten free flour. They come out terrible but taste delicious! Stick to regular flour!!

To make these, one requires patience, the determination to see things through, and a willingness to try new things. It’s analogous to trying to win the heart of a beautiful woman. worth it though, these are delicious, delicate, cozy, and Christmassy. Anyways:

Ingredients: Canadian here, huge thanks to my patriots in the comments. Confirming that it’s fancy molasses and icing sugar. 
Dough: I used a peeler and then a grater. I’m not sure why there are so many people complaining about this, it goes fast. I didn’t freeze the ginger. I learned from the comments and used a pot 4x as big as the butter. Weirdly, when browning the butter foamed up to near the rim so definitely use a big pot - but, I took it off the heat for 4 min and then added the spices, and there was no impact. 
Baking: I rolled mine half the size (one tbsp), froze, and then baked directly from frozen at 375 for 10 min. I made a double batch, was about 75 cookies. watch the last min as these burn fast. Also they do indeed spread a lot, leave a good two inches. 

*These are FUSSY, they need a lot of attention when baking. You need to pan bang, rotate, cool for a very long time, etc. 
* My only regret is not rolling these into cylinders so that the cookies were taller in the middle. I did spheres and they’re flat. Still delish but not as chewy as they could be. 
*These have a real kick to them and are NOT kid friendly. I made them for a family Christmas get together and had to make chocolate chip for the kids.

No one else noted that the butter foams like crazy and overflows while you are browning it, before putting in the ginger. I opted to use a small saucepan and put the ginger in later, and in the browning process the butter foamed over. I had enough for a double batch, but after the butter overflowed from my saucepan I only had enough butter for a single batch. Beware of butter overflow!

Chewy, gingery, taste explosions! These were quite popular here, great accompaniment to hot cocoa on a cold day. The weight notation on the fresh grated ginger is appreciated--50 grams is a large mound! Next time I may replace some or add chopped candied ginger for another ginger note and some texture.

I used blackstrap molasses even though it says not to and they were amazing— so if that’s all you’ve got, don’t worry about it. I put them in a Christmas cookie box and everyone was requesting more of these ones. I have made them quite a few times now and after baking for 10 min they were slightly underdone. I think 11 minutes is really the right number. That could be because of the blackstrap molasses.

I have noticed recent recipes from the Times asking for Diamond Crystal kosher salt. I don't know why they single out this brand. I just buy kosher salt and if happens to be Diamond Crystal that's fine, and if not, also fine. I've never had a problem with any kosher salt.

They specify because Diamond Crystal is coarser than Morton's (probably the main other readily available kosher salt), so if you use the same amount of Morton's salt as is called for in Diamond Crystal salt, it'll be a lot saltier.

Diamond Crystal salt and Morton’s weigh differently even if you use the same volume. That’s why it’s helpful for recipes to identify what it was made with. One teaspoon of Morton kosher salt weighs 4.8 grams; one teaspoon of Diamond Crystal kosher weighs 2.8 grams. There’s a good Cooks a illustrated article on the difference.

Any thoughts on the effect of subbing  powdered monk fruit sugar or stevia instead of sugar in these?  And almond flour as a wheat flour substitute? Trying to be better about carbs.

That's a really bad idea here, because both the confectioner's sugar and the wheat-based flour are important to how the cookie spreads and to its final texture. You're best off looking to a shortbread that's built to work with almond or coconut flour and whose texture relies primarily on butter to hold the flour together, where you can replace the small quantity of sugar more or less safely.

@Chdgreene
Substituting wheat flour in many cookie recipes is undetectable. I’ve been making lots of tried and true recipes with 50% wheat flour 50% AP flour. The results are consistent with 100% AP flour. Tasers cannot tell the difference. There are some articles about this out there—maybe America’s test kitchen, but I can’t remember.

You will not have a problem with the flavor or texture of these cookies if you use wheat flour. You could always start with a 50-50 blend.

I can’t speak to the sugar substitutes. Personally, if I’m going to eat sugar, I want it to be in a great dessert. This is personal choice, of course. So I don’t put sugar in anything else and I don’t eat dessert every day. I’d rather moderate everything else in my nutrition and enjoy a delicious cookie.

Happy baking!

I suspect your gingerbread men will be more like man-shaped blobs, as this is a cookie that spreads. They'll still taste great, of course.
Very spicy cookie - loved it and will definitely be saving this recipe for more batches. For some reason they were pretty underdone for me at 10 minutes and needed another 5 minutes.

My husband took these to an office cookie exchange (Oh, Covid, your ways are not our ways anymore, evidently). I was afraid they would be too gingery, because there's ALOT of ginger in there. But they were a huge hit. The best loved cookies of the day, with requests for the recipe. Thanks, Sohla, for an amazing new Christmas cookie for my rotation.

One of the best gingerbread cookies I’ve had in a long time. The brown butter adds a nice depth of flavor which complements the ginger beautifully. The ground ginger add a nice amount of ginger spice without it becoming overpowering. I wasn’t sure about mixing the sugar with ground ginger to coat the dough as the mixture became clumpy. I may use ground ginger powder for the sugar mixture next time. This is definitely going into my Christmas cookie mix!
Is confectioners sugar granulated sugar?
No, confectioners' sugar is also sold as powdered sugar and is ground much more finely than typical granulated sugar. Most grocery stores should carry both.

I really loved this spicy ginger cookie! I also thought they were too big — next time I’ll try 20 gr per cookie — and they definitely need more than 1 inch between cookies unless you’re going for a sheet pan cookie! I will definitely make these again (though I think my brother-in-law thought they were too spicy).

If I use blackstrap molasses (because I'm halfway through baking these and realized it's all I have) will these still be ok? Or will they be awful enough that I should sub dark corn syrup for some instead? Or some other substitution? Going to the store to get regular molasses is not an option :)
Great cookies! Definitely give at least TWO inches apart; even chilled, these fused together when I baked them about an inch and a half apart. Still good though!
Same here. Any ideas why.
Has anyone tried this with reduced sugar?  That is a lot of sugar for 24 cookies.
Add some cut up candied ginger…triple g dose!

I wonder if one could make this using ginger preserve? Without adding as much sugar. Ginger preserve is easier to retain, and has other uses. It can also serve to bring out subtler flavours in the spices.

These cookies are delicious. But something went wrong. The cookies were completely flat. They didn’t rise at all. I weighed everything and followed the directions closely. I made one deviation, I doubled the vanilla. Was that enough to cause them to fail?

These are delicious cookies.  The large amount of butter has a lot to do with that.  I like the added pepper but if I make them again I will not use koshering salt.  That’s just a personal preference- I don’t like the grittiness.  In my oven they took longer than ten minutes to get done - more like 12-13.
Wonderful

if you freeze ginger to grate, do you peel it and then freeze?  Or do you grate with skin on?

Regarding Salt weighing different amounts, if recipe ingredients were shown by weight (kg) it would be so much easier!
I peeled the ginger first, then froze and it worked great!

Tasty. But after refrigerating the dough, covered, overnight, it developed the strangest texture. Very hard and crumbly. I think this is the combination of the butter and the powdered sugar. Recipe says to scoop or spoon cold dough onto cookie sheets but the Hulk couldn't have done it! Instead I hand-rolled the dough into lumpy little planetoids, using the heat of my hands to soften it. Didn't affect texture of finished cookie. But next time I won't refrigerate it so long.
Not a fan. Too greasy. The size was off. When I switched to tbsp. scoop they came out better. Too sweet for me.

Lovely recipe but way too much pepper!  Looking at the proportions of other spicy gingerbread recipes, one would do better with using only half of what's recommended or forgoing the pepper altogether. Make sure that you use the finest setting when grinding.  Even with a slight pepper aftertaste though - these cookies are scrumptious!

Love David's tip of freezing the peeled ginger root. This made the microplaning much more efficient and the cookies nice and spicy! I had to send most of these off to work with my husband...my willpower is strong, but no match for a batch of these cookies just sitting on the counter...staring at me...
I wish I had read the comments about using a big pot for the butter! I used a small pot and lost some butter when i added the fresh ginger (who knew?!).

I love Ginger and the taste of these cookies is perfect! I agree Microplane is easier than grating. I mixed everything in a food processor and this helped to smooth out the spices and almond flour that was substituted for a gluten-free cookie! My new favorite cookie!

I love gingerbread, and these cookies truly encapsulate the cozy feeling of the holiday season and what I love most about a good gingerbread cookie. They're so good! I didn't have fresh ginger or nutmeg, so I just continued the recipe with only the ground spices and they came out great. I got a little impatient and only let them sit in the fridge for about 1 1/2 hours, but I think they turned out pretty good! I highly recommend this recipe- it's a Christmas staple!

I made these yesterday. They were a hit! This was my first time using fresh ginger in a cookie recipe, and all I can say is, wow! After reading the recipe and all the reviews it was a call into action! Love this cookie, and so did the family!

Refrigerated covered overnight and w/ just a little strength I was able to scoop and roll from cold the next day, still smooth. I used the advice to microplane frozen ginger and it worked well. These are definitely very spicy but delicious. I smacked mine on the counter and they're a little too thin now, probably let them deflate on their own. 
The sugar coating called for at the end yielded 2x too much, I have the rest in a little tupperware for some other use (maybe another batch...)

WOW. These might be the best holiday spiced cookies I've ever made. Or consumed. The ginger is such a delightful bite and the pepper really adds a tiny (but delightful) kick. They also turned out to be the most attractive cookie I've ever made. The roundness and wrinkles were simply perfection. I'll be making these every opportunity I get for sure. Sohla, you're magical.

This is my favorite ginger cookie recipe to date. When rolling the dough balls in sugar and ginger before baking, I subbed some of the granulated sugar for demerara sugar (25 g of each). Compared to the granulated sugar + ginger coating, the demerara sugar addition complemented the crisp edges.
Bleached or unbleached flour?
if i want to use slightly less fresh ginger, just to cut down slightly on spice, do you recommend adding moisture in some other way to compensate?

10/10 Best Ginger cookies I've ever had.. combo of *fresh* ginger & black pepper is divine—will be on repeat every Holiday :) We made these gluten free with a 1-to-1 all purpose flour and they were perfect.
These are the best ginger cookies I have ever eaten. The black pepper makes the recipe. Delicious and easy. These won’t just be for the holidays in my house.

These are very good, but a bit fussy to put together in my opinion. Like others have noted, I added the spices and ginger directly into a smallish saucepan of melted butter only to find the mixture foam uncontrollably, and lost a considerable portion of butter down the sink. I estimated around 25% loss and modified other ingredient amounts accordingly. End result is enjoyable but I will stick to "Grammy's Spice Cookies" for my holiday spice cookie needs.
I didn't have ground ginger so I added more fresh & more pepper. I could have cooked them for another couple of minutes for additional crispness. I just ate two and they were fantastic!

Divine. Thank you to Jenna for idea to use (thawed) ginger cubes from Trader Joe’s. I rolled chilled dough into 19g balls and froze. Baked direct from freezer at 375° (not convection) for 9 1/2 mins. Perfect!

I don't care about too many cookies - I make a ton of them, especially this time of year, but I very rarely eat more than just a taste for flavor. I could not stop coming back to these! The spice is such a lovely and delightful punch.

Excellent flavor.  I made the dough the night before so that I could spread out the steps. Mine look nothing like the photo and spread a lot.  Not sure what I did but the flavor alone means I would make them again.  Perhaps a bit smaller next time though.

Delightfully spicy. I added fresh ground cardamom and cloves to the recipe, because I got excited. I made the cookies 20g each, and banged the pan about 2/3 of the way through baking. Cookies are great - chewy, spicy, and exactly what I want from a gingerbread. Thank you Sohla!!
It says these are best eaten on the day.  I am making them as part of my Christmas gifts.  Will they be bad?
My absolute favorite Christmas cookie. It gives me speculoos vibes. I froze my ginger before grating, which made the process a lot easier. Sohla has done it again!

1) Weigh the freshly grated ginger; don’t use a tablespoon measure. Otherwise quantities can be wildly different depending on compaction. 
2) Make these much smaller. I’d say 20 grams max. 
3) These spread a lot. Six to eight on a sheet max at 30 grams. 
4) The balls need to be slightly flattened when you put them on the sheet so they don’t roll and touch. 
5) Ten minutes was not enough for 30 gram cookies (and my oven temperature is accurate). More like 15.
Exceptional. I added some crystallized ginger to the batter but it wasn’t necessary. Absolutely delicious cookies.

Excellent recipe. Lots of zesty flavor. Wouldn’t change a thing. 

The tip on a medium-sized pot for the spices-in-butter step proved helpful. Props!
These are perfect. Love the punchy flavor. Take care not to use a small saucepan as others mention but these hit it out of the park! Perfect complement to eggnog on Christmas Eve.
Made Xmas 2021, drizzled with chocolate glaze

I made these for the first time for Christmas this year.  They are a universal hit!  I reduced the sugar and substituted eurythriol for the remainder.  I was then able to use sparkly decorative sugars. But the spices seem to make it a more grown up flavour.  
I had to use blackstrap molasses, but it worked.

I have been craving a really good, chewy ginger cookie and these were just PERFECT. I don't often bake, but broke out of my mold for these and even went so far as to weigh every ingredient. Surprising how "off" a few of the ingredients were from tablespoon and cup to weighed, specifically the fresh ginger and butter. Since it's just my husband and me, I only cooked a few cookies and will keep the rest of the dough frozen to have on hand for later...better yet they will be warm from the oven!

I misread the recipe and left out the tablespoon of ground ginger from the dough -- thought it was just for the coating. Ridiculous, I know. I did add about 1/8 tsp each of cloves and cardamom. Even with my error, these are some of the best cookies I've ever made. Tremendously flavorful, excellent aftertaste, and exactly the chewy consistency I wanted. Banged the pan before cooling. Looking forward to making them again with that missing tablespoon back in the mix.
For extra kick, add a half-teaspoon red pepper flakes

Many steps no prob. but the actual timing...with cookies SO buttery - what are "browned" edges..they just sort of get darker and wrinkled while center stays lighter and puffy - out of oven they collapse..but not so "brown" as pix.  then others I cooked a bit longer - bottoms BURNED...testing to see if tops could get darker.  last few was sort of mid-day...but definitely something you have to watch. All ingred. measured on scale so my ingredients were correct.  maybe just do 1 sheet at a time ??

I’ve been looking for a good ginger molasses cookie for awhile now, and this is my new go to. The flavor has some “bite” - which I love - and the texture is exactly what I was looking for. One guest said, “I’m not a ginger cookie guy, but these are REALLY good”

The winner of our holiday cookie lineup this year, just perfect with no changes made!! Simply crisp, chewy and extremely flavorful. I must reiterate that there’s no need to peel fresh ginger, just grate it as is for a quicker process.

Thanks for the tips. I weighed my Morton kosher salt and it came to one teaspoon. I weighed my flour and it was less than 2 cups. But that's what I used. Yes, even for me, the dough balls needed to be 2-3 inches apart. A bright pan cooks the cookies a bit slower than a dark pan - perhaps 11 minutes rather than the perfect 10 in the recipe. I simply slid the parchment paper to the rack with cookies still on the paper. These are delicious.
I made a quarter recipe and weighed all ingredients. I also made smaller balls. They were picture perfect and will impress a ginger cookie lover to tears.
These cookies are the best! I’ve made many ginger cookies in my time, but this recipe is our favorite.

As I wrote previously, these are delicious. I learned that it's quite a time saver to put the fresh ginger in a food processor; I prefer my mini-Cuisinart, the bowl in the large one needs to be scraped too frequently to finely chop. I weighed all my ingredients.

Fantastic recipe! These were delicious, and I'm going to make them again today. I used 1.5 cups of sugar instead of the full 2 C, and that was just fine. Also cut back on the amount of sugar for the coating. I prefer my cookies a little less sweet.
Good, a very spicy chewy cookie. Divided dough in half and froze one half.

Love the flavor of these cookies, but I'll need to try again to get the right shape/consistency.

My cookies spread way to much, to the point that they are pretty much flat.
Things that may have gone wrong?

1. Wrong molasses? I used Grandma's Original (best buy 2018...). Doesn't say it's blackstrap.

2. Melted butter too long at too low a temperature? 

3. Dough to warm? I chilled the dough over night, but maybe they warmed up too much when rolled into balls or waiting on the counter to bake.
Best cookies ever!  Froze half the batch since there's only two of us and SO looking forward to baking a few at a time whenever the spirit moves us!

I've been obsessed with these since they were listed in "24 Days of Cookies".  The first batch turned out flat and greasy. Since then I have reduced the butter to 12 tablespoons, otherwise mix as instructed.  Don't refrigerate dough, scoop size ~17-18 grams, roll into balls, coat with sugar/ginger mix, freeze on rimmed baking tray.  Bake from frozen 8-10 minutes.  Perfect shape and consistency.  My new favorite cookie!

This recipe is a new ‘winner’ at our household. Love the bite from the fresh ginger and black pepper. Didn’t let the brown butter mixture cool completely down, before incorporating the other ingredients and they turned out just fine. Letting the cookies rest on the baking sheet for a couple of minutes allows them to set up. Love, love, love these cookies!
These are incredible.   Made as directed except used Demerara sugar with blitzed crystallized ginger for the coating.  Dynamite.
I love this recipe and expect to be making it periodically. I didn't have fresh ginger and added a bit of extra ground ginger. I also added small pieces of candied ginger and it was amazing.

This is truly the perfect cookie. It's work to make, but all totally worth it by the end. The first time I made it, I dumped the grated ginger in the melted butter as soon as it came off the heat and it frothed up. The end result was good, but the next time, I waited a couple minutes after taking the butter off the heat to let it cool a tad before putting in the ginger, the end result was a cookie with a sharper ginger bite. I always add a little bit of ground cardamom as well.

Wish I knew that my postal scale wasn’t as sensitive as I needed it to be to measure out the grated ginger lol!  I PACKED my tablespoons and it still didn’t come close to 50 grams, so I just used 3 packed Tbsp of the grated ginger and hoped for the best.  The cookies had quite a bite to them, so I’ll probably go lighter next time.  I also used the suggestion to roll the dough into a log before chilling… so much easier/cleaner.  The texture was great fresh out of the oven. Thanks!

I rolled some of the dough into a log per reader suggestion and sliced it before baking. I rolled the rest of the dough into balls, as directed in the recipe.  The balls definitely had the better texture and looked more like the recipe pic.  The sliced cookies looked more like ginger snaps you buy at the grocery store.  Texture was still nice and chewy out of the oven though.
Maybe it's just me but I needed close to like 3-4 cups of flour! These were absolutely delicious!!

Great flavor, a touch too salty for someone who doesnt cook with very much salt (note: i do cook with salt). I might scale the salt back to 3/4 tsp next time or use diamond (as instructed) instead of kosher salt (which may have been the issue).

I made this exactly as written. The cookies are good - BUT there are many ginger cookie recipes out there that are equally deep with respect to spice as well as soft that are MUCH easier to make. No need to over complicate a good thing
Agree. This was one of the most complex  cookie recipes I have ever made.

Butter took longer than expected, but foaming did stop, and it did turn brown. The solids were the size of coffee grounds and not visible until I poured it into the bowl. For shaping, because the texture was quite crumbly, I scooped it up with my hands. Shaped. And weighed on the scale so each was exactly 30g. Taste was quite good. Crystal diamond salt gave an unusual added flavor spike. Just not sure if it was worth all the trouble. Who eats each cookie batch in ONE day?
(Added a bit of cornstarch since I cut back powdered sugar, also didn’t want to use half egg so only used yolk)
I tried this recipe exactly, and these are incredibly good. I'm wishing for some pregrated ginger though. I never fail to grate a knuckle.

The bake needed more than 10 minutes for me, maybe watch them through your oven window. First batch I used the prescribed recipe time, the bottom was crisp, the middle was soggy. Second batch I added 3 minutes. 13-14 minutes seemed to be what I needed, but they taste so good I am going to eat all of them. I subbed 2 cups powdered sugar for regular sugar blended with corn starch, it was fine but a little more grainy. these are incredibly sweet and taste like those Gin Gin candies. Great to share
Make these cookies. Don't wait. They are the best ginger cookies I have ever eaten.

Made these for the first time to be my gingerbread component for cookie boxes, and there is a definite learning curve with the butter- mine spent way longer on the counter, then fridge, than freezer, and I still probably started working with it too soon judging by the fried looking cookies I pulled out of the oven. I’m leaving these out of the box, and am freezing the rest to make and add to ice cream another time.

I followed another baker’s suggestion and made 17 g cookies. Fit 12 on a cookie sheet. Still baked for 10 min. Recipe made 48 cookies. Perfect size. I also pressed the top of the cookies into some candied ginger granules I had. Very good and so gingery!
Very good cookie. My oven required more like 14 minutes or they would be underdone in the center.
Excellent recipe. The flavors and texture are spot on. I followed the recipe exactly. Thanks for a great cookie!
Made these Gluten free and omitted the pepper for my 10 year old. OMG so good!!
When grating your frozen ginger, wear your thinner gardening gloves.

Based off of comments I cooked longer than the directions - mistake! Take out when they still feel VERY soft if you want a chewy cookie.  I also scaled back the black pepper and was glad I did, but I think it's actually the amount of ginger in sugar the coating that really gives a kick!
Departing from the other glowing reviews, these are disappointing. They spread to thin greasy crisps and the ginger flavor is quite muddled. Skip this recipe and go with something else.

I found the ginger too spicy, although I do love ginger. It gave the cookie a bit of a bitter aftertaste for me. Next time I would tone that down. I liked the texture so would keep the rest. I also don’t get the obsession with browning the butter, it just adds more work and washing. I don’t notice the flavor so don’t see the point.

I made these last year and they were perfect but then i made them again and they ended up being too cakey/puffy and not flat or chewy… does anyone have an idea what I may have done wrong? They still taste good tho :)

This is my new go-to gingerbread cookie recipe.  I love how spicy it is, and I love how it uses fresh ginger.  The cookies were crisp at the edges and chewy in the center -- perfection.  And they kept well -- I'm on my third day of enjoying them and they are still as good as on the first day.
try 12 tbsp butter, 40 g ginger, full amt pepper, 70 g molasses, 300 g flour
These cookies are very tasty and a crowd pleaser. They turn rock hard the day after baking them. I’ll be searching for another similar recipe in the future. One that we can enjoy for several days.

These are good, but they spread a lot maybe i didn't let the butter chill long enough maybe the recipe could use more flower. Given the incredible depth of sent of dough of these cookies I am a bit disappointed in the flavour. 

Thank you NY times for putting recipes also in grams!
So so so good
Amazing ginger cookies.  Perfectly chewy and crisp all at the same time.  Everyone raved about them

I made these cookies last year and we're absolutely my favorite! This year I have tried twice and the batter ends up being rock hard which makes it so I can't even bake them. I thought it was the step of cooling the butter and not having it cool enough, but the second time around I was more patient. Any ideas?
Not sure where I went astray, but these didn’t crackle at all for me! Delicious sharp ginger flavor though. They didn’t turn out pretty, but tasted delicious.

Wish i'd seen the tip to use Trader Joe's frozen grated ginger, but what's a little ginger hair between friends? I made these bigger, because I don't have an 1/8 cup measuring cup, and I also don't have a cookie scoop. Eyeballed it, overshot, but just cooked them longer and made adjustments for space based on the comments here. Mine did not run together. They smell divine, and they taste fabulous.

Not blackstrap molasses regular unsulphured molasses like grandmas

Add a touch more sugar like 1/4 c 
Reduce salt if using Morton’s

These were amazing! The cookies were strong in flavor, perfectly soft in the middle, and crispy near the edges. I had a little leftover dough and ended up making about thirty-five cookies. This was most likely because I made the dough balls small; about the sizes of grapes. This was good for the spread, though, since most of them didn’t stick together. Thank you for this recipe, I’ll now bake the cookies often!

I found these to be way too spicy (and I love fresh ginger). Perhaps I had a particularly spicy root, but I used less fresh ginger than the recipe called for and still found these unbalanced. Maybe I accidentally used blackstrap molasses? The jar didn’t specify. Not really sure what was off as there are a lot of positive reviews for this recipe, but I personally found these cookies disappointing and will likely not make them again as the recipe is written. I did like the texture, though.
These are PERFECT. I will be making them every Christmas.

The cookies tasted amazing but they didn't look amazing like in the picture.  But since taste is the most important thing I would make them again.  They really are best eaten on the same day.  I had a couple left over the next day and they were hard as rocks but I saved them to make a crumble on something else.  Also it seemed to take hours to grate 3 tbsps. of fresh ginger so when I got tired and still didn't have nearly enough I substituted more dried.
These spread out so much. Absolutely no crisp to the edges. May toss.
Maybe the best cookie I’ve ever made. Great, great flavor and texture, intensely gingery but also balanced, so good.
Use 3/4 sugar, extra grated ginger (1/2tbsp)
Next time I will substitute Turbinado sugar (raw sugar) for the granulated sugar
Very spicy!

Substituted 1 cup coconut sugar for the confectioners sugar in batter. Amply sweet. Used only 1 YBsp per cookie, and they were big enough. Two tablespoons per cookie would be gigantic. Cooked for 9 minutes. Delicious!
Okay huge slay… I used about 30 grams of pickled ginger cause i only had 20 grams of regular and they’re deluxe. I also added white chocolate chips <3

I've made this recipe twice and immediately thought if the batter is any sign, this willbe scrumptious!  And I was right. EXCEPT, there was nothing I could do to get crinkles! The cookies are smooth and flat. Both times. It tried banging the pans at 5 mins and at the end. I tried sprinkling water from fingertips before popping the cookies in the oven.  Do you think that a gas rather than electric oven could be the problem. I can't get the crinkles no matter what recipe I've used.
The most incredible gingerbread cookies ! This recipe is perfect. Will be making every year.

Delicious cookie! I used half stevia and half sugar. We baked them as is, in little mounds. We also took 30-40 grams and used a gingerbread cookie cutter for Christmas cookies. Found that upping the amount closer to 40 grams works well if you are using a cookie cutter.
Cut the sugar down to 200g and let the dough rest overnight. Otherwise this recipe as written worked out perfectly to make some very tasty cookies!
I followed the recipe, but wonder... Maybe half black pepper and half cayenne. Will try if I bother to make again.
so good and flavorful!
Ditto on time. 10 min came out raw in the middle. Extended to 12 min for just right bite.
These cookies are delicious!!  To kick it up a notch, I added some crystallized ginger to the top mid-way through baking.
I think i like cooks one better.  These are a lot of work and get hotter each day after baking

10/10 *but* Made as written, mine also spread more than expected so they looked like a crinkly pan banging chocolate chip rather than the picture, which is the shape and texture I associate with the *perfect* chewy ginger cookie. Next time I'll try adding a bit more flour to see if this helps me reach the mountain top.

Wow these were delicious lovely combination of spicy, softness.
Followed exactly as written. Wouldn’t want to change a thing.

These cookies are truly amazing. Mine turned out pretty good except for losing some browned butter because of the overflow that others warned about when adding ginger (didn’t see it until after the fact!). Mine did turn out a bit spicy however…perhaps ‘cuz I lost some butter but otherwise they paired perfectly with coffee!

Really finicky cook time. At the recommended time these were underdone. One minute over they became hard as a rock when cooled. Good flavor but textually greasy and hard and the same time. Not worth the fuss.

As others have noted, adding the ginger/spices to hot brown butter can cause it to bubble over (wasting your pricey ingredients and your hard work grating / grinding). I also got a hot butter burn on my hand while making these. 

They're still worth it. Amazingly delicious!

NYT, please update that step to help people avoid this risk.

Sooooooo good! While I usually wouldn’t mess with a Sohla recipe, no one in my family likes molasses, so I replaced it with Lyle’s Golden Syrup and I dialed back on the fresh ginger a bit and added some cardamom. Mine came out a lot thinner than the picture and they spread a ton, but they are still a spicy chewy dream. I want to play around more with this technique because the texture (crackly yielding to a fudgy chew) is so good.
I followed the recipe exactly as written.  I weighed everything on my scale and chilled the dough.  My cookies spread together and were a mess.  Anyone have any thoughts as to why this happened?

no, but the same happened to me. My butter never really browned though I simmered it for nearly 15 minutes. Maybe temp was too low? Also, the ginger never foamed the butter up like everyone complained.
I found I had to watch the cooking super close to not over bake.  Love them!
Used half the pepper, cooked 11. In, smaller balls. Used about 3-4 th ginger which still wasn’t 50g
The fresh ginger is amazing in these – so spicy, perfect level of ginger.

Although the flavor is delicious, find another cookie to make for your ginger fix. After following all directions precisely and noting other’s comments, my cookies were a runny mess and not suitable. What a disappointment
11 minutes from the fridge

Oh, these are so lovely! They'll be my go-to gingerbread cookies from this day forward. Started grating the ginger, realised the effort involved, and then decided to just juice it and mix the pulp back in. Worked perfectly. Thank you for inventing these gorgeous cookies!
These are delicious! Moist and chewy and they definitely have a bit of a kick.

Hard to beat these cookies for explosive flavor and chewy texture. Outstanding. As others have said, recommend to transfer the browned butter off the stove into a large mixing bowl - either of a stand mixer or for hand mixing. That butter foams like the dickens once it hits the hot butter so you need some space to quickly whip down the foam in the bowl.
Once the fresh ground ginger hits the hot butter it foams considerably so recommend transferring to a large mixing bowl of a stand mixer or if mixing by hand.
Good! Theres certainly a kick, so best for an older audience. The cookies didnt turn out quite like the picture.

This is the second batch of these I've made - part of a christmas present for my ginger-loving mom.  Unfortunately, I realized too late that I was about 1/2c short of confectioner's sugar, but I subbed in 1/2 c of light brown sugar and they still came out absolutely delicious!
:-( I don't know what happened. My cookies came out flat and crispy, not chewy.i followed the recipe exactly.

OMG the ultimate ginger cookies. I made them for Christmas this year / my family loves ginger. Next year I'll try the frozen ginger method and make them a more bite-size. GREAT recipe. BTW, I used 3/4 teaspoon of sea salt b/c I ran out of kosher salt but I also used salted butter. Thank you whoever's creation this is!
Delicious. Make sure to brown the butter and use all the spices posted, they all really balance each other.
Make this- so spicy and good. Perfect cookies for those who love something with a kick and not too sweet. Amazing!!
Made a double batch and froze half. Cook just fine in the toaster oven!
Flavor of these cookies was amazing! We had a little trouble with the texture, couldn’t quite get the chewy wrinkly texture right. Any suggestions?

I tried everything (well, almost everything) from pan banging to shifting spots in the oven. Baking longer, baking shorter. And my cookies were never as pictured. Over-baking produces a cookie with a bitter aftertaste.

These are by far the best ginger cookies I have ever eaten!!  For the rolling sugar, I used candied ginger and ran it through the food processor to get it smaller.  Then I added a small amount of coconut sugar to dry it out and get a more granular texture - they were delicious (by all accounts)!
Only had 1 heaping tablespoon of fresh ginger and still came out amazing. Agree with other reviews that the butter will bubble up A LOT so definitely use a large pot or bowl. Will be making again.
Phenomenal! They spread way more than I thought they would and 1 inch spacing wasn't enough but it was easily remedied. Nice spicy bite to it and the perfect balance of chewy and crispy.

I experienced brown butter spill over when adding ginger and spices. Will certainly making these again because they were delicious and chewy with a lot of great gingerbread flavor. Also will space more than 1" apart to allow spread when baking.
Further apart than suggested - maybe 10 per blue baking sheet.

What an interesting recipe! My first batch was too much for me - too greasy, too gingery, too pepper-y. However, I see the potential! I’m going to try again and reduce some things and increase the flour to butter ratio. They’re definitely not boring!!

Super easily adapted to gluten free. Swapped flour for Pamela’s 1:1 with 1/4tsp xanthan gum and they came out PERFECT. Also threw in some chopped non-crystallized candied ginger chopped up super fine, which was killer.

These are overwhelmingly gingery, in the best way. Definitely the punchiest gingerbread cookie I ever tried. Mine came out quite cakey but with a delicate crisp from the gingered sugar coating. I used jarred grated ginger from my local Korean alphabet mart, which has a touch of salt in it. I’d already frozen it in ice cube trays and stored it in cubes in my freezer, so the grated ginger part was a snap. (Pause for tsk-ing from the purists.) Try cream cheese (or CC icing) with them.

Fabulous!
I did make slightly smaller (20g) cookies and place farther apart on cookie sheet. Grated 40 g of ginger until I ran out. Had to eat them hot off the cookie sheet cuz they smelled so good.
Is treacle an appropriate substitute for molasses for this recipe?

They were good, 50 grams of fresh ginger is A LOT of ginger. I got three large knobs (a little less than .2 lbs) and after peeling it, it ended up not being enough. I ended up substituted the rest of the weight with some frozen ginger. My cookies didn't end up getting that beautiful crackle like in the photo which was disappointing. 

If I made them again, I would leave out the black pepper. These were too spicy for my Chinese in-laws during Christmas lol.
I’d recommend for a spicier cookie tripling the amount of cinnamon and adding some allspice but it’s an overall good cookie

These turned out pretty good but i think i prefer a less oily/buttery/gooey cookie in favor of more texture and caki-ness 
The flavor is pretty intense, which i loved. These would be great as one choice on a cookie platter rather than a whole batch
Finally, a ginger cookie ginger-y enough for me! Excellent textures: chewy inside with a solid texture around the perimeter.

These are great cookies. The recipe tells us to freeze the balled cookie dough, then bake and serve as needed - meaning they are great for experimenting.
I made a double batch then studid the baking process: oven temperature, dark vs. light colored cookie sheets, silicone pads, convection versus non-convection and, of course, cooking time. For me: 375° with a silicone pad for 10 minutes was optimal. Readers should experiment. By the way, a teaspoon of orange zest in the dough is delicious here.

I read the notes first then made these for our after church service coffee chat.  I made the balls 15g (instead of 30g) and they came out as 50 beautiful 2.5in-diameter chewy-ish cookies with a little crunch.  I could easily put 12 balls on a standard baking sheet.  I had only half the fresh ginger on hand, but they still have the ginger burst of flavor we all love.

This recipe is labor intensive but so worth it. I thought I could grind up crystallize ginger and sugar to roll the cookies in before baking. It caused the cookies to spread as the ginger melted. The cookies really spread out. I ended up
Using them for ice cream sandwiches with vanilla ice cream. They were good but not as good as using plain ground ginger and 
Sugar. .

Flavor packed! But mine looked nothing like the picture! Much lighter and didn’t fall like a typical cookie - just puffed up. That said, flavor was amazing. I didn’t know if they would be worth the effort, but flavor was definitely there. I added 1/3 c chopped, crystallized ginger because it’s my fave. Can’t imagine them without it. But I think I’ll tweak spices of my Williams Sonoma recipe to better match this, and keep with the simpler method.

Reduced the black pepper by half, did not need to. Excellent cookie. Pan banging good addition halfway through. Cookies will be too soft to directly transfer to rack. I pulled the entire sheet of parchment on to rack at first.
these were incredible! A hit with my whole family at Christmas last year. I intend to make them again

I have no idea what I did wrong but these were a total fail for me. I followed the recipe to a T and used irish butter, which never really turned 'brown'. Maybe that was it? Kept in the fridge overnight and baked the next day. They spread out and took closer to 20 min at 375 rather than 10. Still chewy and flavor is good (spicy!) but they look nothing like the picture!

Make your house smell amazing!!! These taste great! I followed the recipe and the only problem I had was that the cookies spread on the pan, so put less cookies on the tray next time. Use more than 2 trays. DELICIOUS!

Great flavor but the texture/appearance turned out all wrong, despite following the instructions to a T. The cookies spread flat and didn’t have any crinkle to them. Would not repeat this recipe, especially given that they’re more laborious than comparable.

Suggest at least 2 inch spacing.  At 1 inch apart or you'll end up with each of them fusing together. 
That's what just happened to me.

Really delicious! Used Penzeys minced ginger since I didn't have fresh, and did just a few grinds of black pepper and added cardamom. They are very buttery and I would be inclined to cut back on the amount of butter by a couple tablespoons and see if that helps? I plan to mix up enough dough to keep in the freezer all winter long for easy access to these cozy cookies.

I committed a mortal sin and used ginger paste from a tube instead of fresh. These cookies *still* turned out incredible and I can’t imagine a world in which I have the time and the will to change this method in the future. Besides that, I followed the recipe exactly. These came out sweet and spicy and are so wonderful for winter.
These are incredible. Spicy, chewy, and delicious. I was very skeptical of the coloring until they cooled. Absolutely divine.

Followed the recipe as is and they turned out great! As others suggested, once the butter is browned, put that straight into a larger bowl or mixer bowl and then add the grated ginger and spices to avoid bubbling over. Will be making these again!
Spicy…. 50 g is a lot of ginger

Pulse slices of frozen fresh ginger in blender to make a fine powder. No microplane needed.  Super easy!  Golden milk powder instead of ginger powder gave a fantastic earthy flavor.  Loved these cookies and will definitely bake again.
My holiday party guests could not get enough of these cookies. I had multiple people ask me for the recipe and then follow up later to get it!
These are delicious. Definitely be careful of spacing as mine bled into each other.
I used 50 grams of ginger paste from a tube for convenience, the cookie turned out great. This is such a nice holiday cookie.
Seriously be careful about the salt if not using diamond crystal.. these are delicious but im kicking myself for accidentally making them way too salty!!

Salted butter
2 c (or slightly less) swerve confectioners zero calorie sweetener
1 tsp baking soda plus 1/2 tsp baking powder
No salt
2 cup almond flour
Measuring out 30 grams of dough per cookie i was able to make 27 cookies. Will update once done baking as to how they came out.
These came out great
This is our favorite cookie!  It is outstanding.

These cookies are delicious! I followed advice from others, thank you, and cut the size to be baked in half to 15 gm per cookie. I overbaked as the cookie edges did not look browned - in for 10 minutes. Still delicious but more crispy then I wanted. I also left the dough in a very cold fridge for two days and had no difficulty with sticky dough when rolling the cookies. Baked some today and froze the rest for next week. A keeper!
Oh mah gawd!  Bake them exactly as the recipe says.  I don't know how they could be any better.
Followed the recipe exactly with the exception of substituting finely diced candied ginger for the fresh.
These are insanely good! My notes: Used 2tbsp less butter (so 12 total), and baked for ~2-3 min longer.

Smells and tastes like Christmas! Skipped the ginger in the rolling sugar and only used a few cranks of black pepper. Still plenty of ginger and spice kick. My cookies were perfectly set at 11mins. Def give the pan a bang on the rack before you take them out for a nice ripple.
It was taking me awhile to microplane the ginger so I got out my $20 coffee grinder that I used for herbs. It worked much better and was less time consuming.

I did the same thing!  I let my frozen ginger defrost and it was impossible to microplane.  The mini grinder worked quickly, although I did use a knife to chop extra fine to be sure there were no big bits.  Delicious cookies!
Third year in a row that I have made them, and third year in a row that they are the resounding favorite in the holiday cookie box! Grating fresh ginger is worth the effort.

For me, the output didn’t meet the effort.

Did the fresh ginger or browned butter make it much better than the average gingerbread cookie? Meh. Not to my bourgeois palette. Even using a big pan there was massive spillage when I added the fresh ginger to the butter. It was a fun experiment for me, but other recipes would’ve yielded similar results with much less effort.

The cookies are good, but I won’t make them again. p.s. I love The Times’ chefs and learn something new from each of your recipes - thank you!
Don’t put on lower third of oven. Will burn.

These are my new favorite cookies. I was a little worried when I tried the dough, because the dough before baking does not taste great, almost added more sugar. But I trusted the process and they are Delectable. Must makes if you like ginger cookies.
These are the best cookies I have ever made - chewy, slightly spicy, ginger-molasses flavor bombs. Everyone loved them.

Made these as instructed and they turned out perfectly and were a favorite among all who ate them! While these cookies are for sure best on day one (as all cookies are), ours held up really well for  about five days.
These are delicious!  I was worried about burning the butter so I did not cook the butter solids until they were deeply brown.  I will definitely be making these again!!

These ginger cookies were the hit of the holiday! I was concerned my son would be turned off by the strong flavors, especially the pepper, but he wasn’t. Ginger are his favorite and these were a clear winner. If I could change anything, I would hold a touch of the pepper.

Rechill dough after rolling in balls. Or, roll balls directly after making the dough and chill in ball form. Bake the cookies in 2 separate batches. The first time I baked them together and got 1 pan of glorious cookies and 1 pan of weird, spread out ones (even after rotating pans at 5-minute mark). Also, make sure the brown butter is no longer liquid before starting the dough-making process. Think the still-melted butter made the cookies greasy.

I am an avid baker and this is by far my favorite cookie of all time. Do not change a thing! I use the fresh grated ginger in a tube in the herb section of grocery store. Be sure to make enough to freeze into balls for your nightly cookie fix. I keep the ginger sugar in a Tupperware container and roll the frozen balls before I bake.
Amazing ginger sweet taste and zingy bite. Wish I had this recipe years ago! Recipe works great as is, especially the tips. I’ve frozen 10 balls for a future “ fix”.
Just absolutely divine. Follow instructions and they may just be the best cookies you have ever had. I took them out oven at exactly 10 mins and it perfect timing.

This is magic: "Cookie dough portions can be frozen, then coated in sugar and baked from frozen as needed. No need to thaw; bake frozen portions for 11 to 13 minutes."

I froze some, they got buried in the freezer, I found them 9 months later, baked them off in the toaster oven wondering if they'd still be any good and OH MY GOD THE BEST COOKIES EVER. I'm about to make another batch for the freezer and this time I will not let them get lost.
can you shape these into gingerbread men?
@Michelle no. they will spread and not hold shape.

Can someone share with me what might have happened to my cookies?  They are more cakey than to my liking, more even through the texture rather than crisp on the edges and chewy/gooey inside--definitely gingerBREAD but not dry.My variations/failures in doing the recipe:  I used blackstrap molasses and measured it rather than weighing it; all else by grams and as called for. I may have under-mixed slightly.   I microwaved for 5 seconds the butter/spice because I thought it was too chilled.  So..?

@Marguerite Hi.. do not use blackstrap molasses, use unsulphered molasses as instructed in recipe. Also, I wouldn’t put the re-solidified butter in the microwave. It will soften up once out of the fridge and mixed with the molasses. Good luck!! They are sooo good!

These are fabulous.  Instead of sugar and ground ginger for the coating, I ran sugar and a few chunks of candied ginger through my mini food processor.  I think that was a good call, though I'm sure they're equally fabulous exactly as written.
remarkable...added 1 cup of pecans

Love this recipe. I add espresso powder and cayenne pepper to mine. 
People call them the "Spicy christmas cookies" and ask me to make them every year.
Can I substitute sorghum for the molasses?

The comments under recipes makes the subscription to NYT worth it! Loved how the cookies came out, but I have to admit I made a much smaller ball and was able to get about 50 cookies to the recipe. Each cookie weigh about 
12 grams vs 22g and was a perfect size reducing the guilt having 1-2 or even 3.  I left out the pepper by accident and they were still TOP DOG!
Can I make these cookies now and freeze them until Christmas?
I made these by weight and they had way too much butter. Excellent flavour though. Would reduce the butter by 50g if I tried it again.

I made the recipe as written, no substitutions.  They were amazing.  The only thing I’d note is to make sure the cookie dough balls are spaced outside sufficiently when you place them on the baking sheet.  My cookies kind of melded together as they cooked.  These were so good though.  Highly recommend!
Didn't wait the 2 hours for them to chill and they still turned out fantastic!

These are SO good! The perfect amount of sweetness and ginger kick, holiday spices, etc. Balance of chewy and crunchy, exactly what I was craving. I didn’t make the sugar coating at the end and just topped with flaky salt. No notes.

These cookies are insanely delicious.  I didn't have enough ground ginger so I substituted 1 teaspoon with allspice. I used plain sugar to roll them in. They needed to be spaced a little farther apart and a little more time cooking in my oven.

I made this recipe with weight measures. As others noted, the butter foams a lot when you add the ginger and spices, so be careful and use a large pot. I also use an oven thermometer, so I have an accurate oven temp read. Ten minutes at 375 degrees resulted in underbaked cookies for me. Twelve minutes was much better and looked like the picture. It’s a good, spicy cookie overall. I’m usually need to double the salt in recipes, but I actually found these a touch too salty, so I would maybe go with 3/4tsp to 1tsp.

Made these cookies and they may be the best ginger cookie I have eaten. Having a cookie scoop is key here since the dough becomes really stiff in the fridge. You can just scoop and put on the cookie sheet with a flat bottom. Make sure not to over bake, I had one batch that went 2 mins over and they were a little too crunchy. I want to add a little candied ginger next time I make them.
I make these every year, they are the best gingerbread I’ve ever had
I made these exactly as the recipe and these are spicey but so yummy!
I baked these exactly as instructed but they are cakey rather than chewy. Does anyone out there know why this happened?

I was able to make these without the stand mixer, and while they're not crackly on top, they are fudgy in the middle and crisp on the edges, which was everything I hoped for. And the flavor is out of this world!

Way too much work for the final product. 30 grams of cookie dough seemed like a lot, so I cut it to 25. Even so, they spread and connected. I cut back to 20, then 17. Some were soft in the center, some crispy. They still spread. I did chill the dough in the fridge for 24 hours. Not sure what happened.
just incredible!
These came out exactly as advertised--soft, chewy with an intense flavor. Not too sweet.  If you were raised on hard-as-rocks Ginger Snaps, this is a tasty surprise.
Way too much ginger, almost inedible.
Oh. My. Goodness. These cookies are amazing. If you love ginger snaps, these are a wonderful chewy version.

My cookies came out like hard lumps and didn’t spread at all. I followed recipe exactly. I cooled the butter mixture overnight and then brought to room temp before using. The dough was quite still and crumbly. Cooled the dough in fridge a few hours and then cooked when cold. Don’t know what I did wrong.
@Victoria I’m no expert, but you say your dough was dry and crumbly - our dough before chilling was soft and pliable, so it sounds like something in the measurements was off?  Too much flour?
Don't you need to peel the ginger before grating it? Appreciate the help here.
@Mame no, peeling ginger is generally not required
It seemed odd to me also, but I grated it unpeeled and it worked well.
freeze dried ginger works great, i bought it on MilkStreet.com@Butter Goddess

Delicious and easy in a stand mixer, despite the steps and waiting. Used half whole wheat flour. 2nd time I made them, used the powdered sugar measure as written, would decrease 1/4c less. Will also try 12 tbs butter instead of 14 next time.
These are the ginger cookies I make

Amazing recipe, truly! I didn’t realize that there’s a call for fresh ginger so omitted it, but they were still perfect. Next time, I’m going to chop up crystallized ginger and add that in for an extra kick (and probably still omit the fresh ginger).

Also, I scooped and rolled them in the sugar mix, then froze them for 30 mins, then baked them (same day) and they were perfect.
Hands down, the best Gingerbread cookie you will ever eat!  Next time will have to double the recipe - I couldn’t eat just one!
I make these before the holidays and freeze them in balls and bake them whenever we have guests. They make the house smell great. And they are PERFECT!
I love recipes that include the scoop size for allocating perfectly even sized treats. Thank you for focusing on the details!

These are amazing and potentially addictive. So flavorful and chewy. My first time making gingerbread cookies and I won't try another recipe. The fresh grated ginger and black pepper is the key. If you don't like the spice of fresh ginger/black pepper, these may not be for you. But we loved them.

Thanks for all of the helpful comments! I froze the ginger root and microplaned - worked fabulously. I also took the advice regarding the ginger foaming and added the butter to my mixer bowl and then added the ginger. My tongue does not like black pepper at all, so I added just a shake or two. The cookies still had a kick, were super delicious and received good comments.

incredible cookies. this recipe is a keeper. i chilled my dough overnight. i also used sorghum syrup in place of molasses because it is what i had in my pantry. other than that i followed the recipe ingredients and process. 
the blend of spices is delicious. looking forward to trying these with hot chocolate or with atol

based on other reviews, i used a stock pot to brown the butter. that was big enough to accommodate for the foaming that happens when the spices are added.

simply the best (ginger, and possibly any) cookies around. Followed exactly as the recipe stated. 

Instead of using a scoop, I rolled my dough into a log, chilled, then cut into even slices before rolling into by hand which yielded PERFECTLY round cookies all the same size. Also, second the comment about freezing ginger before grating on the microplane - easy peasy. Can't wait to make these again!

These cookies are delicious!  I folded in about 1/2 cup of King Arthur's mini diced ginger pieces that I had left over from Christmas, and swapped turbinado sugar for regular in the ginger/sugar mix.  They were perfect after 12 minutes in my oven at 375.  The black pepper adds a perfect kick and there's no such thing as too ginger-y in my house.  Love it and will absolutely make again.  p.s. my kitchen smells amazing while they're baking!

Soft chewy GINGERBREAD cookie!
We love ginger but the amount used seemed a bit more than I would use for a small batch of cookies. That being said, 
I did reduce the fresh ginger to 1T & added an extra 1/2 tsp of ground ginger to the dough mix.
Next time, I will only use 1/2 tsp of ginger to the 1/4c sugar, to roll the dough balls in,  as it seemed a bit bitter per friends/family.
Baked for 14-16 minutes to achieve dough “set”. They were still soft and chewy and delicious!

These are fantastic. But I disagree that they are best day of baking. They are good for a solid week after baking. Perhaps even better. The flavors come together more and the texture holds its crisp/chewy deliciousness!
3Tbs molasses is only 45g…which do I use?

I've made lots of ginger cookies over the years and these are by far the best ones. The extra work to brown the butter is totally worth it. As others have said, use a bigger pot as the butter foams up big time once you add the fresh ginger. Your house will smell incredible afterwards. The only adjustment I would make is to the sugar-ginger coating at the end. I ended up adding doubling the sugar as I found the ginger-sugar ratio in the recipe wasn't coating the cookies fully in sugar.

Oh My these were over the top fantastic. So much flavor - between the browned butter, the spices and rolling the dough in sugar and ginger…  mmmm, mmmm good. I did add candied ginger that I chopped super fine in a coffee grinder because I had some and figured, Why not?  Will make again and again.
50 grams of ginger is more than 3 tbs. I went with the weight, and the cookies were too gingery. Otherwise, the cookies were delicious. I will make them again, but go down to 40 grams of ginger.

Really good recipe. I’ll make again. Tastes exactly like the ginger snaps I remember as a kid. 
Love the chewiness. 
I needed to bake about 11 min w my oven. Next time I’ll only put in one tray at a time.

Freeze dried fruit is a dried fruit, no sugar added, and has a lighter, crunchy, completely dry texture…not chewy and moist like raisins or dried apricots. Not always easy to find in grocery stores but I’ve had luck at Trader Joe’s for a good price. Great to flavor frostings without adding moisture or sugar!
These are delicious. But I don't know how the yield is 60-70 cookies.  The logs and slices would have to be TINY! I got around 20, though I did make a larger log.

When I saw the picture of this it made my mouth actually water. Can't wait. This will taste good after a wrap with fried chicken, sriracha , lettuce, and the lemon chutney from Indian instant pot cookbook by Pitre. Love NYT Cooking, it's a great place to take a break from you know what.

Absolutely delicious! I used freeze dried raspberries (can be found at Trader Joe’s) and these turned out so well. I am going to make these instead of our usual snowball/bullet cookies for our cookie boxes this year. They have the same texture but with a fruity/tart twist that is a nice spin on a traditional cookie.

These were amazing!  Such a nice light crumb and the fruit flavor is strong, but not overwhelming.  I blended freeze dried strawberries, blueberries and raspberries (heaviest on the raspberry) and they are to die for.  So cute and tiny and WAY too easy to pop in your mouth!  This one's a keeper.  I froze one log for closer to Christmas and got exactly 35 out of one log, so the yield was spot on for me.
I used freeze dried raspberries which were great, but after blending them into a powder I should have put the powder through a sifter to get the seeds out. Still good, but probably better sans seeds.

I used raspberry powder on hand from Kitchen Alchemy that I used for macarons. It was lovely here.  Let the cookies cool slightly before tossing in the sugar or it will melt into a delicious, sticky mess.

Tasty recipe. Yield is correct when making the size described. We used multiple fruits for experimentation. Cranberry, Pineapple, Strawberry, Raspberry, Apple, Coconut and Blueberry. Mixed and matched. Milder flavors were blueberry, pineapple, coconut, and apple. Cranberry was best mixed with apple. If milder flavors are used exclusively, the cookie is not flavor forward, but more of a later flavor. Not recommended alone. Pair mild flavors with bright flavors! Great with GF flour.
Is freeze-dried the same as dehydrated?
Yes.  I’ve gotten them at Trader Joe’s, they have several different fruits. I made these today with raspberries, and they were delicious. Definitely not the usual Christmas cookie!
Target is also a great source as well - lots of unique fruit flavors.
No. Freeze-dried has a completely different texture, sort of like crumbly styrofoam. It would be pretty much impossible to grind regular dried fruit into a fine powder.

I made this recipe exactly as specified (using freeze-dried strawberries) and got 72 gorgeous and delicious little cookies as a reward for my efforts! It's an easy recipe with two exceptions: grinding up the fruit (my blender worked way better than my food processor or mortar/pestle); and "tossing" the cookies (I just used my fingers to "dip" each cookie's top and bottom into the powdered-sugar/powdered-fruit mixture). I'm definitely going to be making this one again, maybe with blueberries!
Which fruit was used in the pictured cookies?

Freeze-dried fruit is available on Amazon in many flavors.

Pulverizing the dried fruit (and sifting out any seeds) yields a beautiful, flavorful powder that is sensational in cookies, frosting, cakes, or even just sprinkled on toast!
I've made these with raspberry several times and they are amazing, but I tried with mango today and I wouldn't recommend it. Far too subtle for me.

These were alright. I made them with freeze dried blueberries. They were definitely a cool way to use freeze dried fruit and kind of an interesting cookie to put in a mixed box, but not mind blowing. Maybe try something a bit punchier than blueberry if you're going to make these. Sohla has way better recipes than this one!

I made the blueberry ones too. I was apprehensive after reading your comment, so I decided to increase the processed freeze dried fruit by 5 grams. They ended up being gobbled up by people who don't like very sweet things!

Tangy, sweet, buttery, brightly colored treats! I'm a complete novice when it comes to baking and I found the dough very easy to work with. I went with freeze dried strawberry and would definitely recommend.
Absolutely loved this recipe!  Used Trader Joe’s dehydrated mixed berries.  Leftover powdered sugar berry mixture is lovely sprinkled on buttered toast!

These were really excellent. We made mango and blueberry and we’ll definitely make them again. So simple. 

I do wish the instructions had said to wait about 5 minutes after taking them out of the oven before tossing with confectioners/fruit and also specified a vague diameter for the log. But I do love this recipe and we learned quickly enough how to do it properly. 

Highly recommend.

Our family loves these. Just made a second batch. Someone may have already provided a method using a food processor, however, I really enjoyed a “one-bowl” procedure: 1) pulverizing  at least 40 of the dried fruit; 2) mixing 20 fruit powder with confectioner’s sugar for the sugar/fruit toss after the bake, cleaning bowl and reserving mix; 3) proceed with the cookie dough recipe.
beware of the extra sugar at end ... i would sift over cookies lightly - cookies are already sweet enough w/o being rolled in more sugar.  also easier..

I made these for Christmas, in addition to the (humble brag here) fourteen other kinds I always make. They were the star of the cookie platter! Simple dough, easy to slice and bake. I did save some raspberry/powdered sugar mixture to give them another coating before plating.  Winner of a recipe!
Question:  Does "freeze-dried fruit" mean frozen fruit from the freezer section of the supermarket, or dried fruit, such as cranberries, dates, mango slices?
Freeze dried fruit is neither frozen fruit or typical dried fruit like dates or raisins - it's the type of dried fruit that is extremely light in weight (no moisture at all) and melts in your mouth.

I just made these with FD raspberries, because I had a bag that needed using up. The dough was initially VERY soft, but firmed up well. They taste good, but I would like a more buttery or richer flavour. Relatively easy and fast to make, and adds a zippy surprise in the Xmas cookie tin. I might experiment by adding melted, cooled white chocolate into dough next time, obviously will need to adjust other ingredients…
Insanely good. What a nice break from too-sweet! I used raspberries. Going on our every-year list for sure.
Somes times you can get freeze dried fruit powders from King Arthur Baking

These were such a fun, colorful, fruity addition to my holiday cookie baking. I made them with vegan butter and they worked perfectly. I split the batch and did strawberry and blueberry, with 60+ little two-bite cookies total. I'll be making them again for sure.

Made these with freeze dried strawberries from TJ. Ground in a spice grinder (grind rice beforehand to get rid of spice odors). No need to sift. Got sidetracked and let dough sit in the fridge for a few days before we got around to baking. Maybe helped deepen flavor? Tart strawberries balanced out powdered sugar well and made these addictive. We had to toss multiple times in sugar to get a good coating.

I made this for my cookie boxes this year. A standout for sure. Yes they are small, but lots of flavor. I used the Trader Joe’s Mixed Berry freeze dried fruit. I did use a sieve to get rid of the seeds. Took about 1.5 bags between dough and coating. The dough was quick and easy to make and froze beautifully. My daughter said it’s a new favorite…she loves fruity cookies. Even my husband who doesn’t go for fruity desserts liked them. Not too sweet and full of flavor.

These were fine. They lacked the fruity punch I’d hoped for—- I used strawberries though and maybe that’s the issue. I upped the salt after the first bunch and added an additional Tbsp of lemon juice to try for something more tart but they were still a bit lack luster. Fine. Not bad. They just didn’t have that tart, fruity finish.

Try upping the processed freeze-dried fruit. I weighed mine, so I know that I added 23g for the strawberry batter and 25g for the blueberry batter. I'm guessing it made a difference because they both turned out to be successfully tangy and approved by those who don't like cloyingly sweet biscuits.

Made these last week, did raspberry and strawberry, both so delicious. Rave reviews from every one that tried them. Looking forward to trying with other flavors next time I'm in a baking mood. Thanks for a great recipe!

Added 2 more tbs strawberry fruit powder to sugar coating
Sift powered sugar before mixing with fruit powder
I was underwhelmed with these. I followed the recipe, but for the effort it isn't worth the result.

I used raspberry as freeze dried fruit, quite tart tasting, and upped the amount when mixing with the confectionary sugar - the result was still nowhere near tart enough for me - added more ground fruit to the mix AND some lemon juice and dunked the cookies in that. An improvement imo.
I mixed freeze dried raspberries and freeze dried strawberries together and cut down the powdered sugar for dusting. Delicious, dainty, and very pretty.

I made these for the first time during the Holidays this year. Trader Joe's was having a production issue (still is) w/freeze-dried raspberries which I really wanted to use; I used strawberries & blueberries instead. They were a hit! I weighed my ingredients (not vanilla & salt, obviously) and decided to increase the processed berries to 23-25g instead of sticking w/the 20g each for the batter & for the coating. Next time, I'll add complementing citrus zest to the batter. YUM!

My brother-in-law made me promise that I'll make these for his future birthdays. I see no problem with that...as these are fairly easy to put together! Plus, the freeze-dried raspberries should be back in stock at Trader Joe's by then! I am going to try lemon or orange zest with the blueberry batter; lemon zest with the strawberry batter; orange zest or finely minced candied orange peel or candies lime peel with the raspberry batter.

NOTE: use only dehydrated products that have nothing but the fruit in them. Big Lots carries an excellent line. The flavors are tart and the product comes out beautifully powdery. Do NOT purchase Aldi brand. The product is mixed with fillers and doesn’t taste as good, nor work so well.

I loved the tartness and beautiful powdery look of the finished cookie so much that I began using finishing coat on some of my spiced sugar cookies. Made stripes on the candy canes- gorgeous!
Aldi does carry the Simply Nature brand which is only fruit, no fillers.

These are delicious and way too easy to eat by the handful.

I made a split batch with three different freeze dried products: strawberry, mango, and roasted sweet corn (found the corn at Whole Foods). 

The mango was the least impressive, just kind of a generic "fruit" flavor. 

Strawberry was amazing, will definitely be making that version again come summertime.

The surprise hit was the corn, though a bit grey looking, I added some Tajin spice to the dough and sugar coating, tasted like elote!

I made these with raspberries, and only realized after I had ground them up that I should remove the seeds, and so I didn't end up with enough to coat them. Luckily the logs lasted in my fridge well until I could procure more, and everyone loved the cookies. I made sure my logs were 10" and though I didn't count, it made A LOT of cookies. I cooked the first pan a little long and they were a bit dry - next time I'd err closer on the side of underbaking. But can't wait to try more flavors!

made last minute for a cookie swap using Karen's Naturals Just Strawberry. I ground them with my wand blender which was a little messy but no big deal. They were easy and unique so I think they worth trying. Flavor was intensely sweet and tangy. Not my favorite nor the kids' for that matter.

These cookies are gross.  Dried-raspberry flavored butter cookies taste very strange.  And the bright red fruit powder bakes up to odd-looking muddy pink cookies.  Once you dust them with the sugar/fruit powder mixture, the cookies look... interesting.  Bright Barbie pink, which I guess is adorable?  But the dusting mixture tastes weird and dries out your mouth.  It's like eating fluoride-flavored desiccant.
