I made them with freeze-dried raspberries, which I found at Target. I ground them in a coffee blade grinder dedicated to spices (wiping it out before and after the raspberries) and sifted them through a strainer to remove the seeds. The sifting goes faster if you stir with a spoon.

The cookies are tart, sweet, and delicious.
Fabulous recipe. I used the mixed berry pack from TJs, no salt for dietary reasons and the cookies were loved by all. Delicate and delicious.

I made this with freeze dried mango. They were good, but I would double the amount and perhaps add some lime zest. Good solid recipe - would like to try with other fruits that have a more assertive flavor

Delicious!  I made these with strawberry and the cookie dough looked a little like spam. I might add a little food coloring or use a different fruit next time for a more vibrant color.  

For the powdered sugar, I would let them cool for only a minute or two before tossing. And only touch the sides of the cookies once they are in the powdered sugar to avoid getting fingerprints on the surface. 

I'd make these again!  They are easy and look cute and people love them.
Think twice about making these with freeze dried strawberries... They're nice but they really do look like weird salami slices... Kinda hilarious!

I used freeze-dried strawberries ($10 worth!) that I chopped up in the blender and then put through a sieve. I tried so hard to get a fine powder so I could avoid the sausage look that other commenters had, but it didn’t work. I was about 20 grams short with the powdered sugar but had enough for coating. I baked one tray at a time as suggested by others and agree that’s necessary. My cooling rack made marks on the bottom of the cookies so I cooled the second batch by moving the parchment paper to the rack, which also kept the counter clean.  They were so tasty and were a big hit with my family and were different from anyone else’s at my cookie exchange. The strawberry flavor comes through intensely. Made 67.
I’ve had success using a bean grinder for grinding the dried fruit. Used a sieve to remove remaining seeds. Lovely recipe.  Thank you!
Hugely popular in our cookie boxes.  We did Blackcurrant, lychee, plum, and plum-raspberry.  Next year I'll make the logs ahead and freeze them.
Where did you find freeze-dried blackcurrants, lychees, and plums?  They all sound delicious!

these were ok. they tasted like shortbread. maybe I didn't cost them correctly.  I tried rolling then in preferred sugar sis veggie baking,  right after baking,  after they were cooled.. I never got them to look like the picture.  and more importantly  taste like the picture.  they were crisp and fruity, but they didn't melt away.  I think i prefer regular shortbread.

If you like tangy stuff- these are for you. Made in raspberry and boy were they good, could eat a dozen!! 
Love these
I'm very confused. The introduction mentions the importance of cornstarch, but cornstarch doesn't appear in the ingredients list or the recipe. Am I missing something?
It says that classic lime meltaways use cornstarch, but in this recipe, "the cornstarch is swapped for your choice of freeze-dried fruit finely ground in a spice grinder or blender."
The recipe claims to make so many because traditionally this kind of cookie is very small. See the boxes of "lime coolers" in gift shops.
Truly delicious. Made one batch with strawberry, another with mango. Huge hit. Will absolutely make again.

I couldn't get a 'soft dough' to form, just lots and lots of crumbly bits. I did you a hand mixer instead of a paddle one, but thats the only thing I did differently. Worried they won't harden enough to make actual cookies. 

I guess worst case scenario I'll just end up with DELICIOUS ice cream toppings.

Question - the recipe says 1 3/4 cup / 224 g of flour. King Arthur flour says 1 cup = 120 g, which would be 210 not 224. Do we follow the provided weight, or use the weight equivalent based upon King Arthur flour’s direction? (Eg 224 g or 210 g). Thank you!
I would use the weight that is provided here. I'd like to think that the recipe testers weighed the amount of flour they used.
The weight would depend on the brand of flour you're using.  If you have King Arthur flour, use their weight.

It depends on the container you store your flour in and if you fluff the flour and then dip and sweep OR if you spoon it into your cup measure. I weigh my flour, so I always get 140g of flour when the amount specifies 1 cup.
Has anyone used freeze-dried fruit powder instead of freeze-dried fruit?  It seems like that would save a step of having to grind up the fruit.
Can you use raspberry or strawberry powder instead?
You had me at "the cookies will keep for 2 weeks at room temperature".
only if you hide them!
Anyone tried these with GF flour?  Curious if they'll stay together.
Hi! I successfully made these gluten- and dairy-free with no issues. I used my favorite gluten-free flour blend King Arthur's measure for measure and Miyoko's vegan butter.
I get freeze dried fruit from North Bay Trading Company. Lots of different kinds of fruits in many quantities. You can even buy it already ground up.
They become much more red after covered in the final sugar mixture. Don't be disappointed, as I was, if they're a bland pink right when they come out of the oven. The sugar mix will transform them!

Sift out seeds after pulverizing the fruit. Necessary with strawberries? We’ll see.  It’s to let cookies cool a bit before coating makes sense so sugar doesn’t melt. Maybe use a split open cardboard roll to help dough chill with no flat bottom.

Baking these tonight. Put the dough overnight in the fridge and the dusting mix is prepped, but I may add more pulverized freeze-dried strawberries to that for a darker color and more of a punch of fruit. Hope they come out as good as they look in the photo!
I used fresh lime and freeze-dried tart cherries left over from a cake project ... great taste and color!  Next time I am rolling balls instead of cutting a log.

I misread the butter quantity on this one and ended up with a melted mess, but it's turned into nice crumbles I'll use as an ice cream topping. It was disappointing, but based on reviews, I will try again because I want to love this cookie!

We had trouble getting the dough to form logs as the recipe was written. It was too dry and "scratchy," as my bread-baker daughter said. We added 1 tablespoon more of lemon juice and brought the vanilla up to 1 full tablespoon, in accordance with a lime meltaway recipe on Southern cooking site. This moistened the dough enough to shape soft logs, which are now in the freezer.

These looked amazing so I was excited to try them. I used targets freeze dried cherry blueberry and grape blend. My Cuisinart pulverized most of the fruit into powder. I did strain out the seeds. The cookies looked like little cut sausages before going in the oven. They taste very bland there is no color at all in the powdered sugar coating. They are really flavorless. I think the fruit you get must make all the difference.

I made these with raspberries, should have mixed them longer to make them stick together better but they sliced well. Dipped in plain icing sugar; a bit boring. Filled as sandwiches with leftover raspberry buttercream (from macarons) and that is tasty. Will try the second log dipping in the called for icing sugar and fruit mix to see if it livens it up a bit.
One oz of dried fruit pulverized is enough for the dough and also the sugar coating.

I agree that grinding up the dried fruit is the most challenging part.  The first time I used my blender - it was difficult to pick the ground up stuff out of the bottom, and it didn't grind it up evenly so there were still some larger pieces.

The second time, when I needed to make more for the powdered sugar topping, I just put the fruit in a ziplock bag and took a hammer to it - that worked much better.

I didn't think these had enough of the fruit flavor.  I used freeze dried strawberries and found the flavor just to be faintly reminiscent.  I would try these again because of the nice shortbread texture but with more fruit in the cookie itself.
Delicious, but took 15 minutes to bake, and I think might have gone longer

These turned out great!  Pretty as a picture, and easy as can be to prepare, slice and bake.  Make 70, and they were small, but I prefer a smaller cookie anyway, because it means you don't fill up on just one cookie!  Will make them again!

Delicious! I only refrigerated the logs for 25 minutes and it no issues. The cookies look exactly the way the dough looks (no spread or puff-up) so make sure the dough coins looks like what you want. I counted on the dusting of sugar and fruit to cover up some flaws but it did not. I tasted the batter with the 3tbs of fruit powder and decided to double+ it for a fruity punch. Maybe my freeze dried fruit (strawberry) wasn't as strong so adjust accordingly after a dough test.

I chilled only 1,5 hours instead of 2 because they seemed adequate and holding at that point. No clue how one is supposed to get 60-70 cookies out of this though. My cookies came out maybe 2 inches in diameter and I got about 40.

These were wonderful!  I found the freeze dried fruit at Trader Joe’s. I could not understand why the description mentioned classic lime meltaways but the recipe called for lemon juice, so I squeezed a lime instead. The favorite on the holiday cookie tray!
They have lemon meltaways too!

These were a hassle to make (grinding the freeze dried fruit in the spice grinder) and even with an overnight in the freezer they spread out. Don’t know what I did wrong, from what I can see no one else commenting experienced any spread. Still taste pretty good, but didn’t turn out for me and not worth the trouble.
I made these and they are delicious.  Everyone who has tried them, loves them.   I made mine with died cherries and raspberries.  I have dried cranberries and will be making those next.

I’m not much of a baker but I’m kind of proud of  these. Made with freeze dried strawberries and @bobsredmill  1-1 gf flour. I had extra strawberries so I ground them up to a fine powder and, using a tiny clean incense holder, tapped a little extra strawberry powder on each. My GF siblings better love them!!!

Went to TJ's and they had mixed fruit and mangoes for freeze dried fruit so I made a half batch of each.  the mixed fruit came out amazing!  The mango had surprisingly weak mango flavor.  Go for the mixed fruit!  Plus, the color is better.  Since I was making a Half recipe, I went ahead and added the flour b4 the fruit and it was fine although it bit harder to get the fruit mixed in well.

I just made these and they are delicious.  My batch yielded 40 cookies and I baked them for 11 mins. 30 seconds.  They melt in your mouth so not all the cookies are going to make it to Christmas dinner today.

I made these cookies with freeze dried strawberries and found them easy to crush with my fingers in a tea strainer without the bother of using a blender.  The strawberries quickly absorb moisture from the air though which renders them almost impossible to crush, so do it while they are fresh from the bag.
I found they did not pick up much of the powdered sugar/strawberry coating even hot from the oven until I sifted it over them, again using the tea strainer.
Will double the fruit next time!
Made these with freeze dried raspberries. They were delicious
I just threw the confectioners sugar and dehydrated fruit in the food processor. Works great!

Made with dehydrated blueberries from Target's Good & Gather line and thought these were just OK, nothing special. Yield is correct if following recipe's precise dimensions. Not sure if the recipe would be better with a different brand/type of dehydrated fruit.
Gorgeous. !!
Gorgeous !!    The dough freezes like a dream too.....
Could you use fruit powder? It is freeze dried, but has the consistency of flour.
I used fruit powders from nuts dot com and both texture and taste are fabulous.  Will be trying mango and açaí powders next, just to see how they taste.
Has anyone drizzled chocolate on the finished cookies?

These are so amazing.  The recipe was easy to follow, and I love that there are so many future adaptations to experiment with in the future!  This time around I had dragon fruit powder on hand, so I did a light dusting with that on top of the original recipe - looked and tasted really wonderful.  (a note that all sorts of different types of quality fruit powders are at nuts.com)  

I don't have a mixer - no problem.
I also like that they are small bites.  Hard to stop eating them, though!

Wow these are so good! I used freeze dried strawberries from Trader Joe’s. The dough felt kind of dry when I first wrapped it up but after chilling and setting it back out for 20 min, it cut so easily. I think I cut slightly thicker that 1/4” and came away with about 45 cookies.

Not sure what happened—as far as I can tell, I followed the directions carefully, and I’m not a novice baker—but mine spread out so far that they became one big cookie (I spaced as directed). Disappointing and unsightly, but tasty. Others didn’t seem to have this issue, so it might be “user error.”

It is best to bake these one sheet at a time. By the time I finished with the first tray of cookies in the extra sugar, the second tray was too cold for the sugar to really stick. Other than that they’re quite delicious. I made them with the freeze dried raspberries from Trader Joe’s

Tart and melt in your mouth! Definitely took 13 min for me, don’t want these to be underdone. Make the log an inch wide or even thinner. I used raspberries and shifted the powder to get out the seeds.

Good recipe! I used Koyah freeze dried organic raspberry powder and added some lime zest/juice  and extra salt. I also made them gluten free with Bob’s GF flour. So good that I ordered Koyah freeze dried mango, lemon and banana powders to try with this recipe.

These are pretty addictive. They’re kind of like a grown up version of sweet tart candies. I made them a little small, which made them fun to eat. I used a combination of strawberry and raspberry powder. As others have said, it’s a good idea to strain out the raspberry seeds.

Success on first try (for me, that means it was easy). Made mine with freeze-dried raspberries. Nice change from all the chocolate, nuts, and butter for the holidays and really bright, fun color. Great addition to my holiday cookie lineup, but don't have to be limited to the winter holidays. I'll make these any day of the year. Looking forward to trying them with blueberries!

This is my second year making these cookies! I use freeze dried cranberry and apple and the texture on these is to die for. I use the brand Super Garden ordered on Amazon and they have a lot of interesting options for freeze dried fruit. This recipe is so easy and adaptable, thank you Sohla!!!
You can find dehydrated strawberries at Aldi's any time of year. Got two bags and will try this recipe this weekend.

These were nice- kind of shortbread/teacake. I prefer the nuttier flavor of Russian tea cakes, but overall a fun twist. Might pick a mildly sweeter freeze dried fruit than strawberries next time. Was thankful for dusting (added a few fine colored sugar sprinkles) bc otw the cookies looked like large sliced Vienna sausage going in and out of the oven….

So delicious. I agree with Halina - before the dusting, my raspberry cookies looked like sliced salami lol. I really enjoy the sweet-tart flavor, definitely adds a new flavor to a holiday cookie platter. Next time I would try with a different color fruit!
This is a delicious recipe. Used mixed berries and gave it great color and flavor. Sugar costing tasty, but not necessary. Will try with rose extract next time for additional depth of flavor.

These were so bright and flavorful! I made them with freeze dried strawberries for my daughter's daycare holiday party. However, I don't think they were the most popular item in the lineup because they looked hilariously like slices of sausage link. In order to get 70 cookies from one batch they were the exact diameter of a typical sausage. They could have easily won a prize in a contest where they looked like one food but tasted so incredibly strongly like another. But still yummy!

Made these while babysitting! We rolled them out and used cookies cutters, and they perfectly withstood all the manhandling a very young child could throw at the dough. Just chilled again for 10 min before we put them in the oven and they came out tender and delicious.
Very small.  Roll shorter, thicker logs.
I tried using a blender and a mortar and pestle to grind the fruit and thought the mortar and pestle worked much better than the blender and  it was easier to clean!
Made a half recipe of these and they are OK.  I love the fruit / sugar dusting but the cookies are too tiny IMHO.  If I make them again, I will make them much larger.
I wish I had used a 1" cookie scoop to get a different shape.

Yes, they definitely look like sliced salami coated in confectioners sugar. I'm going to drizzle with chocolate to improve the appearance. 
A coworker suggested making up a "cheese and crackers" plate using a bar cookie cut into cubes and shortbread that looks like crackers.

12 min was to long in my oven. I'll try 10 but I do think you need to really pay attention at least the first time and watch to see when they're done.  let them cool almost completely before you dip in the powdered sugar, or the sugar melts.  I did mine Luke warm and the sugar layer was very thin and then melted away.  I repeated 3 times but never really built up a visibly thick layer.  however,  when I ate them, it was definitely there. it's nice and tart. I do try choosing when cooked and baking less next time.  Has potential.
Flavor 5-stars; texture 3-stars; degree of difficulty - not work the bother. Unlikely to re-make these.

Made as directed using Trader Joe's freeze dried sour cherries.  They are fine--nothing earth-shattering.  It's a butter cookies with a little tang from the cherries. You can't actually taste the cherry though--it's all butter.
Eight types of Christmas cookies made. All devoured except this one. Days after the holiday, these finally made their way to the compost bin.
Has anyone mad these with a cookie press, instead of the roll/chill/slice approach?

No, but that is an interesting approach.  

I'll probably try that with citrusy meltaways before I try it with this fruity version though.
who has a freezer that can accommodate 3 sheet pans????
Exactly my question! Great recipe if you live in a text kitchen. Otherwise...
I did a lengthy chill in the fridge before the egg/almond topping, then returned to the fridge to re-chill before the oven.  It seemed to work.  I didn't have any spreading.
Live in Canada, and then you just put them outside :P -35 degrees Celsius here this week

Just made these cookies and they are delicious.  I love how snappy they are, and the brown butter frosting is phenomenal.  I would cut back the sugar just a tad in the frosting as I found it a touch too sweet.  Also I rolled the dough out between sheets of floured parchment, which I find prevents sticking better.  The cookies themselves (sans frosting) make great little biscuits for tea and coffee.
Making these with salted pecans on top and bourbon in the filling.
How about dividing the dough and shaping into logs about 1 1/2 inch in diameter than slicing into rounds.  Saves time, no rolling and no scraps.

@Sharon - Thanks so much for the suggestion! I now make these every year and they are much easier this way. I roll them into a log, slide the log into an old paper towel roll and then slice thinly. Works great and since the people demand these every year now, I am glad you threw in that advice!
@Rachel do you slice the cookies while still in the paper towel roll or just use the roll to measure the thickness of the dough?

@Elizabeth-so sorry-I missed this- wrap them in plastic wrap and then put in a paper towel roll. Once you are ready to slice remove the roll and plastic wrap, let it warm just a tiny bit and then slice. Hope that helps and enjoy!

Ok first, cookies are amazing. Second, these are a pain in the butt to make and you need patience. Third, doesn’t make the amount it says. I only got 24 cookies out of this. Lastly, when you take these out of the freezer and start the egg wash, the egg wash freezes fast and almost no time to get the the almonds on so have the almonds in your other hand as soon as brush the egg wash. Lots of steps and time to make these but my god the taste is unreal!

In the video, the dough had a slight crumble that she was able to bring together with a bit of coaxing. My dough came out impossibly crumbly, no amount of coaxing could save it.  It finally came together for me after adding both an extra tablespoon of butter and an extra egg white. The modified dough rolled out easily and baked up snappy. I got 68 2-inch cookies. Sweetness level of icing was right for me, love the brown butter. Tastes best next day.

My dough came out incredibly crumbly as well. But I did exactly what you suggested and it helped immensely! They baked up snappy and delicious and even without the frosting, are PHENOMENAL with a cup of Earl grey tea.

Thank you for sharing.
Anyone brave enough to try these with GF flour and want to report back?
I nominate you!

Hi! I successfully made these gluten-free and dairy-free and they were fantastic! I used my favorite all-purpose gluten-free flour, King Arthur's measure for measure gluten-free flour. I also used coconut milk hard toffee and Miyoko's vegan butter (also hands-down my favorite!). You can absolutely do it! I had no problems whatsoever. I had a dinner party with a picky boyfriend and guests who all could eat gluten and dairy and they loved them!
Did anyone else’s dough come out of the mixer very crumbly? Even when I mix by hand it doesn’t seem to come together…any suggestions?

Mine came up very crumbly - I ended up almost "kneading" it together for after taking it our of the bowl and it stuck together better.
Rolled dough was still super fragile and prone to cracking but if you're gentle they stay together! Turned out great
My dough was crumbly, too. I added about 3 tbsp vodka in 1 tbsp increments to moisten it enough to get it to come together like you do for pie crust. It worked and these cookies are delicious.

Mine was also totally crumbly and not a dough (I read and re-read this recipe 100 times to make sure I didn't miss something). I refrigerated the dough hoping some rest time would help, but it's still not workable. I'm going to try adding another egg white and some butter like another person tried and see if that helps.
Wow, these were delicious. A fun project. For the filling I didn’t use a pastry bag but simply scooped with a teaspoon.

Delicious!! Made using 1 1/2 inch round cookie cutter, good size for sandwich cookies. Rolled the chilled dough out between parchment and used a 2 mm rolling pin guide to get thin enough. Baked 8-9 minutes. This made 60 sandwich cookies. I wish the cookie had just a little more salt to bring out the salty-sweetness. I doubled the salt in the icing and added 1 1/2 tb whiskey as seen in another comment here, this toned the sweetness of the icing down. Add whiskey in when browning butter.

Had to make my own brickle, everything turned out wonderfully! No way will this make 72 2” cookies, everything including the icing will make 36 halves. Also not sure why the urgency to be ready to pipe the icing, it’s just buttercream.
My icing dried pretty quickly once the cookies were together and stuck like glue! Thing that's the reason for the rush

These were a hit - the cookies would have been good on their own and the frosting worked.  Not sure if the almonds added much depth, but they did not hurt either.  

For those without the freezer space or number of pans (like me), I cut the cookies up and then stacked them between folded parchment to freeze.  Then I took the disks out when it was time to bake.  Didn't have any issues.
Can these be frozen once baked?

I made these with pecans instead of almonds and the are absolutely delicious. The recipe worked really well. I kept the dough in the fridge over night and there was absolutely no sticking (used enough flour for rolling).

The first time I made these the dough was too cumbly. So I tried the extra egg white.  But also, after watching Sohla's Youtube video I noticed that when she ground the toffee and flour in the processor she ground the mix very fine. I think that's important. My dough rolled out with no problems. But the cookies swelled up in the oven, which I'm attributing to the extra egg white, which I won't use next time. Also, the baking time to "brown like pretzels" seemed to be more like 15 minutes.

I have officially named this The Best Cookie I've Ever Made. Yes, it was a pain, especially cramming three cookie sheets into the freezer (had to stack one on an angle), and there are a lot of steps, but soooooo worth it! I was nervous about the icing given the instuctions, but it was not a big deal. I am being very choosy about who I share these with :)
Hey, as I am outside the US and Britain - would mixing any other brittle toffee work here? thanks!

I believe it would. My local candy store makes the best toffee I've ever eaten. I am going to break his up in a Ziploc using a meat hammer, and it should work perfectly. I will likely be making these in mid-December and will report back!
If I use convection-bake do I rely have to do all the pan rotating? Thanks for any tips.

I also use a convection oven and I do the rotation. Unless your oven is way better than mine (Thermador Wall oven 3 yrs old) I would do it here as well. Even though convection does indeed move the air around you still get hot spots near the fans. At least I do.

The dough didn’t come together in the mixing bowl I was tempted to add a few drops of water, but the first time I use a recipe I like to follow instructions, it did come together by hand, the only change is I baked one tray at a time, they didn’t come out as dark as the picture, but these cookies are very good…only made 32, the scraps became to dry, again may be adding drops of water would help… I will next time.
I made this recipe twice yesterday and both times the dough did not come together, it was very crumbly and sandy.  I won't make these again, something wrong with my process or the recipe.
I actually had the same problem and eventually added a few drops of water to get the dough to come together. I think my egg white was too small?

These look amazing and I'm looking forward to trying them. I will do one thing differently, though. Instead of rolling out the dough, I will make a 2-inch log, wrap in plastic and chill, then slice. Much easier and you get the same result!

I doubt this this will work, the cookies need to be very thin and even. Your knife will crumble the dough as it slices. You're trying to outsmart Sohla who thinks every step of her recipes through, if something could have been simplified she would have thought it through already.
Roger, I make other cookies this way and it actually works very well. Chilled enough, using a very sharp knife, and, very important, use parchment paper.
Woah. These are seriously good. A perfect Christmas cookie. For the busy amongst us bake the cookies on day one, then make the filling and assemble on day two.  Sohla is the cookie queen!

I used soft toffees as we don't have toffee bits where I live and the dough came out perfectly malleable like Play Dough. The cookies were snappy and sweet after baking. For me they are probably top sweet with the buttercream so I am probably going to fill them with red currant jam.
I was too lazy to roll out the floured dough a third time and formed very small balls out of the remainding dough just as I would with regular cookies but smaller. Worked very well!

These are awesome! I didn’t have room I’m my fridge so I just chilled them in my garage. I couldn’t find any toffee w/o nuts so I made toffee from scratch. Couldn’t put nuts on them so ideas about toppings would be great.
Amen. Who has a freezer that can accommodate three sheet pans? If you live in a cold climate, the great outdoors will provide plenty of space to do the trick!
These are really tasty cookies, well worth the effort. I got only 24 cookies from the recipe, added a little bourbon to the icing, and am definitely putting these into my regular rotation.
Bourbon in the icing sounds perfect!  What a good idea.  Thanks for sharing the idea.

What is the total baking time for these cookies?  Ten-fourteen minutes total seems like more than enough, but the way this is written it seems that 10-14 minutes is the halfway point.  The directions do say “deeply browned like a pretzel,” but almost a half-hour in the oven is one well-done cookie!

These are my new favorite Christmas cookies! I wish I could give them more than five stars, they are so good. Some notes for other bakers: In order to get 72 cookies, I had to roll out the dough about 1 or 2 inches wider than a standard piece of paper. Also, the recipe makes it seem like piping the cookies needs to be mega urgent, but you can take your time. The frosting didn’t harden for me until about 10 minutes after it was made.

Good but not great, probably not worth all the effort. I had no trouble getting the dough to come together, but the resultant cookies’ surface was smooth, not crackly looking as in the picture, and they were crisp like gingersnaps. Should they be? I inadvertently doubled the salt in the dough. Oops. Could that have made then crisper? It did no harm to the flavor, at least. I reduced the powdered sugar in the filling, thinking it would be too sweet as written, which was wise. Loved the filling.
Absolutely the most delicious cookie I have ever eaten. Followed the directions exactly (over two days - cookies then filling) Worth every minute of the effort.

These are unquestionably one of the most delicious cookies I've ever eaten! Time consuming but not particularly difficult and absolutely fantastic! 

As others have mentioned, the egg wash froze when we brushed it on the frozen cookies. The next day, when we baked the remaining two sheets, we used a silicone brush rather than one with typical hair-based bristles and it did not freeze. So either time in the freezer, brush type, or both may solve that issue.

I found the dough to be very very dry and crumbly. I ended up adding a small amount of water and a lot more butter. They ended up tasting great but I wouldn't make them again without changing the recipe.

I made these again for the holidays because I enjoyed making them for the first time last year. This time I actually did end up with 36 sandwich cookies (last year I think I got around 24) but I made sure to really roll out the dough to 1/8”. It’s a lot thinner than you might think but it holds up. The cookies are much snappier this time around because of it too. 

If you have limited room in your freezer like me, I stacked my sheets between parchment paper and that saves a ton of space.
These cookies are amazing, and really not that much effort.  Completely worth it - they are special.
these. are. amazing. i didn’t find them difficult at all. made plenty. icing is amazing. i think i will be making those every year.

Delicious! Rolling the dough out and then chilling it in the fridge before cutting significantly helped in maintaining the cookies' circular shape.  Buttercream needed a smidge more salt than called for.

These are delicious! Agree I couldn’t get 72 halves with 2” - maybe I’m too scared to roll to thickness of two Pennie’s? They were still delicious. My dough was sticky but I didn’t freeze it I just rolled it and did the three sheet pans in freezer after. Agree with egg wash freezing - did we have too much water? They are a pain but directions are methodical and clear and cookies are delishhhhh

Made these cookies yesterday and froze overnight. Today I baked. My oven is fussy so had to watch carefully. I did use my convection option just to keep the temp up as it will drop to 300 or lass in the bake mode at 350 .  Miscalculated butter amt and melted 2sticks of butter instead of 1 1/2. So had to add more p.  sugar and decided on 1/2 tsp of. Bourbon vs vanilla for the xtra amt.  

Everything turned out well, very tasty, but will make more when I’m not making other bake goods.

Made the recipe as is. I ground the toffee chips a lot as someone else recommended and the dough came together nicely. Also I rolled the dough into (2) 2" logs and froze them. After an hour, I sliced and did the egg wash and sliced almonds, much easier and more manageable in the freezer!

I thought these cookies were delicious, but making them was tough. I thought it was incredibly difficult to roll out the dough because the toffee was so sticky that the dough could not be easily rolled out. I ended up eyeballing the size and just rolling them out like you would chocolate chip cookies. I found once I did that I felt much better about the whole process. Happy cookie making.
I am so excited to try this recipe. Thoughts on adding almond extract as well?

I’m going to be trying these Gluton Free.  I always mix various GF flours.  My plan for the flour is to use 2 parts Authentic Foods superfine brown rice flour, 2 parts Bob’s Mill All purpose GF flour, and 1 part almond flour.  And then let the dough rest in my cold kitchen for at least an hour before rolling out.  I would dust with the rice flour.  It’s always an experiment!
I have a problem being gluton free as I love sweets.

The Gluten Free plan worked that I suggested two weeks ago.  Oh my god.  So delicious! I always mix various GF flours. I used 2 parts Authentic Foods superfine brown rice flour (90 g), 2 parts Bob’s Mill All purpose GF flour (90 g) and 1 part almond flour (45 g). And then let the dough, wrapped in plastic, rest in the fridge for an hour before rolling out. I dusted with the rice flour.

Does browning the butter really make it safe to keep these cookies at room temp for a month?  USDA says regular butter can be left at room temperature for "several days" before it goes rancid. The longest time recommended I've seen for keeping butter at room temp is two weeks - and only then if the room temperature doesn't exceed 70F.
I don’t know whether the browning makes a difference, but the sugar and salt in the recipe almost surely do.

I made these cookies today and WOW are they are amazing!! VERY worth the effort to make them - they are quite different from most typical Christmas cookies. I also didn't get as high of a yield from these cookies as other people, but I definitely didn't roll my dough thin enough (as I found out after I'd rolled them all out). It made 26 sandwich cookies for me, but I think I could have gotten to the 32 by rolling them even thinner.
What if I live in a country that doesn't sell toffee pieces? :D
I will be making these with sugar free & low carb ingredients for a diabetic. Wish me luck.
My frosting is super runny - does just cooling it work or should I add more sugar?

These came together for me in the food processor. I just continued the recipe after it said to move the crunbled heath bits, etc into a mixing bowl. I just didn’t move it. Certainly less stuff to clean up. I also found the biscuits great but the filling too sweet. I used chopped almonds as I didn’t have sliced ones. Beware putting too much egg wash on. It made the tops an odd yellow in the spots without nuts and the leftover few I didn’t fill got soggy after a day or two.
Sohla is queen. This recipe is a masterpiece. These cookies are absolutely incredible.

Just made these and they’re worth all the steps! I have to say though I haven’t yet made the icing and I’m not sure I want to — they’re so good on their own. And like most others I also did not get that many cookies out of this recipe, maybe only 36 pieces total
I wonder if using thin blanched sliced almonds would burn them in the oven. Has anyone used those? I’m having a hard time finding the unblanched regular thickness sliced almonds in the pic & video?
I used blanched slivered almonds, they turned out just fine, lightly roasted and look equally good. My cookies were in the oven for 12 minutes.

The cookie itself is loaded with sugar but not too bad, toffee crunch is enjoyable.

However sandwiching with that buttercream frosting was not a good idea, i find it cloyingly sweet and not good :( (I only added 210gm sugar to frosting and even upping the salt didn’t do much, too bad).

I stacked the sheet pans to solve the freezer space problem.  My issue was that my dough was a bit crumbly and I only got about 50 cookie sides/25 constructed cookies.  I left the dough in the fridge overnight -- perhaps I dried the dough out (even though wrapped in plastic).

These are delicious and look impressive without an excessive amount of work. As you might expect from a toffee cookie, they are a little bit over-the-top sweet (be warned!). I also found it difficult to roll out to the thinness of two pennies. It worked much better when I tried rolling between sheets of wax paper.

Well i used a whole stick of butter instead of 3 Tb.... so we will see if the butter melts out... any suggestions to this dilemma? I do not have any more toffee to double the recipe... so more flour??? Another egg white?

These are good! I agree with the comment suggesting cutting back on the sugar. They are sweet and rich. The first batch I baked burned, so watch them. I think they should definitely be baked for 10 mins (with rotations halfway through) and no longer.

Just made them and they are fabulous!  Dough came together easily for me.  Like others, I didn’t get as much yield as indicated. It made 28 sandwich cookies for me.  I found it quite challenging to roll the dough out to the same thickness each time, with the result that the cookies varied in size a bit.  That made it difficult to get them all browned to the same extent, even with pan rotation. But they still look pretty.  And that brown butter filling is phenomenal, not too sweet IMHO.

On the egg wash and almond application, I did one cookie at a time and had no problem getting the almonds to stick firmly.  On the piping of the filling, I piped a half dozen cookies at a time and then topped them.  One irksome thing is that the cookies, being so lightweight, tend to stick to the filling as the piping bag is lifted up.  Need to figure out how to pipe with one hand while holding the cookie with the other.

Followed the directions exactly and ended up with delicious cookies! I saw some people saying the yield was low was able to get 34 cookies from this recipe and just the right amount of filling as well. You do have to be careful when applying the egg wash though, as it freezes quickly on the cookies and there may not be time to do fun designs with the almonds. I highly recommend this recipe and will be making it again in the future!

Rolling thin wasn't a problem for me and I ended up with over 40 cookies. I used my smallest scoop (1 tsp) for the filling and that was plenty.  I had just enough for the higher yield of cookies.

When I rolled the scraps it was really cold dough and cracked.  I had to let it warm a bit to roll out nice and thin without cracking.

I found the dough was way, way too crumbly, so I had to another 2 tablespoons of butter to the dough at the end. As a result, my cookies weren't particularly crisp, but at least it was possible to roll and cut the dough.
Accidentally got crushed heath with chocolate... will this be a problem??
Freeze the Heath Bits with chocolate before using - bet they will be delicious!

I made these exactly as written and they were delicious—maybe the best cookies I've made this year. Perfect blend of crunchy cookie and rich filling. They were a bit crumbly to roll out, but if you persevere and roll the pin in different directions the cracks in the dough will come back together. I'll definitely be making these again next year.
Could the egg wash and almonds be put on Before they go into the freezer?

I did make these and the taste is incredible but not sure if maybe I didn't cook them enough??   They are very hard to bite into so thinking they needed to stay in the over more and become crisp?  Was afraid they would burn.  Will try again as they are that good.

I made some changes too. My dough didn’t get fluffy or creamy so I added more egg white and that did the trick. I also used only 1/2 cup sugar and used dark rum instead vanilla to enhance the toffee flavor. I rolled out 35 pairs so my numbers were similar; these are super thin. I used an egg white wash on the almond half, did not freeze them first and they were done in 8 minutes. In the icing, I cut down to eight T of butter and 1 1/2 c powdered sugar and applied with a small knife. Yummy!

I love these cookies.  But then, I love toffee/butter pecan type profile.  So I figured I'd get to eat most of the batch.  Not this batch....served at a small family gathering with a good assortment of cookies and everyone ate at least two.

I was able to freeze the cut cookies in stages, as I could only get two sheets in my freezer.  I filled the two sheets and froze them.  After a couple hours at most I could put them into a freezer bag.  Then I finished rolling out the scraps.

I rolled the dough out between sheets of floured parchment, which I find prevents sticking better. The cookies themselves (sans frosting) make great little biscuits for tea and coffee.
Making these with salted pecans on top and bourbon in the filling.
 when you take these out of the freezer and start the egg wash, the egg wash freezes fast and almost no time to get the the almonds on so have the almonds in your other hand as soon as brush the egg wash.

Delightful cookie! Watching Sohla's instructional video on YouTube was helpful. The dough was very forgiving IMO - I stuck one half in the fridge as I was rolling out the other. I also didn't have room in the freezer but 45 min in the refrigerator did the trick. Next time around, I would probably try to roll the cookies a tad more precisely (I was a little over 1/8"). The brown butter frosting is delicious - I will add slightly more salt in the future, but it's not necessary at all.

Reilly and others - my dough never came together. It literally fell apart in my hands.   I did see a note below that an extra egg white and/or more butter might have worked.  I was so disappointed; I had to scrap the whole project. :(
Really great texture and flavor. Added a little more powdered sugar to the filling bc it was super loose. These are a great festive cookie.

Made these as written and they are absolutely delicious. The dough came together nicely for me and I would have been able to cut out 72 cookies had my toddler not stolen a small hunk of dough to play with. I didn’t have room in my freezer for the cookies, but an hour in the refrigerator prior to baking worked just fine. These are a bit of a project, but by far the best cookies I made this year.

I made these yesterday and they are delicious, but I overbaked them because I read the instructions too quickly. (Even at too much time, they are delicious.)  Would appreciate other bakers’ thoughts on optimal baking time at 350.  Thanks!

These cookies are legendary. The crispy, caramelized, cookies combined with the creamy, buttery filling is a recipe for obesity and delight. However, do NOT add any liquids to the frosting! I was like "ima be smart and add milk so that it hardens less quickly" and it caused a disastrous oily mess cuz the filling separated after the addition of the liquid. All in all though, these are delicious and would highly recommend. They are worth the effort!

This recipe is so fantastic!! Long time reader, first time commenter just to say how much I love these cookies. I “tested” 3 before I could even get them on a plate. The rest were devoured in record time. Glad I bought a second bag of heath bits so I can make them again as gifts (if I can resist!).

Our dough came out of the mixer crumbly. Based on folks comments and our assessment of the done cookies, we will add butter next time. Icing is too sweet like others said. The flavor is phenomenal. We got 26 cookies, not 36.

No issue with dough as others stated. Cut my butter into cubes and left it on the counter for about 30 minutes along with the egg white. Followed the suggested mixing times, if anything I should have put it in the fridge to firm up a tiny bit after it came together.

I could only find the heath bar pieces with chocolate at my grocery store but they still turned out great! the dough wasn't crumbly at all for me (i weighed my ingredients so maybe that helped?) I also used salted butter and I think that balanced the sweetness better. Seconding the rolling between 2 pieces of parchment!
Overall, great flavor and yes, it's a little more work than the standard cookie but it's worth it!

Wish we knew to roll the dough out before freezing overnight sooner.  The freeze/thaw process was the only challenge with the recipe. We didn’t know and it gave us a tough time.  The cookies are great though!

If you didn't get enough cookies, you're probably either not rolling the dough thin enough, or using a cookie cutter bigger than Sohla's. I used a 2-inch cutter and the recipe yielded 80 cookies. 

Another tip -- in the video, Sohla says you can chill some of these in the freezer and some in the fridge if you don't have enough room. Then, to avoid the frozen egg wash issue that Tommy mentioned, egg wash the refrigerated ones, not the frozen ones.

The toffee flavour of these cookies was outstanding! They’re a little fussy to make but watching the video really helped my process (especially the bit about working fast with the frosting). Sadly, I did over-bake the biscuits a tiny bit (totally my own fault for not knowing my new oven) but they got very hard, almost inedible and weren’t quite the hit I was hoping they’d be on my holiday cookie tray. That said, people were taking them apart and dipping them in their tea so not all was lost!

OMG, these are SO GOOD!  Had to special order the toffee bits from Amazon, even 2 weeks before Christmas; don't know if that's a COVID supply chain thing or what.  Like all cutout cookies, these take time, but totally worth it!  I have a new addition to my Christmas cookie "must bake" list.

I couldn't find toffee bits without chocolate, which I think was the main problem behind my extremely crumbly dough. Once I added an additional eggwhite, the dough came together perfectly. The cookies were a huge hit!

Did anyone cross-test this recipe because it sure doesn't seem that way. Putting hot browned butter into a standmixer to make icing does not work. You just end up with an oily soup as expected - mine is sitting on a -20C balcony chilling off after I second guessed myself and followed the written directions.

Echoing others, the dough is way too dry to come together without additional hydration or oil. 
It's tasty, but a very flawed recipe that requires some wizardry to make work.

I used homemade toffee for this recipe and it worked wonderfully! However, I have two notes: 
1. The brown butter frosting should cool a little before being put into a piping bag (it will be boiling hot right after being done). Don't worry about it becoming too cool- I evaporated all the water off of my butter, but it never got too solid in the piping bag. The heat from your hands will keep it pliable enough. 
2. Probably obvious, but the thicker you make your cookies, the more they spread!

Ok I'll admit that I could not find English toffee, so instead used Wether's Originals (blitzed into a powder)....the taste was...fine? I mean basically it was a Wether's Originals cookie. Super sweet, chewy...but probably NOT what it was supposed to taste like. Likely because of the WOs, the dough was very dry and required addtl butter and water to bind. Only made ~25 very small sandwiches.

Unlike others, I did get exactly 72  2 inch rounds. Like others, I found the snap excellent and the flavor very good (the brown butter icing is wonderful), and the time required excessive. Though the flavor was pleasantly different from most cookies I have made, they are excessively sweet. I'm not sure how the sugar level can be decreased without changing the texture.

If you're like me and didn't look carefully at the recipe before grocery shopping, the toffee with chocolate works out fine. I found it hard to roll out the dough thin enough to get 72 cookies. I also thought it was too sweet (maybe because I used the chocolate toffee), and I halved the amount of frosting. It's a good cookie, but I probably wouldn't make it again -- too sweet and too finicky.
These ere impossible.  Th dough is so crumbly that it couldn't be rolled out.  I tried everything, including water and another egg.  Finally just ended up throwing it out.

The first time, I added the butter into a bowl containing the sugar and ended up with a very soupy mixture (eventually saved by cooling and adding more sugar). The next time, I put the butter in the mixer bowl and slowly added the sugar to it and it came out with a perfect texture (way too hot to pipe, but an offset spatula worked well). So follow directions.... Also be aware that the butter will violently boil when the vanilla is added to the bowl; make sure the bowl is large.

These were delicious and unfortunately incredibly fussy to make as other commenters have already noted. I encouraged everyone I gifted them to enjoy as they would not make it onto my yearly Christmas cookie list.

Cool brown butter before adding the powdered sugar. There is plenty of filling-cut a smaller hole in the pastry bag and pipe the filling on in a circle (still well inside the edge of the cookie) to make it show evenly all the way around.

I made these last year 2021 and they were great! I’ve tried twice this year and failed both times - the dough won’t come together. Did anyone else have problems? I honestly wonder if there’s an error that slipped into the recipe over the last year.

Are you weighing the dry ingredients? That can make a big difference, especially with flour. And maybe try an XL egg white instead of a regular one? I've baked two batches with an XL egg white with great success. Hope it works -- these are so good!

Update for 2022.  I loved these so much last year, I had to dive in again. Like last year, I did have to add a bit more butter to get the dough to come together.  I found the almond topping, while pretty, didn't add much flavor.  So this year I topped with a sanding sugar/flaky salt mixture for a little bling. I also added 1/2 tsp almond extract to both the dough and the icing to amp the almond flavor. Thank you Sola for such a nice recipe.  It's sure to be a classic in my house!

An absolute labor of love- these cookies are delicious but oh boy do they take some time. Don't make these if you are short on stamina or time since it will take a fair deal of both, but they are so tasty they are completely worth it. I have people begging me to make them again!
Perfect! My brother (who doesn’t like sweets) ate three and claimed they were the best cookies he ever had.

I made these with no problems. The dough was a bit crumbly, but I just smooshed it together & put it in the fridge overnight. Rolling it out takes a light hand & I  actually stacked two pennies along side the rolled-out dough to check the thickness.  Also, I added extra salt to the filling, about 3/4 t., to cut the sweetness. Next time I’d make an Italian buttercream. Not a fan of powdered sugar. They are very sweet. 

Weighing your ingredients is key with this recipe.  I made 42 cookies.

Was able to make 72 cookies come out, but they were thin. Cookies were too hard too quickly. Was hoping for a chewier cookie. Between the toffee and the sugared icing these were too sweet for my taste. Guests loved them, but it would probably need some more tweaking before I made them again. 

Also, 1 1/2 hours is a lie. Was closer to 2+. Time consuming.
Delicious cookies that I would make again! They are very crisp and sturdy for storing and shipping as Sohla states in the video. Wonderful with a cup of coffee or tea.
I ended up not sandwiching after frosting. Worked out great.

On my third (fourth?) time making this recipe I accidentally bought the heath bits WITH chocolate although Sohla said not to use those…anyway it turned out fine. I wouldn’t do it on purpose but the cookies are still good.
Forgot to mention my dough was fine, just a tad softer than my biscotti dough and easy to handle.  Perhaps if the flour is beaten too long it gets crumbly.
These will now be a staple in my household. Two changes: use the entire egg it addresses the crumblyness, and double it.

I was nervous about these after reading the comments, but they turned out great! 

I weighed the flour and rolled the dough out between parchment, then chilled two trays at a time.

Pay extra attention to the 1/8 inch thing. I ended up rolling out my scraps 3 times to get the full 72. 

For the Canadians reading this: I couldn't find Skor pieces anywhere so close to Christmas, but 5 Macintosh Creamy Toffee Bars smashed then blitzed up worked well.

TLDR: Fiddly and take a *while*, but delicious!

These are crispy and 
delicious! The brown butter filling is melt in your mouth phenomenal. A couple notes: cut down on sugar(in frosting and batter... a tad too sweet), did not get 72 cookies (more like 44), I did not freeze them, kept in refrigerator for 30 minutes and they baked beautifully. Worth the time!
Really good but a lot of work to roll out and cut. Sweet but no way around that

Overall I loved these. I made my own toffee due to almond allergies. I cut with a 2 inch cutter but they spread quite a bit so I would go to 1.5inches. The brown butter frosting was fantastic and really worked well with the cookies. I took one pan out too early and they were chewy. Those that I cooked long enough stayed crisp for days. Since I could not decorate with almonds I sprinkled on some fine crushed toffee but it melted in. Maybe a little large grain sugar would have been better.

1. Best cookies ever. Everyone will rave about them when you share them.
2. They shockingly make the specific amount of cookies, and I never end up with even a scrap of excess (you must use the exact size cutter, roll out to exact thinness)
3. Because I have a small freezer, I freeze on parchment sheets and layer.
4. You can even make your own "skor" if you want, although it does change the texture slightly for the snap.
Can I make these successfully without freezing them? My freezer is way too small for even one try to fit in.

For anyone looking for this advice, I couldn’t find non-chocolate coated toffee for an affordable price near me (only shipping), and so I used Heath bits. It worked AMAZING. I think the milk chocolate gave it a lovely warmth. The color of the cookies was ever-so-slightly darker, but honestly… not by much at all.

I froze the cookies on my smallest pan touching one another and transfered them to a tupperware to stockpile them due to freezer space constraints. like other people noted, I could not get 72 cookies out of the dough, I think I got around 60 (so 30 cookies when everything is sandwiched). I found the dough easy to work with with a little chilling and a floured cutting board. I think if the dough is too crumbly you might need to procces the toffee bits for longer.

These are amazing! Lots of steps but worth it. My husband gobbled a bunch of the individual cookies before I could even fill them and turn them into sandwiches. So from now on, will definitely make a batch just for the cookie part for him. 

Btw, first time around, Heath Toffee Bits were all sold out. So I scraped down Almond Roca (used the chocolate almond scrapings in another recipe). Worked fine. Second time around, same problem so I made my own toffee (which really isn’t that hard to make). Worked out pretty darn good too.

Amazing. We make these every year and are in love with them. Sure, they take some time but it’s really not difficult at all. There’s great stopping points in the recipe so you don’t have to make it all in one day. Three cookie trays in the freezer is a little tough, but in Wisconsin winters we’ve got outside. We have also alternated and left one dough disk in the fridge while we roll, cut, and bake one batch. Highly recommend these!

I make these smaller than suggested, using a 1” cookie cutter rather than a 2”. The bigger size just feels too big. 
I bake for about 8-10 minutes. Always remind myself to let them get darker than I expect.
Really good. I used half brittle & half candied pecans to reduce the sweetness and added whisky to the buttercream.

My first time baking this recipe so I undoubtedly did things "wrong" but we found them DELICIOUS and too rich to eat an entire one.  Debating changing the size or making them iced, not sandwich, next time.  So yummy though.

I love Sohla's ginger cookie so had to try this one. My confession: I added the whole egg (I am a lazy baker) and I just made the cookies, no filling. And lemme tell you THESE ARE TERRIFIC. Easy recipe, the cookies are snappy and major hit (already) in my house. Thanks Sohla!

Everything I imagined these cookies would be: crispy, crunchy, creamy, and deeply brown-buttery! There are many steps here but the recipe worked perfectly. Well worth the effort. They look amazing too!

title: Banana-Cardamom Cream Pie With Caramel Recipe
author: Sohla El-Waylly

comments.commentBody:
Why are people so resistant on using cardamom without even trying the recipe? Why even comment?

Absolutely delicious. I would make this a thousand times over as is.

I made this pie for a family gathering (with a couple modifications) for a family gathering and it received compliments all around. I was a bit nervous that I wouldn’t be able to pull off the caramel but Sohla’s instructions and a quality pan made the difference. 

I caramelized the bananas to intensify their flavor, pulling from the  Caramelized Banana Pudding NYT cooking recipe for this. My family has pretty vanilla taste and wouldn’t like cardamom in the pudding so I used, well, vanilla.

Matt, regarding your question about using Cardamom in this recipe:   Green cardamom pods is not a spice staple in my cupboard.   While considering this recipe and researching the ingredient, I decided to purchase a small amount from Penzey’s and was glad I did.   The spice added a complex layer of flavor while remaining inoffensive.  It did not overwhelm the dish as I was afraid it might.   I’m not sure I personally could have accomplished the same flavor profile using ground cardamom.
@Jamie that’s where I get my cardamom too and it’s perfect !

This was not as much work as it might seem. The cardamom is subtle and without it the custard would be quite boring. It tasted better after a full 24 hours in the fridge. The one issue is that even though the caramel was quite firm, it became very weepy once assembled. I end up spooning it out of the pan and onto the slices. Mistakes I made: packing the crust too tight, not using all the bananas called for because I didn’t think the custard would fit (it did and would have with all the bananas).

When someone actually makes this with the cardamom, can you share your verdict? Occasionally like a little cardamom in coffee, but I'm struggling to imagine whether this is a good idea or one of those perilous bits of culinary frippery that spoil a simple pleasure.
@Matt - I’ve made this recipe many times exactly as written, with the cardamom. It’s the pie everyone loves and keeps requesting. Fabulous as always—no alterations.

Just a heads up! remember to find the little Silica Gel Packet/Moisture Absorber Drying Bags before blending your freeze dried banana slices. I did not remember this until it was too late…Happy to report that the cardamom IS delicious and subtle in the custard.

This pie took work but it was one of the most amazing things ever to come out of my 
kitchen

Hands down one of the best pies I’ve ever had. We had about 68 people over for thanksgiving (we have a huge family) and everyone who tried this says it was the best pie they’ve ever eaten. Highly recommend! I will be making this again very soon!

I made this recipe for Thanksgiving this year. It was generally a hit. Two guests don't care for bananas, but they ate the pie. The others liked the interesting combination of flavors with the cardamom, the cream filling, and the (for them) not-overpowering banana flavor. I had not made caramel sauce for many years and had to dump out my first try at it, but otherwise I was able to follow the directions and make a delicious dessert.
I say no to the cardamon. Use a vanilla bean instead. Caramel, banana and dark chocolate. What more do you need?

I made this pie with graham cracker crust, and it was delicious!  the bittersweet chocolate shavings were delicious.  I would use about half the cornstarch next time, and cut back a bit on sugar in the filling.  Caramel was a great modification.

Suggestions: Added a generous sprinkling of Maldon salt on caramel layer. For filling, except for bananas and butter, put everything else into bullet blender, cooked as suggested, then strained filling to remove any cardamom pod detritus. Caramelized bananas and deglazed pan with a Tbsp of bourbon.
Delicious. Gorgeous. Over the top dessert. Make it from scratch. You’ll be so proud of yourselves.

Easiest way to evenly disperse a crumb crust into a 9" pan is to use another 8" pie plate to press gently over and into the 9" pan filled with cookie crumbs (before baking.) Prevents the thicker build-up of excess crumbs ithroughout the pie plate providing an even dispersement. And it's quick!

Nothing about this one worked for me, unfortunately, even though I usually have good luck with NYT cooking.  The custard was too stiff, the caramel intruded far too strongly on the rest of the pie, and the cardamom was unnecessary.
The whipped cream topping is a disaster. Just sprinkle the crushed banana on top of regular whipped cream.
Definitely needs more cardamom. The caramel is a bit overwhelming - - not sure if leaving out the honey would have fixed that. I couldn't find freeze dried bananas so just made plain homemade whip.
Instead of Cardamon, can I use ground Cardamon instead and what measurements would I use for for the ground Cardamon?

Absolutely amazing. I couldn't find cardamom pods at my grocery store, so I just put a scant 1/4 teaspoon of ground cardamom instead. I also added a tsp of vanilla extract to the custard because I like vanilla.
@stacy  you didn't make this

LOVED the flavors. Burned the caramel on the first try. Needed lower heat. Cardamom flavor was incredible, I will probably use 1.5x next time but I am a real cardamom fan. Could not find freeze dried banana in my small town so I did 2 layers of fresh banana and it was great. This is an interesting and delicious pie and worth the work!

This was a HUGE hit at a potluck!! I made mine in a springform pan and it held together beautifully. The cardamom was subtle and added a nice floral note to the pudding layer — if you’re unsure about adding it I encourage you to give it a try, it’s not obtrusive at all!

The only modification I made was reducing the sugar in the whip topping by 1 tablespoon because I could only find pre-sweetened dried banana chips. Worked like a charm. This was a great project recipe with a great payoff.
As a liquid, milk is measured in millilitres (ml), not grams.
unless you happen to have a balance...

The recipe already measured the milk in cups rather than milliliters.  Measuring by weight would be more accurate although I doubt that such level of accuracy is necessary.  To paraphrase former Secretary of Defense Donald Rumsfeld:  you cook with the ingredients and equipment you have, not with the ingredients and equipment you wish you had.
Haven't tried this yet but I think I may cheat and buy the caramel at Trader Joe.  They have a great one.
So good. I think it needs a little more salt, but otherwise great and, honestly, not that much work if you break it up.

This recipe is absolutely delicious. I only had biscoff cookies which made for an excellent base. I was worried about the cardamom, but it was perfect. A little different and very inoffensive, even to guests with a very vanilla palate (pun intended). 1.5x to fill my deep dish pie to the brim, arranged my banana coins in offset spirals, and it was a stunner.

I used one teaspoon of cardamom and it turned out great! The pudding in the middle tasted savory with the cardamom which was complimented nicely with the sweet whipped cream and sugary crust. Also, instead of using freeze dried banana I folded in one mashed banana into the whipped cream.

This was a truly phenomenal pie and an absolute joy to make! I couldn't find vanilla wafers so I substituted equal parts graham crackers and it was delicious. Also, I could only find "solar dried" bananas which were chewy rather than brittle, but they worked well in the topping. The textures of the crisp crust, chewy caramel, the bite of the banana, and soft custard and cream were lovely. Not too sweet, and the cardamom really adds a depths of flavor.
Due to an egg allergy, how should I go around replacing the egg & egg yolks in the filling? I'm not much of a baker.

I whipped whipped cream, too long despite the strong morning. It still tasted great. If you’re worried about an untraditional flavor, I would leave out the honey in the caramel and add an extra tablespoon of sugar! We also left out the cardamom.

I wouldn’t make this again. The honey caramel didn’t taste good at all. It also made this pie far too sweet. The pudding has far too much corn starch and was stiff as a board. I recommend finding an alternate recipe. I did like the cardamom flavor, but there wasn’t enough of it. After tasting the pudding, I added more ground cardamon.
Your guests are sure to love the complex flavors of this banana cream pie. At out Thanksgiving meal, the pie was a favorite among all!
loved this dessert, and it was fun to make. only used 3 cardamom pods because it was the birthday cake for my friend who has the palate of a 13 year old. remember to take some extra lactaid.

Putting the hot pudding directly on the caramel causes it to reheat and become displaced. I also think the addition of the honey made it less stable, so it ran out of the bottom of the pie when cut. I would just make a regular caramel, and perhaps more of it for a more distinct layer.

Overall a fine recipe, but felt like an unnecessary amount of work for an unbalanced and overly sweet pie. When others commented on the caramel weeping, I thought this was only when poured on top, so I skipped this and had folks add caramel on top of each slice they took. Unfortunately I found that the base layer of caramel also has this issue. It really wets the crust as it daws moisture from the bananas. 
Wafer crust and sweetened cream top make it all too much imo

This was probably the best pie I ever had! However, there were a few things I would have liked to know in advance:
1. You should add two more pods of cardamom at least if you want a strong flavor (and you should, because straining the custard is very inconvenient).
2. Speaking of straining the custard: Instead of a sieve, you should use a cheese cloth
3. I didn't end up using much banana. Maybe my pan was too shallow? I did two layers of slices
4. Add a THICK layer of chocolate shavings
@Matt also try the cardamom banana break from Milk Street which is the only banana bread I make now

Watched Sohla's video before deciding to make this recipe—so glad I did! Of course, the look and description are winning, but the basic ingredients and prep clinched it. Solha's instructions are uncomplicated but precise. This is a great weekend at home recipe, and frankly, every day of this pandemic is a day at home. I wouldn't ding this recipe on prep time as three of the four hours are completely hands off. That time is spent coasting the meringue bake in the oven at a very low temp. Enjoy.
I would make this again, but cook the shortbread for a shorter time, the long  bake with the meringue made the cookie a bit more crumbly that I would like.
Followed the recipe faithfully and the end result was really dry, really crumbly cookies that don’t cut well. Disappointed with the end result so I won’t take the time to make them again.
This was really great! I did read some of the comments and so I cooked the shortbread for 15 mins and the meringue for 1 hr 30 mins. It tastes delicious and looks beautiful.

It’s way too salty using 3/4 teaspoon of Morton’s kosher salt. The recipe must be expecting you to use Diamond Crystal; 1/4 teaspoon is probably enough for Morton’s. 

The shortbread is very crumbly and the meringue doesn’t want to stay attached. 

The meringue layer is really good.

Pretty easy recipe, but Step 9, the meringue never gets dry while baking. I kept in an additional 30 min to try for dry. Still never felt dry or crisp. Turned off oven and cooled as directed and finally got dry and crisp. When cutting the cookies the meringue cracked and completely separated from shortbread. Tastes great, but failed for xmas cookies to share. Too bad. Now I have to eat them all.

I have to agree it is too salty using Morton's kosher salt. 1/2 tsp would seem more reasonable. Not impressed with the shortbread it's self. After all the cooking it was chalky tasting and too crumbly.

For some reason, my meringue was hard after only an hour of baking, so I stopped it then! I think that prevented it from being as dry as other reviewers said. It’s crumbly but in a good way, at least to me!

I love shortbread cookies and I love that Sohla gave me a chocolate shortbread cookie! And you don’t have to add the peppermint to the icing...  you can do whatever you want with it! this can be very versatile! Sohla always says do what works for you! That’s why I love watching her cook and I love her recipes! She always makes them so versatile that you can make it how you like it!! she just gives you a really good foundation! 
Thank you so much Sohla!!
Made these today; biscuit is far too dry and way too salty. The meringue topping worked well and was very tasty. Would not make these again; far too much effort for a mediocre treat.
Can this recipe be frozen?

The cookies were extremely dry and disappointing. I pulled the shortbread out 15 minutes early during the initial bake based on the comments of others. The end result was still super dry. I don’t think I would make this again- but if I did I would pull it out after 1 hr 40 min so the meringue would still be kind of chewy and not so hard.
I have made these twice and both times they looked beautiful out of the oven but shattered into crumbs when I tried to cut them.

The texture of the shortbread was really chalky and dry. I’d love to know if it was because of the temp of the butter - really room temp and not cold, or because I used King Arthur’s Double Dutch cocoa. I baked for only 30 minutes and at correct oven temperature. I had to scrap the meringue topping because the peppermint oil i used was overpowering even at 1/4 tsp. So I tried the shortbread before restarting the meringue recipe. I just didn’t like it and decided not to spend anymore time on it.

This didn't work for me. I followed the recipe faithfully - I even watched the awful video - and ended up with a dry, crumbly shortbread that resulted in a pile of crumbs and ragged pieces. 

I liked the meringue and the finished product tasted good, but I wasn't able to cut it into anything presentable even though I used a sharp knife and thoroughly cleaned it between cuts. 

For me, not worth the time and effort and I won't make it again.
Chocolate shortbread was disappointingly dry — a real shame because it smelled so good! Meringue also did not want to stay put on top of cookie. Won’t be making this again.

This smells and looks lovely, but it’s way too dry and crumbly. I could only manage 4 pieces when cutting because the rest turned into a pile of crumbs. It tastes pretty good, but I wouldn’t make this again. A lot of time for a very underwhelming result.

It is very rare that I bake cookies that are inedible, but these were IT.  The shortbread has a horrible texture and harsh taste. The meringue did not rescue it, and for the most part did not stay attached. A ROYAL MESS.  I bake cookies all the time, from shortbread to sugar cookies, to macarons, and springerle.  No operator error here. The recipe is simply not good. AT ALL.

The meringue layer is beautiful and tasty, but I would skip this recipe. The shortbread is so dry that I was embarrassed I had shared these with others. They lose the charm of the swirled top because the cookie disintegrates when you touch it. I even reduced the bake time from reviews above.

So taking all my fellow commenters’ advice, I made a few adjustments: cut the kosher salt in the chocolate layer to 1/4 tsp, baked it for 15 minutes, and I baked the meringue layer at 215 degrees for about half hour. The textures were perfect (not too crumbly or over baked) *but* the meringue cracked on cutting it, making it unserve-able. Oh well, guess I have a whole pan to myself!

Unfortunately the end result was very disappointing. I followed the recipe carefully, but the shortbread was chalky and very dry, almost inedible. The merengue had a nice taste, but was also a bit too chewy. I made this for a cocktail party and will not be serving this - may have been some unknown error on my part, but this was quite a bit of effort for a disappointing result.
Wow, should have read the comments. These were totally inedible. Texture of chalk like everyone says. Just absolutely awful to bite down on.  This recipe should be taken down.

I made these tonight & they turned out great! My meringue topping wasn't quite as smooth after baking but still looked gorgeous 
I also doubled the recipe and it fit perfectly in a 13x9 with a little meringue leftover (made meringue cookies with it!)

I LOVED the idea for this recipe, so I tried it out for cookie boxes I wanted to gift to friends. When I made it though (exactly as written), the meringue did not stick to the chocolate base after baking, so when I cut it into shards it just crumbled off and I was unable to use it in my boxes since it was just a big mess. Additionally, it's very dry. I know meringue is supposed to be fairly dry and so is shortbread, but the level of dryness here impeded the flavors.
Wanted these to be as amazing as they look. I even watched the video. Honestly these turned out to just be OK for the effort.

Shortbread is so dry and not sweet enough. I actually wondered if I messed up the recipe until I came to the comments section and saw that this is a shared experience. I just wish I would have read them first. After such a time investment to have inedible shortbread is such a disappointment.

The cookies look a little better than they taste. I found the shortbread too dry and the merinque tasty but not worth the 3 hour cooking time. I agree with the comment that the cookie tasted chalky and was crumbly. It was also difficult to cut without ending up with cracks on the top. Not a repeat.

I was really excited to make these cookies after watching the video and planned on shipping them to my family because we can’t be together for the holidays. Unfortunately the shortbread came out so crumbly it wouldn’t hold its shape when it was cut. The meringue came out great but the texture of the shortbread made it so the meringue topping separated from the shortbread. All in all, I was pretty disappointed and can’t ship this crumbly mess to my relatives.
GF bakers: I made the shortbread with King Arthur gluten free measure for measure flour and it came out perfectly with no other subs or modifications! Go for it!

Appreciate all the notes about the salt - adjusted down to 1/2 tsp of Morton’s.
Also adjusted down the peppermint extract to 1/4 tsp and it was still VERY Minty, enough to linger long after tasting.
I cooked at 350 for 30 minutes checking at 25 minutes. I used the guidance in the video about looking for the smell and dry centre for doneness. It was still slight soft in the centre, but I was hoping to avoid the dry/crumbly cookie some of you mentioned.
Not too sweet! Great for cookie boxes

I used less than 1/4 tsp peppermint extract (because I ran out, not by design). I imagine these would be inedible had I used the full amount. Mine turned out well and fairly close to how the texture looked in the video. My meringue was crispy and my shortbread dense and crumbly.  After storing them in an airtight container at room temp. the shortbread and the meringue are decidedly more chewy. Which IMHO is an improvement. These were fun to make and the video is a delight. Won’t make again.

Shortbread was a dry, powdery mess.  The meringue was a nightmare. I have never had any trouble making meringue.

Recipe was "made for a stand mixer" but the volume of the meringue mixture was such that the whisk does not even touch the substrate. Add a third egg white and increase the other ingredients accordingly. Made two attempts, this could probably be rewritten into an actually useful recipe but it is absolutely not worth the time.
Like others: dry, crumbled when cut (both cookie and meringue). Will make excellent ice cream topping, but otherwise low ROI. Too much bother to ever make again.

Similar end result as others - far too dry and half was reduced to rubble when I tried to cut it.  I guess a good ice cream topping?  Don't know where it went wrong.  I love chocolate-mint combos so was hoping to add this to my holiday baking repertoire.  Not without some tweaks.  Flavor was good, though.  I was originally thinking of foregoing the meringue and just making a peppermint infused white chocolate drizzle - that may be my next attempt.

i read all the notes beforehand and the recipe was great! it is quite lengthy, so i made them in the evening and left them overnight to cool. a couple of things i changed:
-baked the cookies for about 25 minutes and i also left them in the tin when cooking the meringue so the edges would not dry out
-i also don't have a cooking thermometer, so i was nervous the meringue would not work, but i just heated it for about 8 mins and it whipped up fine!
-i reduced the amount of salt to ~1/4 tsp

Really not a good recipe… very dry. No short bread should be in an oven that long- and I under cooked it for fear of dryness per the other comments. I would definitely discourage people from making this. I made the nigella cinnamon squares to replace them. Excellent.

I had the same issues as other commenters: I baked the shortbread and meringue for the amount of time as written, and they both came out very dry. It was my first time making meringue, so maybe I messed something up elsewhere, too. Everything crumbled as soon as I tried to cut it, and some of the meringue topping separated from the shortbread. The flavors were good, but I had to leave them out of the cookie tins.

Note to self: ALWAYS read the comments before you embark on a recipe. The dough is too salty, the meringue not enough to hold the cookie together. I have no idea how the picture on this recipe is possible. We will eat it but it will not be made again.
