Marinated overnight (no obvious difference).  More of the listed spices.  Added garam masala and cumin.  Skipped frizzled onions - yummy but not necessary.

You really need just the cardamom seeds - not the husks too. I'd also question whether a chicken korma is truly authentic with both coconut (ground or cream) and, especially, ground almonds. The amount of liquid (from the yoghurt) in this recipe also appears very mean: a good korma should be lush and creamy.
Who cares if it's "authentic?"  Make something that tastes good.  The way I make something is authentic to me.

Please note that this is specifically a Bangladeshi korma and Sohla is Bengali-American so likely as authentic as they come!  You may be more familiar with the Indian style of korma which is heavier, creamier, and sweeter.

@Janet.  Actually some of us care about authenticity a lot.  I know it's helpful if a 'foreign' recipe is accessible, but we lose a lot by thinking that any old way we want is fine.  Moreover, it's really nice to learn how dishes are made and presented.  In this case, you're actually making Amu's chicken - authentic to her.

This might be one of the best things I've ever made from NYT Cooking.  I only had nonfat plain yogurt so I added that with a touch of sour cream.  Otherwise I made it as written using a cut up whole chicken.  I initially thought there wasn't enough sauce for cooking but the released juices really brought it all together.  I never regret using whole cracked cardamom pods in recipes.  I recommend using these if possible, versus the ground.

Wow. This one is a keeper. I used cubed boneless breast, which is what I had. Next time I will double the recipe. I made white rice and the cucumber tomato salad suggested--the perfect accompaniment. Thank you so much for posting this, Sohla!

I do not know why I trusted this recipe over my truth-telling eyes.  There is no sauce, which is what I love about korma. Monitor it constantly so it does not burn, and your reward will be relatively plain chicken.  Sorry I’m pissy - RBG just died and I made this trying to have comfort food.  Missed the mark.

This was fabulous as written. I might make a full onion's worth of the puree next time, because I'm using a fairly large Dutch oven and it was spread thin. I had to watch it pretty carefully to keep it from burning. But it did come together into a very tasty paste; the sauce being drier allowed the flavors to concentrate and caramelize. I also used turkey breast instead of chicken, which made a nice variation.
I didn't have an onion on hand and made it without one. It turned out great, nonetheless. I used Indian mirchi (Rani brand) instead of green chiles.
Thank you! I don't eat onions and was wondering if it would still be OK. Appreciate the note!

Loved this. As the recipe states, it’s milder than some other restaurant Indian dishes I’ve had. You can’t really taste the individual spices (except cardamom if you try), because they’re a lot of adjacent flavors and they merge. I think adding a bit more ghee and/or yogurt would work, depending on the desired sauciness. Next time I think I’ll try adding some additional Indian spices like cloves or turmeric.

Wow! This is so wildly good.  The smell of the onion paste and spices cooking is ethereal. Marinated overnight and took the meat off the bones for serving. Added some roasted peppers and cilantro to the suggested salad. Big dollops of yogurt in hot pita. Wowza!
Made this last night. Great flavor! I used bone-in chicken thigh, skinless. I crashed the cardamon, otherwise as written. A keeper.

Amazing flavor. Not as “wet” as other chicken kormas I’ve made, maybe because it’s a Bangladeshi variation. Either way, this dish was so good as is. It’s the perfect combo of creamy, sweet and spicy. Thank you Sohla!!
Could you please specify which type of green chili pepper to use? Looking extremely forward to making this dish. Thank you.

If you google Indian green chili you'll see images of a long, dark green chili, also called finger chilis sometimes. Those are the kind we typically use in our cooking. They are available at Asian markets. Only use these if you are comfortable with spicy food, and still best to deseed them to bring down the level of heat.

I would think you could easily use tempeh, but you may need to make up for the lack of fat and savoriness that would’ve come from the lamb.  So maybe some coconut oil, additional spice, and maybe add some other sort of flavorful vegetable to it to give it some extra oomph.
Could i serve this with polenta? I know this is a culture clash, but i’’m trying to go with what i have & I have pot of leftover polenta.

If I made this again I would debone the chicken and cube it before cooking in sauce. The seasonings should be heightened and sauce volume increased. The way it is, the chicken is fairly bland. It may be authentic but I didn't really like it as written.

I didn’t even make this right, accidentally skipping a step and adding the onion purée to the marinade. It still had such an amazing depth of flavor I can’t stop thinking about it and will definitely make it again. Will try the correct version next time!
I cardamom pod is roughly 1/6 teaspoon of ground cardamom
Want to replace the yoghurt with something non-dairy. What do you suggest?
Do you put the cardamom pods in whole, crush them and use seeds and husks or just the seeds?
So disappointing...  I expected a burst of flavor in my mouth and this was very meh.  I wouldn't make again.

I made this with boneless chicken cut into large chunks. Had only about a pound of meat (recipe calls for 4). I halfed all other quantities and it turned out great. The onion pure is a super interesting approach to get a dish started. Had no idea!
OkAy Sohla is a literal queen this was a hit no notes.
I adore this recipe as is. It’s absolutely perfect. Leftover make a delicious sandwich with white bread and Mayo, just as Sohla said.

Enjoyed this as is using a whole cut up chicken. For my taste next time I think I will use all dark meat and use a whole pureed onion in the sauce, double the yogurt, bump up the spices a bit and a smaller onion to caramelize. (I'm a saucy gal). 
Overall, delicious!!!
Up the spice and sauce portions - probably best doubled.
FWIW, a karahi is similar to--but much smaller than--a wok.

Made this with cut up boneless thighs and it was delicious. Don’t understand those saying it came out dry - mine was very saucy.  Used a jalapeño bc it’s what I had and somehow didn’t get any heat from it. Will also prob double down on spices next time bc they were more subtle than I thought they’d be. As long as you do a little mise en place, it’s perfect for my new category  “weeknight recipes  I can make while on the PTA zoom.”

This was outstanding. I actually added a whole onion to the marinade (by mistake but turned out delicious) and doubled all the marinade ingredients so I would have more sauce. I pureed another onion for sautéing along with the spices, and separately caramelized another small onion to add in at the end. Otherwise followed using 3 lbs of skinless drumsticks. Kids and adults gobbled this up. Served with bombay potatoes; squash sautéed with cumin seeds, garlic and tomato paste; garlic naan and rice.
Easily the best thing I’ve made from NYT. We followed directions, used milk, loved it beyond words.

Used a combination of breasts and thighs.  Breasts were large so I sliced them in half.  Used Serrano chilies since we like spicy.  Was delicious.  Added some peas at the end and served with rice.  I would make even more caramelized onions for the topping next time.
I have been making triple batches of the two purees in the blender, and freezing the extra. Filling the blender up more like this seems to get me a smoother texture on the puree.
Honestly needed a splash or two of water once everything was in the pot, but that helped with the braise. Awesome recipe!

I liked this, but the resulting flavor was a bit surprising to me. The sauce was reminiscent of a  mildly spiced chicken gravy, and despite using lots of onions they somehow got lost. I think maybe I would double or triple the onion, caramelize longer, and then pull only half of it out as a topping, leaving the rest in the dish as it simmers to give a deeper umami to the stew. I also reduced for about 1.5 hrs instead of 30 minutes, and didn't need extra water—the chicken was super tender!
Would this work with a vegan meat like tempeh?
1. Use chicken thighs, skinless and boneless. 2. Instead of coriander powder, substitute with poppy seeds, gently roasted and ground to a powder.
Must sautee chicken on medium high fire until you can't see any blood. Then turn the fire on low and simmer while covered.
Tried the recipe today and it came out very well. Used extra onion, ginger, cardamom and garlic as my boys like extra curry with their chappatis.
It's so good. Thank you, Sohla!
Has anyone got any suggestions on how to do this as a vegetarian recipe?
Paneer should work
Leave fried onions for topping only.
Used diced boneless thighs
1/2 the salt! I didn't use as much as it called for, and it still nearly ruined the otherwise wonderful dish.
I can't eat chillis.  Could I cook this without the chillis?
Consider other peppers to add some heat. Used diced jalapeño with seeds and spine removed - - no heat effect.
my family and myself found this blah. Easy to make all components ahead of time.

Who would have thought that such humble ingredients - onion, chicken, yoghurt and a fantastic spice mix would pack in so much depth of flavour? My only tweak was cheating by using bought crispy onions. Home cooking at its best.
Prepared as written.  Recipe was good but lacked complexity and was pretty mild (heat wise).
This is a winner. I would recommend adding more yogurt to increase the amount of sauce, as written it's barely more than a marinade.

I cut the cinnamon by two thirds, added an extra tablespoon of chicken masala, and along with the chicken,  4 large tomatoes cut into quarters and then, during the 30 min simmer, added 4 good squirts of tomato paste. And at the end, I threw on a handful of freshly chopped cilantro. My first time (1) using a food processor, (2) making an NYT recipe, (3) cooking meat, and (4) making anything that wasn’t vegetarian South Indian food. What an amazing experience — thanks guys!!
Different and delicious!

per others’ suggestions. I used 2 lb boneless skinless chicken thighs, slightly more than 1/2 cup of yogurt, 1 big onion for purée and 1 small onions for frying, 6 cloves of garlic, 2 inch of ginger, 1 cinnamon stick, 5 cardamom pods and 2 bay leaves. (not creamy buttery Korma)

About halfway through the 35 min cook I thought it was going wrong and starting to burn; I sloshed in a bunch of chicken broth, scraped up the brown bits and kept it cooking---it's fantastic. Served it with the NYT mint chutney. Worth blowing money on local grass fed ghee
